```
        +----- process return ----------+

   Legend of 6+ P&ID Symbols Used:
   1. P-101         → Centrifugal pump (circle with C)
   2. V-101         → Horizontal vessel (elongated ellipse)
   3. LT-301 (●)    → Field-mounted Level Transmitter
   4. LIC-301 (⊖)   → DCS-mounted Level Indicating Controller
   5. LCV-301       → Level Control Valve (globe body + actuator)
   6. PSV-301       → Pressure Safety Valve (spring-loaded)
   7. - - - - - -   → Dashed line = 4-20 mA electrical signal
   8. ===========   → Solid line = process piping
```

**Key Engineering Concepts Tested:**

- Pump is **discharge** into the vessel.
- Level transmitter is at the **bottom tap** of the vessel (differential or guided wave radar).
- Control valve is on the **outlet** line to regulate drainage.
- PSV protects against **overpressure** scenarios.
- Dashed line represents **electrical signal** per ISA-5.1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Bubble Confusion:** Many students use only plain circles for every instrument. ISA-5.1 distinguishes **field (●)** vs **DCS (⊖)** vs **Computer (⊜)**. A DCS instrument MUST have the horizontal bar through the bubble.
> 2. **Tag Order Error:** Students sometimes write tag letters in the wrong order. Example: Writing "TL" instead of "LT" for a Level Transmitter. The **variable letter ALWAYS comes first**.
> 3. **Signal Line Mixing:** Using solid lines for signals and dashed for process. This is the **opposite** of the standard. Solid = process, dashed = signal.
> 4. **Valve Symbol Incomplete:** Drawing the body but forgetting the **actuator** (spring, piston, diaphragm, motor, solenoid).
> 5. **Forgetting the Loop Number:** A tag without a number is incomplete. A bubble labelled only "FT" without "-101" loses at least 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 6. **PSV vs Control Valve Confusion:** PSV has a specific **spring-loaded body** symbol — do not draw it like a globe control valve.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes:**

- ✅ **ISA-5.1** is the master standard for P&ID symbols. Memorize: bubbles, tag format, signal lines.
- ✅ **Tag Format** = `1st Letter (Variable) + Subsequent Letters (Functions) + Loop Number`.
- ✅ **Bubble Types:** ● Field, ⊖ DCS, ⊜ Computer/PLC.
- ✅ **Signal Lines:** Solid = Process, Dashed = Pneumatic/Electric, Dotted = Software.
- ✅ **First letter** = measured variable (F, P, L, T, A, etc.).
- ✅ **Subsequent letters** = function (T=Transmit, I=Indicate, R=Record, C=Control, V=Valve, S=Switch, A=Alarm, H=High, L=Low, Y=Relay).
- ✅ **Valve Symbol = Body + Actuator**. Always include both.
- ✅ **Actuator Types:** Diaphragm, Piston, Spring, Manual Handwheel, Solenoid, Motor.
- ✅ **PSV symbol** = spring-loaded + valve body. PSV opens at set pressure.
- ✅ **Differential modifier "D"** appears right after the first letter: `PDT`, `PDIT`.
- ✅ **Loop number** is typically 3-4 digits; the first digit often indicates plant area.
- ✅ **Suffix A/B/C** indicates redundant or parallel instruments (e.g., TT-101A, TT-101B).
- ✅ **P&ID is a schematic** — not to scale. Pipe sizes are written as annotations.
- ✅ For KTU exams: always show **signal flow direction** with arrows on dashed lines.
- ✅ Master at least **30 symbols** (10 instruments, 8 valves, 6 equipment, 6 actuator/signal types) to score 14/14.
- ✅ When asked to "explain a P&ID", follow the **8-step interpretation framework** from Section 4.3.
- ✅ Cross-verify **process intent** vs **loop intent** to ensure the diagram makes engineering sense (e.g., level control valve should be on the **outlet** line, not the inlet).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line Numbering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Line Numbering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Line Numbering** in Instrumentation System Design refers to the systematic assignment of unique logical addresses (numerical identifiers) to multiple field instruments, sensors, actuators, or data acquisition nodes that share a common physical communication line (bus or channel). It is a fundamental protocol-level addressing mechanism that enables a master controller, PLC, RTU, or central data logger to unambiguously identify, poll, command, and receive data from a specific device among many connected in parallel on a single transmission medium.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Line Numbering is the architectural decision that converts a *physically shared* communication channel into a *logically partitioned* set of independent point-to-point virtual links, each uniquely identified by an integer tag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Line Numbering is the foundation of multi-drop instrumentation networks (e.g., RS-485 multidrop, HART, Foundation Fieldbus, IEEE 1451 smart transducer interface). Without a robust line numbering scheme, no supervisory controller can disambiguate the source or destination of any telemetry frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction to Remember:** *Line Numbering* is **not** the same as *Wiring/Channel Numbering*. Line numbering is a **logical software-protocol address**, whereas channel numbering refers to the **physical pin or wire** on a connector or terminal block.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hostel Room Numbering System

Imagine a long hostel corridor (the shared communication line) with 64 identical rooms. Every room has the same furniture, the same paint, the same doorbell. Without a unique room number painted on each door, the warden shouting *"Breakfast is ready!"* would create chaos — every student would walk out of every room.

The warden solves this by assigning **Room Numbers** (Line Numbers): *Room 01, Room 02, … Room 64*. Now when the warden wants only the student in Room 27 to come for a parent meeting, he shouts *"Room 27, please come to the office."* Only that one student responds.

In instrumentation:
- **Corridor** = RS-485 twisted pair / two-wire bus
- **Rooms** = field sensors (temperature, pressure, flow transmitters)
- **Room numbers** = Line Numbers (1, 2, 3 … N)
- **Warden** = Master controller (PLC / SCADA / DCS controller)
- **Shout** = Poll/Request frame

Each device wakes up and replies **only when its line number is called**, just as each student responds only when their room number is announced.

## 1.3 Why Line Numbering is Necessary in Instrumentation

| Engineering Need | Role of Line Numbering |
|---|---|
| Multiple sensors on one cable | Provides unique identity to each slave |
| Cable cost reduction | Enables multi-drop topology instead of star wiring |
| Deterministic polling | Master sequentially addresses known line numbers |
| Fault localization | A non-responding line number pinpoints the failed device |
| Scalability | New devices can be appended by extending the line number range |
| Redundancy | Dual-redundant masters can be addressed by separate numbering planes |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards Referenced

- **ASCII printable range for tag IDs:** 0x20 to 0x7E (32 to 126 decimal)
- **Industrial tag length (HART/Fieldbus):** 8 characters typical, 32 characters maximum
- **Maximum recommended nodes on an RS-485 line:** **32** unit loads (per EIA-485 standard), expandable to **256** with modern transceivers
- **Foundation Fieldbus address range:** 0 to 255 (8-bit line number)
- **PROFIBUS DP address range:** 0 to 127 (7-bit line number, address 126 reserved for commissioning)
- **HART short frame address:** 0 to 15 (4-bit in polling mode)
- **IEC 61131-3 compliant tag naming:** Begins with a letter, followed by letters/digits/underscore, max 24 characters

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard Reference:** The **IEEE 1451** family of standards (specifically IEEE 1451.2 and IEEE 1451.4) defines the *Transducer Electronic Data Sheet (TEDS)* and the 8-bit *Node Address / Line Number* field that uniquely identifies each smart transducer on a multidrop line.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Line Number

A complete line number in a modern instrumentation protocol typically consists of **three logical sub-fields**, even when transmitted as a single integer byte:

1. **Network Segment Identifier** (high-order bits) — Which physical cable / segment
2. **Device Address** (middle bits) — Which node on the segment
3. **Port / Channel Sub-address** (low-order bits) — Which sub-channel within the node (e.g., channel A, B, C, D of a 4-channel RTD module)

This three-level hierarchy is what allows a single project to scale from a 10-sensor pilot plant to a 10,000-sensor refinery without renumbering.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The Polling Sequence

The line numbering system operates through a deterministic **Master–Slave Polling** cycle. The logic unfolds in these six steps:

1. **Master initialization:** The master controller loads a **polling table** that contains every valid line number on the bus, in scan order.
2. **Frame transmission:** The master transmits a request frame containing the *target line number* in its address field.
3. **Slave filtering:** Every slave on the bus receives the frame, but only the slave whose *internally configured line number* matches the address field activates its transmitter and prepares a response.
4. **Response transmission:** The addressed slave returns a data frame whose header echoes the line number, allowing the master to confirm the source.
5. **Master acknowledgment:** The master validates the echoed line number, processes the payload (e.g., 4–20 mA equivalent in engineering units), and moves to the next line number in the table.
6. **Watchdog / Timeout:** If no response is received within the *line-number-timeout* (typically 1× to 3× the worst-case propagation delay), the master flags that line number as **FAILED** and raises an alarm in the HMI/SCADA layer.

## 2.3 Address Capacity — The Fundamental Counting Relation

The number of unique line numbers a protocol can support is governed by the **bit-width of the address field** in its frame format:

$$N_{\text{addresses}} \;=\; 2^{n} \;-\; N_{\text{reserved}}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of address bits in the frame
- $N_{\text{reserved}}$ = number of address values reserved for broadcast, master, and diagnostics

For a 4-bit address (HART short frame), this gives $2^{4} - 1 = 15$ usable slave addresses plus 1 broadcast (15). For an 8-bit address (Fieldbus, Modbus RTU), it gives $2^{8} - 3 = 253$ usable addresses (0 is broadcast, 247–255 reserved in Modbus).

## 2.4 Polling Cycle Time — The Scan-Time Equation

The total time taken by the master to complete one full poll of all line numbers on a single bus is the **scan time**, a critical metric in real-time control:

$$T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{M}\Bigl(t_{\text{frame},i} \;+\; t_{\text{prop},i} \;+\; t_{\text{proc},i} \;+\; t_{\text{turnaround},i} \;+\; t_{\text{guard},i}\Bigr) \;+\; t_{\text{overhead}}$$

For a homogeneous network where every line number is identical in its parameters, this simplifies to:

$$T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; M \cdot t_{\text{node}} \;+\; t_{\text{overhead}}$$

where:
- $M$ = number of line numbers (slaves) on the bus
- $t_{\text{node}}$ = per-node response + propagation + turnaround time
- $t_{\text{overhead}}$ = master processing, inter-frame gap, and any retries

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address capacity | $N = 2^{n} - N_{\text{reserved}}$ | $n$ = address bits; $N_{\text{reserved}}$ = reserved codes | Sizing the address field of a custom protocol |
| 2 | Scan time (homogeneous) | $T_{\text{scan}} = M \cdot t_{\text{node}} + t_{\text{overhead}}$ | $M$ = nodes; $t_{\text{node}}$ in seconds | Verifying whether the bus meets control-loop deadlines |
| 3 | Propagation delay | $t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{L}{v_{\text{prop}} \cdot c}$ | $L$ = cable length (m); $v_{\text{prop}} \approx 0.66$ (velocity factor); $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | Worst-case line-number timeout calculation |
| 4 | Throughput efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{\text{data}}}{t_{\text{data}} + t_{\text{overhead}}}$ | dimensionless, 0 to 1 | Comparing line-numbering efficiency between protocols |
| 5 | Maximum cable length (RS-485) | $L_{\max} = \dfrac{10^{7}}{R_{\text{bps}} \cdot \text{(in metres, at 10 Mbps → 10 m)}}$ | empirical from EIA-485 | Physical layer design for line-number network |
| 6 | Unit-load check | $N_{\text{real}} \leq 32 \times \text{(UL rating of transceiver)}$ | Unit Loads (UL) | RS-485 multi-drop feasibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall Rule:** When the professor asks *"How many devices can you connect?"* the **complete** answer is the *minimum* of: (a) address capacity $N$, (b) electrical unit-load limit (32 UL for legacy RS-485), and (c) scan-time deadline from the control specification. The address bits alone do **not** determine feasibility.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Line Numbering is the silent workhorse behind:
- **SCADA systems** in power-grid substations — every feeder has a line number
- **DCS in oil & gas** — wellhead pressure/temperature transmitters on a single Fieldbus H1 segment
- **Automotive CAN bus** — every ECU has an 11-bit or 29-bit arbitration ID
- **Building automation (BACnet MS-TP)** — every VAV box has a MAC address
- **Smart transducer plug-and-play (IEEE 1451.2)** — the 8-bit node ID lets a transducer identify itself automatically when plugged into the bus

Without line numbering, these systems would degenerate into an undifferentiated "data soup" — every controller would hear every frame with no way to know *who* is talking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Address Capacity from Frame Bit-Width

We derive the maximum number of unique line numbers a protocol can support, given the bit-width of its address field.

**Step 1 — Frame format definition.**  
A generic instrumentation frame has the structure:

$$F = \{\,S,\; A_{n},\; D,\; P\,\}$$

where $S$ is the start delimiter (preamble), $A_{n}$ is the $n$-bit address field, $D$ is the data payload, and $P$ is the parity/CRC trailer.

**Step 2 — Total states of the address field.**  
An $n$-bit field can take $2^{n}$ distinct binary states. Each state is, in principle, a candidate line number.

$$\text{Total states} = 2^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract reserved states.**  
Industry convention reserves a small subset of states for: (a) *broadcast* (master speaks to all slaves), (b) *master itself* (so a slave does not echo to the master), and (c) *diagnostic/service* addresses. If $R$ states are reserved:

$$\text{Usable line numbers} = 2^{n} - R$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{usable}} = 2^{n} - R\,}$$

**Step 4 — Worked example for Modbus RTU.**  
Modbus RTU uses 8-bit addressing with $R = 3$ reserved values (0 = broadcast, 248–255 = reserved per Modbus organization; 0 already counted in broadcast). Taking the canonical Modbus specification:

$$N_{\text{usable}} = 2^{8} - 3 = 256 - 3 = 253 \text{ slave addresses}$$

**Step 5 — Worked example for HART short frame.**  
HART short frame uses 4-bit address with $R = 1$ (address 0 is the broadcast "all" address; the master itself does not have a slave address). So:

$$N_{\text{usable}} = 2^{4} - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15 \text{ slave addresses}$$

> [!NOTE]
> HART long frame uses 5 bytes (40 bits), of which only 38 bits are address (1 byte "master" + 5 bytes "long address"), giving vastly larger capacity. The 4-bit short-frame limit of 15 is what the KTU examiner will expect for an *introductory* answer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Worst-Case Scan Time for a Homogeneous Multi-Drop Bus

We derive the time taken by a master to poll every line number exactly once on a bus where all $M$ slaves have equal response characteristics.

**Step 1 — Per-node time budget.**  
A single poll-response round trip for line number $i$ consumes:

$$t_{\text{node},i} = t_{\text{frame},i} + t_{\text{prop},\rightarrow} + t_{\text{proc},i} + t_{\text{prop},\leftarrow} + t_{\text{turnaround},i} + t_{\text{guard},i}$$

**Step 2 — Two-way propagation simplification.**  
Since the bus length is the same for every node (worst case = farthest node), the two propagation terms can be combined. For the maximum cable length $L$ at propagation velocity factor $v_p$:

$$t_{\text{prop},\rightarrow} + t_{\text{prop},\leftarrow} = \frac{2L}{v_p \cdot c}$$

**Step 3 — Homogeneous assumption.**  
In a *homogeneous* multi-drop (same baud, same cable, same transceivers), every $t_{\text{node},i}$ is identical:

$$t_{\text{node},i} = t_{\text{node}} \quad \forall i$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all line numbers.**  
The total scan time over $M$ line numbers becomes:

$$T_{\text{scan}} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} t_{\text{node},i} + t_{\text{overhead}}$$

Substituting the homogeneous constant:

$$\boxed{\,T_{\text{scan}} = M \cdot t_{\text{node}} + t_{\text{overhead}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration.**  
A refinery DCS segment has $M = 16$ transmitters on a 1 km cable. Baud rate = 9600 bps, frame size = 20 bytes = 160 bits. Frame time = 160/9600 ≈ 16.67 ms. Two-way propagation at $v_p = 0.66$:

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{2 \times 1000}{0.66 \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 10.1~\mu s$$

Negligible compared to frame time. Turnaround ≈ 1 ms, guard ≈ 0.5 ms, processing ≈ 2 ms. So:

$$t_{\text{node}} \approx 16.67 + 0.01 + 2 + 1 + 0.5 = 20.18~\text{ms}$$

$$T_{\text{scan}} = 16 \times 20.18 + 5 \approx 327.9~\text{ms}$$

This is well within a 1-second update requirement, confirming feasibility.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Protocol Line-Numbering Schemes

| Protocol | Address Bits (n) | Usable Line Numbers (N) | Reserved Addresses | Typical Cable | Scan Cadence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HART short frame** | 4 | 15 | 0 (broadcast) | 2-wire point-to-point or multi-drop | 3–4 per second |
| **Modbus RTU** | 8 | 247 | 0, 248–255 | RS-485 2-wire | 100 ms – 1 s |
| **PROFIBUS DP** | 7 | 126 | 0, 126, 127 | RS-485 2-wire | 5–10 ms |
| **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | 8 | 255 | 0, 255 | 2-wire, 31.25 kbps | configurable LB |
| **CAN 2.0A** | 11 | 2,046 | reserved error frames | 2-wire differential | 1 ms typical |
| **CAN 2.0B Extended** | 29 | 536,870,911 | SAE J1939 PS range | 2-wire differential | 1–10 ms |
| **EtherCAT / EtherNet/IP** | 48 (MAC) | 2^48 theoretical | multicast ranges | Cat-5/6 | 100 µs cycle |
| **IEEE 1451.2** | 8 | 254 | 0, 255 | 4-wire (powered) | hot-plug |
| **RS-485 generic (legacy)** | 8 | 253 | 0, 254, 255 | twisted pair, ≤1.2 km | baud-dependent |
| **BACnet MS-TP** | 8 | 254 | 255 (broadcast) | EIA-485 | 1 s – 10 s |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — "How Many Sensors on a 7-bit Line?"

**Problem (KTU 2024 style):** A custom instrumentation protocol uses a 7-bit address field. Two values (0 and 127) are reserved for broadcast and master. How many sensors can be uniquely line-numbered on a single bus?

**Solution:**

$$\text{Total states} = 2^{7} = 128$$

$$\text{Reserved} = 2 \;(\text{broadcast} + \text{master})$$

$$N_{\text{usable}} = 128 - 2 = 126 \text{ sensors}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Trick:** Many students answer "128" and lose 2 marks. The reserved-address deduction is **always** expected at KTU level.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Line Numbering in a Master–Slave Multi-Drop

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts a master controller addressing four field instruments (each with a unique line number) on a shared two-wire RS-485 bus.

```mermaid
graph LR
    masterA[Master Controller PLC] -->|Poll Frame Address 01| busA[Shared RS485 Two Wire Bus]
    masterB[Master Controller PLC] -->|Poll Frame Address 02| busA
    masterC[Master Controller PLC] -->|Poll Frame Address 03| busA
    masterD[Master Controller PLC] -->|Poll Frame Address 04| busA
    busA -->|Respond Only If Match| slave1[Temp Transmitter Line Number 01]
    busA -->|Respond Only If Match| slave2[Pressure Transmitter Line Number 02]
    busA -->|Respond Only If Match| slave3[Flow Transmitter Line Number 03]
    busA -->|Respond Only If Match| slave4[Level Transmitter Line Number 04]
    slave1 -->|Engineering Units| hmiA[HMI SCADA Layer]
    slave2 -->|Engineering Units| hmiA
    slave3 -->|Engineering Units| hmiA
    slave4 -->|Engineering Units| hmiA
```

## 4.2 Line Number Polling Sequence Flow

The diagram below traces the master’s sequential scan across all line numbers, with a watchdog/timeout branch for any non-responsive device.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start of Scan Cycle] --> initA[Load Polling Table Line 01 to Line M]
    initA --> sendA[Transmit Poll Frame with Target Line Number k]
    sendA --> waitA[Wait for Response within Timeout]
    waitA -->|Response Received| valA[Validate Echoed Line Number]
    waitA -->|Timeout Exceeded| failA[Flag Line Number k as FAILED Log Alarm]
    valA -->|Valid| procA[Process Payload Update HMI]
    valA -->|Invalid CRC| errA[Increment CRC Error Counter]
    procA --> nextA[k equals k plus 1]
    errA --> nextA
    failA --> nextA
    nextA -->|k less than M| sendA
    nextA -->|k equals M| endA[End of Scan Cycle Restart]
```

## 4.3 Line-Number Address Field Bit Map

The diagram below illustrates the three-tier logical hierarchy of a line number (Segment, Device, Sub-Channel) packed into a single 16-bit field for a custom industrial protocol.

```mermaid
graph LR
    wordA[16 Bit Line Number Word] --> segA[Bits 15 to 12 Segment ID 0 to 15]
    wordA --> devA[Bits 11 to 4 Device Address 0 to 255]
    wordA --> subA[Bits 3 to 0 Sub Channel 0 to 15]
    segA --> descA[Identifies which physical bus segment in a multi segment plant]
    devA --> descB[Identifies which node on the chosen segment]
    subA --> descC[Identifies which channel within a multi channel device]
```

## 4.4 Address-Reservation Layout — Typical 8-Bit Fieldbus

```mermaid
graph LR
    mapA[8 Bit Address Space 0 to 255] --> brA[Address 0 Broadcast]
    mapA --> usrA[Address 1 to 253 Usable Line Numbers]
    mapA --> rsvA[Address 254 255 Reserved for Service and Diagnostics]
    brA --> brB[Master addresses all slaves simultaneously]
    usrA --> usrB[Unique line numbers assigned to each field device]
    rsvA --> rsvB[Handheld configurator service tool addresses]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Define Line Numbering in an instrumentation system. Why is it essential in a multi-drop communication network?**

**Model Answer:**

Line Numbering is the assignment of a unique logical numerical address (typically an integer) to each field device — sensor, transmitter, or actuator — connected to a shared physical communication line, allowing the master controller to unambiguously identify and communicate with a specific device.

It is essential in multi-drop networks for three reasons:

1. **Disambiguation:** Multiple devices share the same physical cable; without unique addresses, the master cannot determine *which* device sent a particular response.
2. **Selective Polling:** The master can query devices individually by transmitting the target line number, enabling deterministic round-robin or event-driven scans.
3. **Fault Diagnostics:** A timeout on a specific line number immediately pinpoints the failed device for maintenance, instead of indicating a generic bus failure.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]

**A custom protocol has a 6-bit address field. One address is reserved for broadcast. Calculate the maximum number of field devices that can be uniquely line-numbered on a single bus.**

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- Address bit width, $n = 6$
- Reserved codes, $R = 1$ (broadcast)

Applying the address-capacity formula:

$$N_{\text{usable}} = 2^{n} - R = 2^{6} - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63 \text{ devices}$$

**Answer: 63 field devices** can be uniquely line-numbered on the bus.

(3 marks: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptor Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**  
Explain the concept of Line Numbering in a multi-drop instrumentation network. With a neat block diagram, describe how a master controller addresses four field instruments connected to a shared RS-485 bus. Discuss the role of broadcast and reserved addresses.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**  
A HART short-frame network uses 4-bit addressing with one address reserved for broadcast. A FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 segment uses 8-bit addressing with two addresses reserved. Compare the maximum number of addressable devices on each network. If a refinery requires **300 instruments** on a single segment, which protocol (or modification) would you recommend and why? Justify with a numerical example.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Line Numbering assigns a unique integer address to each field device on a shared bus, enabling the master to communicate selectively. Refer Section 1.1 of these notes for the formal definition.

2. **Block diagram (2 marks):** The Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 illustrates the four slaves (Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level) on a single RS-485 bus, with the master transmitting poll frames containing the unique line numbers 01, 02, 03, 04.

3. **Operational description (2 marks):** The master transmits a request frame with the target line number. Every slave receives the frame, but only the slave whose internally configured line number matches the address field activates its transmitter and returns a response. The master validates the echoed line number, processes the data, and moves to the next line number in its polling table. A watchdog timer flags any non-responsive line number.

4. **Role of broadcast and reserved addresses (2 marks):**
   - **Broadcast address** (typically 0): Allows the master to send a single command (e.g., *zero calibration*, *reset all*) that every slave receives and acts upon simultaneously — saving scan time for global operations.
   - **Reserved addresses** (e.g., 254, 255): Used for service tools, handheld configurators, or future expansion. They must never be assigned to operational field devices, otherwise collisions with maintenance tools would corrupt production data.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — HART short frame capacity (1 mark):**

$$N_{\text{HART}} = 2^{4} - 1 = 15 \text{ devices}$$

**Step 2 — Foundation Fieldbus H1 capacity (1 mark):**

$$N_{\text{FF}} = 2^{8} - 2 = 254 \text{ devices}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**  
HART short frame supports only 15 devices, while Fieldbus H1 supports 254 devices — about **17× more**. Both are insufficient for the 300-instrument requirement stated in the problem.

**Step 4 — Recommendation (2 marks):**  
Since 300 > 254, neither HART short frame nor standard 8-bit Fieldbus is sufficient on a *single* segment. Two viable options:

- **Option A (Recommended):** Use **two parallel Fieldbus H1 segments**, each carrying ≤ 150 devices. This keeps scan time manageable and respects the 32-unit-load electrical limit (a Fieldbus H1 segment supports 32–64 physical devices depending on power consumption; the *address* field alone is not the binding constraint — the *electrical unit load* is).
- **Option B:** Use **HART long-frame mode** (5-byte address, up to $2^{40}$ devices) over a single bus. This eliminates the addressing bottleneck but may not be supported by legacy instruments.

**Step 5 — Justification with numerical example (2 marks):**  
For Option A: With 150 devices on a 1 km Fieldbus H1 segment at 31.25 kbps and 32 bytes/frame, $t_{\text{frame}} \approx 8.2$ ms. Total scan time:

$$T_{\text{scan}} = 150 \times 8.2 + 20 \approx 1.25~\text{seconds}$$

This meets typical refinery control loops (1–2 s update) and is recommended. ✦

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**  
Derive the relationship between the number of address bits and the number of line numbers that can be assigned on a multi-drop bus. Apply this derivation to a Modbus RTU network (8-bit address, 3 reserved codes) and a HART short frame (4-bit address, 1 reserved code). Comment on the practical implication when the project size exceeds the address capacity of a chosen protocol.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**  
A multi-drop RS-485 bus connects **20 identical field transmitters** to a master controller. The frame size is 32 bytes, baud rate is 19,200 bps, cable length is 500 m, velocity factor is 0.66, processing time per slave is 3 ms, turnaround time is 0.5 ms, and inter-frame guard is 0.2 ms. Compute the worst-case scan time. If the control system requires the bus to be scanned in **less than 1 second**, state whether the design is feasible.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Derivation (3 marks):**  
Let the address field in the frame contain $n$ bits. The number of distinct binary patterns representable is $2^{n}$. From these, $R$ patterns are reserved for broadcast, master, diagnostics, or future use. Hence:

$$N_{\text{usable line numbers}} = 2^{n} - R$$

**Step 2 — Modbus RTU application (1.5 marks):**  
$n = 8$, $R = 3$ (per Modbus organization convention: address 0 = broadcast, addresses 248–255 = reserved; counting 0 plus two from 248–255 as canonical reserved for broadcast + 2 reserved = 3, as typically taught).

$$N_{\text{Modbus}} = 2^{8} - 3 = 253 \text{ devices}$$

**Step 3 — HART short frame application (1.5 marks):**  
$n = 4$, $R = 1$ (address 0 = broadcast only).

$$N_{\text{HART}} = 2^{4} - 1 = 15 \text{ devices}$$

**Step 4 — Practical implication (1 mark):**  
When project size exceeds the address capacity of a protocol, the engineer must either (a) split the network into multiple physical segments, each with its own address space, (b) upgrade to a protocol with a wider address field (e.g., HART long frame, CAN 2.0B with 29-bit ID), or (c) introduce a hierarchical addressing scheme (segment + device + sub-channel) using the three-tier bit map described in Section 4.3 of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Frame transmission time (1.5 marks):**  
Frame size = 32 bytes = 256 bits. Baud rate = 19,200 bps.

$$t_{\text{frame}} = \frac{256}{19{,}200} \approx 13.33~\text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Two-way propagation delay (1.5 marks):**  
Cable length $L = 500$ m, velocity factor $v_p = 0.66$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{2L}{v_p \cdot c} = \frac{2 \times 500}{0.66 \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 5.05~\mu s$$

(Negligible compared to frame time — safely ignored for the scan-time estimate.)

**Step 3 — Per-node time (1.5 marks):**

$$t_{\text{node}} = t_{\text{frame}} + t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{proc}} + t_{\text{turnaround}} + t_{\text{guard}}$$

$$t_{\text{node}} = 13.33 + 0.005 + 3 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 17.035~\text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — Total scan time (1.5 marks):**  
$M = 20$ slaves, overhead $t_{\text{overhead}} \approx 5$ ms (master processing).

$$T_{\text{scan}} = M \cdot t_{\text{node}} + t_{\text{overhead}} = 20 \times 17.035 + 5 = 345.7~\text{ms}$$

**Step 5 — Feasibility check (1 mark):**  
Required: $T_{\text{scan}} < 1000$ ms.  
Computed: $T_{\text{scan}} = 345.7$ ms.

**Result: 345.7 ms < 1000 ms ⇒ the design is FEASIBLE.** The bus will be scanned approximately 2.9 times per second, comfortably meeting the 1-second control deadline. ✦

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the reserved-address deduction:** Students frequently write $N = 2^{n}$ without subtracting reserved codes. Always reserve at least one for broadcast. (Loss: up to 1 mark per question)
> 2. **Confusing address capacity with electrical capacity:** RS-485 is limited to 32 unit loads electrically, even if your 8-bit address field can hold 253 nodes. The binding constraint is whichever is *smaller*.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** Frame time must use **bits**, not bytes. Baud rate is in **bits per second**, not bytes per second. (Loss: 0.5 mark per slip)
> 4. **Dropping the velocity factor:** Propagation delay at the speed of *light in vacuum* is physically impossible in a copper cable. Always use $v_p \cdot c$.
> 5. **Mixing polling with broadcast:** Broadcast does not "scan" all devices — it is a one-to-many command. Polling is one-to-one. Examiners will deduct for confusing the two.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Line Numbering** = unique integer address per device on a shared bus; it is a *logical*, not *physical*, identifier.
- The **address capacity formula** $N = 2^{n} - R$ is the most-tested equation in this module — memorize it, its variables, and its units.
- **Reserved addresses** (broadcast, master, service) are *always* subtracted from the gross $2^{n}$ count.
- **HART short frame** = 15 devices; **Modbus RTU** = 247 (or 253 depending on convention); **Fieldbus H1** = 254; **PROFIBUS** = 126.
- **Three-tier hierarchy** of a line number: Segment ID + Device Address + Sub-Channel — enables large plants with small address fields.
- **Scan time** is the *sum* of per-node times plus overhead; for a homogeneous bus, $T_{\text{scan}} = M \cdot t_{\text{node}} + t_{\text{overhead}}$.
- **Propagation delay** uses $v_p \cdot c$ (velocity factor × speed of light), not $c$ alone.
- The **binding constraint** for a multi-drop network is the *minimum* of: (a) address bits, (b) electrical unit loads, and (c) scan-time deadline.
- **Broadcast address** (typically 0) is a one-to-many command, not a polled device — never count it in usable device count.
- **Watchdog timeout** for a line number = 1× to 3× the worst-case propagation + processing delay; if exceeded, that line number is flagged as FAILED.
- **Hot-plug & plug-and-play** instruments (IEEE 1451.2) use the line-number slot to auto-announce themselves when added to the bus, simplifying commissioning.
- **KTU 2024 Examiner Loves:** numerical problems combining $N = 2^{n} - R$ with $T_{\text{scan}}$ calculation in the same question — practice this combination.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line Schedule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Line Schedule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Line Schedule (also called Line List or Piping Line Class):** A line schedule is a comprehensive **tabular engineering document** that consolidates all essential design, operational, and material data for every individual process pipe (line) in a plant. It serves as the *master data sheet* that links the **P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)**, **Plot Plan**, **Equipment List**, and **Instrument Index** into a single, traceable information repository.

In a KTU/industrial context, a line schedule is the **single source of truth (SSOT)** for every pipe segment — it tells engineers, designers, fabricators, and instrument engineers exactly *what* a line is made of, *how* it operates, *how* it is tested, and *how* it is insulated.

The line schedule is governed by:
- **ASME B31.1** — Power Piping
- **ASME B31.3** — Process Piping
- **ISA-5.1** — Instrumentation Symbols and Identification
- **PIP (Process Industry Practices)** — PIC001, PLE00001

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a line schedule as the "Aadhaar Card / Medical Record" of every pipe in a plant.**

Imagine a hospital patient record:
- **Patient ID** ↔ **Line Number** (e.g., `6"-HC-1001-P`)
- **Patient Name** ↔ **Service Description** (e.g., "Hot Condensate from E-101")
- **Blood Group / Allergies** ↔ **Material of Construction, Corrosion Allowance**
- **Vital signs (BP, Temp)** ↔ **Operating Pressure, Operating Temperature**
- **Treatment plan** ↔ **Insulation, Tracing, Painting specifications**
- **Doctor notes** ↔ **Remarks, Special Instructions**

Just as a doctor never treats a patient without checking the medical file, **no engineer can design, fabricate, install, or instrument a pipe without consulting the line schedule first**. Lose the line schedule, and the plant becomes a maze of unidentified pipes — a literal "ghost town" of instrumentation.

---

## 1.3 Why Line Schedule is the *Backbone* of Instrumentation Design

In an instrumentation project, the line schedule plays three critical roles:

| Role | Explanation |
|------|-------------|
| **🔗 Cross-Reference Hub** | Connects every line to its **P&ID, Plot Plan, Instrument Index, Equipment List, and Purchase Order**. |
| **🛠️ Construction Bible** | Fabricators use it to know pipe size, schedule, material, fittings, flanges, gaskets, and bolts. |
| **🎯 Instrument Sizing Input** | Instrument engineers extract **flow, pressure, temperature, and phase data** to size flowmeters, control valves, safety valves, and pressure transmitters. |

> [!TIP]
> **Without a line schedule, you cannot select an orifice plate size, calibrate a control valve, or specify a thermowell insertion length.** It is the *first document* the instrument engineer requests at project kickoff.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Conventions

- **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS):** Measured in **inches** (e.g., 2", 6", 24")
- **Pipe Schedule (SCH):** Defines wall thickness — common values: **SCH 10, SCH 20, SCH 40, SCH 80, SCH 160, XXS**
- **Design Pressure:** Typically **10% above Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**
- **Design Temperature:** **25 °C above** Maximum Operating Temperature (per ASME B31.3 §301.2)
- **Corrosion Allowance:** Usually **1.5 mm to 3 mm** for carbon steel; **0 mm** for SS (in most services)
- **Test Pressure (Hydrotest):** **1.5 × Design Pressure** (per ASME B31.3 §345.4)

---

## 1.5 Typical Line Designation Format (ISA-5.1 / PIP Standard)

A KTU-style line designation is read from **left to right** as follows:

$$ \text{Line Tag} = \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Size (NPS)}} - \underbrace{\text{YY}}_{\text{Service Code}} - \underbrace{\text{ZZZZ}}_{\text{Seq. Number}} - \underbrace{\text{A}}_{\text{Class}} $$

**Example:** `6"-HC-1001-P`

| Field | Value | Meaning |
|-------|-------|---------|
| Size | 6" | Nominal Pipe Size = 6 inches |
| Service Code | HC | Hot Condensate |
| Sequence Number | 1001 | Unique plant-wide identifier |
| Class Code | P | Process class (per piping class table) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Line Schedule

A complete line schedule is a **multi-column master table**. Below is the **KTU-recommended column structure** (typically 20–35 columns in industrial projects, simplified to ~15 for academic use):

| # | Column Name | Engineering Purpose |
|---|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Line Number** | Unique plant-wide identifier (cross-ref to P&ID) |
| 2 | **Service** | Description of fluid / purpose |
| 3 | **Size (NPS)** | Nominal bore in inches |
| 4 | **Schedule / Wall Thickness** | Mechanical strength rating |
| 5 | **Piping Class** | Material + rating combo (e.g., A1, B3, S6) |
| 6 | **Material of Construction (MOC)** | Carbon Steel, SS304, SS316, CS+SS Lined, etc. |
| 7 | **Operating Pressure (OP)** | Bar(g) at normal flow |
| 8 | **Operating Temperature (OT)** | °C at normal flow |
| 9 | **Design Pressure (DP)** | OP × 1.1 (or process max × 1.1) |
| 10 | **Design Temperature (DT)** | OT + 25 °C (per ASME B31.3) |
| 11 | **Phase** | Liquid / Vapor / Two-Phase / Slurry / Gas |
| 12 | **Flow Rate** | kg/hr or m³/hr |
| 13 | **Density / Viscosity** | For flowmeter sizing |
| 14 | **Insulation Type & Thickness** | Mineral wool, calcium silicate, cellular glass |
| 15 | **Tracing** | Steam-trace / Electric-trace / None |
| 16 | **Painting / Coating** | Primer, epoxy, galvanized |
| 17 | **Hydrotest Pressure** | 1.5 × Design Pressure |
| 18 | **Corrosion Allowance** | mm |
| 19 | **P&ID Reference** | DWG-D-101 Rev-3 |
| 20 | **Remarks** | Special notes (NACE, Sour service, Fire-safe, etc.) |

---

## 2.2 Standard Service Codes (PIP / ISA Convention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Service codes are the **shorthand language** of a line schedule. KTU examiners expect you to know at least 8–10 common ones.

| Code | Service | Code | Service |
|------|---------|------|---------|
| **CW** | Cooling Water | **HC** | Hot Condensate |
| **PW** | Potable Water | **SC** | Steam Condensate |
| **RW** | Raw Water | **MS** | Medium Pressure Steam |
| **FW** | Fire Water | **LS** | Low Pressure Steam |
| **N2** | Nitrogen | **HPS** | High Pressure Steam |
| **AR** | Air | **V** | Vent |
| **IA** | Instrument Air | **D** | Drain |
| **NG** | Natural Gas | **O** | Oil |
| **CWR** | Cooling Water Return | **CWS** | Cooling Water Supply |
| **ALK** | Caustic | **HCl** | Hydrochloric Acid |
| **PR** | Process | **P** | Process (Generic) |
| **SL** | Slurry | **FL** | Flare |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) 🧮

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are **mandatory derivations** for any KTU 2024 problem on line schedule / piping design.

### **Formula 1: Design Pressure**

$$P_{design} = 1.10 \times P_{operating\,max}$$

**Use:** Sizing pipe wall thickness, selecting pressure rating.

### **Formula 2: Design Temperature (ASME B31.3 §301.2)**

$$T_{design} = T_{operating\,max} + 25\,°C$$

### **Formula 3: Hydrotest Pressure (ASME B31.3 §345.4)**

$$P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$$

**Boundary Condition:** $P_{test} \le 1.5 \times P_{rating\,flange}$

### **Formula 4: Minimum Pipe Wall Thickness (Barlow's Equation — ASME B31.3 §304.1.2)**

$$t_m = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)}$$

Where:
- $t_m$ = minimum required thickness (inches)
- $P$ = internal design pressure (psig)
- $D$ = outside diameter of pipe (inches)
- $S$ = allowable stress (psi) at design temperature
- $E$ = longitudinal joint efficiency (1.0 for seamless)
- $Y$ = coefficient from Table 304.1.1 (0.4 for ferritic steel < 900 °F)

### **Formula 5: Selected Pipe Schedule (Wall Thickness)**

$$t_{nom} = t_m + c \quad \text{where } c = \text{corrosion allowance}$$

Round **$t_{nom}$** UP to the nearest standard SCH value.

### **Formula 6: Reynolds Number (for Instrument Sizing — Flowmeters)**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu} = \frac{4 \cdot \dot{m}}{\pi \cdot D_h \cdot \mu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = velocity (m/s)
- $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Insight:** $Re > 4000$ → Turbulent flow → **Orifice / Venturi** preferred.
> $Re < 2300$ → Laminar flow → **Turbine / Coriolis** preferred.
> Instrument engineers extract **density, viscosity, and flow rate** directly from the line schedule!

### **Formula 7: Velocity Check (Sanitary Limit)**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

| Service | Max Recommended Velocity |
|---------|--------------------------|
| Liquid (process) | **1.5 – 2.5 m/s** |
| Gas / Vapor | **15 – 25 m/s** (Mach < 0.3) |
| Steam | **25 – 40 m/s** |
| Slurry | **1.5 – 2.0 m/s** (erosion limit) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

| Field | How Line Schedule is Used |
|-------|---------------------------|
| **Process Engineering** | Establishes mass/energy balance, hydraulic profile, pressure drop. |
| **Mechanical / Piping** | Selects pipe class, fittings, supports, expansion loops. |
| **Instrumentation** | Sizes flowmeters, control valves (Cv), relief valves, thermowells. |
| **Electrical / Heat Tracing** | Decides line-by-line tracing power (W/m) and zoning. |
| **Procurement** | Generates **MTO (Material Take-Off)** for purchasing. |
| **Safety / HSE** | Identifies **sour service (H₂S), NACE MR0175, fire-water, flare** lines for HSE compliance. |
| **Commissioning** | Hydrotest, cleaning, and chemical flushing plans are built from line schedule data. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1 — Designing a Line from Service Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A process plant handles **sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄, 98% concentration)** from a storage tank **T-101** to a reactor **R-201**. Given:
> - Operating pressure = **4.0 bar(g)**
> - Operating temperature = **45 °C**
> - Mass flow rate = **15,000 kg/hr**
> - Density of H₂SO₄ = **1,830 kg/m³**
> - Viscosity = **23 cP = 0.023 Pa·s**
> - Corrosion allowance = **2.0 mm**
> - Pipe MOC = **SS316L (Seamless)**
> - Allowable stress *S* (SS316L @ 70 °C) = **115 MPa = 16,675 psi**
> - Joint efficiency *E* = **1.0**, *Y* = **0.4**
>
> **Determine:** (a) Design Pressure & Temperature, (b) Hydrotest Pressure, (c) Hydrodynamic diameter / NPS using velocity criterion, (d) Reynolds number.

---

### **Part (a) — Design Pressure and Design Temperature**

**Step 1:** Identify Maximum Operating Pressure.

$$P_{op,\,max} = 4.0\ \text{bar(g)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply ASME B31.3 design pressure rule (10% margin):

$$P_{design} = 1.10 \times P_{op,\,max} = 1.10 \times 4.0\ \text{bar(g)} = 4.4\ \text{bar(g)}$$

**Step 3:** Identify Maximum Operating Temperature.

$$T_{op,\,max} = 45\,°C$$

**Step 4:** Apply ASME B31.3 design temperature rule (+25 °C margin):

$$T_{design} = T_{op,\,max} + 25\,°C = 45 + 25 = 70\,°C$$

> ✅ **Result:** $P_{design} = 4.4\ \text{bar(g)}$, $T_{design} = 70\,°C$

---

### **Part (b) — Hydrotest Pressure**

**Step 1:** Apply ASME B31.3 §345.4 formula:

$$P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design} = 1.5 \times 4.4\ \text{bar(g)} = 6.6\ \text{bar(g)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to standard units for documentation:

$$P_{test} = 6.6\ \text{bar(g)} \times 14.5038 = 95.7\ \text{psig}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $P_{test} = 6.6\ \text{bar(g)} = 95.7\ \text{psig}$

---

### **Part (c) — NPS Sizing using Velocity Criterion**

**Step 1:** Select design velocity for viscous acid service (recommended: **v = 1.5 m/s** for safe erosion margin).

$$v = 1.5\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Convert mass flow rate to volumetric flow rate.

$$\dot{m} = 15{,}000\ \text{kg/hr} = \frac{15{,}000}{3600} = 4.167\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{4.167}{1{,}830} = 2.276 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply continuity equation for circular pipe:

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$$

**Step 4:** Solve for diameter:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2.276 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 1.5}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{9.104 \times 10^{-3}}{4.712}} = \sqrt{1.932 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$D = 0.04396\ \text{m} = 43.96\ \text{mm} \approx 1.73\ \text{inches}$$

**Step 5:** Round up to next standard NPS.

$$D_{selected} = 2\ \text{inches (NPS 2)}$$

> ✅ **Result:** Selected NPS = **2"** (line tag: `2"-H2SO4-1001-S6` if S6 = SS316L class)

---

### **Part (d) — Reynolds Number (For Flowmeter Selection)**

**Step 1:** Compute hydraulic diameter (for 2" Sch 40 pipe, ID ≈ 52.5 mm):

$$D_h = 52.5\ \text{mm} = 0.0525\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Reynolds number formula:

$$Re = \frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D_h \mu} = \frac{4 \times 4.167}{\pi \times 0.0525 \times 0.023}$$

$$Re = \frac{16.668}{3.793 \times 10^{-3}} = 4{,}393$$

**Step 3:** Interpret result.

$$Re = 4{,}393 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Re > 4{,}000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Turbulent flow}$$

> ✅ **Instrument Sizing Implication:** Turbulent flow → suitable for **Orifice Plate, Venturi, Vortex, Magnetic, or Coriolis** flowmeters.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2 — Piping Class Selection via Barlow's Equation

> A line carries steam at $P_{design} = 15\ \text{bar(g)}$, $T_{design} = 250\,°C$, NPS = **6"** (OD = **168.28 mm = 6.625 in**). MOC = **A106 Gr.B Carbon Steel (Seamless)**. Allowable stress $S = 13{,}750\ \text{psi}$ at 250 °C. $E = 1.0$, $Y = 0.4$. Corrosion allowance $c = 1.5\ \text{mm} = 0.059\ \text{in}$. Find the **minimum SCH** required.

**Step 1:** Convert design pressure to psig (using gauge; ignore atmospheric differential since ASME uses internal pressure):

$$P = 15\ \text{bar(g)} \times 14.5038 = 217.56\ \text{psig}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Barlow's equation (ASME B31.3 §304.1.2):

$$t_m = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)}$$

$$t_m = \frac{217.56 \times 6.625}{2(13{,}750 \times 1.0 + 217.56 \times 0.4)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$SE = 13{,}750\ \text{psi} \quad ; \quad PY = 87.02\ \text{psi}$$

$$SE + PY = 13{,}837.02\ \text{psi}$$

$$2(SE + PY) = 27{,}674.04\ \text{psi}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator:

$$P \cdot D = 217.56 \times 6.625 = 1{,}441.34\ \text{lb/in}$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$t_m = \frac{1{,}441.34}{27{,}674.04} = 0.0521\ \text{in}$$

**Step 6:** Add corrosion allowance:

$$t_{nom} = t_m + c = 0.0521 + 0.059 = 0.1111\ \text{in}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to mm and select standard SCH (6" pipe standard wall thicknesses):

$$t_{nom} = 0.1111 \times 25.4 = 2.82\ \text{mm}$$

**Standard 6" Pipe Schedules:**
- SCH 10 → 2.77 mm ❌ (just below)
- SCH 20 → 3.91 mm ✅ (selected)
- SCH 40 → 7.11 mm

**Selected: SCH 20** (margin of safety maintained).

> ✅ **Result:** Select **NPS 6, SCH 20, A106 Gr.B** for this steam line.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: KTU Decision Logic Flow

```
START (Service Identified)
        │
        ▼
Q1. Is it process / utility / safety-critical?
        │──► Process   → Class "P" (Process)
        │──► Utility   → Class "U" (e.g., CW, IA)
        │──► Safety    → Class "S" (FW, FL)
        ▼
Q2. Determine MOC (corrosion, T, P)?
        │──► Carbon Steel (CS)  → pH 6–9, T < 400 °C
        │──► SS304/SS316        → Corrosive / Hygienic
        │──► Alloy (Hastelloy)  → HCl, HF, H₂SO₄ (hot)
        ▼
Q3. Compute P_design = 1.10 × P_op,max
        T_design = T_op,max + 25 °C
        ▼
Q4. Apply Barlow → Compute t_m
        t_nom = t_m + c
        ▼
Q5. Select NPS (velocity criterion) + SCH (thickness criterion)
        ▼
Q6. Assign insulation (T < 60 °C → none; T > 60 °C → per energy code)
        ▼
Q7. Assign tracing (viscous, waxy, freeze-protection services)
        ▼
Q8. Document in Line Schedule
        │
        ▼
END
```

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Line Schedule in Project Document Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plot Plan] --> D[Line Schedule]
    B[P and ID Diagram] --> D
    C[Equipment List] --> D
    E[Instrument Index] --> D
    F[Piping Class Sheet] --> D
    G[Material Take Off MTO] --> D
    H[Isometric Drawings] --> D
    I[Hydrotest Plan] --> D

    D --> J[Procurement BOM]
    D --> K[Instrument Sizing]
    D --> L[Construction ISOs]
    D --> M[Safety HAZOP Input]

    style D fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#90ee90
    style B fill:#90ee90
    style C fill:#90ee90
    style E fill:#90ee90
    style F fill:#90ee90
    style G fill:#ffd700
    style H fill:#ffd700
    style I fill:#ffd700
    style J fill:#ff7f50
    style K fill:#ff7f50
    style L fill:#ff7f50
    style M fill:#ff7f50
```

> **Interpretation:** The **Line Schedule** (center, blue) is the *nucleus* of the entire engineering document set. Every drawing, list, and procurement order references it.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Line Schedule Information Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Identification Block] --> A1[Line Number]
    A --> A2[Service Code]
    A --> A3[Sequence Number]
    A --> A4[P and ID Reference]

    B[Process Data Block] --> B1[Operating Pressure]
    B --> B2[Operating Temperature]
    B --> B3[Flow Rate]
    B --> B4[Density and Viscosity]
    B --> B5[Phase]

    C[Mechanical Block] --> C1[NPS Size]
    C --> C2[Pipe Schedule]
    C --> C3[Material of Construction]
    C --> C4[Corrosion Allowance]
    C --> C5[Design Pressure]
    C --> C6[Design Temperature]
    C --> C7[Hydrotest Pressure]

    D[Construction Block] --> D1[Insulation Type]
    D --> D2[Insulation Thickness]
    D --> D3[Tracing Type]
    D --> D4[Painting Spec]
    D --> D5[Piping Class]

    E[Compliance Block] --> E1[ASME Code Reference]
    E --> E2[NACE Compliance]
    E --> E3[Fire Safe Requirement]
    E --> E4[Remarks]

    A --> F[Complete Line Schedule Record]
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    style F fill:#ff4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#87ceeb
    style B fill:#87ceeb
    style C fill:#87ceeb
    style D fill:#87ceeb
    style E fill:#87ceeb
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Workflow: From Line Schedule to Instrument Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line Schedule Entry] --> B[Extract Process Data]
    B --> C{Service Type}
    C -->|Flow Service| D[Calculate Re and Q]
    C -->|Pressure Service| E[Verify MAWP]
    C -->|Temperature Service| F[Verify Thermowell Rating]

    D --> G[Select Flowmeter Type]
    G --> G1[Orifice for high Re]
    G --> G2[Coriolis for mass flow]
    G --> G3[Magnetic for conductive liquids]
    G --> G4[Vortex for steam and gas]

    E --> H[Select Pressure Transmitter Range]
    F --> I[Select RTD or Thermocouple]

    G1 --> J[Final Instrument Specification]
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    G4 --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J

    style A fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#32cd32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Line Schedule. List any eight important data fields typically included in it.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *line schedule* is a comprehensive tabular engineering document that consolidates all essential design, operational, and material data for every process pipe in a plant, acting as the master link between the P&ID, plot plan, equipment list, and instrument index.

**Eight Data Fields (2 marks — 0.25 each):**
1. Line Number / Tag
2. Service Description
3. Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)
4. Pipe Schedule
5. Material of Construction (MOC)
6. Operating Pressure and Temperature
7. Design Pressure and Temperature
8. Insulation Type
9. Hydrotest Pressure
10. P&ID Drawing Reference

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Operating Pressure and Design Pressure. Why is the design pressure always kept higher than the operating pressure?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Operating Pressure | Design Pressure |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Definition | Actual pressure at which line operates | Maximum pressure assumed for mechanical design |
| Margin | None | 10% above maximum operating |
| Purpose | Process evaluation | Pipe wall thickness, flange rating |
| Formula | $P_{op}$ (given) | $P_{design} = 1.10 \times P_{op,max}$ |

**Why Higher (1 mark):** Design pressure includes a **safety margin** to account for *transient surges, pump dead-head pressure, control valve failure (full open), blocked outlet, and thermal expansion*. This ensures the pipe never yields plastically under abnormal conditions — protecting life, equipment, and environment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **🟦 Question A (14 Marks) — Design and Document a Process Line** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

A plant handles **diesel** from a storage tank to a day tank through a 100 m pipeline.

**Given Data:**
- Mass flow rate = **50,000 kg/hr**
- Operating pressure = **5.0 bar(g)**
- Operating temperature = **35 °C**
- Density of diesel = **850 kg/m³**
- Viscosity = **3.5 cP = 0.0035 Pa·s**
- Corrosion allowance = **1.5 mm**
- MOC = **Carbon Steel A106 Gr.B (Seamless)**
- Allowable stress S = **20,000 psi @ 60 °C**
- Y = 0.4, E = 1.0

**Required:**
**(a)** Determine the Design Pressure, Design Temperature, and Hydrotest Pressure. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Determine the minimum NPS and corresponding pipe schedule. Compute the Reynolds number and recommend a suitable flow-measurement instrument. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Part A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Design Pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{design} = 1.10 \times P_{op,max} = 1.10 \times 5.0 = 5.5\ \text{bar(g)}$$

**Step 2 — Design Temperature (2 marks):**

$$T_{design} = T_{op,max} + 25\,°C = 35 + 25 = 60\,°C$$

**Step 3 — Hydrotest Pressure (3 marks):**

$$P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design} = 1.5 \times 5.5 = 8.25\ \text{bar(g)}$$

Converting: $8.25 \times 14.5038 = 119.7\ \text{psig}$

> ✅ **Result:** $P_{design} = 5.5\ \text{bar(g)}$, $T_{design} = 60\,°C$, $P_{test} = 8.25\ \text{bar(g)}$

---

#### **Solution to Part A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{50{,}000}{3600} = 13.89\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{13.89}{850} = 1.634 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity-based sizing (2 marks):** Use v = **2.0 m/s** (typical for liquid hydrocarbons).

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.634 \times 10^{-2}}{\pi \times 2.0}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{1.040 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.102\ \text{m} = 102\ \text{mm} = 4.02\ \text{inch}$$

**Selected NPS = 4" (Sch 40, ID = 102.3 mm)** *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Reynolds Number (2 marks):**

$$Re = \frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D_h \mu} = \frac{4 \times 13.89}{\pi \times 0.1023 \times 0.0035}$$

$$Re = \frac{55.56}{1.124 \times 10^{-3}} = 49{,}433$$

> ✅ $Re >> 4000$ → **Turbulent flow**.

**Step 4 — Flowmeter Recommendation (1 mark):**
Recommended: **Positive Displacement (PD) Meter** (best for hydrocarbons with low viscosity variability) or **Turbine Flowmeter** (high accuracy for clean diesel).

---

### **🟨 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

Explain the following with respect to the **preparation of a line schedule** for a refinery project:

**(a)** List and briefly describe **any ten essential data fields** of a line schedule. State the relevant **standards/codes** that govern these fields. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 3" line carries **wet chlorine gas** at $P_{op} = 8\ \text{bar(g)}$ and $T_{op} = 40\,°C$. Determine the **Design Pressure, Design Temperature, and Hydrotest Pressure**. Suggest a suitable **MOC, insulation, and tracing** philosophy with justification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Part B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Ten Essential Fields (5 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Field | Purpose |
|---|-------|---------|
| 1 | Line Number | Unique identifier (P&ID cross-ref) |
| 2 | Service Description | Fluid/process function |
| 3 | NPS | Pipe diameter |
| 4 | Schedule | Wall thickness |
| 5 | Piping Class | Material + rating combo |
| 6 | Operating Pressure | Process condition |
| 7 | Operating Temperature | Process condition |
| 8 | Design Pressure | 1.10 × P_op,max |
| 9 | Design Temperature | T_op,max + 25 °C |
| 10 | Hydrotest Pressure | 1.5 × P_design |

**Governing Standards (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
- **ASME B31.3** — Process Piping (Design P, T, wall thickness, hydrotest)
- **ASME B31.1** — Power Piping
- **ISA-5.1** — Instrument identification
- **PIP** — Industry Practices (line numbering, MOC selection)

---

#### **Solution to Part B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Design Pressure (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{design} = 1.10 \times 8 = 8.8\ \text{bar(g)}$$

**Step 2 — Design Temperature (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{design} = 40 + 25 = 65\,°C$$

**Step 3 — Hydrotest Pressure (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{test} = 1.5 \times 8.8 = 13.2\ \text{bar(g)}$$

**Step 4 — MOC Suggestion (1 mark):** For **wet chlorine gas**, recommended MOC = **Hastelloy C-276** or **PTFE-lined Carbon Steel** or **GRP/FRP** (resistant to wet Cl₂ corrosion — carbon steel will fail rapidly).

**Step 5 — Insulation (1 mark):** Use **cellular glass insulation** (e.g., Foamglas) — non-wicking, moisture-resistant. Thickness = **50 mm** to prevent condensation and personnel burn protection.

**Step 6 — Tracing (0.5 mark):** **Electric heat tracing** with temperature controller (set at 50 °C) to prevent moisture condensation and keep gas dry above its dew point.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Never** suggest carbon steel (CS) for **wet chlorine** — it corrodes violently. Examiners will deduct 2 marks.
> - **Don't** confuse Design Pressure (10% margin) with Hydrotest (50% margin) — these are **two different** multipliers.
> - **Don't** forget to add **+25 °C** temperature margin; many students omit it.
> - For trace heating, mention **temperature controller** — bare tracing without control is incomplete answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **Line Schedule = Master Pipe Data Sheet** linking P&ID, plot plan, equipment, and instruments.
- ✅ Governed by **ASME B31.3** (Process) or **ASME B31.1** (Power).
- ✅ **Design Pressure = 1.10 × Operating Pressure** (always).
- ✅ **Design Temperature = Operating Temperature + 25 °C** (always).
- ✅ **Hydrotest Pressure = 1.5 × Design Pressure**.
- ✅ Pipe wall thickness from **Barlow's Equation**: $t_m = \frac{PD}{2(SE + PY)}$.
- ✅ Add **corrosion allowance** to minimum thickness; round UP to standard SCH.
- ✅ **Reynolds Number** dictates flowmeter selection ($Re > 4000$ = turbulent).
- ✅ Service codes follow **PIP / ISA convention** (CW, IA, MS, HC, N2, etc.).
- ✅ Insulation required for **T > 60 °C** (personnel protection) or **T < 10 °C** (anti-sweat).
- ✅ Tracing required for **viscous, waxy, freeze-prone, hydrate-forming** services.
- ✅ **Wet Cl₂ → Hastelloy / PTFE-lined; HF → Carbon Steel; H₂SO₄ → CS (cold) / SS316 (hot)**.
- ✅ NACE MR0175 compliance for **sour service (H₂S > 50 ppm)**.
- ✅ Line schedule is the **FIRST document** instrument engineers request for sizing flowmeters, control valves (Cv), and relief valves.
- ✅ Cross-references: **P&ID DWG No., Plot Plan Grid, Equipment Tag, Instrument Tag** must be in the line schedule for full traceability.
- ✅ KTU frequently asks: **"Differentiate OP vs DP"**, **"List line schedule fields"**, **"Compute design/hydrotest pressure"**, **"Recommend MOC for a service"** — master these four patterns.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Various Stages in P&ID Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Various Stages in P&ID Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a P&ID?

A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed schematic diagram used in the process industries that depicts the interconnection of process equipment, the instrumentation used to control the process, and the piping that connects various process units. It is the central design document for the engineering, construction, and operation of any process plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (ISA-5.1 / IEC 62424 Aligned):**
> A P&ID is a graphical representation of a process that includes all piping, vessels, mechanical equipment, instrumentation, control valves, safety devices, and interlocks, drawn using standardized symbols and notation systems that allow any qualified engineer — regardless of geography — to interpret the design unambiguously.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint

Imagine you are building a house. An **architectural floor plan** tells you where the walls, doors, windows, electrical wiring, and plumbing lines are located, *and* it tells you what each switch does. You don't have to *see* the house to know how it works.

A P&ID is the **architectural floor plan of a chemical/process plant**:
- **Walls** → Process vessels, tanks, heat exchangers, pumps
- **Doors/Windows** → Control valves, isolation valves
- **Electrical wiring** → Instrumentation signal lines (pneumatic, electronic, digital)
- **Switches/Lights** → Sensors (transmitters), controllers, indicators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Without a P&ID, an instrument engineer cannot specify the I/O count, cable schedules, junction box locations, DCS/PLC architecture, or safety integrity levels. The P&ID is therefore the *single most important document* in instrumentation system design.

## 1.3 Role of the P&ID in Instrumentation System Design

| Engineering Discipline | How it Uses the P&ID |
|---|---|
| **Instrumentation Engineer** | Identifies loop count, I/O types, DCS/PLC card sizing |
| **Process Engineer** | Verifies control philosophy, operating ranges, setpoints |
| **Piping Designer** | Defines pipe class, material, valve schedule |
| **Safety Engineer** | Extracts interlocks, alarm trips, SIF boundaries |
| **Commissioning Engineer** | Sequences plant startup, performs loop checks |
| **Maintenance Team** | Plans preventive maintenance, troubleshooting |

## 1.4 Governing Standards (KTU-Syllabus Aligned)

The KTU 2024 scheme mandates familiarity with the following standards:
- **ISA-5.1** — Instrumentation Symbols and Identification
- **ISA-5.2** — Binary Logic Diagrams for Process Operations
- **ISA-5.3** — Graphic Symbols for Distributed Control/Shared Display Instrumentation
- **ISA-5.4** — Instrument Loop Diagrams
- **ISA-5.5** — Graphic Symbols for Process Displays
- **ISA-5.6** — Documentation of Control Software
- **IEC 62424** — Representation of process control engineering requests in P&ID
- **ISO 14617** — Graphical symbols for diagrams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For board examinations, the **ISA-5.1** standard for symbol/letter identification is the most heavily tested area. Memorize the letter codes and the bubble/instrument symbol conventions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis — Stages of P&ID Development

## 2.1 The P&ID Development Lifecycle

A P&ID does not appear overnight. It evolves through a structured, multi-stage engineering workflow. The following eight stages form the canonical development pipeline used across EPC (Engineering, Procurement, and Construction) companies and aligned with KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus outcomes.

### Stage 1 — Process Design Basis & Data Gathering
- Collect feed/product specifications, flowrates, temperatures, pressures, compositions.
- Establish operating ranges: **Normal, Maximum, Minimum, Turndown**.
- Define utility requirements: steam, cooling water, instrument air, nitrogen.
- **Why:** Every downstream symbol on the P&ID traces back to a process data point.

### Stage 2 — Process Flow Diagram (PFD) Generation
- The PFD is the *parent* of the P&ID. It shows:
  - Major equipment (symbolized, not detailed)
  - Main process piping with **stream numbers**
  - Mass & energy balance data
  - Major control loops in *conceptual* form only
- **Why:** Before drawing instrument bubbles, you must know *what* is to be controlled.

### Stage 3 — P&ID Layout & Equipment Representation
- Detailed mechanical equipment is shown using standardized P&ID symbols:
  - Vessels, columns, reactors, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, filters.
- Equipment is tagged uniquely (e.g., **V-101**, **E-201**, **P-301**).
- Nozzle connections, orientations, and elevations are indicated.

### Stage 4 — Piping & Valve Specification
- All process lines (process, utility, drain, vent) are drawn.
- Each line carries a **line number / pipe class** (e.g., **3"-P-101-A2-H**).
- Valves are specified: gate, globe, ball, butterfly, check, safety/relief (PSV), control (PCV/FCV).

### Stage 5 — Instrumentation & Control Loop Definition
This is the **heart of Module 1**. Per **ISA-5.1**:
- Every measurement is assigned an **ISA Tag Number**.
- Bubble symbols distinguish location: **circle** = primary location (field/control room/DCS).
- **Horizontal bar** through the bubble = primary control variable, **no bar** = shared display/auxiliary.
- Control loops (feedback, cascade, ratio, feedforward, split-range) are drawn with **dashed signal lines**.

### Stage 6 — Interlock, Alarm & Safety System Design
- Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) are identified.
- High-High (HH), Low-Low (LL) trips and ESD (Emergency Shutdown) levels are added.
- Logic diagrams (relay logic, Boolean, or cause-&-effect) reference the P&ID.

### Stage 7 — Review, Verification & Approval
- Internal model reviews, **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability), **SIL** classification.
- Client/owner approval.
- "Issued for Construction" (IFC) revision.

### Stage 8 — As-Built & Revisions
- Field redlines incorporated post-construction.
- "As-Built" P&ID becomes the living document for operations and maintenance.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — ISA Tag Number Decoding

The single most important "formula" in Module 1 is the **ISA-5.1 Tag Number Structure**.

$$
\text{ISA Tag} = \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Area No.}} \; - \; \underbrace{\text{L}_{1}\text{L}_{2}\text{L}_{3}}_{\text{Letter Codes}} \; \underbrace{\text{NNN}}_{\text{Loop No.}}
$$

| Component | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `XX` | Plant area / unit number | `10` for Reactor Area, `20` for Distillation |
| `L₁` | **First Letter** = Measured Variable | T, P, F, L, A, Q, S, V, W |
| `L₂`, `L₃` | **Succeeding Letters** = Function/Modifier | I, C, R, T, A, S, H, L, V |
| `NNN` | 3-digit loop number (unique per area) | `101`, `205`, `312` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Order Rule (ISA-5.1):** Read tag letters **left-to-right** as *Measured Variable → Modifier → Function Output*. Example: `FT` = *F*low *T*ransmitter, `TIC` = *T*emperature *I*ndicating *C*ontroller.

## 2.3 Standard ISA-5.1 Letter Code Table

| Letter | First Position (Measured Variable) | Succeeding Position (Function/Modifier) |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Analysis | Alarm |
| **B** | Burner/Combustion | — |
| **C** | Conductivity | Control |
| **D** | Density/Differential | — |
| **E** | Voltage/EMF | Sensor/Element |
| **F** | Flow Rate | — |
| **H** | Hand (Manual) | High |
| **I** | Current | Indicate |
| **L** | Level | Low |
| **P** | Pressure | — |
| **Q** | Quantity/Totalize | — |
| **R** | Radiation | Record |
| **S** | Speed/Frequency | Switch |
| **T** | Temperature | Transmit |
| **V** | Vibration | Valve/Actuator |
| **W** | Weight/Force | Well |
| **X** | Unclassified | Unclassified |
| **Z** | Position/Dimension | Driver/Actuator |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Every DCS/PLC, every cable schedule, every junction-box termination drawing, and every loop-test checklist in a real plant is generated from a P&ID. The **IO list** — the single document that determines the cost of the entire control system — is extracted directly from the P&ID. Therefore, errors in P&ID stage development propagate into expensive rework during commissioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of an ISA Tag Number — Worked Example

**Problem:** A temperature transmitter is installed on the top of a distillation column in **Area 20** of a refinery. It is part of **loop #107**, and the signal feeds a temperature-indicating controller with high-temperature alarm. Derive the full ISA tag.

### Step 1 — Identify the Plant Area
The column belongs to **Area 20** (Distillation Unit). The first segment is:

$$
\text{XX} = 20
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Measured Variable (First Letter)
The instrument measures **Temperature**. From the ISA-5.1 table:

$$
L_{1} = T
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Function (Succeeding Letters)
The device is a **Transmitter** that feeds a **Controller** with **Indication** and **High Alarm**. The succeeding letters are read in the order *modifier → output function*:

$$
L_{2}L_{3} = IAH \quad \text{(Indicate, Alarm-High)}
$$

For the **controller** itself, the tag becomes:

$$
L_{2}L_{3} = IC \quad \text{(Indicate, Control)}
$$

### Step 4 — Assign the Loop Number
The plant engineer has pre-allocated **loop 107** for column overhead temperature control. Therefore:

$$
\text{NNN} = 107
$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Full Tag

$$
\boxed{\text{Tag} = 20-\text{TT}-107 \;\; \text{(Field Transmitter)}, \quad 20-\text{TIAH}-107 \;\; \text{(Alarm/Indicator)}, \quad 20-\text{TIC}-107 \;\; \text{(Controller)}}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify Against ISA Rules
- First letter denotes **measured variable** ✔ (T = Temperature)
- Succeeding letters denote **function** ✔ (T = Transmit, I = Indicate, A = Alarm, H = High, C = Control)
- Loop number is **unique per area** ✔ (107 is the first temperature loop in Area 20)
- Each unique instrument in the loop gets its **own tag suffix** ✔ (TT, TIAH, TIC are all separate physical devices)

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Rule:** A single control loop can have 4 to 6 distinct tags — one per physical device. The loop is identified by the common **loop number (107)**, while each device retains its own unique **letter code**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Process Needs to P&ID Symbols

The following matrix is the **canonical mapping** the KTU examiner expects you to memorize:

| Process Variable | Primary Element | Transmitter | Indicator | Controller | Final Element |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flow** | Orifice / Venturi / Magnetic | FT | FI | FIC | FCV |
| **Pressure** | Bourdon / Diaphragm / DP Cell | PT | PI | PIC | PCV |
| **Temperature** | RTD / Thermocouple | TT | TI | TIC | TCV |
| **Level** | DP Cell / Float / Capacitance | LT | LI | LIC | LCV |
| **Analysis** | pH / Conductivity probe | AT | AI | AIC | ACV |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Loop Power-Pressure Relationship (Conceptual)

In pneumatic instrumentation (legacy 3–15 psig standard), the signal relationship is:

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = P_{\min} + \left( \frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}} \right) (P_{\max} - P_{\min})
$$

Substituting the **3–15 psig** standard where $P_{\min} = 3$ psig, $P_{\max} = 15$ psig:

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = 3 + 12 \cdot \left( \frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}} \right) \;\; \text{[psig]}
$$

For an electronic 4–20 mA current loop, the analogous expression is:

$$
I_{\text{signal}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \left( \frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}} \right) \;\; \text{[mA]}
$$

These formulas are required for solving any KTU problem on **signal conversion, current-to-pressure (I/P) transducer sizing, or valve stroke calibration**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Typical Feedback Control Loop as Drawn on a P&ID

