| Self-heating error | $\Delta T_{sh} = P / \delta$ | °C | $\delta$ = dissipation const. |
| Common-mode rejection | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$ | dB | IA spec, typically **≥ 80 dB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value expressions are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table pipes. Example: $\vert V_{CM} \vert \le 10$ V (NOT |V_CM|).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermocouples + SCC:** Used in furnaces, gas turbines, exhaust manifolds of IC engines, cryogenics (Type E/T).
- **RTDs + SCC:** Used in HVAC, pharmaceutical reactors, food processing, precision labs (PT100 dominant).
- **Thermistor + SCC:** Used in medical thermometers, 3D printer hot-ends, battery management systems (BMS), inrush current limiters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage with RTD

**Setup:** A classic unbalanced Wheatstone bridge with the RTD forming one arm.

Let the four arms be: $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, and $R_4 = R_{RTD}$. Excitation voltage is $V_{ex}$ across nodes A (top) and D (bottom). Output is taken between nodes B and C.

The voltage at node B (voltage divider):
$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The voltage at node C (voltage divider):
$$V_C = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

Differential output voltage:
$$V_{out} = V_B - V_C = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Balance condition** ($V_{out} = 0$):
$$V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

Cross-multiplying (assuming $V_{ex} \ne 0$):
$$R_2 (R_3 + R_4) = R_4 (R_1 + R_2)$$
$$R_2 R_3 + R_2 R_4 = R_4 R_1 + R_4 R_2$$

The $R_2 R_4$ terms cancel:
$$R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$$

Therefore the unknown RTD resistance at balance:
$$R_4 = \frac{R_2 R_3}{R_1}$$

For small deviations from balance, **linearize** by setting $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ at the operating point (null-bridge design). Let $R_4 = R + \Delta R$, with $\Delta R \ll R$.

Substitute into the $V_{out}$ expression. Using $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$:
$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

$$V_C = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{R + (R + \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} - V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

Taking common denominator:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}(2R + \Delta R) - 2V_{ex}(R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}(2R + \Delta R - 2R - 2\Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}(-\Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

For $\Delta R \ll R$, we have $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$. The negative sign simply indicates polarity:
$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

This is the **standard small-signal bridge sensitivity** used in every KTU numerical:
$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A PT100 RTD is used in a bridge with $V_{ex} = 5$ V. At 100 °C, $\Delta R = 100 \times 0.385 = 38.5$ Ω. Calculate $V_{out}$:
$$V_{out} \approx \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{38.5}{100} = 1.25 \times 0.385 = 0.481\ \text{V}$$

This 481 mV is then amplified by an IA with gain ~10 to reach ~4.81 V full-scale for a 0–5 V ADC.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Three-Wire RTD Lead-Wire Compensation

**Setup:** Three wires $a$, $b$, $c$ connect the RTD to the bridge. Wires $a$ and $b$ carry excitation current (each has resistance $r$); wire $c$ is a sense lead.

Bridge arms:
- Upper-left: $R + r_a$ (RTD + lead $a$)
- Upper-right: $R_3$ (fixed precision)
- Lower-left: $R_2$ (fixed precision, equal to $R_3$)
- Lower-right: $R + r_b$ (dummy RTD-like resistor + lead $b$)

At node B (where lead $c$ senses):
$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3 + r_c}{(R + r_a) + (R_3 + r_c)}$$

If $r_a = r_b = r_c = r$ (identical wires, the standard assumption) and $R_2 = R_3 = R$:
$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + r}{(R + r) + (R + r)} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

$$V_C = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + r}{R + (R + r)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + r}{2R + r}$$

If $r \ll R$ (typical; $r$ ≈ 0.1–1 Ω, $R$ ≈ 100 Ω), then:
$$V_C \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

So:
$$V_{out} = V_B - V_C \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**The lead resistance $r$ cancels in the difference.** The compensation is perfect only if $r_a = r_b$ exactly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Instrumentation Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

The classic **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** has gain:
$$G = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

**Derivation sketch:** Stage 1 (two op-amps) provides differential gain with the resistor $R_{gain}$ between them. If both inputs $V_1, V_2$ differ by $V_{id}$, the current through $R_{gain}$ is $V_{id}/R_{gain}$, and each $R_1$ drops the same voltage. The differential output of stage 1 is:
$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = V_{id} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}} \right)$$

Stage 2 is a unity-gain difference amplifier that converts the differential signal to a single-ended output, preserving the gain:
$$V_{out} = V_{id} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}} \right)$$

This is why a single external resistor $R_{gain}$ controls the gain over a wide range (1 to 1000+).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cold Junction Compensation (Software/Hardware)

**Problem:** Thermocouple voltage $V_{TC}(T_{hot}, T_{ref})$ is referenced to $T_{ref}$, the cold-junction (terminal) temperature — NOT to 0 °C.

We want the equivalent reading that would be obtained if the cold junction were at 0 °C:
$$V_{eq} = V_{TC}(T_{hot}, 0°C)$$

By the **Law of Intermediate Metals**:
$$V_{TC}(T_{hot}, 0°C) = V_{TC}(T_{hot}, T_{ref}) + V_{TC}(T_{ref}, 0°C)$$

So we must **add** the thermocouple voltage corresponding to the terminal-board temperature $T_{ref}$. Practically:
- A silicon sensor (e.g., LM35) measures $T_{ref}$.
- A look-up table or scaled amplifier converts this to an equivalent $V_{TC}(T_{ref}, 0°C)$.
- The two voltages are summed at the input of the IA.

For Type K, the Seebeck coefficient near room temperature is approximately **40 μV/°C**. If the board is at 25 °C, we must add:
$$V_{CJC} = 40\ \mu V/°C \times 25°C = 1\ \text{mV}$$

Without CJC, the system would read **25 °C too low** for every temperature.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison

| Parameter | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | NTC Thermistor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active/Passive | **Active** (generates V) | **Passive** (resistive) | **Passive** (resistive) |
| Output | μV to mV | Resistance change | Resistance change (exponential) |
| Range | **−270 °C to +2300 °C** | **−200 °C to +850 °C** | **−55 °C to +150 °C** |
| Sensitivity | 5–80 μV/°C | 0.385 Ω/°C | **−4 %/°C** typical |
| Linearity | Moderate (polynomial) | **Best (Callendar-Van Dusen)** | **Worst (exponential)** |
| Accuracy | ±1–2 °C (after CJC) | ±0.1 °C (ITS-90) | ±0.05–0.2 °C |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | Very Low |
| Self-heating | None | 1 mA excitation critical | Very sensitive (μA) |
| Conditioning complexity | High (CJC, amp, linearize) | Medium (bridge, lead comp) | Medium-High (linearization) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Universal Signal Conditioning Chain

This flowchart represents the universal analog front-end (AFE) used for **all three** temperature sensors. The active blocks change based on sensor type (shown by the decision node).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sensA[Thermocouple Active Sensor]:::act
    sensB[RTD Passive Sensor]:::pas
    sensC[Thermistor Passive Sensor]:::pas
    src1[Constant Current Source 1 mA]:::src
    src2[Constant Voltage Source]:::src
    cjc[Cold Junction Compensation CJC Block]:::cjc
    bridge[Wheatstone Bridge 3-wire or 4-wire]:::brg
    div[Voltage Divider with R_ref]:::brg
    ia[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 10 to 1000]:::ia
    lpf[Low-Pass Filter 50 Hz Notch]:::ia
    lin[Linearization Block Analog or Digital LUT]:::lin
    adc[Analog to Digital Converter 12 to 24 bit]:::adc
    cpu[Microcontroller or DAQ Display]:::cpu

    sensA --> cjc
    cjc --> ia

    sensB --> src1
    src1 --> bridge
    bridge --> ia

    sensC --> src2
    src2 --> div
    div --> ia

    ia --> lpf --> lin --> adc --> cpu

    classDef act fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pas fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003380,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cjc fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#804000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef brg fill:#e0d6ff,stroke:#1a0066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ia fill:#d6ffe0,stroke:#00501a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lin fill:#ffd6f0,stroke:#660033,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#202020,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Schematic — 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier with CJC Input (Thermocouple Channel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tc[Thermocouple Hot Junction]:::tc --> iaIn1((IN+))
    lm35[CJC Sensor LM35 at Terminal Block]:::cjc2 --> sumNode((Sum Node))
    ref[0 V Ground Reference]:::ref --> sumNode
    sumNode --> iaIn2((IN-))
    iaIn1 --> a1[Op-Amp A1]:::opamp
    iaIn2 --> a2[Op-Amp A2]:::opamp
    a1 --> rg[R_gain External]:::res --> a2
    a1 --> r1a[R1]:::res
    a2 --> r1b[R1]:::res
    a1 --> a3[Op-Amp A3 Difference Amp]:::opamp
    a2 --> a3
    a3 --> r2a[R2]:::res
    a3 --> r2b[R2]:::res
    a3 --> r3a[R3]:::res
    a3 --> r3b[R3]:::res
    a3 --> out[Conditioned Output 0 to 5 V]:::outNode

    classDef tc fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef cjc2 fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#804000,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef opamp fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003380,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#d6ffe0,stroke:#00501a,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic — RTD 4-Wire Kelvin Connection with Constant Current Excitation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    currSrc[Constant Current Source 1 mA]:::src --> nA((Node A))
    nA --> rtd[PT100 RTD Element]:::rtd
    rtd --> nB((Node B))
    nB --> currRet[Return to Current Source]:::src
    sense1[Kelvin Sense Wire S+]:::wire --> hiInput((IA Hi Input))
    sense2[Kelvin Sense Wire S-]:::wire --> loInput((IA Lo Input))
    nA -.Kelvin tap.-> hiInput
    nB -.Kelvin tap.-> loInput
    hiInput --> instAmp[Instrumentation Amplifier]:::ia
    loInput --> instAmp
    instAmp --> conditionedOut[Conditioned Voltage 0 to 5 V]:::outNode

    classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef rtd fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef wire fill:#e0d6ff,stroke:#1a0066,color:#000
    classDef ia fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003380,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#d6ffe0,stroke:#00501a,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — How the Three Conditioning Pipelines Differ

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Select Temperature Sensor] --> q1{Sensor Type?}
    q1 -->|Thermocouple| p1[Acquire mV from Seebeck Effect]
    q1 -->|RTD| p2[Excite with 1 mA constant current]
    q1 -->|Thermistor| p3[Excite with low constant current]

    p1 --> s1[Apply CJC using board sensor]
    p2 --> s2[Convert to Voltage via Bridge or Kelvin]
    p3 --> s3[Convert to Voltage via Divider]

    s1 --> amp[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain G]
    s2 --> amp
    s3 --> amp

    amp --> filt[Low-Pass Filter 50 Hz Notch]
    filt --> lin[Linearization LUT or Polynomial]
    lin --> end[ADC + Microcontroller Display]

    classDef q fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    class q1 q
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET795, Q1a]**
> **Q1. What is Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) in a thermocouple measurement system? Why is it essential?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) is the technique of correcting the thermocouple output voltage for the temperature of the **cold junction** (the terminal block where thermocouple wires connect to the instrument), which is normally at ambient room temperature and not at the standard reference of **0 °C (273.15 K)**. It is essential because, by the Law of Intermediate Metals, the thermocouple voltage $V_{TC}(T_{hot}, 0°C)$ is the sum $V_{TC}(T_{hot}, T_{ref}) + V_{TC}(T_{ref}, 0°C)$; the second term must be added back. Without CJC, the reading would be offset by an amount equivalent to the ambient temperature — for example, a 25 °C board temperature causes an error of approximately **1 mV** for a Type K thermocouple, which equals an error of about **25 °C** in the measured hot-junction temperature. CJC is typically implemented using a silicon temperature sensor (e.g., LM35) mounted at the terminal block, whose output is scaled to match the Seebeck coefficient and summed at the instrumentation amplifier input.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET795, Q1b]**
> **Q2. Distinguish between 2-wire, 3-wire, and 4-wire RTD connections. State one application of each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **2-wire RTD:** The two lead wires carry both the excitation current and the sensing voltage. The lead resistance is added directly to the RTD resistance, introducing error. Used only in low-accuracy, short-lead applications such as **HVAC duct sensors**.
> - **3-wire RTD:** Two wires carry the excitation current (one to each end of the RTD) and a third wire senses the voltage at one terminal. The lead resistance errors cancel if the two current-carrying wires are identical in length and gauge. This is the **most common industrial configuration**, used in **process control loops and PLC analog inputs**.
> - **4-wire RTD (Kelvin connection):** Two wires source the excitation current, and two **separate** wires sense the voltage. Because the sense wires carry virtually no current, there is no IR drop across them, and lead resistance is completely eliminated. Used in **precision laboratory measurements, calibration baths, and reference thermometry**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing the Seebeck equation without mentioning the temperature limits of the polynomial coefficients (e.g., Type K polynomial is different above 0 °C vs. below 0 °C). **Always state the applicable temperature range.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that the bridge sensitivity formula $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \Delta R / (4R)$ is valid **only for a null-balanced bridge with small deviations**. Writing it as $V_{out} = V_{ex} \Delta R / (4R)$ (without approximation) is a partial-mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to mention **self-heating** when discussing thermistor conditioning. Examiners specifically look for the words "low excitation current (μA)" or "dissipation constant."
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing an instrumentation amplifier with the wrong resistor labeled ($R_2$ vs. $R_{gain}$). Label carefully.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Sample Paper – PEAET795, Q2 Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a signal conditioning circuit used with a **Type K thermocouple** for temperature measurement in a furnace. Discuss the role of the **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** block and the **instrumentation amplifier** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PT100 RTD** is connected in a Wheatstone bridge with all four arms nominally equal to 100 Ω at 0 °C. The bridge excitation is **V_ex = 5 V**. The temperature rises to **150 °C**.
  (i) Calculate the change in RTD resistance. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) Calculate the bridge output voltage using the small-signal approximation. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) Calculate the instrumentation amplifier gain required to obtain a full-scale output of **5 V** at 150 °C. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thermocouple Signal Conditioning — 7 marks**

A Type K thermocouple generates a Seebeck voltage of approximately **40 μV/°C** between the hot junction (inside the furnace) and the cold junction (at the instrument terminal block). The signal conditioning circuit consists of:

1. **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) block (2 marks):** Since the cold junction is not at 0 °C but at ambient (say 25 °C), the measured voltage $V_{TC}(T_{hot}, T_{ref})$ is offset by $V_{TC}(T_{ref}, 0°C)$. By the Law of Intermediate Metals, we must add an equivalent voltage. A precision silicon sensor (e.g., LM35) mounted at the terminal block measures $T_{ref}$ and produces a proportional voltage. This voltage is passed through a Seebeck-coefficient scaling network (resistor divider) and summed with the thermocouple voltage at the IA input, providing true reference to 0 °C.

2. **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) (2 marks):** A three-op-amp topology (e.g., AD620, INA128) provides:
   - High differential gain (typically **G = 10 to 100**),
   - Very high **CMRR (≥ 80 dB)** to reject common-mode noise picked up by long thermocouple wires,
   - Very high input impedance (≥ 1 GΩ) so that no current is drawn from the thermocouple (which would otherwise be loaded and give erroneous readings).

3. **Filtering and Output Stage (1 mark):** A second-order low-pass filter (e.g., Sallen-Key topology with f_c ≈ 10 Hz) removes mains-frequency pickup and RF noise. The conditioned output is then routed to a 16-bit ADC and microcontroller.

4. **Block diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to schematic in Section 4.2]

**(b) PT100 RTD Calculations — 7 marks**

Given: $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ /°C$, $T = 150\ °C$, $V_{ex} = 5\ V$.

**(i) Change in RTD resistance (2 marks):**
Using the linear approximation $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$:
$$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot T = 100 \times 0.00385 \times 150$$
$$\Delta R = 57.75\ \Omega$$

At 150 °C, $R_T = 100 + 57.75 = 157.75\ \Omega$.

**(ii) Bridge output voltage (2 marks):**
Using the small-signal bridge formula (assuming null-balanced bridge, $\Delta R \ll R$):
$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{57.75}{100}$$
$$V_{out} = 1.25 \times 0.5775 = 0.7219\ \text{V} \approx 722\ \text{mV}$$

**(iii) Required Instrumentation Amplifier Gain (3 marks):**
We want the IA output to reach 5 V (full-scale) when $V_{out} = 0.7219$ V:
$$G = \frac{V_{out,\,FS}}{V_{bridge}} = \frac{5\ \text{V}}{0.7219\ \text{V}} = 6.926 \approx 6.93$$

Using $G = 1 + 2R_1 / R_{gain}$, with $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (typical internal):
$$R_{gain} = \frac{2R_1}{G - 1} = \frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{6.93 - 1} = \frac{20{,}000}{5.93} = 3373\ \Omega$$

A standard value of **3.32 kΩ** yields $G \approx 7.02$, giving full-scale output of 5.07 V — within ±0.2 % error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Sample Paper – PEAET795, Q3 Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the operation of a signal conditioning circuit for an **NTC thermistor**. Discuss the importance of keeping the excitation current **very low (μA level)** and explain the **Steinhart–Hart equation** for linearization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a signal conditioning circuit for a **3-wire PT100 RTD** connected to a 0–5 V ADC. The RTD operates from **0 °C to 200 °C**. Use a 1 mA constant current excitation and specify:
  (i) The voltage range developed across the RTD. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The instrumentation amplifier gain required. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The role of the third (sense) wire and any error if it is omitted. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thermistor Signal Conditioning — 7 marks**

1. **Excitation (2 marks):** Because an NTC thermistor is highly sensitive (typically −4 % to −6 % resistance change per °C) and has a very small dissipation constant (often 1–5 mW/°C in air), the excitation current must be kept in the **microampere range (typically 10 μA – 100 μA)**. The power dissipated is $P = I^2 R$. If $I = 100\ \mu A$ and $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, then $P = 100\ \mu W$. The self-heating error is then $\Delta T_{sh} = P/\delta = 100\,\mu W / 2\,\text{mW/°C} = 0.05\ °C$ — acceptable. Higher currents would introduce unacceptable self-heating drift.

2. **Voltage Divider / Bridge (1 mark):** The thermistor is placed in a half-bridge with a precision reference resistor $R_{ref}$. The differential voltage is amplified by the IA.

3. **Steinhart–Hart Linearization (3 marks):** The thermistor resistance vs. temperature is **strongly exponential**:
$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$
This equation is not linearizable with simple analog circuits. The industry-standard approach is the **Steinhart–Hart equation**:
$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R_T) + C [\ln(R_T)]^3$$
where $A$, $B$, $C$ are calibrated constants specific to the thermistor (typically provided by the manufacturer). A microcontroller computes $1/T$ from the digitized resistance and inverts to get $T$ in Kelvin, achieving an accuracy of **±0.01 °C** over a 100 °C span. Analog linearization using a parallel resistor network is approximate and used only when no microcontroller is available.

4. **Filter and ADC (1 mark):** A low-pass filter suppresses noise; a 16/24-bit Σ-Δ ADC handles the small signal.

**(b) 3-Wire PT100 RTD Design — 7 marks**

Given: PT100, range 0–200 °C, $I_{ex} = 1\ \text{mA}$.

**(i) Voltage range across RTD (2 marks):**
At 0 °C: $R = 100\ \Omega$, so $V_{RTD} = I \cdot R = 1\ \text{mA} \times 100\ \Omega = 100\ \text{mV}$.

At 200 °C: $R = 100 + (0.00385 \times 200 \times 100) = 100 + 77 = 177\ \Omega$,
so $V_{RTD} = 1\ \text{mA} \times 177\ \Omega = 177\ \text{mV}$.

Voltage span: $\Delta V = 177 - 100 = 77\ \text{mV}$.

**(ii) Required IA gain (2 marks):**
We want a 0–5 V output from a 100–177 mV input span (assuming the IA offset compensates for the 100 mV zero):
$$G \approx \frac{5000\ \text{mV}}{77\ \text{mV}} \approx 64.9$$
A gain of **65** is set by choosing $R_{gain} = 2 R_1 / (G - 1) = 2 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega / 64 \approx 313\ \Omega$.

**(iii) Role of third (sense) wire — error if omitted (3 marks):**
In the 3-wire configuration, two wires ($a$ and $b$) carry the excitation current and each has lead resistance $r_a = r_b = r$. The third wire ($c$) is a **sense lead** that taps the voltage at the top of the RTD directly, with negligible current. By measuring the voltage at the top of the RTD via the sense wire, the IR drop in wire $a$ is excluded from the measurement. The bridge then has equal lead resistance on both arms ($R_{RTD} + r_a$ in one arm and $R_{ref} + r_b$ in the opposite arm), and these cancel in the differential measurement provided $r_a = r_b$.

If the third wire is omitted (falling back to a 2-wire connection), the lead resistance $r$ is added directly to the RTD resistance. For a 10 m lead of copper wire (AWG 24, ~0.08 Ω/m), the total lead resistance is ~1.6 Ω, equivalent to **1.6 / 0.385 ≈ 4.2 °C** of measurement error. This error grows linearly with lead length and is unacceptable in precision measurements.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - [ ] **Thermocouple:** Always needs **CJC**; uses **active** Seebeck voltage; IA must have **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** and **high input impedance**.
> - [ ] **Type K Seebeck coefficient** ≈ **40 μV/°C** (commit to memory for numericals).
> - [ ] **RTD:** PT100, $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385$; needs **constant current excitation (1 mA)**; **4-wire (Kelvin) is best**.
> - [ ] **Bridge sensitivity formula** (small-signal): $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \Delta R / (4R)$ — **valid only for null-balanced bridges with small deviations**.
> - [ ] **Thermistor:** **Steinhart–Hart** is the standard linearization; excitation must be in **μA range** to avoid self-heating.
> - [ ] **Self-heating formula:** $\Delta T_{sh} = P / \delta$, where $\delta$ is the dissipation constant (mW/°C).
> - [ ] **IA gain formula:** $G = 1 + (2R_1 / R_{gain})$.
> - [ ] **Cold-junction omission error** = ambient temperature (e.g., 25 °C board → 25 °C measurement error for Type K).
> - [ ] **Lead-resistance error for 2-wire RTD** = $r_{lead} / \alpha R_0$ in °C.
> - [ ] **3-wire RTD cancellation works ONLY if the two current leads have identical resistance**.
> - [ ] Always include **filtering** (50 Hz notch) and **isolation** in the final block diagram.
> - [ ] **ADC selection:** For thermocouples → ≥ 16-bit; for RTDs → ≥ 16-bit; for thermistors → ≥ 12-bit typically.
> - [ ] **Always quote units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> - [ ] **Draw the bridge/IA diagram with proper labels** — unlabeled circuits receive zero credit even if the analysis is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Cold Junction Compensation and Linearization - Software and Hardware Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Cold Junction Compensation and Linearization - Software and Hardware Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)?

**Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** is the process of correcting the output voltage of a thermocouple to account for the temperature of the *reference (cold) junction* — the terminal where the thermocouple wires connect to the measuring instrument. Since a thermocouple inherently measures the **temperature difference** between its hot junction (measuring point) and its cold junction (terminal block), one must know the cold junction temperature to determine the *true* hot junction temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Cold Junction Compensation is the technique of adding (algebraically) a compensation voltage equal to the Seebeck voltage that would be generated between 0 °C and the actual cold junction temperature, so that the net measured EMF corresponds exclusively to the temperature difference between the hot junction and 0 °C.*

## 1.2 What is Linearization?

**Linearization** is the process of converting the inherently non-linear thermoelectric EMF-versus-temperature characteristic of a thermocouple into a linear (or near-linear) relationship between the instrument output and the measured temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Linearization refers to mathematical or electronic techniques used to remove the non-linearity present in the transfer function of a temperature sensor so that the final output is directly proportional to the physical variable being measured.*

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Translator at the Airport"**
> Imagine you are measuring the height of a building using a *flexible rubber measuring tape* (the thermocouple). Two problems occur:
> 1. **Cold Junction Problem:** The tape was originally marked at the factory with the *starting end anchored at sea level* (0 °C). But when you use it on top of a building, the loose end of the tape is sitting on the *fifth-floor floor* (cold junction at room temperature). You must mentally "add back" the length from sea level to the fifth floor. CJC does exactly this — it digitally adds back the EMF corresponding to the cold junction temperature.
> 2. **Non-linearity Problem:** Because the tape is rubber, it stretches non-uniformly (small near the top, large near the bottom). To get an accurate height, you must look up a *stretch-correction table* every time you read. Linearization is that correction table.

## 1.4 Why are These Topics Critical?

| Phenomenon | Reason It Matters |
|-------------|------------------|
| Thermocouple Seebeck Voltage | Small (microvolts to millivolts) — any error is significant |
| Reference Junction Drift | Causes large temperature errors (typically **±10 °C** or more without compensation) |
| Non-linearity | Can cause errors of **2–6 %** of full scale across a wide range |

## 1.5 Standard Reference Points and Constants

- **Ice-Point Reference:** **0.0 °C (273.15 K)** — the ITS-90 reference temperature.
- **Standard Polynomial Coefficients:** Defined by the **ITS-90 (International Temperature Scale of 1990)** for each thermocouple type (K, J, T, E, R, S, B, N).
- **Seebeck Coefficient:** Thermocouple-type-dependent; for **Type K** it is approximately **40 µV/°C** at 25 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermocouple Working Principle (Recap)

A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metals joined at the **measuring (hot) junction**. When the two ends are at different temperatures, a thermoelectric EMF is generated due to the **Seebeck effect**. The generated voltage is described by:

$$
E_{\text{measured}} = \int_{T_{c}}^{T_{h}} S(T) \, dT
$$

where:
- $T_h$ = Temperature of hot (measuring) junction
- $T_c$ = Temperature of cold (reference) junction
- $S(T)$ = Seebeck coefficient of the thermocouple pair (V/°C), itself a function of temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The Seebeck coefficient $S(T)$ is **not constant** — it varies with temperature, which is precisely why the EMF-vs-temperature relationship is non-linear and why linearization becomes essential.

## 2.2 Cold Junction Compensation — Operational Breakdown

The thermocouple voltage actually delivered to the measuring instrument is:

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = E(T_h, 0) - E(T_c, 0)
$$

To obtain the true hot-junction temperature, the instrument must *add back* the EMF that would be generated if the hot junction were at 0 °C and the cold junction were at $T_c$:

$$
E_{\text{compensated}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E(T_c, 0) = E(T_h, 0)
$$

The compensated EMF $E(T_h, 0)$ can then be converted to temperature $T_h$ using the standard ITS-90 tables or polynomial.

## 2.3 Two Engineering Approaches to CJC

### 2.3.1 Hardware CJC Approach
- **Ice-Bath Method:** The cold junction is physically maintained at 0 °C in a stirred ice-water slurry. Highly accurate but impractical for field use.
- **Electronic Reference Junction:** A precision temperature sensor (e.g., **LM35**, **AD590**, **PT100**, or a silicon diode) is mounted at the terminal block. Its output is amplified and added to the thermocouple EMF using an op-amp summing network.
- **Bridge Compensation:** A Wheatstone bridge incorporating a temperature-sensitive resistor compensates for ambient drift.

### 2.3.2 Software CJC Approach
- A **digital temperature sensor** (DS18B20, TMP36, or integrated ADC channel) measures the cold junction temperature.
- The microprocessor/microcontroller computes the equivalent thermocouple EMF at that temperature using either:
  - A **Look-Up Table (LUT)** stored in memory
  - The **ITS-90 polynomial equation** for the thermocouple type
- This calculated EMF is digitally added to the measured thermocouple voltage before the final temperature is computed.

## 2.4 Linearization — Operational Breakdown

### 2.4.1 Hardware Linearization
- **Piecewise Linear (Breakpoint) Amplifiers:** Different gain resistors switched in at different voltage levels to approximate the inverse of the thermocouple curve.
- **Logarithmic Amplifiers:** Use the non-linear V–I characteristics of diodes or transistors to "shape" the response.
- **Analog Function Fitter Circuits:** Combinations of op-amps, diodes, and resistors emulate the inverse polynomial.

### 2.4.2 Software Linearization
- **Polynomial Computation:** Use the standard ITS-90 inverse polynomial:
  $$
  T = a_0 + a_1 E + a_2 E^2 + a_3 E^3 + \ldots + a_n E^n
  $$
  (where $E$ is the compensated EMF in mV)
- **Look-Up Table with Interpolation:** Store a finite set of (EMF, temperature) points and interpolate linearly between them.
- **Piecewise Polynomial Approximation:** Divide the range into multiple sub-ranges, each fit by a low-order polynomial.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Seebeck EMF (general) | $E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S(T) \, dT$ | $S(T)$: Seebeck coefficient |
| 2 | CJC compensation | $E_{\text{comp}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E(T_c, 0)$ | Adds cold-junction EMF back |
| 3 | Hot-junction temperature | $E_{\text{comp}} = E(T_h, 0) \rightarrow T_h$ | Use inverse polynomial / LUT |
| 4 | ITS-90 inverse polynomial | $T = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i E^i$ | Coefficients in NIST tables |
| 5 | Polynomial segmentation | $T = \sum_{j} \sum_{i} a_{j,i} \, (E - E_{j,0})^i$ | Used for sub-zero and high ranges |
| 6 | Piecewise linear approx. | $T \approx T_k + \frac{T_{k+1}-T_k}{E_{k+1}-E_k} (E - E_k)$ | LUT-based interpolation |
| 7 | Sensitivity (Type K) | $S \approx 40 \,\mu\text{V}/°\text{C}$ at 25 °C | Reference value |
| 8 | Cold-junction error | $\Delta T_{\text{err}} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{CJC}}}{S(T_c)}$ | Critical for design margins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** CJC and linearization together enable a **±0.1 °C to ±0.5 °C** accuracy with modern digital thermocouple instruments, whereas an uncompensated, non-linearized system would be limited to **±5 °C to ±10 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Cold Junction Compensation Equation

### Step 1 — Define the Two EMF Contributions

By the **Law of Intermediate Metals**, the total thermoelectric EMF around a circuit depends only on the junction materials and their temperatures. So for a thermocouple with hot junction at $T_h$ and cold junction at $T_c$, the measured EMF can be expressed as:

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = E_{\text{type}}(T_h) - E_{\text{type}}(T_c)
$$

where $E_{\text{type}}(T)$ is the standard Seebeck EMF of the thermocouple (with reference junction at 0 °C) at temperature $T$.

### Step 2 — Solve for the Hot-Junction EMF

Rearrange the equation to isolate the unknown $E_{\text{type}}(T_h)$:

$$
E_{\text{type}}(T_h) = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{type}}(T_c)
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Compensated EMF to Temperature

Using the **inverse ITS-90 polynomial**, with $E_{\text{type}}(T_h)$ in millivolts:

$$
T_h = a_0 + a_1 E_{\text{comp}} + a_2 E_{\text{comp}}^{2} + a_3 E_{\text{comp}}^{3} + \ldots + a_n E_{\text{comp}}^{n}
$$

where $E_{\text{comp}} = E_{\text{type}}(T_h)$.

> **Reasoning:** This two-step method (CJC first, then linearization) is the foundation of every modern digital thermocouple instrument.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Type K Thermocouple)

> **Problem:** A Type K thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The instrument reads a thermocouple voltage of $E_{\text{meas}} = 20.512$ mV, and the cold junction is measured at $T_c = 25$ °C. Determine the hot-junction temperature $T_h$. (Use $E_{\text{type}}(25\,°\text{C}) = 1.000$ mV and the standard inverse polynomial coefficients for Type K.)

**Standard inverse polynomial coefficients for Type K (Range: 0 to 500 °C):**
- $a_0 = 0$
- $a_1 = 25.17350$
- $a_2 = -0.214419$
- $a_3 = 0.00208045$
- $a_4 = -1.04749 \times 10^{-5}$

### Step 1 — Apply Cold Junction Compensation

$$
E_{\text{comp}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{type}}(T_c)
$$

$$
E_{\text{comp}} = 20.512 + 1.000 = 21.512 \,\text{mV}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Linearization Polynomial

Substitute $E_{\text{comp}} = 21.512$ mV into the inverse polynomial:

$$
T_h = a_1 E_{\text{comp}} + a_2 E_{\text{comp}}^{2} + a_3 E_{\text{comp}}^{3} + a_4 E_{\text{comp}}^{4}
$$

#### Compute each term step by step:

**Term 1 — Linear term:**
$$
a_1 E = (25.17350)(21.512) = 541.532 \,°\text{C}
$$

**Term 2 — Quadratic term:**
$$
E^2 = (21.512)^2 = 462.766
$$
$$
a_2 E^2 = (-0.214419)(462.766) = -99.226 \,°\text{C}
$$

**Term 3 — Cubic term:**
$$
E^3 = (21.512)^2 \times 21.512 = 462.766 \times 21.512 = 9955.75
$$
$$
a_3 E^3 = (0.00208045)(9955.75) = 20.713 \,°\text{C}
$$

**Term 4 — Quartic term:**
$$
E^4 = 9955.75 \times 21.512 = 214,191
$$
$$
a_4 E^4 = (-1.04749 \times 10^{-5})(214,191) = -2.244 \,°\text{C}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum All Terms

$$
T_h = 541.532 - 99.226 + 20.713 - 2.244
$$

$$
T_h = 460.775 \,°\text{C} \;\approx\; 461 \,°\text{C}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The actual hot-junction temperature is approximately **461 °C**, even though the raw EMF reading alone would have suggested a much lower temperature (around 516 °C using only linear scaling). This clearly demonstrates the necessity of both **CJC and linearization**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hardware vs Software Approaches

| Parameter | Hardware CJC + Linearization | Software CJC + Linearization |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Accuracy | ±1 °C to ±2 °C (typical) | ±0.1 °C to ±0.5 °C |
| Cost | Moderate (precision resistors, op-amps) | Low (single IC sensor + MCU) |
| Flexibility | Fixed for one thermocouple type | Easily reconfigurable |
| Calibration | Manual, time-consuming | Software-controlled |
| Drift susceptibility | Analog components drift with age | No drift; sensor does |
| Multiplexing | Difficult | Easy across many channels |
| Speed | Continuous (real-time analog) | Limited by ADC + computation |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher (due to MCU + sensor) |
| Best suited for | Field instruments, low-cost devices | Lab-grade, multi-channel systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware CJC + Linearization Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **analog signal chain** used in traditional hardware-based thermocouple instrumentation:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Junction Sensor]:::sensor -->|Thermocouple wires| B[Terminal Block Cold Junction]
    B -->|Differential mV signal| C[Preamplifier Instrumentation Amp]
    D[Bridge Sensor LM35 / AD590]:::sensor -->|Cold junction temperature| E[Compensation Amplifier]
    C -->|Amplified thermocouple EMF| E
    E -->|Summed compensation| F[Piecewise Linear Amplifier]
    F -->|Shaped analog signal| G[Output Stage 0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA]
    G --> H[Display or Controller]

    classDef sensor fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#1E5BB8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Functional Walkthrough:**
- The **Hot Junction Sensor** produces a low-level mV signal (typically 0–50 mV).
- The **Cold Junction** at the terminal block introduces a variable offset voltage.
- The **Preamplifier** (instrumentation amplifier) boosts the small differential signal with high CMRR.
- The **Bridge Sensor** (e.g., LM35) produces a voltage proportional to the cold-junction temperature.
- The **Compensation Amplifier** (summing op-amp) adds the cold-junction EMF to the thermocouple signal, effectively moving the reference to 0 °C.
- The **Piecewise Linear Amplifier** applies hardware linearization using breakpoint diodes/resistors.
- The **Output Stage** converts the corrected, linearized signal to a standard industrial format.

## 4.2 Software CJC + Linearization Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the **digital signal processing** path used in modern microcontroller-based thermocouple instruments:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Thermocouple EMF Acquisition] --> B1[16 bit or 24 bit ADC]
    D1[Digital Temperature Sensor at Cold Junction] --> E1[MCU Reads CJ Temperature]
    B1 --> F1[Software CJC Module]
    E1 --> G1[Lookup Table or Polynomial for CJ Voltage]
    G1 --> F1
    F1 --> H1[Compensated EMF in mV]
    H1 --> I1{Linearization Method}
    I1 -->|Polynomial| J1[ITS 90 Inverse Polynomial Compute]
    I1 -->|LUT| K1[Lookup Table Linear Interpolation]
    J1 --> L1[Final Temperature in deg C]
    K1 --> L1
    L1 --> M1[Display / UART / Industrial Bus]
```

**Functional Walkthrough:**
- The thermocouple EMF and the cold-junction temperature are acquired in parallel.
- The **ADC** digitizes the thermocouple signal at high resolution (≥16-bit).
- The **Software CJC Module** retrieves the equivalent voltage at the CJ temperature from either a LUT or a forward polynomial.
- After compensation, the resulting EMF is fed to the chosen **linearization method** (polynomial or LUT).
- The **Final Temperature** is displayed or transmitted via a digital bus (Modbus, HART, UART).

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing Hardware vs Software Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Design Start] --> B0{Is multi type thermocouple support required}
    B0 -->|Yes| C0[Choose Software CJC and Linearization]
    B0 -->|No| D0{Is high channel count required}
    D0 -->|Yes| C0
    D0 -->|No| E0{Is cost the primary driver}
    E0 -->|Yes| F0[Choose Hardware CJC with Piecewise Linear Amplifier]
    E0 -->|No| G0[Choose Software CJC with Polynomial Linearization]
    C0 --> H0[End Recommendation]
    F0 --> H0
    G0 --> H0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> In modern industrial design, **software CJC + software linearization** has become the dominant approach because it provides reconfigurability, self-calibration, and superior accuracy at minimal incremental cost. Hardware CJC is now largely confined to intrinsically safe (IS) and low-power analog transmitters.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**What is Cold Junction Compensation? Why is it necessary in thermocouple-based temperature measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) is the technique of correcting the thermocouple output to account for the temperature of the reference (cold) junction, so that the measured EMF corresponds to the temperature difference between the hot junction and 0 °C.
- **Working principle (1 mark):** It involves adding a compensation voltage equal to the Seebeck EMF corresponding to the cold-junction temperature to the measured thermocouple voltage.
- **Necessity (1 mark):** A thermocouple inherently measures only the *temperature difference*. Without CJC, errors of ±10 °C or more can occur because the cold junction drifts with ambient temperature.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between hardware and software methods of linearization in thermocouple instruments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hardware Linearization | Software Linearization |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Method | Uses analog circuits (op-amps, diodes) to shape the response | Uses mathematical polynomials or look-up tables in a processor |
| Accuracy | ±1 °C to ±2 °C | ±0.1 °C to ±0.5 °C |
| Flexibility | Fixed; difficult to modify for different thermocouple types | Easily reconfigurable in software |

(1 mark for any two valid differences, 1 mark for definition clarity, 1 mark for technical accuracy)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **Instructions (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer either Question A or Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **hardware approach to cold junction compensation** in a thermocouple-based temperature measurement system. Mention the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
The student should draw a block diagram showing:
- Thermocouple Hot Junction → Terminal Block (Cold Junction) → Instrumentation Amplifier → Compensation Amplifier ← Temperature Sensor (LM35/AD590) at cold junction → Linearization Circuit → Output Stage

**Explanation of each block (3 marks):**
1. **Thermocouple & Hot Junction:** Generates mV-level Seebeck EMF proportional to temperature difference.
2. **Terminal Block (Cold Junction):** Reference point whose temperature must be known/compensated.
3. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Boosts the small differential thermocouple signal with high CMRR, rejecting common-mode noise.
4. **Temperature Sensor (LM35 / AD590):** Senses the ambient temperature at the cold junction.
5. **Compensation Amplifier:** Sums the cold-junction sensor voltage with the amplified thermocouple signal.
6. **Linearization Circuit:** Piecewise amplifier that shapes the non-linear curve into a linear response.
7. **Output Stage:** Produces a standard industrial signal (0–10 V or 4–20 mA).

**Role of CJC in this chain (1 mark):** The compensation amplifier effectively "translates" the thermocouple reference from $T_c$ (room temperature) to 0 °C, so the displayed temperature corresponds to the absolute hot-junction temperature.

---

**(b)** A Type K thermocouple measures a furnace temperature. The instrument reads **18.40 mV** and the cold-junction temperature is **30 °C**. Given that the equivalent Seebeck EMF for a Type K thermocouple at 30 °C is **1.203 mV**, calculate the **compensated EMF** and then determine the **hot-junction temperature** using the inverse polynomial:

$$
T = 25.1735 E - 0.214419 E^{2} + 0.00208045 E^{3} - 1.04749 \times 10^{-5} E^{4}
$$

valid for 0–500 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply Cold Junction Compensation (2 marks):**
$$
E_{\text{comp}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E(T_c)
$$
$$
E_{\text{comp}} = 18.40 + 1.203 = 19.603 \,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear term (1 mark):**
$$
a_1 E = 25.1735 \times 19.603 = 493.52
$$

**Step 3 — Quadratic term (1 mark):**
$$
E^2 = (19.603)^2 = 384.25
$$
$$
a_2 E^2 = -0.214419 \times 384.25 = -82.39
$$

**Step 4 — Cubic term (1 mark):**
$$
E^3 = 384.25 \times 19.603 = 7531.5
$$
$$
a_3 E^3 = 0.00208045 \times 7531.5 = 15.67
$$

**Step 5 — Quartic term + Final Sum (2 marks):**
$$
E^4 = 7531.5 \times 19.603 = 147,649
$$
$$
a_4 E^4 = -1.04749 \times 10^{-5} \times 147,649 = -1.55
$$
$$
T_h = 493.52 - 82.39 + 15.67 - 1.55 = 425.25 \,°\text{C} \;\approx\; 425 \,°\text{C}
$$

**Final Answer:** The hot-junction temperature is approximately **425 °C**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **software approach to cold junction compensation and linearization** in a digital thermocouple instrument. Describe the role of look-up tables and inverse polynomials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Software CJC Process (3 marks):**
1. A digital temperature sensor (e.g., DS18B20) is mounted at the cold-junction terminal block.
2. The microcontroller reads both the thermocouple EMF (via ADC) and the cold-junction temperature.
3. The MCU computes the equivalent Seebeck EMF at the cold-junction temperature using a pre-stored look-up table or the standard polynomial for the thermocouple type.
4. This computed EMF is added digitally to the measured thermocouple EMF to obtain the compensated value.

**Software Linearization Process (3 marks):**
1. The compensated EMF is converted to temperature using the **ITS-90 inverse polynomial**:
   $$
   T = a_0 + a_1 E + a_2 E^2 + a_3 E^3 + \ldots
   $$
2. Alternatively, a **look-up table** of pre-computed (EMF, Temperature) pairs is stored in memory, and the MCU performs **linear interpolation** between the two nearest points.
3. Multi-range polynomial segmentation may be used for sub-zero or high-temperature ranges to maintain accuracy.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High accuracy (±0.1 °C)
- Easy reconfigurability across thermocouple types (K, J, T, E, etc.)
- Eliminates analog drift; calibration done in software

---

**(b)** Compare the **hardware and software approaches** of cold junction compensation and linearization. State **three advantages** and **two limitations** of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Hardware Approach | Software Approach |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Reference sensor | Analog (LM35, AD590) | Digital (DS18B20) |
| Compensation method | Op-amp summing | Digital addition in MCU |
| Linearization method | Piecewise analog circuits | Polynomial / LUT |
| Accuracy | ±1 °C to ±2 °C | ±0.1 °C to ±0.5 °C |
| Cost | Moderate | Low to moderate |
| Reconfigurability | Difficult | Easy |

**Hardware — Three Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. No software dependency — works even in bare-metal or noisy environments.
2. Lower power consumption — suitable for intrinsically safe and loop-powered devices.
3. Continuous real-time processing — no ADC latency.

**Hardware — Two Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Drift in analog components affects long-term accuracy.
2. Difficult to switch between thermocouple types without hardware changes.

**Software — Three Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Superior accuracy through high-order polynomial fitting.
2. Easily reconfigured for different thermocouple types by software selection.
3. Built-in self-calibration and diagnostic features.

**Software — Two Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Requires an MCU and digital sensor — increases BOM and power consumption.
2. Computation latency may limit sampling rate in high-speed applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CJC with Linearization:** CJC corrects the *reference*; linearization corrects the *shape*. Examiners often test this distinction. Mixing them up costs up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign Error in CJC Equation:** Students often *subtract* the cold-junction EMF instead of *adding* it. The correct relation is $E_{\text{comp}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E(T_c, 0)$.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in the Polynomial:** ITS-90 polynomials expect **EMF in mV** for thermocouple types K, J, T, E and **EMF in µV** for types R, S, B. Mixing units is a frequent error.
> 4. **Skipping the Diagram:** In 7-mark questions, a labelled block diagram is *mandatory* and carries up to **3 marks** by itself. Skipping it is a guaranteed deduction.
> 5. **Not Mentioning the Thermocouple Type:** Always state whether you are working with Type K, J, etc., since the polynomial coefficients differ.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ A **thermocouple measures temperature difference**, not absolute temperature.
- ⭐ **CJC equation:** $E_{\text{comp}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E(T_c, 0)$ — always *add* the cold-junction EMF.
- ⭐ **ITS-90 Standard** defines the reference polynomial coefficients for all standard thermocouple types.
- ⭐ **Hardware CJC** uses analog temperature sensors (LM35, AD590) at the cold junction; **Software CJC** uses digital sensors (DS18B20) and polynomial / LUT computation in an MCU.
- ⭐ **Hardware linearization** uses piecewise amplifiers; **Software linearization** uses polynomials or LUTs.
- ⭐ **Type K sensitivity** ≈ 40 µV/°C at 25 °C.
- ⭐ **Error without CJC** can be ±10 °C or more — significant in precision applications.
- ⭐ **Software approach is dominant** in modern digital instruments due to higher accuracy and reconfigurability.
- ⭐ **Reverse-polarity caution:** Compensation voltage must have the correct sign; otherwise the error is doubled, not cancelled.
- ⭐ For polynomial linearization, always use **mV for base-metal** thermocouples (K, J, T, E) and **µV for noble-metal** thermocouples (R, S, B).
- ⭐ The compensated EMF $E_{\text{comp}}$ represents the EMF that *would* be generated if the reference were at 0 °C — this is the input to the linearization step.
- ⭐ In exam problems, always show: (1) CJC calculation, (2) intermediate powers of $E$, (3) individual polynomial terms, (4) final summation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Transmitters - Overview of 2-Wire and 4-Wire Transmitters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Transmitters - Overview of 2-Wire and 4-Wire Transmitters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Process Transmitter?

A **process transmitter** is a field-mounted electronic instrument that converts a physical measurement (such as temperature, pressure, flow, or level) from a primary sensor into a standardized, noise-immune electrical signal that can be transmitted over long distances to a controller, recorder, or display unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A transmitter is a device that converts the output of a primary sensor into a signal that is suitable for transmission over long distances and that is proportional to the measured process variable. The standard industrial output is a **4–20 mA current signal** (per **ISA 50.1** / IEC 60381 standards).

The three fundamental jobs of any transmitter are:
1. **Sense** – Accept a low-level signal (mV, Ω, µV) from a sensor.
2. **Condition** – Amplify, linearize, isolate, and compensate (cold-junction, zero, span).
3. **Transmit** – Send a robust, standardized output (typically **4–20 mA DC** or a digital protocol such as **HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus**).

---

## 1.2 The Two-Wire (Loop-Powered) Transmitter

A **two-wire transmitter** uses the **same pair of wires** to carry both the DC power supply to the instrument *and* the output measurement signal. The instrument literally "steals" its operating power from the signal loop itself.

**Key Identifier:** A 2-wire transmitter has only **2 terminals** that connect to the outside world (often labelled `+SIG/PWR` and `−SIG/PWR`).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Candle that Eats its Own Wick"
Imagine a small lantern standing in a dark field. The lantern needs electrical power to glow, but the only cable leading to it is also the very cable that carries the message "how bright the lantern should glow." The lantern cleverly siphons just enough energy from that same two-wire line to run its internal electronics, while the remainder of the current is the *information*. That is exactly what a loop-powered (2-wire) transmitter does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Live 0% / Live Zero (4 mA):** The current never falls below 4 mA even when the measured variable is at its lowest value. This 4 mA "offset" simultaneously (a) powers the transmitter electronics and (b) acts as a **live zero** — if the loop current drops to 0 mA, the controller immediately knows the loop is broken (a fault condition), which a 0–20 mA signal could not distinguish from a true zero reading.

---

## 1.3 The Four-Wire Transmitter

A **four-wire transmitter** uses **two separate pairs of wires**:
- **Pair 1 (Power pair):** Carries AC or DC mains power *to* the transmitter.
- **Pair 2 (Signal pair):** Carries the measurement output *away* from the transmitter.

The signal wires are therefore completely **galvanically isolated** from the power lines.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Hoses at a Petrol Pump"
Picture a fuel dispenser at a station. One large hose (the power pair) brings in abundant, high-pressure electrical energy dedicated solely to running the pump's motor and electronics. A second, smaller hose (the signal pair) returns the actual measurement (the litres dispensed) back to the cashier's display. The two flows are independent, just like the wires in a 4-wire transmitter.

> [!NOTE]
> Because power and signal are separated, a 4-wire transmitter can source or sink **higher output currents (0–20 mA or 0–10 V)** with **greater accuracy, faster response, and higher power** at the sensor — ideal for high-precision laboratory and process-critical applications.

---

## 1.4 Why the 2-Wire / 4-Wire Choice Matters

| Choice | Power Source | Loop Current Range | Typical Supply | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2-Wire | From the signal loop | **4–20 mA** (live zero) | **24 V DC** | Hazardous areas (intrinsic safety), remote field sites |
| 4-Wire | Mains AC or separate DC | 0–20 mA, 0–10 V, ±10 V | **110/230 V AC** or 24/48 V DC | High-power sensors, fast-responding control loops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The term *"loop-powered"* is synonymous with 2-wire. The terms *"self-powered"* or *"line-powered"* typically describe 4-wire devices. Always write this distinction explicitly in 14-mark answers to earn full marks.

---

## 1.5 The 3-Wire Compromise (Bridge Concept)

A brief but important intermediate topology: a **3-wire transmitter** shares a common return/ground between power and signal but uses separate forward paths. It is most commonly used in RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) bridges and some strain-gauge signal conditioners. The KTU syllabus focuses on 2-wire and 4-wire, so the 3-wire form will appear only as a comparison point.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Architecture of a Transmitter

Regardless of the wire-count, every smart transmitter contains the same logical sub-blocks:

1. **Sensor Interface / Bridge Excitation** – energizes the primary sensor (RTD, thermocouple, strain gauge, capacitive cell).
2. **Signal Conditioning Block** – instrumentation amplifier, cold-junction compensation, linearization, span/zero trim.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** – for microprocessor-based "smart" units.
4. **Microcontroller / Firmware** – performs characterization, range-change, engineering-unit conversion.
5. **Output Driver** – converts digital word to a 4–20 mA current sink (loop interface) or voltage output.
6. **Power Supply / Regulator** – derives internal rails (typically ±5 V, +3.3 V, +24 V) from the loop or mains.

> In a **2-wire** device, blocks (1)–(5) must operate on a total of **less than 4 mA × V_loop**, which forces the designer to use low-power CMOS and efficient switching regulators.
> In a **4-wire** device, block (6) takes mains or separate DC and can deliver up to several hundred mA to the sensor, enabling heater drive, LCD displays, and higher sensor excitation.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### 2.2.1 2-Wire Transmitter Operation
- A **24 V DC** power supply is placed in series with the loop.
- The transmitter regulates its internal voltage from the loop drop; the difference between supply voltage and transmitter drop becomes the voltage available for the loop wiring and the receiver burden.
- The transmitter acts as a **variable current sink**: the sensor signal is mapped to a current between **4 mA and 20 mA**.
- The receiver (e.g., controller analog input) typically has a **250 Ω burden resistor**, converting 4–20 mA into **1–5 V** for the ADC.

### 2.2.2 4-Wire Transmitter Operation
- The mains pair powers the internal supply; the signal pair is **electrically isolated** via an optocoupler or transformer.
- The output stage can be a true current *source* (active 4–20 mA) or a voltage output.
- Higher drive capability allows driving long cables and high-impedance loads.
- The 4-wire design supports **3-way isolation** (power, input, output) for superior noise rejection.

---

## 2.3 The Fundamental Loop Equation

The single most important formula for a 2-wire design is the **voltage budget equation**:

$$V_{supply} \;=\; V_{drop,\,Tx} \;+\; V_{drop,\,wire} \;+\; V_{drop,\,load}$$

Equivalently, the **maximum allowable loop resistance** is:

$$R_{loop,\,max} \;=\; \frac{V_{supply} \;-\; V_{drop,\,Tx(min)}}{I_{max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** Voltages in **V (volts)**, currents in **A (amperes)**, resistances in **Ω (ohms)**. A 24 V supply with a 12 V transmitter drop and 20 mA maximum loop current allows only **R_loop = 600 Ω**.

---

## 2.4 Signal Conversion Relationships

The current signal is linearly related to the process variable:

$$I_{out} \;=\; 4\,\text{mA} \;+\; 16\,\text{mA} \times \left(\frac{PV \;-\; PV_{LRV}}{PV_{URV} \;-\; PV_{LRV}}\right)$$

where:
- $PV$ = instantaneous process variable
- $PV_{LRV}$ = Lower Range Value
- $PV_{URV}$ = Upper Range Value

After a 250 Ω burden resistor, the receiver voltage is:

$$V_{rec} \;=\; I_{out} \times R_{burden} \;=\; I_{out} \times 250\,\Omega$$

This yields the familiar **1–5 V** standard (or **0–5 V** if a 0–20 mA / 250 Ω combination is used).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{supply} = V_{Tx} + I_{loop}\,R_{wire} + I_{loop}\,R_{load}$ | Loop voltage budget |
| 2 | $R_{loop,\,max} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{Tx(min)}}{I_{max}}$ | Maximum allowable series loop resistance |
| 3 | $I_{out} = 4 + 16\!\left(\dfrac{PV - PV_{LRV}}{PV_{URV} - PV_{LRV}}\right)\,\text{mA}$ | Current-loop PV to mA conversion |
| 4 | $V_{rec} = I_{out} \times R_{burden}$ | Receiver voltage (e.g., 250 Ω → 1–5 V) |
| 5 | $P_{Tx} = (V_{supply} - V_{load}) \times I_{min}$ | Power available to 2-wire transmitter |
| 6 | $\%\,\text{of span} = \dfrac{PV - LRV}{URV - LRV} \times 100$ | Engineering percent |
| 7 | $P_{supply,\,load} = I_{loop} \times R_{load}$ | Power dissipated in receiver burden |
| 8 | $e_{noise} = 4 k T R \Delta f$ | Johnson–Nyquist noise across loop wires |
| 9 | $t_{step} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_{3dB}}$ | Relationship between −3 dB bandwidth and step rise-time |
| 10 | $V_{sense} = I_{exc} \times R_{sensor}$ | Sensor excitation to voltage conversion (RTD, strain gauge) |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbols use `\vert` style separators to prevent markdown table breaks. Use them when copying into your own notes.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas** — A 2-wire HART pressure transmitter on an offshore platform draws < 4 mA so it can be certified **Intrinsically Safe (IS)** in Zone 1 hazardous areas.
- **Power Plants** — A 4-wire flow transmitter with 110 V AC supply can drive heated vortex-shedding sensors that need > 50 mA.
- **Pharmaceutical & HVAC** — 4-wire temperature/humidity transmitters are preferred in clean rooms for their higher accuracy (±0.05 °C vs ±0.2 °C) and galvanic isolation.
- **Aerospace Test Rigs** — 4-wire strain transmitters operate at ±10 V output to drive long shielded cables to data-acquisition racks.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Loop Resistance of a 2-Wire Transmitter

We need to express $R_{loop,\,max}$ in terms of measurable supply and transmitter parameters. We start from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.

### Step 1 — State the closed-loop KVL

The supply voltage must equal the sum of all voltage drops around the series loop:

$$V_{S} \;=\; V_{Tx(min)} \;+\; I_{max} \cdot R_{wire} \;+\; I_{max} \cdot R_{load}$$

Here $V_{Tx(min)}$ is the **minimum voltage drop** the transmitter needs across its terminals to operate, $R_{wire}$ is the round-trip cable resistance, and $R_{load}$ is the receiver burden.

### Step 2 — Group the resistive terms

Factor the loop current $I_{max}$ on the right:

$$V_{S} \;=\; V_{Tx(min)} \;+\; I_{max}\bigl(R_{wire} + R_{load}\bigr)$$

The total series resistance seen by the supply is:

$$R_{total} \;=\; R_{wire} + R_{load}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the maximum allowable total resistance

Subtract $V_{Tx(min)}$ from both sides:

$$V_{S} \;-\; V_{Tx(min)} \;=\; I_{max} \cdot R_{total}$$

Divide both sides by $I_{max}$:

$$\frac{V_{S} \;-\; V_{Tx(min)}}{I_{max}} \;=\; R_{total}$$

Therefore the **maximum allowable loop resistance** is:

$$\boxed{\,R_{loop,\,max} \;=\; \frac{V_{S} \;-\; V_{Tx(min)}}{I_{max}}\,}$$

This is exactly the same expression listed in the KTU formula sheet, now rigorously derived.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2-Wire Loop Sizing

> **Problem (KTU model):** A pressure transmitter is to be installed **800 m** away from the control room. The cable is **1.0 mm² copper** (resistance ≈ 36 Ω/km round-trip, so 28.8 Ω for 800 m). The receiver burden is **250 Ω**. The transmitter requires a **minimum 12 V** at its terminals and operates on a standard **24 V DC** supply. Verify that the design is feasible at **20 mA** loop current, and compute the spare headroom.

### Step 1 — Total loop resistance

$$R_{total} \;=\; R_{wire} + R_{load}$$

Substitute $R_{wire} = 28.8\,\Omega$ and $R_{load} = 250\,\Omega$:

$$R_{total} \;=\; 28.8 + 250 \;=\; 278.8\,\Omega$$

### Step 2 — Voltage drop across the resistive elements at 20 mA

$$V_{drop,\,R} \;=\; I_{max} \cdot R_{total}$$

Convert current: $I_{max} = 20 \text{ mA} = 0.020\,\text{A}$.

$$V_{drop,\,R} \;=\; 0.020 \times 278.8 \;=\; 5.576\,\text{V}$$

### Step 3 — Voltage remaining for the transmitter

$$V_{Tx} \;=\; V_{S} \;-\; V_{drop,\,R}$$

$$V_{Tx} \;=\; 24 \;-\; 5.576 \;=\; 18.424\,\text{V}$$

### Step 4 — Check against the 12 V minimum

$$V_{Tx} = 18.424\,\text{V} \;\geq\; V_{Tx(min)} = 12\,\text{V} \;\;\checkmark$$

The design is feasible.

### Step 5 — Spare headroom (useful for design margin)

$$V_{margin} \;=\; V_{Tx} - V_{Tx(min)} \;=\; 18.424 - 12 \;=\; 6.424\,\text{V}$$

This 6.4 V headroom will absorb temperature drift in the cable resistance and any ageing effects.

### Step 6 — Maximum allowable cable length (reverse calculation)

If the designer wants to know how far the cable could be extended before failure, set $V_{Tx} = V_{Tx(min)} = 12\,\text{V}$:

$$V_{drop,\,R,\,max} \;=\; V_{S} - V_{Tx(min)} \;=\; 24 - 12 \;=\; 12\,\text{V}$$

$$R_{total,\,max} \;=\; \frac{V_{drop,\,R,\,max}}{I_{max}} \;=\; \frac{12}{0.020} \;=\; 600\,\Omega$$

$$R_{wire,\,max} \;=\; 600 - 250 \;=\; 350\,\Omega$$

$$L_{max} \;=\; \frac{R_{wire,\,max}}{R_{per\,km}} \;=\; \frac{350}{36} \;\approx\; 9.72\,\text{km}$$

So the design is comfortable — the cable could be **roughly ten times longer** before the transmitter under-voltages.

---

## 3.3 Conversion Derivation: PV ↔ Current ↔ Voltage

The controller never sees current directly; it sees voltage across a precision burden. Derive the explicit transfer function.

### Start with the linear current equation

$$I_{out} \;=\; 4 + 16\!\left(\frac{PV - LRV}{URV - LRV}\right)\,\text{mA}$$

### Multiply by the burden to obtain receiver voltage

$$V_{rec} \;=\; R_{b} \cdot I_{out}$$

For $R_{b} = 250\,\Omega$ and $I_{out}$ in mA:

$$V_{rec} \;=\; 250 \times \bigl[4 + 16\,x\bigr] \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}, \quad x = \frac{PV - LRV}{URV - LRV}$$

Simplify:

$$V_{rec} \;=\; 1.0 + 4.0\,x \;\; \text{V}$$

So the **1 V = LRV** and **5 V = URV** convention emerges naturally. A simple ADC reading of 2.5 V means the process is at 37.5 % of span.

### Inverting the relationship

To recover the process variable from a measured current $I$:

$$PV \;=\; LRV \;+\; \left(\frac{I_{out} - 4}{16}\right) (URV - LRV)$$

And from receiver voltage with $R_b = 250\,\Omega$:

$$PV \;=\; LRV \;+\; \left(\frac{V_{rec} - 1}{4}\right) (URV - LRV)$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — 2-Wire vs 4-Wire

| Parameter | 2-Wire (Loop-Powered) | 4-Wire (Line-Powered) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of conductors in the field | **2** | **4** (sometimes 3 with shared GND) |
| Power source | Same loop (typically **24 V DC**) | Separate mains **110/230 V AC** or 24/48 V DC |
| Available transmitter power | 4 mA × (V_S − V_load) ≈ **36–90 mW** | Up to several **watts** |
| Output signal | 4–20 mA only (current sink) | 0–20 mA, 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, ±10 V |
| Cable length capability | Limited by V budget (≤ ~5 km) | Practically unlimited by V budget |
| Intrinsic Safety (IS) | **Inherently IS** | Requires zener barrier or isolator |
| Sensor excitation | Very low (cold-junction, mV-level) | High (drives heaters, RTD bridges) |
| Accuracy (typical) | ±0.1 % of span | ±0.05 % of span or better |
| Cost | Lower (single twisted pair) | Higher (separate power cable) |
| Typical use | Field transmitters, hazardous areas | Laboratory, control-room rack units |
| Galvanic isolation | Single barrier needed | Three-way isolation standard |
| Failure mode if cable breaks | Current → 0 mA (diagnostic!) | Power lost; signal goes undefined |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "diagnostic 0 mA" property of the 2-wire 4–20 mA loop is what made it the de-facto industry standard in 1960s process control and why it survives today. Memorize this for KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.5 Decision Algorithm for Wire-Count Selection

A field engineer typically walks through this tree when picking a topology:

1. **Is the area hazardous (Zone 0/1/2)?** → Choose **2-wire** for IS compliance.
2. **Is the cable run > 1 km?** → Verify V budget; if insufficient, switch to **4-wire with higher V_S**.
3. **Does the sensor need > 4 mA excitation (heated probe, strain-gauge bridge ≥ 10 V)?** → Choose **4-wire**.
4. **Is sub-mA accuracy required?** → Choose **4-wire** (better signal conditioning).
5. **Is the field bus option needed (HART, FF)?** → Both support HART; only **4-wire** supports high-power Foundation Fieldbus.
6. **Cost constraint dominates?** → Choose **2-wire** — half the cabling cost.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 2-Wire Loop-Powered Transmitter

The following Mermaid block captures the entire signal-and-power flow in a single 2-wire loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    psuA[24 V DC Power Supply] --> termA[+ Terminal Block]
    termA --> wireA[Twisted Pair Cable]
    wireA --> txPWR[Tx Power Conditioner]
    txPWR --> reg[Internal 5 V and 3.3 V Regulators]
    reg --> mcu[MICROCONTROLLER AND ADC]
    sensorA[Primary Sensor RTD TC Strain] --> cond[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    cond --> mcu
    mcu --> vToI[Voltage to Current Converter 4 to 20 mA]
    vToI --> termB[- Terminal Block]
    termB --> wireB[Return Wire]
    wireB --> psuB[Return to Power Supply Negative]
    wireA --> termB
    psuA -.parallel branch.-> recA[Controller Analog Input]
    recA --> burden[250 ohm Burden Resistor]
    burden --> recB[1 to 5 V to ADC]
```

### Reading the Diagram
- The **power supply** on the left and the **current sink inside the transmitter** form a single series loop.
- Sensor information travels: **Sensor → Conditioner → MCU → V-to-I converter → Loop wire → Burden → ADC**.
- The dashed parallel branch represents the **controller's analog input module**, which simply taps across the 250 Ω burden.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — 4-Wire Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mains[110 230 V AC Mains] --> psu[Internal SMPS and Isolated DC DC]
    psu --> reg[Plus 5 V Plus 15 V Internal Rails]
    reg --> mcu[Microcontroller and ADC]
    sensorB[Primary Sensor Bridge RTD pH Probe] --> cond2[Signal Conditioner with Excitation]
    cond2 --> mcu
    mcu --> dac[DAC and Output Driver]
    dac --> iso[Galvanic Isolator 3 kV]
    iso --> outA[Output Plus Terminal 4 to 20 mA OR 0 to 10 V]
    outA --> cableA[Signal Cable Pair 1]
    cableA --> ctrl[Controller Analog Input Module]
    ctrl --> cableB[Signal Cable Pair 2]
    cableB --> outB[Output Minus Terminal]
    psu --> mcu
```

### Reading the Diagram
- The **top rail** (mains → SMPS → regulators) handles *power only*.
- The **bottom rail** (sensor → conditioner → DAC → isolator → output terminals) handles *signal only*.
- A 3 kV galvanic isolator between the two rails prevents ground loops.

---

## 4.3 Combined Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TwoWireSystem[2-Wire Loop Powered System]
        direction TB
        s1[24 V DC Supply] --- l1[Loop Cable 2 Wires]
        l1 --- tx1[Transmitter with V to I Converter]
        tx1 --- s1
        s1 -.tap.-> r1[Receiver 250 ohm to 1 to 5 V]
    end
    subgraph FourWireSystem[4-Wire Line Powered System]
        direction TB
        s2[Mains AC or 24 V DC] --- p1[Power Cable 2 Wires]
        p1 --- tx2[Transmitter with Isolated Output]
        tx2 --- s2
        out1[Signal Cable 2 Wires] --- r2[Receiver ADC or Indicator]
        tx2 --- out1
    end
```

This side-by-side block makes the **single shared loop** of the 2-wire versus the **two independent paths** of the 4-wire immediately obvious — a frequent 7-mark KTU diagram.

---

## 4.4 Pinout Schematic (Conceptual)

### 2-Wire Pinout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Terminal 1: Plus Loop and Power] --> sensorBlock[Internal Circuitry]
    p2[Terminal 2: Minus Loop and Power] --> sensorBlock
```

### 4-Wire Pinout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pA[Terminal A: AC Live or DC Plus Power] --> psBlock[Internal Power Supply]
    pB[Terminal B: AC Neutral or DC Minus Power] --> psBlock
    pC[Terminal C: Signal Plus Output] --> sigBlock[Isolated Output Stage]
    pD[Terminal D: Signal Minus Output] --> sigBlock
```

> [!NOTE]
> In 4-wire current output, the *signal return* is often labelled "COMM" or "SIG−" and is **not** the same node as the power return. Students frequently lose marks by tying them together in answers — keep them electrically distinct.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a two-wire transmitter. State any two advantages. *(3 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A two-wire (loop-powered) transmitter is one in which **the same pair of wires carries both the DC supply power to the instrument and the output measurement current** (4–20 mA). Advantages: (i) only a single twisted pair is needed, reducing cabling cost; (ii) it is naturally suited for **Intrinsic Safety** certification in hazardous areas because the loop current is bounded below 4 mA + 16 mA. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. What is meant by the "live zero" of a 4–20 mA current loop? Why is it preferred over 0–20 mA? *(3 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**
The **"live zero"** refers to the fact that the minimum usable signal is **4 mA**, not 0 mA. This guarantees that the loop is continuously energised — a 0 mA reading therefore unambiguously indicates a **broken wire or power failure**, which 0–20 mA cannot distinguish from a true zero process value. It also provides the ~4 mA of current needed to power the 2-wire transmitter electronics. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Internal choice is allowed** between two full questions.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a) Draw the block diagram of a 2-wire loop-powered transmitter and explain the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Sensor / Primary Element (1 mark)** — Converts the physical variable (pressure, temperature, flow) into a low-level electrical signal (mV, Ω, µA).
2. **Signal Conditioner (1 mark)** — Instrumentation amplifier, cold-junction compensation for thermocouples, linearization, zero and span trim.
3. **ADC and Microcontroller (1 mark)** — Digitizes the conditioned signal, applies characterization polynomials, and computes the engineering-unit value.
4. **Voltage-to-Current Converter (1 mark)** — Converts the digital output word into a precise 4–20 mA current sink, which the loop current pulls through the external wiring.
5. **Power Conditioner (1 mark)** — Accepts the residual loop voltage (after wire and burden drops) and regulates it to stable +5 V and +3.3 V rails for the internal electronics.
6. **Loop Terminals (1 mark)** — Only **two** external connections; they simultaneously deliver power and return the current signal.
7. **Optional Display & HART Modem (1 mark)** — LCD for local readout, HART modem for digital communication superimposed on the 4–20 mA.

The single most distinguishing feature is that **the power-supply block, the signal-output block, and the field wiring all share the same two conductors** — this is the essence of "loop-powered."

---

**(b) A 2-wire 4–20 mA pressure transmitter has a minimum terminal voltage of 12 V and is supplied from a 24 V DC source. The receiver burden is 250 Ω. Calculate: (i) the maximum allowable loop-wire resistance, (ii) the maximum cable length for a copper cable having 36 Ω/km loop resistance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum allowable loop-wire resistance (3 marks):**

Apply the loop budget equation:

$$V_S \;=\; V_{Tx(min)} + I_{max}(R_{wire} + R_{load})$$

$$24 \;=\; 12 + 0.020\,(R_{wire} + 250)$$

$$12 \;=\; 0.020\,(R_{wire} + 250)$$

$$R_{wire} + 250 \;=\; \frac{12}{0.020} \;=\; 600\,\Omega$$

$$R_{wire,\,max} \;=\; 600 - 250 \;=\; \boxed{350\,\Omega}$$

**(ii) Maximum cable length (4 marks):**

Given loop resistance per kilometre = 36 Ω/km:

$$L_{max} \;=\; \frac{R_{wire,\,max}}{R_{per\,km}} \;=\; \frac{350}{36}$$

$$\boxed{L_{max} \;\approx\; 9.72\,\text{km}}$$

**Conclusion:** The transmitter can be installed up to ~9.7 km away from the control room, well beyond the typical 1–2 km plant boundary. This is a key reason 2-wire 4–20 mA dominates long-distance plant wiring.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice to Question A)*
`[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternate Set]`

**(a) Explain the operating principle of a 4-wire transmitter with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a 2-wire transmitter on the basis of (i) power source, (ii) output capability, (iii) intrinsic safety, and (iv) accuracy. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
In a 4-wire transmitter, the **power pair** (AC mains or separate DC) feeds an internal SMPS that generates the regulated DC rails for the sensor and the electronics. The **signal pair** is **galvanically isolated** from the power pair and delivers the measurement output — typically 4–20 mA, 0–20 mA, or 0–10 V — to the receiver. Because the signal stage is powered independently, it can act as a true **voltage or current source** (not merely a current sink) and drive long cables and high impedances.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| # | Parameter | 2-Wire | 4-Wire |
|---|---|---|---|
| i | Power source | Loop itself (24 V DC) | Mains AC or separate DC |
| ii | Output | 4–20 mA only (current sink) | 0–20 mA, 4–20 mA, 0–10 V (current or voltage) |
| iii | Intrinsic safety | Inherently safe | Requires zener barriers or galvanic isolators |
| iv | Accuracy | ±0.1 % to ±0.2 % of span | ±0.05 % or better of span |

---

**(b) For a level transmitter calibrated for 0–5 m water column, calculate the current signal at levels of 1.0 m, 2.5 m, and 4.8 m, and the corresponding receiver voltage across a 250 Ω burden. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The linear current transfer function is:

$$I_{out} \;=\; 4 + 16\!\left(\frac{PV - LRV}{URV - LRV}\right)\,\text{mA}$$

Here $LRV = 0\,\text{m}$ and $URV = 5\,\text{m}$.

**Case 1: PV = 1.0 m (1 mark)**

$$I \;=\; 4 + 16 \times \frac{1.0}{5} \;=\; 4 + 3.2 \;=\; 7.2\,\text{mA}$$

$$V_{rec} \;=\; 7.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 \;=\; 1.80\,\text{V}$$

**Case 2: PV = 2.5 m (1 mark)**

$$I \;=\; 4 + 16 \times \frac{2.5}{5} \;=\; 4 + 8.0 \;=\; 12.0\,\text{mA}$$

$$V_{rec} \;=\; 12.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 \;=\; 3.00\,\text{V}$$

**Case 3: PV = 4.8 m (1 mark)**

$$I \;=\; 4 + 16 \times \frac{4.8}{5} \;=\; 4 + 15.36 \;=\; 19.36\,\text{mA}$$

$$V_{rec} \;=\; 19.36 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 \;=\; 4.84\,\text{V}$$

**Verification of span behaviour (1 mark):**
- 0 % of span → 4 mA → 1 V
- 50 % of span → 12 mA → 3 V
- 100 % of span → 20 mA → 5 V

All three computed points lie perfectly on this line, confirming the linear relationship.

**Engineering conversion check (1 mark):**
At 4.8 m, % of span = (4.8/5) × 100 = **96 %**, so current = 4 + 16 × 0.96 = 19.36 mA ✓

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
