> 1. **Mixing up "current sink" vs "current source."** A 2-wire transmitter is a **current sink**; the energy flows *into* its + terminal. A 4-wire transmitter is typically a **current source**. Drawing the arrow in the wrong direction loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mA to A** in the loop-resistance equation. Using 20 mA directly in $R = V/I$ gives an answer **50× too large**. Always write $I = 0.020$ A.
> 3. **Assuming 0–20 mA when the loop is 4–20 mA.** The 4 mA offset matters both for live-zero detection and for powering the transmitter.
> 4. **Calling 4-wire "self-powered."** That phrase technically refers to a transmitter that generates its own signal power (e.g., a piezoelectric accelerometer). Use "line-powered" or "mains-powered."
> 5. **Omitting the 250 Ω burden** in transfer-function questions. Examiners check for it explicitly.
> 6. **Conflating 3-wire and 4-wire.** A 3-wire device shares the ground return; a 4-wire device does *not*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two-wire transmitter = loop-powered** — only 2 conductors carry both 24 V DC supply and the 4–20 mA signal.
- **Four-wire transmitter = line-powered** — separate power pair and signal pair, with galvanic isolation.
- **Live zero at 4 mA** doubles as (i) transmitter power feed and (ii) broken-loop diagnostic.
- **250 Ω burden** converts 4–20 mA → **1–5 V** for the controller ADC.
- **Loop voltage budget** is the key sizing equation: $V_S \geq V_{Tx(min)} + I_{max}(R_{wire} + R_{load})$.
- **Maximum cable length** in 2-wire designs is set by $R_{loop,max} = (V_S - V_{Tx(min)})/I_{max}$.
- **2-wire is inherently Intrinsically Safe**; 4-wire is preferred for **high accuracy and high-power sensors**.
- **HART protocol** can be superimposed on either 2-wire or 4-wire 4–20 mA.
- **Diagnostic signature** of 2-wire failures: 0 mA = open loop; 4 mA = sensor below LRV; 20 mA = sensor above URV; 3.6 mA or 21 mA = NAMUR NE 43 fault.
- **Always include units** in the final numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less answers.
- **Mention at least one application** (hazardous-area field transmitter, heated-probe flow meter, etc.) in 14-mark answers to demonstrate industry context.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of RTD-Based Temperature Transmitter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of RTD-Based Temperature Transmitter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** is a passive temperature-sensing element whose terminal resistance varies predictably and reproducibly with temperature, exploiting the positive temperature coefficient (PTC) of pure metals (typically platinum, nickel, or copper). An **RTD-based temperature transmitter** is an electronic instrumentation system that converts the low-level resistance change of an RTD into a standardized, noise-immune output signal (typically a **4–20 mA current loop** proportional to the measured temperature).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> The design of an RTD transmitter encompasses: (a) the sensing element, (b) lead-wire compensation, (c) excitation & bridge circuit, (d) signal conditioning (amplification & linearization), and (e) current-loop output stage. The platinum **PT100** (R₀ = **100 Ω at 0 °C**) is the industrial benchmark per **IEC 60751**.

The mathematical model governing RTD behavior is the **Callendar–Van Dusen equation**:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100)\, T^3 \right]$$

where $T$ is temperature in °C and $R_0$ is the nominal resistance at 0 °C.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Thermometer That Whispers in Ohms"

Imagine a long, thin metal ruler that subtly changes its length with heat. The hotter it gets, the longer (more resistive) it becomes, and by simply *measuring how hard it pushes back* against a tiny current, you know the exact temperature. The RTD is this ruler — but for it to "speak" to a control room miles away over a noisy industrial cable, we must translate its tiny Ohmic whisper into a robust, standardized "shout" — a **4–20 mA current loop** that is immune to cable resistance, EMI, and ground loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Beginners**
> - **Sensor** (RTD) = Converts Temperature → Resistance
> - **Transmitter** (Electronics) = Converts Resistance → 4–20 mA current
> - **Receiver** (PLC/DCS) = Converts 4–20 mA → Digital °C reading

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Nominal Resistance of PT100 | $R_0$ | **100.00** | Ω |
| Nominal Resistance of PT1000 | $R_0$ | **1000.00** | Ω |
| Mean Temperature Coefficient (Platinum) | $\alpha$ | **0.00385** | °C⁻¹ |
| Quadratic Coefficient | $\beta$ | **−5.775 × 10⁻⁷** | °C⁻² |
| Cubic Coefficient (for T < 0) | $\gamma$ | **−4.183 × 10⁻¹²** | °C⁻⁴ |
| Standard Loop Supply | $V_{sup}$ | **24 V DC** (typ.) | V |
| Output Current Range | $I_{out}$ | **4 to 20** | mA |
| Excitation Voltage (Bridge) | $V_{ex}$ | **2.5 to 5** | V DC |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Decomposition

The RTD transmitter is built as a five-stage chain. Each stage performs a specific transformation:

- **Stage 1 — Sensing Element (PT100):**
  The platinum wire's resistance increases nearly linearly with temperature. The fundamental "Why": as temperature rises, lattice vibrations increase, scattering conduction electrons more aggressively (Matthiessen's rule), thus increasing resistivity.

- **Stage 2 — Lead-Wire Compensation (3-Wire / 4-Wire):**
  Copper lead wires connecting the RTD to the bridge add their own series resistance (~2.5 Ω per 100 m for AWG 18). Without compensation, this introduces errors of several °C. The **3-wire Kelvin connection** uses a third lead to cancel this parasitic resistance.

- **Stage 3 — Excitation & Wheatstone Bridge:**
  A balanced d.c. excitation voltage drives a Wheatstone bridge. The RTD is one arm. Temperature imbalance produces a small differential voltage $V_o$ proportional to $\Delta T$.

- **Stage 4 — Instrumentation Amplifier & Linearization:**
  The millivolt-level bridge output is amplified to a 0–5 V or 0–10 V span. A **linearization network** (polynomial, piecewise-linear, or ASIC-based) corrects the residual quadratic/cubic non-linearity of the platinum curve.

- **Stage 5 — Voltage-to-Current Converter (V/I):**
  The conditioned voltage is converted into a current regulated to **4 mA at lower range value (LRV)** and **20 mA at upper range value (URV)**, which is then transmitted over a 2-wire loop.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 4 mA and not 0 mA?**
> A 4 mA "live zero" enables **open-circuit / loop-fault detection**: any current below 3.6 mA is interpreted as a broken wire or sensor failure. This is the foundation of **NAMUR NE 43** fault signaling.

## 2.2 The Callendar–Van Dusen Equation — Piecewise Definition

For **T ≥ 0 °C** (simplified quadratic form):

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right)$$

For **T < 0 °C** (full quartic form):

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100)\, T^3 \right]$$

## 2.3 Wheatstone Bridge Output Equation

For a bridge with arms $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ excited by $V_{ex}$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

For a *quarter-bridge* with $R_1 = R_2 = R$ (fixed) and $R_4$ = RTD, $R_3$ = reference:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4R} \cdot \Delta R \quad \text{(for small } \Delta R\text{)}$$

## 2.4 Current-Loop Transfer Function

The standardized 4–20 mA output is governed by:

$$I_{out} = 4\,\text{mA} + 16\,\text{mA} \cdot \frac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}}$$

The inverse (used by the receiver):

$$T = T_{min} + (T_{max} - T_{min}) \cdot \frac{I_{out} - 4}{16}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the formula for $R_T$ and the current-loop transfer function. They appear in nearly every KTU question on RTD design.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | First-order linear approximation | Ω |
| 2 | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$ | Quadratic (T ≥ 0 °C) — full | Ω |
| 3 | $\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Small-signal sensitivity | Ω |
| 4 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R}\Delta R$ | Quarter-bridge output (linearized) | V |
| 5 | $V_{span} = V_o(T_{max}) - V_o(T_{min})$ | Required bridge voltage span | V |
| 6 | $A_v = \dfrac{V_{span,\,des}}{V_{span,\,brg}}$ | Instrumentation amplifier gain | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}}$ | Current loop transfer (mA) | mA |
| 8 | $T = T_{min} + (T_{max} - T_{min}) \cdot \dfrac{I_{out} - 4}{16}$ | Inverse transfer (receiver side) | °C |
| 9 | $\text{Error}_{lead} = \dfrac{2 r_{lead}}{R_0 \alpha}$ | Lead-resistance error (2-wire) | °C |
| 10 | $P_{RTD} = \dfrac{V_{ex}^2}{4R_{RTD}}$ | Self-heating power budget | W |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

RTD transmitters are the **workhorse of process industries** — petrochemical reactors, pharmaceutical clean rooms, food and dairy pasteurization, power-plant steam headers, and HVAC systems. They outperform thermocouples in **stability (typically ±0.1 °C drift/year)**, **repeatability**, and **accuracy** over the range **−200 °C to +850 °C**. The 4–20 mA current loop allows signal transmission over **hundreds of meters** of unshielded twisted pair without degradation, forming the backbone of legacy **4–20 mA HART** and modern **fieldbus** installations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Resistance Variation of a Platinum RTD

Starting from the fundamental temperature coefficient of resistance for a pure metal, we define the *mean* temperature coefficient $\alpha$ over the range 0 °C → 100 °C:

$$\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0}$$

For the standard **PT100**, $R_{100} = 138.50\ \Omega$ and $R_0 = 100.00\ \Omega$, which yields:

$$\alpha = \frac{138.50 - 100.00}{100 \times 100.00} = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$$

The exact Callendar–Van Dusen relation accounts for the slight bow in the $R$–$T$ curve:

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right),\quad T \geq 0\,°C$$

Substituting the IEC 60751 values $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$ and $\beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$:

$$R_T = 100 \left( 1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} T - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} T^2 \right)$$

**Verification at T = 100 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{100} &= 100 \left( 1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 100^2 \right) \\
&= 100 \left( 1 + 0.385 - 0.005775 \right) \\
&= 100 \times 1.379225 \\
&= 137.9225\ \Omega \quad (\text{very close to nominal } 138.50\ \Omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

The small discrepancy (~0.4 %) arises because the strict IEC formulation uses $\alpha = 0.00385055$ and the relation is actually defined against $R_0 = 100.00$ at 0 °C and $R_{100} = 138.51$ — but for KTU exam purposes the simplified quadratic above is the accepted model.

## 3.2 Derivation — Wheatstone Bridge Output for a Quarter-Bridge RTD Configuration

Consider a bridge with fixed arms $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and the lower arms $R_3 = R$ (reference) and $R_4 = R + \Delta R$ (RTD). The two mid-point voltages are:

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

$$V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

The differential output is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_B - V_D = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} - V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} \\
&= V_{ex} \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} \right] \\
&= V_{ex} \cdot \frac{(2R + \Delta R) - 2(R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \\
&= V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2R + \Delta R - 2R - 2\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \\
&= V_{ex} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the magnitude (sign depends on connection polarity):

$$|V_o| = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4R} \cdot \Delta R \quad \text{(for } \Delta R \ll R\text{)}$$

This *small-signal approximation* is valid because for a PT100 over 0–500 °C, $\Delta R_{max} \approx 158\ \Omega$ while $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, giving a non-linearity of about **6–8 %**. This is exactly the residual that the **linearization stage** must correct.

## 3.3 Worked Design Example — 0–500 °C PT100 Transmitter with 4–20 mA Output

**Given:**
- Sensor: PT100 ($\alpha = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$)
- Range: $T_{min} = 0\,°C$, $T_{max} = 500\,°C$
- Output: 4–20 mA into 250 Ω load
- Excitation: $V_{ex} = 5.0\ \text{V DC}$

**Step 1 — Calculate RTD resistance at range limits:**

At 0 °C: $R_{T,min} = 100.00\ \Omega$

At 500 °C:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{500} &= 100 \left( 1 + 0.00385 \times 500 - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 500^2 \right) \\
&= 100 \left( 1 + 1.925 - 0.14438 \right) \\
&= 100 \times 2.78062 \\
&= 278.06\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\Delta R = 178.06\ \Omega$ and the mean sensitivity is $\Delta R / \Delta T = 0.356\ \Omega/°C$.

**Step 2 — Bridge output span (small-signal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o(T_{min}) &\approx 0\ \text{V} \\
V_o(T_{max}) &\approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4R_0} \cdot \Delta R = \frac{5.0}{4 \times 100} \times 178.06 \\
&= 0.0125 \times 178.06 \\
&= 2.2258\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required amplifier gain (assuming 0–5 V output before V/I):**

$$A_v = \frac{V_{span,\,des}}{V_{span,\,brg}} = \frac{5.0}{2.2258} \approx 2.246$$

Use a standard INA128 instrumentation amplifier with $R_G = \dfrac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{A_v - 1} = \dfrac{49400}{1.246} \approx 39.6\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 4 — Linearization correction:**

Without correction, the curve has a built-in bow of about **−6 % at 250 °C**. A typical linearization IC such as the **Burr-Brown XTR105** or **Analog Devices AD598** applies a 4th-order polynomial inverse. The maximum residual error after correction is < **±0.1 °C**.

**Step 5 — V/I conversion:**

The conditioned 0–5 V is fed to a Howland current pump. The output current becomes:

$$I_{out} = 4\,\text{mA} + 16\,\text{mA} \cdot \frac{V_{cond}}{5\,\text{V}}$$

For a 250 Ω load, the receiver voltage drop is $20\ \text{mA} \times 250\ \Omega = 5\ \text{V}$, well within the 24 V loop supply budget (with 19 V headroom for cable resistance and protection).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Summary

| Specification | Value | KTU Acceptance Criterion |
|--------------|-------|--------------------------|
| Accuracy class | IEC 60751 Class A | ±(0.15 + 0.002 \vert T \vert) °C |
| Linearity (after correction) | ±0.05 % of span | ✓ |
| Lead-wire error (3-wire, equal leads) | < 0.05 °C | ✓ |
| Self-heating error | 0.05 °C/mW | Depends on excitation |
| Loop compliance voltage | 24 V − 5 V = 19 V | Up to 760 Ω at 20 mA |
| Output resolution | 16-bit (with HART) | > 65,000 steps |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Block Diagram — RTD Temperature Transmitter (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["PROCESS TEMPERATURE T"] --> node2["RTD SENSOR PT100"]
    node2 --> node3["3 WIRE LEAD COMPENSATION"]
    node3 --> node4["WHEATSTONE BRIDGE QUARTER CONFIG"]
    node4 --> node5["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN ADJUSTABLE"]
    node5 --> node6["LINEARIZATION STAGE POLYNOMIAL 4TH ORDER"]
    node6 --> node7["V TO I CONVERTER HOWLAND PUMP"]
    node7 --> node8["4 TO 20 MA CURRENT LOOP"]
    node8 --> node9["RECEIVER PLC DCS HMI"]
    node8 --> node10["24 V DC LOOP SUPPLY"]
    node4 --- node11["CONSTANT VEX 5V REFERENCE"]
    node11 --> node4
    node5 --- node12["ZERO AND SPAN ADJUST TRIM POTS"]
    node12 --> node5
```

## 4.2 Functional Sub-Block — 3-Wire RTD Bridge Configuration

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph bridgeQuarter["WHEATSTONE QUARTER BRIDGE WITH 3 WIRE RTD"]
        vex["VEX DC EXCITATION"] --> nodeR1top
        nodeR1top --> nodeR1["R1 EQUAL TO R0"]
        nodeR1 --> nodeB["NODE B BRIDGE OUTPUT POSITIVE"]
        nodeR1 --> leadA["LEAD r EQUAL TO 2.5 OHM"]
        leadA --> rtdElement["PT100 RTD ACTIVE ELEMENT"]
        rtdElement --> leadC["LEAD r COMPENSATION"]
        leadC --> nodeD["NODE D BRIDGE OUTPUT NEGATIVE"]
        nodeR2["R2 EQUAL TO R0"] --> nodeB
        vex --> nodeR2
        nodeR3["R3 REFERENCE EQUAL TO R0"] --> nodeD
        vex --> nodeR3
    end
    nodeB --> diffAmp["DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT VO MILLIVOLTS"]
    nodeD --> diffAmp
```

## 4.3 Signal-Conditioning & Linearization Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeStart["BRIDGE OUTPUT VO 0 TO 2.23V"] --> nodePre["BUFFER STAGE HIGH IMPEDANCE"]
    nodePre --> nodeInamp["INSTRUMENTATION AMP GAIN 2.25"]
    nodeInamp --> nodeLUT["LOOKUP TABLE OR POLY FITTER"]
    nodeLUT --> nodeCorr["LINEARIZED VOLTAGE 0 TO 5V"]
    nodeCorr --> nodeVi["V TO I TRANSCONDUCTANCE STAGE"]
    nodeVi --> nodeLoop["4 TO 20 MA LOOP OUTPUT"]
    nodeLoop --> nodeMon["FAULT MONITOR LESS THAN 3.6 MA"]
    nodeMon --> nodeAlarm["NAMUR NE43 ALARM"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Each rectangular block is a functional sub-system. The arrows indicate the *direction of signal flow*. The two parallel paths in diagram 4.1 between the bridge and the V/I stage represent the **active signal path** (top) versus the **auxiliary supplies and calibration ports** (bottom). In a KTU 14-mark question, always annotate the **purpose of each block** — examiners specifically award marks for the *role* of the linearization stage.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define an RTD. With the help of the Callendar–Van Dusen equation, explain the principle of operation of a PT100 sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** is a passive, resistive temperature-sensing element whose terminal resistance varies predictably with temperature according to:

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right),\quad T \geq 0\,°C$$

For a **PT100**, $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C and the mean temperature coefficient $\alpha = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$ (IEC 60751). The **principle of operation** relies on the increase in resistivity of pure platinum as temperature rises: lattice vibrations scatter conduction electrons more strongly, raising the resistance in a near-linear manner. The quadratic term $\beta T^2$ introduces a small downward bow in the $R$–$T$ curve, which must be compensated in the transmitter's linearization stage.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare 2-wire, 3-wire, and 4-wire RTD connection schemes in terms of lead-resistance error and typical industrial use.**

**Model Answer:**

| Configuration | Lead Compensation | Error Source | Typical Use |
|--------------|-------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **2-wire** | None | Both leads add directly to $R_T$; ~2.5 Ω/100 m each | Short leads, low accuracy |
| **3-wire** | One lead in adjacent arm cancels the other | Bridge unbalance from lead mismatch | **Most common industrial** |
| **4-wire** | Kelvin sensing — no current in voltage leads | Negligible | Laboratory / high accuracy |

The lead-resistance error in a 2-wire setup is $E = \dfrac{2r_{lead}}{R_0\,\alpha}$ °C, which for a 100 m run equals **~13 °C** — totally unacceptable. A 3-wire connection places the lead resistance in *both* arms of the bridge, achieving automatic cancellation if the leads are equal. A 4-wire Kelvin connection forces a constant current through the RTD and senses voltage with high-impedance leads, eliminating lead error entirely.

---

## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the design of an RTD-based temperature transmitter used in industrial process control.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *For a PT100 RTD used in a 0–300 °C measurement range with a 5 V bridge excitation, calculate (i) the resistance at 100 °C and 300 °C, and (ii) the instrumentation amplifier gain required to obtain a 0–5 V output span.*

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation:**

The transmitter consists of **five cascaded stages**:

1. **Sensor Stage (PT100):** Converts process temperature to resistance, following $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$.
2. **Lead-Wire Compensation:** A 3-wire Kelvin connection routes the RTD through two arms of the bridge to cancel copper-lead resistance.
3. **Wheatstone Bridge with 5 V excitation:** Converts the resistance change into a differential millivolt signal.
4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (e.g., AD620 / INA128):** Amplifies the differential bridge output to a 0–5 V span. The gain is set by a single external resistor $R_G$.
5. **Linearization + V/I Converter:** Corrects the platinum non-linearity and converts the conditioned voltage to a **4–20 mA** current loop. An **XTR105** or **AD693** IC performs both functions in industrial designs.
6. **Loop Supply and Receiver:** A 24 V DC supply powers the loop, with the receiver (PLC/DCS) typically presenting a 250 Ω burden resistor that converts 4–20 mA back to 1–5 V.

**Part (b) — Numerical Design:**

**(i) RTD Resistance Calculation:**

At 100 °C:
$$R_{100} = 100 \left( 1 + 0.00385 \times 100 - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 100^2 \right)$$
$$R_{100} = 100 \times (1 + 0.385 - 0.005775) = 137.92\ \Omega$$

At 300 °C:
$$R_{300} = 100 \left( 1 + 0.00385 \times 300 - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 300^2 \right)$$
$$R_{300} = 100 \times (1 + 1.155 - 0.05198) = 210.30\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Bridge Output at Range Limits:**

At 0 °C: $R_T = 100\ \Omega$, $V_o \approx 0\ \text{V}$

At 300 °C: $\Delta R = 210.30 - 100 = 110.30\ \Omega$

$$V_o(300\,°C) = \frac{V_{ex}}{4R_0} \cdot \Delta R = \frac{5.0}{4 \times 100} \times 110.30 = 1.379\ \text{V}$$

**Required Amplifier Gain:**

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out,\,span}}{V_{brg,\,span}} = \frac{5.0 - 0}{1.379 - 0} = 3.626$$

The gain-setting resistor for an AD620 is:

$$R_G = \frac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{A_v - 1} = \frac{49400}{2.626} = 18.81\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Select a standard **18.7 kΩ, 0.1 %** resistor. **Final gain = 3.64**, giving a 0–5.04 V output — a 1 % over-range is acceptable for a NAMUR-compliant transmitter.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the output voltage equation of a Wheatstone bridge when a 3-wire RTD is connected in one arm. Show how the lead-resistance error is cancelled.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain with a circuit diagram the working of a 4–20 mA current-loop transmitter. The transmitter is calibrated for 50 °C to 250 °C. Calculate the current output when the measured temperature is 175 °C.*

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Derivation of 3-Wire Bridge Output:**

Let the three leads have resistance $r_1, r_2, r_3$. In the 3-wire configuration:
- Lead 1 ($r_1$) is in series with the RTD $R_T$ in the lower-left arm.
- Lead 2 ($r_2$) is in series with reference resistor $R_3$ in the lower-right arm.
- Lead 3 ($r_3$) carries the excitation to the top of the RTD.

The bridge output equation is:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3 + r_2}{R_T + r_1 + r_3 + R_3 + r_2} \right)$$

By symmetry, if $r_1 = r_2 = r_3 = r$ and $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{ex} \left( \frac{R}{2R} - \frac{R + r}{R_T + 3r + R + r} \right) \\
&= V_{ex} \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{R + r}{2R + R_T + 4r} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For the *balanced* condition ($R_T = R$): $V_o = 0$. Now when $R_T \to R_T + \Delta R$, the lead terms **$4r$** appear in *both* numerator and denominator equally and partially cancel. The residual lead error is proportional to $(r_1 - r_2)$, which is zero for matched leads. **Hence, the 3-wire connection cancels the lead-resistance error to first order.**

**Part (b) — 4–20 mA Loop Working & Calculation:**

The 4–20 mA current loop has a transmitter, a 24 V DC supply, and a receiver (typically a 250 Ω burden resistor). The current is regulated by a Howland current pump whose input is the conditioned RTD voltage. The output current is governed by:

$$I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}}$$

Given $T_{min} = 50\,°C$ and $T_{max} = 250\,°C$ and $T = 175\,°C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{out} &= 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{175 - 50}{250 - 50} \\
&= 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{125}{200} \\
&= 4 + 16 \times 0.625 \\
&= 4 + 10 \\
&= 14\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding receiver voltage across the 250 Ω burden is $14\ \text{mA} \times 250\ \Omega = 3.5\ \text{V}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Error in Bridge Equation:** Many students write $V_o = \frac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$ with the *wrong* numerator/denominator pairing. Use the standard **voltage-divider form** consistently: $V_o = V_B - V_D$.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\beta T^2$ Term:** Omitting the quadratic correction gives $R_{100} = 138.50\ \Omega$ instead of $137.92\ \Omega$. The KTU key explicitly checks whether you used the *full* Callendar–Van Dusen equation.
> 3. **Confusing 2-wire with 3-wire:** Writing the lead-resistance error formula $E = 2r/(R_0\alpha)$ for a 3-wire system will cost 2 marks.
> 4. **Units Mistake in Current Loop:** Forgetting that the 16 mA span comes from $20 - 4 = 16$ is a recurring KTU mistake.
> 5. **Gain-Setting Resistor:** Failing to use $R_G = \dfrac{49.4\,k\Omega}{A_v - 1}$ for the AD620/INA128 IC will lose 1–2 marks in numerical sub-parts.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **RTD** = Resistance Temperature Detector; PT100 is the platinum benchmark (R₀ = **100 Ω at 0 °C**).
- 🔹 **Callendar–Van Dusen:** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$ for T ≥ 0 °C; add $\gamma(T-100)T^3$ for T < 0 °C.
- 🔹 **α = 0.00385 °C⁻¹** (IEC 60751) and **β = −5.775 × 10⁻⁷ °C⁻²** are the KTU-mandated coefficients.
- 🔹 **3-wire RTD** is the industrial standard — it cancels lead resistance to first order.
- 🔹 **4-wire Kelvin** = highest accuracy; used in calibration labs.
- 🔹 **Wheatstone bridge** small-signal output: $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R} \Delta R$.
- 🔹 **Instrumentation amplifier** sets span and provides CMRR > 100 dB (INA128, AD620).
- 🔹 **Linearization** is mandatory — platinum has ~6–8 % bow over 0–500 °C; use polynomial or ASIC.
- 🔹 **4–20 mA loop:** $I = 4 + 16 \cdot (T - T_{min})/(T_{max} - T_{min})$.
- 🔹 **NAMUR NE 43** fault currents: < 3.6 mA or > 21 mA = sensor/loop fault.
- 🔹 **Self-heating** error: keep $P_{RTD} < 1\ \text{mW}$ for Class A accuracy.
- 🔹 **Loop compliance** = 24 V − receiver drop − cable drop; for 250 Ω + 100 m cable, still has > 19 V headroom.
- 🔹 **KTU favourite** sub-topics: (i) derive $V_o$ for 3-wire bridge, (ii) numerical gain calculation, (iii) current loop transfer at intermediate temperature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermocouple-Based Temperature Transmitter and Smart Transmitters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermocouple-Based Temperature Transmitter and Smart Transmitters # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Thermocouple-Based Temperature Transmitter **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** A Thermocouple-Based Temperature Transmitter is an electronic field instrument that accepts the low-level millivolt (mV) signal generated by a thermocouple sensor (based on the **Seebeck Effect**), conditions it through amplification, **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)**, linearization, and current conversion, and transmits a standardized industrial output (typically **4–20 mA DC**) proportional to the measured temperature to a remote controller, recorder, or DCS. > [!NOTE] > **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The transmitter must convert a non-linear mV signal (from a thermocouple) into a standardized, linear 4–20 mA current loop signal that is immune to lead resistance and noise. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Translator at a Multilingual Conference" Imagine a foreign delegate (the **thermocouple**) who can only whisper (outputting a tiny mV signal) in a strange dialect (non-linear, cold-junction dependent). The **Temperature Transmitter** acts like a professional translator: 1. It **listens carefully** (amplifies the weak mV). 2. It **understands the dialect** (linearizes the non-linear Seebeck curve). 3. It **adjusts for the room temperature** (Cold Junction Compensation — because the delegate's words change meaning based on where they are standing). 4. It **announces clearly and loudly** in a universal language (4–20 mA or digital HART) that every controller in the factory can understand. Without this translator, the rest of the plant cannot interpret the thermocouple's message. --- ## 1.2 Smart Transmitter **Formal Definition:** A **Smart Transmitter** is a microprocessor-based intelligent field instrument that combines a primary sensor, an analog signal conditioner, an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, a microprocessor, a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)**, and a digital communication modem (typically **HART** or **Foundation Fieldbus**). It offers features such as remote calibration, remote ranging, self-diagnostics, higher accuracy, and bidirectional digital communication superimposed on the analog 4–20 mA loop. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** > - **Analog Transmitter** → "Dumb": Fixed range at the bench, one-way 4–20 mA output. > - **Smart Transmitter** → "Intelligent": Range can be changed remotely via HART, two-way digital communication, self-diagnostics, ±0.05% accuracy. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smartphone vs. The Landline Phone" An **analog transmitter** is like an old **landline phone**: it can only do one job (transmit a 4–20 mA signal), cannot be reconfigured remotely, and cannot tell you if it's broken. A **smart transmitter** is like a **smartphone**: it can still make the basic "call" (4–20 mA), but it also has apps (HART commands), can be reprogrammed over the air (remote calibration), can self-diagnose its health (diagnostic flags), and can communicate digitally (data transmission) — all over the **same two wires** (the current loop). ### Key Physical Constants & Standards (KTU Board Standard) | Parameter | Standard Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Analog Output Standard | **4–20 mA DC** | ANSI/ISA-50.00.01 / IEC 60381-1 | | HART Frequency | **1200 Hz / 2200 Hz** | FSK on top of 4–20 mA | | HART Average Current | **± 0.5 mA (peak-to-peak)** | Does not disturb 4–20 mA | | HART Data Rate | **1200 bits/sec** | Bell 202 standard | | Cold Junction Reference | **0 °C (273.15 K)** | NIST ITS-90 thermocouple tables | | Thermocouple Seebeck Coefficient | typically **40–60 µV/°C** | Varies with T/C type and temperature | > [!NOTE] > The **HART protocol** is the **Highway Addressable Remote Transducer** protocol — an open-standard master-slave communication protocol widely used in process industries. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermocouple Fundamentals — The Seebeck Effect When two dissimilar metals (A and B) are joined at a **measurement (hot) junction** at temperature **T₁**, and the other two ends are kept at a **reference (cold) junction** at temperature **T₂**, an EMF **E** is generated. $$E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] \, dT$$ where **S(T)** is the **Seebeck Coefficient** (µV/°C) of each material. ### The Three Laws of Thermocouples (KTU High-Yield) 1. **Law of Homogeneous Materials:** A thermocouple circuit generates EMF only if the two metals are *different* and the two junctions are at *different* temperatures. The EMF does not depend on the temperature distribution along the wires. 2. **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal (C) into the circuit produces no error, **provided both junctions of C are at the same temperature**. 3. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** The EMF generated with junctions at T₁ and T₃ equals the sum of EMFs at (T₁, T₂) and (T₂, T₃). > $$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$$ ## 2.2 Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) In practice, the cold junction is at the **terminal block of the transmitter** (not at 0 °C). The transmitter measures the terminal temperature T₂ using an internal sensor (typically a **silicon diode, RTD, or semiconductor IC**) and electronically adds the equivalent EMF to the thermocouple signal. $$E_{\text{measured}}(T_1, T_{\text{ref}}) = E_{\text{actual}}(T_1, T_2) + E_{\text{compensated}}(T_2, T_{\text{ref}})$$ where **T_ref = 0 °C** (the virtual ice-bath reference). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Without CJC, the transmitter would interpret the cold-junction temperature as part of the process temperature — causing errors of tens of degrees!** ## 2.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Thermocouple Transmitter The signal flow is: **Thermocouple → Input Protection/Filtering → Amplifier → CJC Circuit → Linearizer → V-to-I Converter → 4–20 mA Output** ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Input Protection:** Surge suppressors (TVS diodes), EMI/RFI filters, and isolation. 2. **Pre-Amplification:** Instrumentation amplifier with very high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR ≥ 100 dB) and high input impedance (>10 MΩ) to avoid loading the high-impedance thermocouple. 3. **Cold Junction Compensation:** A precision temperature sensor at the terminal block provides a correction voltage. 4. **Linearization:** The Seebeck EMF is highly non-linear. Either an **analog piecewise linearizer** (resistor-diode network) or a **digital polynomial fit** in the microprocessor is used. 5. **Voltage-to-Current Conversion:** A precision current loop driver produces **4 mA at LRV** and **20 mA at URV**. ## 2.4 Smart Transmitter Architecture (Deep Dive) A smart transmitter adds the following on top of the analog front-end: - **Multiplexer (MUX)** → Selects between the primary sensor and the CJC/RTD sensor. - **16/24-bit Sigma-Delta ADC** → Digitizes the conditioned signal. - **Microprocessor (µP)** → Performs linearization, range scaling, engineering unit conversion, and diagnostics. - **Non-Volatile Memory (EEPROM/Flash)** → Stores calibration constants, tag, range, and configuration. - **DAC + Output Stage** → Converts the digital value back to 4–20 mA. - **HART Modem** → Modulates/demodulates FSK (1200/2200 Hz) signals on the loop. - **Display (LCD)** → Local indication of PV (Primary Variable) in engineering units. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Parameter | Formula | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Seebeck EMF | $E = \int_{T_{2}}^{T_{1}} \Delta S(T)\, dT$ | mV | Fundamental T/C output | | 2 | CJC Correction | $E_{\text{comp}} = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, 0\,°C)$ | mV | Adding reference EMF | | 3 | 4–20 mA Conversion | $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{PV}} - T_{\text{LRV}}}{T_{\text{URV}} - T_{\text{LRV}}}$ | mA | Linear scaling | | 4 | PV in % of Range | $\%\,\text{PV} = \dfrac{I_{\text{out}} - 4}{16} \times 100$ | % | Controller input | | 5 | HART FSK Frequencies | $f_1 = 1200\,\text{Hz},\; f_2 = 2200\,\text{Hz}$ | Hz | Digital communication | | 6 | HART Peak Current | $\Delta I = \pm 0.5\,\text{mA}$ (p-p) | mA | Non-disruptive overlay | | 7 | Loop Voltage Compliance | $V_{\text{loop}} = V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{drop}} \ge 12\,\text{V}$ | V | Powering 2-wire device | | 8 | Rangeability (Turndown) | $\text{TD} = \dfrac{T_{\text{URV}}}{T_{\text{LRV}}}$ (ratio) | – | Smart transmitter flexibility | | 9 | NIST Polynomial (Type K) | $E = \sum_{i=0}^{n} c_i (T)^i$ | mV | Linearization | | 10 | Output Current vs. Load | $R_{\text{load,max}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} - 12}{0.020}$ | Ω | Loop design | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** In refineries (e.g., BPCL Kochi, CPCL Chennai), smart temperature transmitters monitor furnace tube skin temperatures, reactor beds, and distillation column trays. HART allows engineers in the control room (10 km away via multidrop) to re-range, re-tag, and diagnose a transmitter without climbing a tower. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: 4–20 mA Current from Temperature Input **Given:** A Type K thermocouple is used to measure a process temperature range of **T_LRV = 200 °C to T_URV = 800 °C**. **Required:** Derive the expression for the loop current as a function of measured temperature, and compute the current for **T = 500 °C**. ### Step 1: Define the Linear Transfer Function By the **KTU/IEEE standard for analog process signals (ISA 50.00.01)**, the output current is linear with respect to the calibrated range: $$ I_{\text{out}}(T) = 4\,\text{mA} + \left(20\,\text{mA} - 4\,\text{mA}\right) \cdot \frac{T - T_{\text{LRV}}}{T_{\text{URV}} - T_{\text{LRV}}} $$ ### Step 2: Simplify the Constant $$ I_{\text{out}}(T) = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{T - T_{\text{LRV}}}{T_{\text{URV}} - T_{\text{LRV}}} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute the Given Values Substitute **T_LRV = 200 °C** and **T_URV = 800 °C**: $$ I_{\text{out}}(T) = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{T - 200}{800 - 200} $$ $$ I_{\text{out}}(T) = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{T - 200}{600} $$ ### Step 4: Compute for T = 500 °C $$ I_{\text{out}}(500) = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{500 - 200}{600} $$ $$ I_{\text{out}}(500) = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{300}{600} $$ $$ I_{\text{out}}(500) = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.5 = 4 + 8 = 12\,\text{mA} $$ ### Step 5: Result and Physical Interpretation The transmitter outputs **12 mA** for a process temperature of **500 °C** — exactly at the midpoint of the range. This confirms the **50% mark** corresponds to **500 °C**, which is mathematically consistent with a linear transfer function. > **Conclusion:** The standard 4–20 mA signal is engineered so that **4 mA = 0% of range** and **20 mA = 100% of range**, providing a "live-zero" that detects open loops (0 mA = fault). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) Correction Voltage **Given:** A Type J thermocouple is connected to a transmitter. The hot junction is at **T₁ = 600 °C**. The terminal block (cold junction) is at **T₂ = 25 °C**. The thermocouple's sensitivity at T₂ is approximately **S = 55 µV/°C**. **Required:** Calculate the CJC compensation voltage that must be added to the thermocouple's measured EMF. ### Step 1: Physical Principle The thermocouple generates an EMF based on the *difference* between the hot and cold junctions. To convert the measurement to a **0 °C reference**, we must add the EMF that the thermocouple *would* have produced if the cold junction were at 0 °C. ### Step 2: The CJC Compensation Equation $$ E_{\text{comp}} = E_{T_2 \to 0\,°C} = \int_{0}^{T_2} \Delta S(T)\, dT $$ For a small range around room temperature, we can approximate the EMF linearly using the Seebeck coefficient: $$ E_{\text{comp}} \approx S \cdot T_2 $$ ### Step 3: Substitute the Values $$ E_{\text{comp}} \approx 55\,\mu\text{V}/°C \times 25\,°C $$ $$ E_{\text{comp}} \approx 1375\,\mu\text{V} = 1.375\,\text{mV} $$ ### Step 4: Total Corrected EMF (Reference to 0 °C) The corrected EMF referenced to 0 °C is: $$ E_{\text{corrected}} = E_{\text{measured}}(T_1, T_2) + E_{\text{comp}}(T_2, 0\,°C) $$ This value is then linearized and converted to 4–20 mA. > **Conclusion:** If CJC were *not* applied, the transmitter would under-read by ~1.375 mV, corresponding to a temperature error of **~25 °C** — catastrophic for a 600 °C measurement! --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Smart Transmitter vs. Analog Transmitter | Feature | Analog Transmitter | Smart Transmitter | |---|---|---| | **Accuracy** | ±0.5% to ±0.25% of span | ±0.05% of span (or better) | | **Range Adjustment** | Manual (potentiometers on bench) | Remote (HART handheld/AMS) | | **Communication** | One-way 4–20 mA only | 4–20 mA + HART (bidirectional) | | **Diagnostics** | None | Continuous self-diagnostics (sensor health, loop integrity) | | **Turndown Ratio** | 3:1 typical | 100:1 or 200:1 | | **Sensor Type Switch** | Hardware change required | Software-configurable (RTD ↔ T/C) | | **Memory** | None | Non-volatile (calibration, tag, range) | | **Display** | Optional analog meter | Local digital LCD with engineering units | | **Cost (approx.)** | ₹ 5,000–15,000 | ₹ 25,000–1,00,000 | | **Power Supply** | 2-wire loop-powered | 2-wire loop-powered (HART) or 4-wire | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: HART Signal Average Value (KTU High-Yield) **Given:** HART uses FSK with two frequencies **f₁ = 1200 Hz** (representing '1') and **f₂ = 2200 Hz** (representing '0'), with a peak-to-peak amplitude of **0.5 mA** superimposed on the 4–20 mA DC. **Required:** Show that the DC average current due to HART signaling is approximately zero, so it does not affect the analog measurement. ### Step 1: Express the HART Current Component The HART current is a sinusoidal AC waveform: $$ i_{\text{HART}}(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t), \quad A = 0.25\,\text{mA (peak)} $$ ### Step 2: Time Average Over One Period $$ \langle i_{\text{HART}} \rangle = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} A \sin(2\pi f t)\, dt $$ ### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral For any integer number of full periods, the integral of a pure sine wave is zero: $$ \langle i_{\text{HART}} \rangle = 0\,\text{mA} $$ ### Step 4: Total Loop Current $$ I_{\text{total}}(t) = I_{\text{DC}} + i_{\text{HART}}(t) = I_{\text{DC}} + A \sin(2\pi f t) $$ Since the analog input card has a low-pass filter (bandwidth << 1200 Hz), it sees only **I_DC**. The HART modem, however, bandpass-filters around 1200/2200 Hz to extract the digital data. > **Conclusion:** HART is **non-disruptive** — it adds digital communication without disturbing the analog signal. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Thermocouple-Based Analog Temperature Transmitter ```mermaid flowchart LR TC[Thermocouple Sensor
Hot Junction at T1] -->|mV signal| IP[Input Protection
TVS Diodes, EMI Filter] IP --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier
CMRR greater than 100 dB] IA --> CJC[Cold Junction Compensation
Reference Sensor at T2] CJC --> LIN[Linearization Network
Piecewise Analog] LIN --> VIC[Voltage to Current Converter
V to I stage] VIC -->|4 to 20 mA| LOOP[Two Wire Current Loop] LOOP --> CTRL[DCS / PLC / Recorder] PS[24 V DC Power Supply] --> LOOP TJ[Cold Junction at Terminal Block
T2 measured internally] -.-> CJC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Signal flow is **left → right**. The dashed line from **TJ** to **CJC** indicates the *feedback* of the cold-junction temperature to the compensation block. The loop is **2-wire powered** — the same wires carry power and signal. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Smart Temperature Transmitter (HART-enabled) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSING["SENSOR INPUT STAGE"] TC[Thermocouple mV Input] RTD[RTD / Ohm Input] CJ[Cold Junction Sensor
Internal RTD or Silicon Diode] end subgraph FRONTEND["ANALOG FRONT END"] MUX[Analog Multiplexer] IA[Instrumentation Amplifier] FILT[Anti Aliasing Filter] end subgraph DIGITAL["DIGITAL PROCESSING CORE"] ADC[16 to 24 bit Sigma Delta ADC] CPU[MICROPROCESSOR
Linearization, Range, Diagnostics] ROM[Non Volatile EEPROM
Calibration Constants, Tag] DAC[Digital to Analog Converter] end subgraph COMMS["OUTPUT AND COMMUNICATION"] V2I[Voltage to Current Output Driver] HART[HART Modem
FSK 1200 to 2200 Hz] LCD[Local LCD Display
PV in Engineering Units] end LOOP[4 to 20 mA Current Loop to DCS] --> HART HART --> V2I V2I --> DAC DAC --> CPU CPU --> ADC ADC --> FILT FILT --> IA IA --> MUX MUX --> TC MUX --> RTD MUX --> CJ CPU --> ROM CPU --> LCD ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Architectural Difference:** The smart transmitter uses a **closed digital loop** — the microprocessor continuously adjusts the DAC so that the output current matches the calculated PV. This is called **Digital Trim** and is what gives the smart transmitter its superior accuracy and stability. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: HART Communication Protocol Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB HOST[Host System: DCS, PLC, or HART Handheld] -->|Master Command| PHY1[Physical Layer
Bell 202 FSK Modem] PHY1 -->|1200 Hz or 2200 Hz over 4 to 20 mA| WIRE[Two Wire Current Loop] WIRE --> PHY2[Physical Layer
HART Modem in Transmitter] PHY2 --> DL[Data Link Layer
Burst Mode, Slave Address 0 to 15] DL --> APP[Application Layer
Universal, Common Practice, Device Specific Commands] APP --> SENSOR[Process Variable Read
Range Configuration, Diagnostics] ``` ### 4.4 HART Frame Structure (for advanced understanding) ```mermaid flowchart LR PRE[Preamble 5 to 20 bytes FF hex] --> DEL[Delimiter 1 byte] DEL --> ADDR[Address 1 to 5 bytes Long or Short] ADDR => CMD[Command 1 or 2 bytes] CMD => BYTES[Byte Count 1 byte] BYTES => DATA[Data 0 to 253 bytes] DATA => CHK[Checksum 1 byte] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Quick Note:** HART is a **master-slave** protocol. A typical field transmitter is **slave** (address 1–15 in short frame). The host (DCS) is the **master**. Two masters can exist (primary = DCS, secondary = handheld calibrator), managed by token-passing. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Explain the Seebeck Effect and state the three laws of thermocouples.** **Model Answer:** The **Seebeck Effect** is the direct conversion of a temperature difference into an electromotive force (EMF) at the junction of two dissimilar conductors. When two dissimilar metals A and B are joined at one end (hot junction at T₁) and the other ends are kept at a different temperature T₂, an EMF is generated proportional to the temperature difference. The three laws are: 1. **Law of Homogeneous Materials:** A thermocouple circuit cannot generate an EMF if both conductors are of the same material, regardless of the temperature distribution. EMF requires *two different metals* and *a temperature difference* between the two junctions. 2. **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Introducing a third metal into the circuit (e.g., at the measuring instrument terminals) introduces **no error** as long as the two new junctions created with the third metal are at the **same temperature**. This is why copper lead wires can be connected to the thermocouple at the transmitter terminal block. 3. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** If a thermocouple produces EMF₁ between temperatures T₁ and T₂, and EMF₂ between T₂ and T₃, then the EMF between T₁ and T₃ equals EMF₁ + EMF₂. This is the basis for **Cold Junction Compensation**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1.5 for three laws, 0.5 for relevance)* --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a conventional analog transmitter and a smart transmitter (any four points).** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Analog Transmitter | Smart Transmitter | |---|---|---| | 1 | Based on analog signal conditioning (op-amp circuits). | Based on microprocessor + digital signal processing. | | 2 | Communication is one-way (4–20 mA only). | Communication is two-way (4–20 mA + HART/Fieldbus). | | 3 | Range is set manually with potentiometers; fixed at the bench. | Range can be reconfigured remotely via HART commands. | | 4 | Accuracy is limited to ±0.25% to ±0.5% of span. | High accuracy of ±0.05% of span or better. | | 5 | No self-diagnostics; failure is silent. | Continuous self-diagnostics and asset management. | | 6 | Limited turndown ratio (~3:1). | High turndown ratio (100:1 or 200:1). | | 7 | No on-board memory for calibration. | Non-volatile memory stores tag, range, calibration. | | 8 | No local digital display (only optional analog meter). | Local LCD with engineering units (°C, bar, etc.). | *(3 marks: 1 mark per correct valid point, up to 4 points)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU ESE Pattern: Internal Choice — Select ONE of TWO options]** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **thermocouple-based temperature transmitter**. Discuss the role of **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** and **linearization** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Type K thermocouple is used to measure temperatures in the range **300 °C to 900 °C**. The transmitter output is 4–20 mA. Calculate: - (i) The output current when the measured temperature is **600 °C**. - (ii) The output current when the measured temperature is **450 °C**. - (iii) The temperature corresponding to an output current of **13.6 mA**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) A thermocouple produces a low-level mV signal proportional to the difference between the hot and cold junction temperatures. The transmitter conditions this signal into a robust industrial output. **Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** **Working of Each Block:** 1. **Thermocouple Input:** Generates mV based on the Seebeck Effect. Example: Type K gives ~40 µV/°C. 2. **Input Protection & Filtering:** Protects the transmitter from voltage surges (lightning, switchgear), filters out EMI/RFI from adjacent power cables, and provides electrical isolation. 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Amplifies the small (typically <50 mV) thermocouple signal to a usable level (typically 0–5 V). Must have: - Very high input impedance (>10 MΩ) to avoid loading the thermocouple. - Very high CMRR (>100 dB) to reject common-mode noise from the plant. 4. **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC):** The thermocouple's reference is the **terminal block** of the transmitter, not 0 °C. The terminal temperature **T₂** varies with ambient. The CJC block uses an internal temperature sensor (silicon diode or RTD) to measure T₂ and generates a compensation voltage **E(T₂, 0 °C)** which is added to the thermocouple's measured EMF. Without CJC, an ambient change of 10 °C would cause an error of **~400 µV** in a Type K thermocouple — equivalent to **~10 °C measurement error**! 5. **Linearization:** The Seebeck EMF vs. temperature curve is non-linear (e.g., a Type K curve is slightly S-shaped, with sensitivity varying from 35 µV/°C at 0 °C to 42 µV/°C at 1000 °C). Linearization corrects this using: - **Analog method:** Piecewise linear approximation using resistor-diode networks (4–6 breakpoints). - **Digital method:** Polynomial fit $E = \sum c_i T^i$ in the microprocessor (much more accurate). 6. **V-to-I Converter:** Converts the linearized voltage to a current in the range 4–20 mA. The 4 mA "live zero" allows the system to distinguish between a true zero and a broken loop (0 mA = fault). **Role of CJC and Linearization:** - **CJC** is essential to provide a stable virtual reference at 0 °C, regardless of ambient temperature. - **Linearization** ensures that the relationship between process temperature and 4–20 mA output is exactly linear, enabling direct use in PID controllers without correction. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 1 for CJC role, 1 for linearization role)* --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** Type K thermocouple, range T_LRV = 300 °C, T_URV = 900 °C, Output = 4–20 mA. **Span (S):** $$ S = T_{\text{URV}} - T_{\text{LRV}} = 900 - 300 = 600\,°C $$ **General transfer function:** $$ I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{T - 300}{600} $$ **Part (i): At T = 600 °C:** $$ I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{600 - 300}{600} = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.5 = 4 + 8 = 12\,\text{mA} $$ **Part (ii): At T = 450 °C:** $$ I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{450 - 300}{600} = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.25 = 4 + 4 = 8\,\text{mA} $$ **Part (iii): For I_out = 13.6 mA, find T:** $$ 13.6 = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{T - 300}{600} $$ $$ 13.6 - 4 = 9.6 = 16 \cdot \frac{T - 300}{600} $$ $$ \frac{9.6}{16} = 0.6 = \frac{T - 300}{600} $$ $$ T - 300 = 0.6 \times 600 = 360 $$ $$ T = 300 + 360 = 660\,°C $$ **Final Answers:** - (i) **12 mA** - (ii) **8 mA** - (iii) **660 °C** *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for (i), 1 for (ii), 2 for (iii) including rearrangement)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **smart transmitter** and explain how **HART protocol** enables bidirectional digital communication on a 4–20 mA loop. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **Cold Junction Compensation** with a suitable derivation. A Type J thermocouple is connected to a transmitter with its cold junction at **30 °C**. The thermocouple sensitivity is **55 µV/°C**. If the hot junction is at **500 °C**, calculate the compensation voltage that must be added by the CJC circuit. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Architecture of a Smart Transmitter (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):** A smart transmitter consists of: 1. **Sensor + Multiplexer (MUX):** Accepts input from multiple sensor types (T/C, RTD, mV, ohms) via a software-controlled analog MUX. 2. **Instrumentation Amplifier + Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Conditions the small signal and prevents aliasing in the ADC. 3. **Sigma-Delta ADC (16 to 24-bit):** Converts the analog signal to a high-resolution digital value. 4. **Microprocessor (µP):** Performs: - **Linearization** (polynomial or lookup table from NIST). - **Engineering unit conversion** (mV → °C). - **Range scaling** (LRV, URV). - **Diagnostics** (sensor health, loop integrity, ambient temperature). - **Damping** (digital first-order low-pass filter). - **PID** (in some smart valve positioners and field-mounted controllers). 5. **Non-Volatile Memory (EEPROM/Flash):** Stores calibration coefficients, tag name, range, units, and HART configuration. 6. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) + V/I Driver:** Converts the digital PV back to a 4–20 mA signal. 7. **HART Modem:** Encodes/decodes digital data on the 4–20 mA loop using FSK. 8. **Local LCD Display:** Shows PV, engineering units, and diagnostic messages. **Bidirectional Communication via HART:** - **Physical Layer:** HART uses **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** — binary '1' is represented by **1200 Hz**, and binary '0' is represented by **2200 Hz**. These AC signals are superimposed on the 4–20 mA DC loop. - The **average current** of a pure sine wave is **zero**, so HART does not affect the analog DC measurement. The peak-to-peak amplitude is **±0.5 mA**, which is small compared to the 4–20 mA range. - **Data Link Layer:** Master-slave protocol. The DCS (or HART handheld) is the master; the transmitter is the slave. Frames include preamble, delimiter, address, command, data, and checksum. - **Application Layer:** Three command sets — **Universal** (read PV, read tag), **Common Practice** (re-range, set damping), and **Device-Specific** (manufacturer-specific functions). - **Bidirectionality:** The master *requests* a command; the slave *responds* with data. The transmitter can also send **burst mode** data continuously without polling. - The analog 4–20 mA signal is **always present** as a backup — if HART fails, the analog signal still drives the controller (fail-safe). *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for block working, 2 for HART explanation)* --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Concept of Cold Junction Compensation (CJC):** A thermocouple measures the *temperature difference* between the hot junction (T₁) and the cold junction (T₂, at the transmitter terminal block). The output EMF is **E(T₁, T₂)**, but the standard reference is **0 °C** (ice bath). To convert the measurement to a 0 °C reference, the transmitter must add the EMF that the thermocouple *would* have produced if the cold junction were at 0 °C. **Derivation:** The thermocouple EMF, from the Seebeck integral: $$ E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] dT $$ By the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures**: $$ E_{AB}(T_1, 0\,°C) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, 0\,°C) $$ Therefore, the **CJC compensation voltage** is: $$ E_{\text{CJC}} = E_{AB}(T_2, 0\,°C) = \int_{0}^{T_2} \Delta S(T)\, dT $$ For a small range around room temperature, the Seebeck coefficient can be approximated as constant: $$ E_{\text{CJC}} \approx S_{\text{avg}} \cdot T_2 $$ **Calculation:** **Given:** T₂ = 30 °C, S = 55 µV/°C, T₁ = 500 °C (not needed for the compensation itself). $$ E_{\text{CJC}} = 55\,\mu\text{V}/°C \times 30\,°C $$ $$ E_{\text{CJC}} = 1650\,\mu\text{V} = 1.65\,\text{mV} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** The CJC circuit adds 1.65 mV to the measured thermocouple signal, effectively translating the reference from 30 °C to 0 °C. Without CJC, the temperature would be under-read by **30 °C** — a serious error for a 500 °C measurement! *(7 marks: 2 for concept, 2 for derivation, 2 for calculation, 1 for interpretation)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "CJC voltage" with "thermocouple EMF"** — The CJC voltage is the *additional* voltage that must be *added* to bring the reference to 0 °C. Do not confuse it with the total thermocouple voltage. > 2. **Forgetting the Law of Intermediate Metals in CJC** — Many students write that the introduction of copper leads "creates an error". Wrong — it does not, *if both connections are at the same temperature*. This is the very reason CJC works. > 3. **Units Confusion in µV vs. mV** — Type K T/C at 500 °C gives **~20.64 mV**, not 20.64 V. Always include units and check orders of magnitude. > 4. **HART is *not* a replacement for 4–20 mA** — HART *coexists* with 4–20 mA. The analog signal is always available as a fail-safe. Writing "HART replaces 4–20 mA" loses 2 marks. > 5. **Skipping the Block Diagram in 14-mark questions** — A neat, labelled block diagram is worth 2–3 marks. Students often write paragraphs without one. **Always draw the diagram first.** > 6. **Forgetting to convert temperatures** — Many Type K calculations require the **inverse polynomial** (temperature from voltage), not just the forward polynomial. KTU may ask either. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] The **Seebeck Effect** is the foundation: EMF = ∫ΔS dT between two dissimilar metals. - [x] **Three Laws of Thermocouples** — Homogeneous Materials, Intermediate Metals, Intermediate Temperatures — are **guaranteed 2-mark questions**. - [x] **CJC is mandatory** in every thermocouple transmitter. Without it, ambient temperature changes corrupt the reading. - [x] The standard industrial output is **4–20 mA DC** (live zero). Live zero distinguishes "true zero" from "broken loop". - [x] **Smart transmitter = Sensor + ADC + µP + DAC + HART modem + EEPROM + LCD**. - [x] **HART = Highway Addressable Remote Transducer**; uses **FSK at 1200/2200 Hz** superimposed on 4–20 mA. - [x] HART is **bidirectional, non-disruptive, and open-standard**. Peak AC amplitude = ±0.5 mA; average = 0 mA. - [x] **Turndown ratio** (rangeability) is a key advantage of smart transmitters (100:1 or more). - [x] **Linearization** is done via **piecewise analog** (old) or **polynomial/digital** (smart) methods. - [x] **2-wire vs. 4-wire**: Thermocouple and RTD are typically 2-wire loop-powered (4 mA for power, 16 mA span for signal). - [x] The transfer function: $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{T - T_{\text{LRV}}}{T_{\text{URV}} - T_{\text{LRV}}}$. - [x] **HART frame**: Preamble → Delimiter → Address → Command → Byte Count → Data → Checksum. - [x] Master-slave protocol: 2 masters allowed (primary DCS + secondary handheld) with token passing. - [x] Smart transmitters offer **±0.05% accuracy**, remote re-ranging, and **continuous self-diagnostics** — none of which are available in analog transmitters.

topics.title: Control Valves - Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Valves - Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control Valve?

A **Control Valve** is the final control element in any process control loop. It is a power-operated device (typically pneumatically, hydraulically, or electrically actuated) that modifies the flow rate of a process fluid (liquid, gas, steam, or slurry) in response to a control signal received from a controller. The control signal is usually a pneumatic pressure (3–15 psi), a current (4–20 mA), or a digital signal, which the valve actuator converts into a mechanical position change of the valve trim (plug/stem).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A control valve is a variable orifice area device whose primary function is to regulate the flow of process fluid by varying the flow area in response to a control signal, thereby maintaining a process variable at its desired set-point.

### 1.2 What are Control Valve Characteristics?

The **characteristic of a control valve** is defined as the relationship between the **flow rate (Q)** through the valve and the **valve travel (l)** — i.e., the position of the stem relative to its total stroke — under constant upstream and downstream pressure conditions.

Mathematically, the inherent characteristic is expressed as:

$$Q = f(l)$$

where $l$ is the fractional stem travel (0 to 1) and $Q$ is the fractional flow.

There are **two distinct characteristics** that must be clearly distinguished:
1. **Inherent Characteristic** — measured under *constant pressure drop* across the valve.
2. **Installed Characteristic** — measured under *actual process pressure drop* (which varies with flow due to system piping losses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **Inherent Characteristic** = Factory/Manufacturer Characteristic
> - **Installed Characteristic** = Effective Characteristic in the field

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen tap (faucet)** at home. When you turn the tap handle:

- In the **first 20% of rotation**, almost no water comes out — you are essentially threading the rubber seal tight.
- In the **middle 40% of rotation**, a moderate stream flows.
- In the **last 40% of rotation**, the flow surges dramatically and becomes hard to control.

This non-linear behavior is what an **equal-percentage valve** is designed to *compensate* for. Conversely, a **quick-opening valve** would behave like a fire hydrant — full flow in the first 10% of handle movement.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Intuition:**
> A good control valve must be **linear in installed conditions**, even if the inherent characteristic is non-linear. The choice of inherent characteristic is therefore a *design trick* to overcome the non-linearity of the piping system.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Valve Travel (l)** | 0 to 1 (dimensionless, fractional) |
| **Pneumatic Signal Range** | **3–15 psi** (0.2–1.0 bar) |
| **Current Signal Range** | **4–20 mA DC** |
| **Standard Valve Size** | DN 15 to DN 600 (½″ to 24″) |
| **Cv (Valve Coefficient)** | GPM of water at 1 psi pressure drop |
| **Rangeability (Turndown)** | Typically **30:1 to 50:1** for equal-percentage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A control valve works on the **variable orifice principle**. The flow through the valve is governed by the standard fluid mechanics equation:

$$Q = C_v \cdot N \cdot F_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G}}$$

where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate
- $C_v$ = Valve flow coefficient
- $N$ = Numerical constant (1.0 for liquids, depends on gas equations)
- $F_p$ = Piping geometry factor
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across the valve
- $G$ = Specific gravity of fluid

The **stem travel $l$** directly modifies the effective flow area $A_{eff}$:

$$A_{eff} = A_{max} \cdot \phi(l)$$

where $\phi(l)$ is the trim shape function determined by the plug profile.

## 2.2 Classification of Valve Characteristics

### 2.2.1 Quick-Opening Characteristic

The flow rises rapidly with small stem movement, then plateaus. The mathematical form is:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = 1 - (1 - l)^n$$

where $n$ is typically a small integer (1 to 3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application:** On-off service, safety relief, isolation valves. Not used in modulating control.

### 2.2.2 Linear Characteristic

Flow is directly proportional to stem travel.

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = l$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application:** Used when the piping system pressure drop is much larger than the valve pressure drop (i.e., $\Delta P_{valve} \gg \Delta P_{system}$). This is rare in real plants.

### 2.2.3 Equal-Percentage Characteristic

Each equal increment of stem travel produces an equal *percentage* change in flow.

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{(l-1)}$$

where $R$ is the **rangeability** (typically 30, 50, or 100).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** This is the **most widely used** characteristic in the process industry because it compensates for the non-linear pressure drop behavior of long piping systems and fittings.

### 2.2.4 Modified Parabolic Characteristic

A compromise between linear and equal-percentage, used for specific process conditions.

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = l^n, \quad \text{where } n \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Key Parameters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G}$ | Liquid flow equation | $C_v$ in GPM, $\Delta P$ in psi |
| $Q/Q_{max} = l$ | **Linear characteristic** | $l \in [0, 1]$ |
| $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(l-1)}$ | **Equal-percentage** | $R = 30, 50, 100$ |
| $Q/Q_{max} = 1 - (1-l)^n$ | **Quick-opening** | $n = 1, 2, 3$ |
| $\Delta P_{valve} = \Delta P_{system} - K Q^2$ | System pressure balance | $K$ = system resistance |
| $l = \Delta P_{valve} / \Delta P_{system}$ | Valve authority relation | $l \in [0.3, 0.5]$ ideal |
| $\beta = \Delta P_{valve,0} / \Delta P_{system}$ | **Valve authority** | $\beta \in [0.25, 0.75]$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol in Tables:** Notice that the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ and division $\vert$ are intentionally rendered using $\vert$ to keep the markdown table parsable.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

| Industry Segment | Preferred Characteristic | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Oil & Gas Refineries** | Equal-Percentage | High turndown, handles wide flow variations |
| **Chemical Reactors** | Linear or Modified Parabolic | Predictable process gain |
| **Power Plant Boiler Feedwater** | Equal-Percentage | Wide rangeability requirement |
| **HVAC Systems** | Linear | Short piping, low pressure loss |
| **Safety Shutdown Systems** | Quick-Opening | Fast positive closure on demand |

## 2.5 The Concept of Valve Authority

**Valve Authority ($\beta$)** is one of the most critical concepts in control valve selection, defined as:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta P_{v,0}}{\Delta P_{v,0} + \Delta P_{piping,0}}$$

where subscript 0 denotes shut-off condition.

When the valve is open, the pressure drop distributes between the valve and the piping according to the flow:

$$\Delta P_{valve} = \Delta P_{system} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\beta + (1-\beta)Q^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** For good controllability, valve authority should be in the range **0.25 to 0.75**, with an ideal target of **0.5**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Ideal Orifice to Inherent Characteristic

### Step 1: Fundamental Flow Equation

For an incompressible fluid flowing through a restriction (the valve orifice), Bernoulli's equation combined with the continuity equation gives the classical orifice equation:

$$Q = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_{valve}}{\rho}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Here, $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient (dimensionless), $A_{eff}$ is the effective cross-sectional area of the flow passage, $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $\Delta P_{valve}$ is the pressure drop across the valve trim only.

### Step 2: Replacing Flow Area with Travel Function

The valve plug profile determines how the effective area changes with stem travel. The trim designer shapes the plug such that:

$$A_{eff}(l) = A_{max} \cdot \phi(l)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The trim function $\phi(l)$ is the geometric signature of the plug. For a linear plug profile, $\phi(l) = l$. For an equal-percentage plug, $\phi(l)$ is exponentially increasing with travel.

### Step 3: Substituting into Flow Equation

Inserting the area relation into the orifice equation:

$$Q(l) = C_d A_{max} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_{valve}}{\rho}} \cdot \phi(l)$$

Since under *inherent* conditions, $\Delta P_{valve}$ is held constant:

$$Q_{inherent}(l) = Q_{max} \cdot \phi(l)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The maximum flow $Q_{max}$ occurs when the valve is fully open ($l = 1$), and the inherent characteristic is purely a function of the trim geometry.

### Step 4: Deriving Equal-Percentage Mathematical Form

For an equal-percentage plug, the designer requires that:

$$\frac{dQ/Q}{dl} = k \quad (\text{constant})$$

Integrating this differential equation:

$$\ln Q = k \cdot l + C_1$$

Applying the boundary condition $Q(0) = Q_{max} / R$:

$$\ln(Q_{max}/R) = C_1$$

Applying $Q(1) = Q_{max}$:

$$\ln Q_{max} = k + \ln(Q_{max}/R) \implies k = \ln R$$

Substituting back:

$$\ln Q - \ln Q_{max} = (l-1) \ln R$$

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{(l-1)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the canonical equal-percentage equation. When $l = 0$, $Q = Q_{max}/R$. When $l = 1$, $Q = Q_{max}$. The ratio of maximum to minimum controllable flow is the rangeability $R$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Installed Characteristic from System Pressure Balance

### Step 1: System Pressure Equation

The total available pressure drop in a process loop is consumed by the valve and the piping system:

$$\Delta P_{system} = \Delta P_{valve} + \Delta P_{piping}$$

For turbulent flow in piping, the friction loss is proportional to the square of flow:

$$\Delta P_{piping} = K \cdot Q^2$$

### Step 2: Defining Valve Authority

At zero flow (design condition), the entire pressure drop appears across the valve:

$$\Delta P_{v,0} = \Delta P_{system}$$

At any flow $Q$, the valve pressure drop reduces:

$$\Delta P_{valve}(Q) = \Delta P_{v,0} - K Q^2$$

Introducing the valve authority at design flow:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta P_{v,0}}{\Delta P_{system}}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P_{valve}(Q) = \beta \cdot \Delta P_{system} - (1-\beta) \cdot \Delta P_{system} \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{Q_{max}}\right)^2$$

### Step 3: Effective Flow Relation

The actual flow through the valve is:

$$Q = C_v(l) \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{valve}(Q)}{G}}$$

Squaring both sides and rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{Q}{Q_{max}}\right)^2 = \frac{\beta}{\beta + (1-\beta)(Q/Q_{max})^2} \cdot \phi^2(l)$$

Solving for the installed characteristic:

$$\frac{Q_{installed}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{\phi(l)}{\sqrt{\beta + (1-\beta)\phi^2(l)}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the master relation connecting inherent characteristic $\phi(l)$ to the installed characteristic through the valve authority $\beta$. When $\beta \to 1$ (all pressure drop on the valve), installed = inherent. When $\beta \to 0$, the installed characteristic becomes linear regardless of inherent shape.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Characteristic

| Condition | $\Delta P_{valve}$ vs $\Delta P_{system}$ | Valve Authority | Recommended Inherent | Resulting Installed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Condition 1** | $\Delta P_{valve} \gg \Delta P_{system}$ | $\beta > 0.7$ | Linear | Linear |
| **Condition 2** | $\Delta P_{valve} = \Delta P_{system}$ | $\beta \approx 0.5$ | Equal-Percentage | Linear |
| **Condition 3** | $\Delta P_{valve} \ll \Delta P_{system}$ | $\beta < 0.3$ | Quick-Opening | Approx. Linear |
| **Condition 4** | $\Delta P_{valve}$ negligible | $\beta \approx 0$ | Any | Highly distorted |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A control valve has an equal-percentage trim with rangeability $R = 50$. The flow at $l = 0.4$ is to be computed and expressed as a percentage of maximum flow.

**Solution using derived equation:**

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{(l-1)} = 50^{(0.4-1)} = 50^{-0.6}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the exponent: $-0.6$
**Step 2:** Compute $\log_{10}(50) = 1.69897$
**Step 3:** Multiply: $1.69897 \times (-0.6) = -1.01938$
**Step 4:** Take antilog: $10^{-1.01938} = 0.0956$

Therefore:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = 9.56\%$$

**Interpretation:** At 40% stem travel, the valve passes only 9.56% of its maximum flow. This demonstrates the high gain at high travel and low gain at low travel characteristic of equal-percentage valves.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Inherent Characteristic Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX[Stem Travel l : 0 to 1] --> plotArea
    axisY[Flow Q/Qmax : 0 to 1] --> plotArea
    plotArea[Characteristic Curve Plot]
    plotArea --> linearCurve["LINEAR : Q/Qmax equals l"]
    plotArea --> epCurve["EQUAL PERCENTAGE : R power l-1, R equals 50"]
    plotArea --> qoCurve["QUICK OPENING : 1 minus 1 minus l power n"]
    linearCurve --> noteA[Slope is constant 1.0]
    epCurve --> noteB[Exponential rise, low gain at low travel]
    qoCurve --> noteC[Rapid rise, plateaus near 1.0]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Control Valve

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Controller Output 4-20 mA] --> convA[Current to Pressure Converter I to P]
    convA --> actA[Pneumatic Actuator Diaphragm plus Spring]
    actA --> stemA[Stem plus Plug Assembly]
    stemA --> orificeA[Variable Orifice Trim]
    procA[Process Fluid Inlet] --> orificeA
    orificeA --> procOut[Process Fluid Outlet]
    fbA[Position Feedback Sensor] --> stemA
    fbA --> ctrlA[Controller for Closed Loop Position]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Installed vs Inherent Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA[Inherent Characteristic phi l] --> mergeA
    inputB[Valve Authority beta] --> mergeA
    inputC[Piping System Resistance] --> mergeA
    mergeA[Mathematical Synthesis Equation] --> outputA[Installed Characteristic]
    outputA --> decisionA{Is beta near 0.5?}
    decisionA -->|YES| resultA[Installed is approximately linear]
    decisionA -->|NO, beta high| resultB[Installed approaches inherent]
    decisionA -->|NO, beta low| resultC[Installed is distorted toward linear]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Characteristic Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start Selection] --> qA{Is flow rangeability greater than 10 to 1?}
    qA -->|YES| branchA[Choose Equal Percentage]
    qA -->|NO| qB{Is valve authority high above 0.7?}
    qB -->|YES| branchB[Choose Linear]
    qB -->|NO| qC{Is fast opening required?}
    qC -->|YES| branchC[Choose Quick Opening]
    qC -->|NO| branchD[Choose Modified Parabolic]
    branchA --> endA[Result : Equal Percentage R equals 50]
    branchB --> endB[Result : Linear Trim]
    branchC --> endC[Result : Quick Opening n equals 1]
    branchD --> endD[Result : Modified Parabolic n equals 2]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Effect of Valve Authority on Installed Characteristic

```mermaid
graph LR
    idealA[Ideal Inherent: Equal Percentage] --> authA
    authA[High Authority beta equals 0.8] --> retainA[Retains EP Shape]
    authA --> authB[Medium Authority beta equals 0.5] --> linearA[Becomes Approximately Linear]
    authA --> authC[Low Authority beta equals 0.2] --> distortA[Becomes Quick Opening Shape]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define inherent and installed characteristics of a control valve. State one distinguishing factor between them.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Inherent Characteristic:** It is the relationship between the flow rate through the valve and the stem travel, measured under **constant pressure drop** across the valve. It depends solely on the geometry of the valve trim (plug profile).
- **Installed Characteristic:** It is the relationship between flow and stem travel when the valve is **mounted in the actual process piping**, where the pressure drop across the valve varies with flow due to piping friction losses.
- **Distinguishing Factor:** Inherent characteristic is a property of the valve alone (factory-tested), whereas installed characteristic is a property of the valve plus the piping system (field-determined).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is meant by valve authority? Why is it important in control valve selection?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Valve Authority ($\beta$):** It is defined as the ratio of the pressure drop across the valve at maximum (design) flow to the total pressure drop available in the system, expressed as a fraction between 0 and 1.
- **Importance:** Valve authority determines the *shape* of the installed characteristic. For good control, $\beta$ should lie between **0.25 and 0.75**, with **0.5 being ideal**. A very low authority leads to poor controllability and distorted characteristic; a very high authority wastes pumping energy. The choice of inherent characteristic must be made by considering the expected authority in service.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] — Derive the mathematical expression for the equal-percentage characteristic of a control valve. Sketch the curve and explain its advantages.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

The defining requirement of an equal-percentage characteristic is that **equal increments of stem travel produce equal percentage changes in flow rate**. This is expressed as:

$$\frac{dQ/Q}{dl} = k \quad \text{(constant)}$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = k \, dl$$

Integrating both sides from the initial flow $Q_0$ to $Q$, and from travel 0 to $l$:

$$\int_{Q_0}^{Q} \frac{dQ}{Q} = k \int_0^l dl$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{Q}{Q_0}\right) = k \cdot l$$

Exponentiating:

$$Q = Q_0 \cdot e^{kl}$$

**Applying boundary conditions:**
- At $l = 0$: $Q = Q_{min} = Q_{max}/R$
- At $l = 1$: $Q = Q_{max}$

From the second condition:

$$Q_{max} = \frac{Q_{max}}{R} \cdot e^{k} \implies e^{k} = R \implies k = \ln R$$

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{(l-1)}}$$

**Sketch of the curve:**

The curve is *convex* — at low stem travel, flow rises slowly; at high travel, flow rises steeply. (Reference: see the EP curve in Section 4.1.)

**Advantages:**
1. Provides excellent **rangeability** (typically 50:1), making it suitable for processes requiring wide flow variation.
2. **Compensates** for the non-linear pressure drop behavior of long piping systems, yielding an approximately linear installed characteristic.
3. Provides **low flow sensitivity at low travel** — allowing fine throttling in the small-flow region.
4. **Resists cavitation and flashing** at low openings because flow velocity remains controlled.

---
