**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **pneumatic valve positioner** based on the **force-balance principle**. Derive the relationship between the input signal pressure and the valve stem displacement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pneumatic positioner has a signal bellows area of $1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ and a feedback spring constant of $2000 \text{ N/m}$. If the input pressure is 12 psi, calculate (i) the signal force, (ii) the resulting stem displacement, and (iii) the steady-state stem position if the process pressure creates an additional opposing force of 5 N on the valve plug.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle & Derivation

**Block Diagram** — refer to the **Functional Block Diagram of a Pneumatic Valve Positioner** rendered above in Section 4.1.

**Working description (4 marks):**
1. The 3–15 psi input signal enters the **signal bellows**, producing a force $F_s$ on one end of the **beam**.
2. The beam carries a **flapper** that moves relative to a fixed **nozzle**. Deflection changes the gap, modulating the **nozzle back-pressure** $P_b$.
3. $P_b$ enters a **pneumatic relay** (high-capacity pilot) that routes supply air (typically 20 psi) to the **actuator diaphragm**.
4. As diaphragm pressure changes, the **stem moves**, altering the process flow.
5. The stem motion is sensed by a **feedback linkage** that compresses a **feedback spring**, producing force $F_f$ opposing $F_s$.
6. Equilibrium is reached when $F_s = F_f$, at which point the flapper returns to a balanced position.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
F_s = P_{in} \cdot A_s
$$

$$
F_f = k_f \cdot x
$$

At steady state, $F_s = F_f$:

$$
P_{in} \cdot A_s = k_f \cdot x
$$

$$
\boxed{x = \dfrac{A_s}{k_f} \cdot P_{in}}
$$

*Logic:* Stem displacement is directly proportional to input pressure — a linear relationship. This is the desired output of a positioner: to linearize the inherently non-linear valve characteristic.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $A_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $k_f = 2000 \text{ N/m}$
- $P_{in} = 12 \text{ psi}$
- Additional opposing force $F_{opp} = 5 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Convert pressure to SI units (1 mark)**

$$
P_{in} = 12 \times 6894.76 = 82{,}737.12 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the signal force (2 marks)**

$$
F_s = P_{in} \cdot A_s = 82{,}737.12 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 99.28 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Compute stem displacement without disturbance (2 marks)**

$$
x = \dfrac{F_s}{k_f} = \dfrac{99.28}{2000} = 0.0496 \text{ m} \approx 49.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Compute stem displacement WITH 5 N opposing force (2 marks)**

Modified force balance: $F_s = F_f + F_{opp}$

$$
k_f \cdot x_{new} = F_s - F_{opp}
$$

$$
x_{new} = \dfrac{99.28 - 5}{2000} = \dfrac{94.28}{2000} = 0.04714 \text{ m} \approx 47.1 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* The 5 N opposing force reduces stem travel by 2.5 mm, which is exactly why smart positioners include integral action — they automatically compensate for such process disturbances to maintain the commanded position.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $F_s = 99.28 \text{ N}$
- (ii) $x = 49.6 \text{ mm}$
- (iii) $x_{new} = 47.1 \text{ mm}$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a **smart (digital) valve positioner** with a block diagram. Discuss its advantages over a conventional pneumatic positioner.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A smart positioner receives a 12 mA signal and has a total stroke of 50 mm. Calculate (i) the percentage of input signal, (ii) the corresponding stem position in mm, and (iii) the air pressure required in the actuator diaphragm if the diaphragm area is $0.025 \text{ m}^2$ and the net stem force required to overcome friction and process load is 250 N.

---

#### Model Solution Outline for Question B

**Part (a):** Smart positioner uses a **microprocessor** that performs **PID control** on the position error. Input 4–20 mA is converted to digital, compared with **Hall-effect position sensor** feedback, and corrected using **piezoelectric pilot valves**. **Advantages:** self-calibration, HART diagnostics, partial stroke testing for SIS, ±0.1% accuracy, automatic characterization of equal-percentage or linear trim.

**Part (b):**
- (i) Signal % = $\dfrac{12 - 4}{20 - 4} \times 100 = 50\%$
- (ii) Stem position = $0.50 \times 50 = 25 \text{ mm}$
- (iii) Diaphragm pressure $P = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{250}{0.025} = 10{,}000 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.45 \text{ psi}$

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert **psi to Pa** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in numerical problems. Always write the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** When an opposing force (like $F_{opp} = 5$ N) is given, students forget to **subtract** it from the signal force before computing displacement. Re-read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Confusing Positioner vs. Controller:** The positioner is a **local feedback device on a single valve**, NOT the main process controller. Examiners deduct marks for incorrect functional descriptions.
> 4. **Missing Block Diagram Labeling:** In 7-mark theory questions, a **labelled block diagram** is mandatory. An unlabeled sketch = 0 marks for the diagram portion.
> 5. **HART Protocol Detail:** Smart positioner questions often test whether you know that HART uses **1200 Hz and 2200 Hz** FSK frequencies superimposed on the 4–20 mA loop. Memorize this.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Valve Positioner** = force-balance feedback device mounted on a pneumatic control valve.
- ✅ Core equation: $F_s = F_f \;\Rightarrow\; P_{in} \cdot A_s = k_f \cdot x \;\Rightarrow\; x = \dfrac{A_s}{k_f} P_{in}$
- ✅ Standard pneumatic signal: **3–15 psi** or **20–100 kPa**
- ✅ Standard electronic signal: **4–20 mA DC**
- ✅ Four major types: **Pneumatic, Electro-Pneumatic, Smart/Digital, Hydraulic**
- ✅ Smart positioners use **piezoelectric pilot valves** + **microprocessor** + **Hall-effect position sensor**.
- ✅ HART protocol: **1200/2200 Hz FSK** over 4–20 mA.
- ✅ Positioner accuracy: **±0.5% to ±0.1%** (smart) vs **±1–2%** (pneumatic).
- ✅ Positioners enable **Partial Stroke Testing (PST)** for **SIL 2/3** safety loops.
- ✅ **Always convert units** (psi → Pa, in → m) before any derivation.
- ✅ **Force-balance** is the universal working principle — memorize the equation.
- ✅ Smart positioner advantages: **self-calibration, remote diagnostics, characterization, faster response**.
- ✅ Applications: oil & gas, petrochemical, power plants, pharmaceuticals, HVAC, food & beverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Selection Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Valve Selection Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Valve Selection Criteria** refers to the systematic engineering methodology used to evaluate and choose the most appropriate control valve for a given process application. A control valve is the final control element in any closed-loop process control system, modulating the flow of process fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, or steam) in response to signals received from a controller. The selection process integrates process parameters, fluid properties, mechanical considerations, and economic constraints to ensure safe, reliable, and accurate process regulation.

According to the **ISA-75** standards (Instrument Society of America), valve selection involves determining the valve type, size, materials, and actuator configuration such that the valve can handle the required flow capacity with appropriate authority, rangeability, and dynamic response throughout its operational life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Valve Selection is treated as a multidisciplinary design problem requiring the student to integrate knowledge of fluid mechanics, control theory, materials science, and process safety. Questions are typically framed around Cv calculation, characteristic curve selection, and cavitation/flare analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **garden tap (faucet) connected to a complex irrigation system** in a large farm. You are the controller, and the tap is the valve. Now, if you need to water different crops:
- Some crops need a gentle drizzle (low flow, precise control) → You need a **needle-type fine-control valve**.
- Some crops need a sudden burst of water (on-off) → A simple **ball valve** will work.
- Some pipes carry muddy water with stones → You need a **robust gate valve** that won't get clogged.
- Some pipes carry hot steam → You need **metal-seated valves with thermal expansion allowance**.

The selection of the right tap (valve) depends on:
1. **What is flowing** (water, mud, hot steam, acid)
2. **How much** needs to flow (capacity)
3. **How precisely** you need to control the flow (control quality)
4. **How fast** you need to respond (dynamics)
5. **How safe** the system must be (fail-safe considerations)

In industrial process plants — refineries, chemical factories, power plants — the stakes are much higher. A wrong valve choice can lead to:
- Process instability and poor product quality
- Cavitation damage and pipe erosion
- Valve failure and unplanned shutdowns (loss of **lakhs of rupees** per hour)
- Safety hazards including toxic leaks or explosions

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> - **Valve Authority ($\beta$)**: The ratio of valve pressure drop to total system pressure drop at rated flow. $\beta = \frac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{system}}$
> - **Rangeability (Turndown Ratio)**: The ratio between maximum and minimum controllable flow rates, typically **50:1** for globe valves.
> - **Inherent Characteristic**: The relationship between valve travel (%) and flow coefficient (Cv) at constant pressure drop.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Valve Flow Coefficient ($C_v$)**: Defined as the number of **US gallons per minute (GPM)** of water at **60°F** that will flow through a valve with a pressure drop of **1 psi** across it. The metric equivalent is **$K_v$** (flow in m³/h at 1 bar pressure drop).
- **Universal Gas Constant ($R$)**: **8.314 J/(mol·K)** — used in gas valve sizing.
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP)**: **0°C (273.15 K)** and **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**.
- **NPT (National Pipe Thread)**: Standard US thread for valve end connections.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Valve Selection Process

The selection of a control valve follows a structured decision tree, generally executed in the following sequence:

### Step 1: Define the Process Service Requirements
- **Service Type**: On-off, throttling, or modulating?
- **Failure Mode**: Fail-Open (FO) or Fail-Closed (FC)?
- **Shut-off Class**: Class II, III, IV, V, or VI (per ANSI/FCI 70-2)?
- **Noise and Cavitation Limits**: Maximum permissible dBA level and cavitation index limits.

### Step 2: Characterize the Process Fluid
- **State of Fluid**: Liquid, gas, vapor, steam, or two-phase mixture?
- **Physical Properties**: Density ($\rho$), viscosity ($\mu$), vapor pressure ($P_v$), molecular weight ($M$), specific heat ratio ($\gamma$), compressibility factor ($Z$).
- **Chemical Nature**: Corrosive, abrasive, fouling, or clean service?

### Step 3: Determine Operating Conditions
- **Maximum and Normal Flow Rates ($Q_{max}$, $Q_{normal}$)**
- **Inlet and Outlet Pressures ($P_1$, $P_2$)**
- **Operating Temperature Range ($T_{min}$, $T_{max}$, $T_{normal}$)**
- **Allowable Pressure Drop ($\Delta P_{valve}$)**

### Step 4: Calculate Required Valve Coefficient ($C_v$ or $K_v$)

This is the **most critical step** — selecting the right valve size. The flow coefficient determines the valve's flow-handling capacity at a given pressure drop.

### Step 5: Select Valve Type and Body Style
- **Globe Valve**: Best for throttling and tight shut-off.
- **Ball Valve**: Best for on-off and moderate throttling.
- **Butterfly Valve**: Best for large pipe sizes (> 4 inches), low pressure drop.
- **Gate Valve**: Best for fully open/closed isolation service (not for throttling).
- **Plug Valve**: Best for slurry and viscous services.
- **Diaphragm Valve**: Best for sanitary, corrosive, or sterile applications.

### Step 6: Select Materials of Construction
- **Body**: Cast iron, carbon steel (WCB), stainless steel (CF8M), alloy 20, Monel, Hastelloy.
- **Trim**: 316 SS, Stellite (for hard-facing), 17-4 PH SS.
- **Seat**: PTFE (Teflon), metal-to-metal, PEEK, graphite.
- **Packing**: PTFE V-rings, graphite, live-loaded packing.

### Step 7: Select the Actuator
- **Pneumatic Diaphragm**: Most common, simple, fail-safe by spring action.
- **Pneumatic Piston**: Higher thrust, faster response.
- **Electric (Motorized)**: Precise positioning, suitable for remote locations.
- **Hydraulic**: Very high thrust, used for high-pressure drop valves.
- **Electro-Hydraulic**: Combines electric signal with hydraulic power.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use $\vert$ or \mid for absolute value notation in tables to prevent markdown parsing errors.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Applicable Service | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Liquid $C_v$** | $C_v = \frac{Q}{N_1} \sqrt{\frac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$ | Incompressible (non-choked) flow | $Q$ in GPM, $\Delta P$ in psi |
| 2 | **Gas $C_v$ (Standard)** | $C_v = \frac{Q}{N_5} \sqrt{\frac{G_f \cdot T \cdot Z}{\Delta P \cdot (P_1 + P_2)}}$ | Compressible gas, subsonic | $Q$ in SCFH, $P$ in psia |
| 3 | **Gas $C_v$ (Expanded)** | $C_v = \frac{Q}{N_6} \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{T \cdot Z \cdot x \cdot M \cdot \Delta P}}$ | Gas with $x \leq 0.5$ (choking limit) | $Q$ in lb/hr |
| 4 | **Steam $C_v$** | $C_v = \frac{W}{N_8 \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot (P_1 + P_2)}}$ | Saturated or superheated steam | $W$ in lb/hr |
| 5 | **Conversion $K_v$ to $C_v$** | $C_v = 1.156 \cdot K_v$ | Metric to US units | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Valve Authority** | $\beta = \frac{\Delta P_v}{\Delta P_v + \Delta P_{pipe}}$ | Control loop quality | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{123.9 \cdot d \cdot Q}{v}$ | Viscosity correction factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Choked Flow Condition** | $\Delta P_{choked} = F_L^2 \left( P_1 - F_F \cdot P_v \right)$ | Liquid cavitation check | $F_L$ = pressure recovery factor |
| 9 | **Sizing Safety Factor** | $C_{v,selected} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times C_{v,calculated}$ | Allowance for upsizing | dimensionless multiplier |
| 10 | **Flashing Criterion** | $P_2 < P_v$ at outlet | Liquid-to-vapor phase change | check at vena contracta |

### Table 2.1: N-Factor Constants for $C_v$ Equations (ISA-75.01)

| Constant | Value | Unit System |
|----------|-------|-------------|
| $N_1$ | 1.00 | GPM, psi, SG = 1.0 |
| $N_5$ | 1360 | SCFH, psia, SG = 1.0 |
| $N_6$ | 7320 | lb/hr, psia, $M$ = 1.0 |
| $N_8$ | 0.417 (sat. steam) | lb/hr, psi, psia |
| $N_8$ | 0.468 (super. steam) | lb/hr, psi, psia |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **oil refineries**, valve selection for fractionator reflux control involves balancing tight shut-off (Class IV) with resistance to coke formation — leading to **caged globe valves with hard-faced Stellite trim**. In **pharmaceutical plants**, sanitary diaphragm valves with **3-A and EHEDG certification** are mandatory. In **nuclear power plants**, ASME Section III qualified valves with rigorous seismic qualification are specified. In **offshore platforms**, super-duplex stainless steel valves with NACE MR0175 compliance handle sour (H₂S-containing) service.

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between valve travel and flow is non-linear in most valves. The three standard **inherent characteristics** are:
> 1. **Linear**: $C_v = C_{v,max} \cdot \tau$ (where $\tau$ is fractional travel)
> 2. **Equal Percentage**: $C_v = C_{v,min} \cdot R^{\tau - 1}$ (where $R$ is rangeability)
> 3. **Quick Opening**: $C_v = C_{v,max} \cdot \sqrt{\tau}$
>
> Equal-percentage trim is preferred when the **installed characteristic** must compensate for varying process gain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Liquid $C_v$ Equation

The valve flow coefficient $C_v$ is fundamentally derived from the **orifice flow equation**, calibrated for water at standard conditions.

### Starting Point: Volumetric Flow Through an Orifice

The theoretical volumetric flow rate through a restriction is given by the standard orifice equation:

$$
Q_{theoretical} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (dimensionless)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the restriction (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve (Pa)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)

### Step 1: Express in US Customary Units

We need to express this in GPM and psi. Converting:
- 1 GPM = 0.00006309 m³/s
- 1 psi = 6894.76 Pa
- Water density at 60°F: $\rho_w$ = 62.37 lb/ft³ = 998.4 kg/m³

Rearranging the orifice equation to solve for the area:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{C_d} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{2 \Delta P}}
$$

### Step 2: Define $C_v$ as a Calibration Constant

By convention, the ISA committee defined $C_v$ as the flow of **water** ($G_f = 1.0$, $\rho = \rho_w$) in **GPM** at a pressure drop of **1 psi**. Substituting these standardized values and empirically grouping constants, we obtain:

$$
C_v = N_1 \cdot \frac{Q}{C_d} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{2 \Delta P \cdot \rho_w}}
$$

Through experimental correlation, the constant $N_1$ and the discharge coefficient combine to yield:

$$
\boxed{C_v = \frac{Q}{N_1} \sqrt{\frac{G_f}{\Delta P}}}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow in GPM
- $G_f$ = specific gravity of the fluid (ratio to water)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve in psi
- $N_1$ = 1.00 (units constant)

### Step 3: Derivation of the Gas $C_v$ Equation (Simplified)

For compressible gas flow, the density at the vena contracta is not constant; we must use the **isothermal or adiabatic expansion** model. Using the mass conservation principle:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_1 A v_1 = \rho_2 A_{vc} v_2
$$

For adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) flow of an ideal gas:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho_1^\gamma} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_2^\gamma}
$$

Applying the steady-flow energy equation and integrating, the mass flow rate is:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{\frac{2 M}{\gamma R T_1} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma} \right]}
$$

Expanding and simplifying through the standard ISA correlation yields:

$$
\boxed{C_v = \frac{Q}{N_5} \sqrt{\frac{G_f \cdot T \cdot Z}{\Delta P \cdot (P_1 + P_2)}}}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = gas flow in SCFH (standard cubic feet per hour)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in °R (Rankine)
- $Z$ = compressibility factor
- $P_1, P_2$ = inlet and outlet pressures in psia
- $G_f$ = gas specific gravity relative to air
- $N_5$ = 1360

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Selection Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most important high-yield tool for KTU 2024 ESE questions.** Memorize the trade-off axes.

| Selection Criterion | Globe Valve | Ball Valve | Butterfly Valve | Gate Valve | Diaphragm Valve |
|---------------------|-------------|------------|------------------|------------|------------------|
| **Throttling Quality** | Excellent (Class IV–VI) | Good (Class VI) | Fair (Class III–IV) | Poor (not for throttling) | Good |
| **Pressure Drop** | High | Low | Very Low | Very Low | Medium |
| **Capacity ($C_v$ per size)** | Lowest (best $C_d$) | High | Highest | Highest | Medium |
| **Cost per size** | Highest | Moderate | Lowest | Low | High |
| **Temperature Limit** | Up to 600°C+ | Up to 400°C | Up to 200°C | Up to 600°C+ | Up to 175°C (PTFE) |
| **Slurry Service** | Poor | Fair (V-ball) | Good (no cavities) | Poor | Excellent (weir-type) |
| **Sanitary / Pharma** | Fair | Good | Good | Fair | Excellent (3-A cert.) |
| **Size Range** | ½" – 16" | ½" – 24" | 2" – 96" | ½" – 48" | ½" – 12" |
| **Leakage Class (FCI 70-2)** | II, III, IV, V, VI | V, VI | III, IV | I, II | VI (bubble-tight) |
| **Maintenance** | High (stem packing) | Low | Low | Medium | High (diaphragm) |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A control valve is to be selected for a water service with the following conditions:
> - Volumetric flow: $Q$ = 250 GPM
> - Inlet pressure: $P_1$ = 150 psia
> - Outlet pressure: $P_2$ = 110 psia
> - Specific gravity: $G_f$ = 1.05
> - Temperature: $T$ = 80°F
> - Vapor pressure: $P_v$ = 0.5 psia
> - Pipe size: 3 inches
> - Pressure recovery factor: $F_L$ = 0.85

### Step 1: Calculate the Pressure Drop

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 150 - 110 = 40 \text{ psi}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Theoretical $C_v$

$$
C_{v,calc} = \frac{Q}{N_1} \sqrt{\frac{G_f}{\Delta P}} = \frac{250}{1.0} \sqrt{\frac{1.05}{40}}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Square Root Term

$$
\sqrt{\frac{1.05}{40}} = \sqrt{0.02625} = 0.1620
$$

### Step 4: Compute $C_v$

$$
C_{v,calc} = 250 \times 0.1620 = 40.5
$$

### Step 5: Apply Safety Factor (1.25 for normal service)

$$
C_{v,selected} = 1.25 \times 40.5 = 50.625 \approx 51
$$

### Step 6: Check for Choked Flow / Cavitation

Calculate the choked pressure drop:

$$
\Delta P_{choked} = F_L^2 (P_1 - F_F P_v)
$$

For water, $F_F \approx 0.96$:

$$
\Delta P_{choked} = (0.85)^2 \times (150 - 0.96 \times 0.5) = 0.7225 \times 149.52 = 108.03 \text{ psi}
$$

Since $\Delta P_{actual}$ (40 psi) < $\Delta P_{choked}$ (108.03 psi), the flow is **un-choked**, and the basic $C_v$ equation is valid.

### Step 7: Select Valve from Manufacturer Catalog

From a standard manufacturer catalog (e.g., Fisher, Samson, Flowserve), a **2-inch globe valve** with linear trim typically offers $C_v$ around 50–60, so we select the **2-inch globe valve**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Valve Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Service Requirements] --> B{On-Off or Modulating?}
    B -- On-Off --> C{Fluid Type?}
    B -- Modulating --> D{Fluid Type Liquid or Gas or Steam?}
    C -- Liquid Clean --> E[Ball Valve or Gate Valve]
    C -- Liquid Slurry --> F[Plug Valve or Knife Gate]
    C -- Gas Clean --> G[Ball Valve]
    D -- Liquid --> H{Cavitation Risk?}
    D -- Gas --> I{High Pressure Drop?}
    D -- Steam --> J[Globe Valve with Hard Facing]
    H -- High --> K[Anti-Cavitation Trim: Caged or Multi-Stage]
    H -- Low --> L[Globe Valve Single Stage]
    I -- Yes --> M[Angle Valve or Multi-Stage]
    I -- No --> N[Globe or Ball Valve]
    E --> O[End: Select Actuator Pneumatic or Electric]
    F --> O
    G --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Check: Materials, Shut-off Class, Standards]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Pneumatic Control Valve System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL[Controller 4-20 mA] --> I2P[Current to Pressure Converter I/P]
    I2P --> ACT[Diaphragm Actuator]
    ACT --> STEM[Valve Stem]
    STEM --> TRIM[Valve Trim Plug and Seat]
    TRIM --> PROC[Process Fluid Flow Regulation]
    PROC --> FT[Flow Transmitter Feedback]
    FT --> CTRL
    SPRING[Spring] -.-> ACT
    POS[Positioner Optional] --> ACT
    PNEU[Air Supply 20 psi] --> I2P
    PNEU --> POS
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: $C_v$ Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Process Data Q, P1, P2, T, Gf, Pv] --> B{Is Fluid Liquid?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Check Choking: dP vs dP_choked}
    C -- Unchoked --> D[Use Liquid Cv Equation]
    C -- Choked --> E[Use Modified Equation with FL and FF]
    B -- No --> F{Is Fluid Gas or Steam?}
    F -- Gas --> G{Is x Less or Greater Than 0.5?}
    G -- Less --> H[Use Expanded Form]
    G -- Greater --> I[Use Standard Form]
    F -- Steam --> J{Saturated or Superheated?}
    J -- Saturated --> K[Use N8 = 0.417]
    J -- Superheated --> L[Use N8 = 0.468]
    D --> M[Apply Safety Factor 1.2 to 1.5]
    E --> M
    H --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Select Nearest Standard Cv from Catalog]
    N --> O[Final Valve Size and Model Selection]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Valve Body Styles (Text Representation)

```
   GLOBE VALVE          BALL VALVE            BUTTERFLY VALVE
   ____________         ____________          ____________
  |  |        |        |    (o)    |        |   |    |   |
  |  |___|    |        |   /---\   |        |   | /  |   |
  |  |   |    |        |  |     |  |        |   |/   |   |
  |  |   |  < |        |  |  V  |  |        |  <|    |   |
  |  |___|    |        |   \___/   |        |   |\   |   |
  |  |        |        |    (o)    |        |   | \  |   |
  |__|________|        |__|_______|_|        |___|____|___|
   Z-pattern flow      Straight-through     Disc rotates 90°
   (high recovery)     (low recovery)       (very low recovery)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Valve Authority and explain its significance in control valve selection.
**Model Answer:**

Valve authority is defined as the ratio of the pressure drop across the control valve to the total pressure drop in the control loop (valve + piping system) at the rated (maximum) flow condition.

$$
\beta = \frac{\Delta P_{v,max}}{\Delta P_{v,max} + \Delta P_{pipe}}
$$

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **installed characteristic** of the valve — how the actual flow varies with valve travel in the real system.
2. A low authority ($\beta < 0.25$) means most of the system pressure drop is in the pipe, leading to **poor controllability**, sluggish response, and limit cycles.
3. A high authority ($\beta > 0.5$) gives a **near-inherent characteristic** but wastes pumping energy.
4. The recommended range for good control is **0.4 to 0.7**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1M), Formula (1M), Significance (1M).

---

### Question 2: Compare the three standard inherent characteristics of control valves.
**Model Answer:**

| Characteristic | Equation | When Used |
|----------------|----------|-----------|
| **Linear** | $C_v = C_{v,max} \cdot \tau$ | When $\Delta P$ across valve is constant; suitable for level control. |
| **Equal Percentage** | $C_v = C_{v,min} \cdot R^{\tau - 1}$ | When $\Delta P$ varies with flow; most common in flow and pressure control. |
| **Quick Opening** | $C_v = C_{v,max} \cdot \sqrt{\tau}$ | On-off or two-position service; high initial capacity. |

**Key Insight:** Equal-percentage trim is **self-compensating** — as the valve closes, the small $C_v$ change per percent travel matches the high process gain at low flow rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Three types (1.5M), Use-case distinction (1.5M).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the safety factor** of 1.2 to 1.5 to the calculated $C_v$ (loss of 2 marks).
> 2. **Skipping the cavitation/choked-flow check** before using the basic $C_v$ equation (loss of 2–3 marks).
> 3. **Using wrong units** — GPM vs. m³/h, psia vs. psig, °F vs. °R (loss of 2 marks).
> 4. **Not stating the selection rationale** — simply giving a $C_v$ number without justifying the valve type is incomplete (loss of 2 marks).

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for selecting a control valve** for an industrial process. Discuss the role of process data, fluid characterization, and safety considerations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The control valve selection procedure is a multi-stage engineering process:

**Stage 1: Define the Functional Requirements** (1 Mark)
- Service type: on-off, throttling, or emergency shutdown (ESD)
- Failure mode: fail-open (FO), fail-closed (FC), or fail-last-position
- Shut-off class per FCI 70-2 (typically Class IV or VI for modulating)
- Operating temperature and pressure ranges

**Stage 2: Collect Process Data** (1 Mark)
- Maximum, normal, and minimum flow rates ($Q_{max}$, $Q_{norm}$, $Q_{min}$)
- Inlet and outlet pressures ($P_1$, $P_2$)
- Fluid temperature ($T$)
- Fluid properties: specific gravity ($G_f$), viscosity ($\mu$), vapor pressure ($P_v$), molecular weight ($M$)
- Required valve authority and rangeability

**Stage 3: Fluid Characterization** (1 Mark)
- Classify the fluid as clean liquid, viscous liquid, slurry, gas, vapor, steam, or two-phase
- Identify corrosion, abrasion, fouling, or toxicity concerns
- Determine if cavitation, flashing, or choked flow is a risk

**Stage 4: Calculate the Required $C_v$** (1.5 Marks)
- Use the appropriate ISA-75 equation for the fluid type
- Apply a safety factor of 1.2 to 1.5
- Check for choked flow using $F_L$ and $F_F$ factors
- Verify that the calculated $C_v$ lies within the normal operating range (typically 20% to 80% of maximum) for good control

**Stage 5: Select the Valve Type and Body Style** (1.5 Marks)
- Based on fluid, capacity, shut-off, and cost — refer to the selection matrix
- Choose the inherent characteristic (linear, equal percentage, quick opening) based on process gain

**Stage 6: Select Materials of Construction and Actuator** (0.5 Marks)
- Body, trim, seat, packing based on corrosion, temperature, and pressure
- Actuator based on thrust, response time, and fail-safe requirements

**Stage 7: Verify and Document** (0.5 Marks)
- Confirm noise level, emissions compliance, and applicable codes (ASME B16.34, ISA-75, API 6D, NACE)

---

**Q. (b)** A control valve handles **saturated steam at 200 psig** with a flow rate of **5000 lb/hr**. The outlet pressure is **150 psig**. The valve has a pressure recovery factor $F_L$ = 0.9. Compute the required $C_v$ and recommend a valve size from a standard catalog where 2-inch valves have $C_v$ = 55 and 3-inch valves have $C_v$ = 110. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify Fluid and Select Equation** (1 Mark)
The fluid is **saturated steam**, so we use:

$$
C_v = \frac{W}{N_8 \sqrt{\Delta P (P_1 + P_2)}}
$$

With $N_8 = 0.417$ for saturated steam.

**Step 2: Convert Pressures to Absolute Units** (1 Mark)

$$
P_1 = 200 + 14.7 = 214.7 \text{ psia}
$$

$$
P_2 = 150 + 14.7 = 164.7 \text{ psia}
$$

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 214.7 - 164.7 = 50 \text{ psi}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the $C_v$ Equation** (2 Marks)

$$
C_v = \frac{5000}{0.417 \cdot \sqrt{50 \times (214.7 + 164.7)}}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the Denominator** (1 Mark)

$$
\sqrt{50 \times 379.4} = \sqrt{18970} = 137.73
$$

$$
0.417 \times 137.73 = 57.43
$$

**Step 5: Compute $C_v$** (1 Mark)

$$
C_v = \frac{5000}{57.43} = 87.06
$$

**Step 6: Apply Safety Factor (1.25)** (0.5 Mark)

$$
C_{v,design} = 1.25 \times 87.06 = 108.83
$$

**Step 7: Select Valve Size** (0.5 Mark)

From the catalog:
- 2-inch valve: $C_v$ = 55 (insufficient)
- 3-inch valve: $C_v$ = 110 (sufficient, 108.83 < 110)

**Recommendation: Select a 3-inch globe valve with equal-percentage trim.** For saturated steam at 200 psig, **stainless steel body with Stellite-faced trim** is recommended for erosion resistance.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **types of valve trim** used for cavitation and noise reduction. Include sketches or descriptive diagrams of caged trim, multi-stage trim, and low-noise trim. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Cavitation and aerodynamic noise are major concerns in control valve selection. They cause **erosion, vibration, and premature failure** of the valve and downstream piping. Specialized trims mitigate these effects:

**1. Anti-Cavitation Trim (Caged Design):** (2 Marks)
- Uses a **cage or sleeve** surrounding the plug with multiple precisely machined holes.
- Creates a **multi-stage pressure drop** — each hole acts as a small orifice.
- The high velocity jets from each hole dissipate in the center of the cage, providing a "shock absorber" effect.
- The pressure at the vena contracta is kept above vapor pressure, eliminating cavitation.
- Best for moderate pressure drops where cavitation is the primary concern.

**2. Multi-Stage (Stacked Disc) Trim:** (2 Marks)
- Uses a **stack of discs** (similar to a labyrinth) inside the valve.
- Each disc creates a small incremental pressure drop.
- Pressure is reduced in many small stages rather than one large stage.
- Suitable for **very high pressure drops** (e.g., boiler feedwater, HP letdown stations).
- Prevents both cavitation and flashing.

**3. Low-Noise Trim (Aerodynamic):** (2 Marks)
- Uses **small parallel passages or tortuous paths** (resembling a honeycomb or drilled holes).
- Breaks up large flow streams into many small high-velocity jets that mix and dissipate.
- Reduces aerodynamic noise by 10–20 dBA compared to standard trim.
- Essential for **gas and steam service** near compressor or letdown stations.

**4. Material Selection for Cavitation Resistance:** (1 Mark)
- Stellite (cobalt-chrome alloy) hard-facing on plug and seat
- Tungsten carbide inserts
- Ceramic (alumina) trim for severe service

---

**Q. (b)** A control valve is to be sized for a **gas service** with the following data:
- Volumetric flow: $Q$ = 100,000 SCFH
- Inlet pressure: $P_1$ = 200 psia
- Outlet pressure: $P_2$ = 100 psia
- Temperature: $T$ = 100°F
- Specific gravity: $G_f$ = 0.6 (relative to air)
- Compressibility factor: $Z$ = 0.98

Compute the required $C_v$ and verify if the flow is choked. The ratio $x = \Delta P / P_1$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute the Pressure Drop** (0.5 Mark)

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 200 - 100 = 100 \text{ psi}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Pressure Ratio $x$** (0.5 Mark)

$$
x = \frac{\Delta P}{P_1} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3: Determine Which $C_v$ Equation to Use** (1 Mark)

Since $x = 0.5$ (which is the transition value), and the flow is on the borderline, we check choking. For air with $\gamma = 1.4$, the critical pressure ratio is:

$$
x_{critical} = 0.72 - 0.3 = 0.42 \text{ (approx for air with Fk factor)}
$$

Since $x = 0.5 > 0.42$, the flow is **choked**. We must use the **expanded form** with a $Y$ or expansion factor correction, or use the standard form with the (P1 + P2) approximation if $x < 0.5$. Here, $x = 0.5$ — we use the standard form and check the result.

**Step 4: Use the Standard Gas $C_v$ Equation** (2 Marks)

$$
C_v = \frac{Q}{N_5} \sqrt{\frac{G_f \cdot T \cdot Z}{\Delta P (P_1 + P_2)}}
$$

Convert temperature: $T = 100 + 460 = 560$ °R.

$$
C_v = \frac{100{,}000}{1360} \sqrt{\frac{0.6 \times 560 \times 0.98}{100 \times (200 + 100)}}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the Square Root Term** (1 Mark)

$$
\frac{0.6 \times 560 \times 0.98}{100 \times 300} = \frac{329.28}{30000} = 0.010976
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.010976} = 0.1048
$$

**Step 6: Compute $C_v$** (1 Mark)

$$
C_v = \frac{100{,}000}{1360} \times 0.1048 = 73.53 \times 0.1048 = 7.71
$$

**Step 7: Apply Safety Factor and Recommend** (1 Mark)

$$
C_{v,design} = 1.25 \times 7.71 = 9.63 \approx 10
$$

For a gas service with high pressure drop and potential noise issues, select a **globe valve with low-noise multi-hole trim**, **1.5-inch or 2-inch** size, with equal-percentage characteristic. Since $x = 0.5$, the flow is borderline choked — use the **expanded form** for more accuracy:

$$
C_v = \frac{Q}{N_5 \cdot Y} \sqrt{\frac{T \cdot Z \cdot G_f}{x \cdot M \cdot P_1^2}}
$$

The selection criterion is to take the **larger** of the two $C_v$ values computed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Valve Selection Criteria**

- **$C_v$ Definition:** GPM of water at 60°F with 1 psi pressure drop. The metric equivalent $K_v$ = m³/h at 1 bar $\Delta P$. Conversion: $C_v = 1.156 \cdot K_v$.

- **Master the Three $C_v$ Equations:** Liquid ($N_1 = 1.0$), Gas ($N_5 = 1360$), Steam ($N_8 = 0.417$ sat / $0.468$ super).

- **Always Apply a Safety Factor of 1.2 to 1.5** on the calculated $C_v$ before selecting the catalog size.

- **Choked Flow Check (Liquid):** $\Delta P_{actual} < F_L^2(P_1 - F_F P_v)$. If not, use the choked flow modification.

- **Choked Flow Check (Gas):** If $x = \Delta P / P_1 > x_{critical}$ (≈ 0.42 for air), flow is choked — apply the expansion factor $Y$.

- **Valve Authority Rule:** Aim for $\beta$ between **0.4 and 0.7** for good control. Below 0.3, control is poor.

- **Rangeability:** Equal-percentage trim gives 50:1; linear gives 30:1; quick opening is below 5:1.

- **Body Style Selection Quick Guide:**
  - *Clean liquid, throttling* → **Globe valve**
  - *Gas, low noise needed* → **Globe with low-noise trim**
  - *On-off, low cost* → **Ball or butterfly**
  - *Slurry / pulp* → **Knife gate or plug valve**
  - *Sanitary / pharma* → **Diaphragm valve**
  - *High capacity, low pressure drop* → **Butterfly**

- **Failure Modes:**
  - *Fail-Open (FO)*: Spring pushes valve open; air signal closes. Used in cooling water, firewater.
  - *Fail-Closed (FC)*: Spring closes valve; air signal opens. Used in fuel gas, steam, hazardous services.

- **Valve Coefficient Conversion Table (for reference):**
  - $C_v = 1.156 \cdot K_v$
  - $C_v = 29.9 \cdot K_v$ (alternative — confirm units)
  - $K_v = 0.865 \cdot C_v$

- **KTU-Favorite Question Patterns:**
  1. *Calculate $C_v$ for liquid/gas/steam service → choose catalog size.*
  2. *Compare inherent characteristics → select for given process.*
  3. *Explain cavitation and how anti-cavitation trim works.*
  4. *Discuss valve authority and its effect on controllability.*
  5. *Recommend valve type for a given fluid and service condition.*

- **Key Codes & Standards (for citation in answers):**
  - **ISA-75.01** — Control valve sizing equations
  - **ANSI/FCI 70-2** — Control valve seat leakage classification
  - **ASME B16.34** — Valve design and testing
  - **API 6D** — Pipeline valves
  - **NACE MR0175** — Sulfide stress cracking resistance
  - **IEC 60534** — Industrial-process control valves (international standard)

- **One-Liner Quick-Recall Sentences for the Exam:**
  1. "Equal-percentage trim compensates for varying process gain."
  2. "Low valve authority leads to poor controllability and hunting."
  3. "The pressure recovery factor $F_L$ indicates how much of the pressure drop is recovered downstream — lower $F_L$ means more recovery and higher cavitation risk."
  4. "Anti-cavitation trim breaks one large pressure drop into many small ones."
  5. "Selection of a control valve is a 7-step process: service, fluid, conditions, $C_v$, type, materials, actuator."

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Valve Selection Criteria**
> *Prepared for KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech — PEAET795 Instrumentation System Design*

topics.title: Orifice and Control Valve Sizing - Orifice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Orifice and Control Valve Sizing - Orifice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **orifice plate** is a thin, flat (or specially contoured) metallic disc, precisely bored at its centre, that is inserted concentrically inside a pipe carrying the process fluid. It constitutes the most widely used **primary flow element (PFE)** for flow measurement based on the **differential-pressure (DP) principle**. The plate creates a localized constriction; as the fluid accelerates through the bore, a measurable **differential pressure (ΔP)** develops between the upstream and downstream tapping points, which, by application of **Bernoulli's energy equation and the continuity principle**, yields the volumetric or mass flow rate.

Mathematically, the volumetric flow rate through a sharp-edged concentric orifice is expressed as:

$$Q = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

where every symbol carries the standard KTU/ISA meaning listed in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The orifice is treated as the *reference standard* against which all other primary elements (venturi, flow nozzle, annubar, wedge meter) are benchmarked. Mastery of its derivation, sizing methodology, and tap-location conventions is *mandatory* for ESE Module 3.

## 1.2 Physical Principle at a Glance

| Physical Quantity | Upstream of Orifice | At the Vena Contracta | Downstream of Orifice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pipe cross-section | Full (D) | Reduced (d, β ratio) | Recovers gradually |
| Velocity | Lower | **Maximum** | Decreases |
| Static Pressure | **Maximum** | **Minimum** | Recovers partially |
| Reynolds Number | Moderate | High | High |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Pinch"

Imagine pressing your thumb partially over the end of a flowing garden hose. Three things happen *simultaneously*:

1. The water **speeds up** where the opening is smallest (continuity).
2. The pressure on the **upstream side of your thumb rises** (you can feel the hose stiffen).
3. The pressure on the **downstream side (the jet) falls** sharply.

The difference between the higher upstream pressure and the lower jet pressure is exactly the **differential pressure (ΔP)**. By measuring this ΔP, you can mathematically back-calculate how much water is flowing — without ever having to catch the water in a bucket. **The orifice plate does precisely this, but engineered to international standards (ISO 5167-2).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world "vena contracta":** Just past the orifice bore, the jet continues to contract for a short distance before re-expanding. The point of minimum cross-section is called the *vena contracta*, and it is here that the static pressure reaches its absolute minimum. The placement of pressure tappings is therefore a critical design decision (see Section 2).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard tapping locations per ISO 5167-2:** Flange taps (1 in / 25.4 mm from plate faces), D–D/2 taps, and corner taps.
- **Recommended β-ratio range:** $0.20 \le \beta \le 0.75$ (β = d/D).
- **Acceptable Reynolds Number range:** $Re_D \ge 10^4$ (minimum) for validity of the standard discharge coefficient.
- **Standard density of water (reference):** $\rho_w = 998.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at **20 °C**.
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Beta ratio (β):** Dimensionless — the single most important geometric parameter.

## 1.5 Practical Industrial Significance

- **Cost:** Orifice plates are 5× to 20× cheaper than venturi tubes or coriolis meters.
- **Repeatability:** Typical ±0.5 % of rate when manufactured to ISO 5167-2.
- **Turndown:** Limited (4:1 typical) because the signal is proportional to $\sqrt{\Delta P}$.
- **Applications:** Steam, water, natural gas, oil, air, and most clean single-phase fluids in refineries, power plants (BTG package), and chemical process industries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Orifice Theory

The derivation rests on three classical pillars from fluid mechanics, all examinable in KTU questions:

1. **Continuity Equation** (conservation of mass for steady, 1-D flow):
$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

2. **Bernoulli's Equation** (conservation of energy, inviscid, incompressible, along a streamline):
$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$

3. **Empirical correction factor — Discharge Coefficient $C_d$** to account for the real-world deviation of the vena contracta geometry and viscous losses from the ideal case. For ISO 5167-2 sharp-edged orifice:
$$C_d \approx 0.6 \text{ (typical, varies with } \beta \text{ and } Re_D)$$

## 2.2 Geometry & Beta Ratio

The **beta ratio (β)** is the ratio of the orifice bore diameter to the internal pipe diameter:

$$\beta = \frac{d}{D}$$

The corresponding **area ratio** is $\beta^2 = (A_2 / A_1)$.

A small β means a small bore (low flow, high ΔP), while a large β means a large bore (high flow, low ΔP, but more risk of poor accuracy at low Reynolds numbers).

## 2.3 Velocity-of-Approach Factor ($E_v$)

The upstream velocity $v_1$ is not strictly zero; the fluid must *approach* the orifice. The correction factor is:

$$E_v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the fourth power?** Because the kinetic-energy term in Bernoulli uses $v^2$, and continuity converts $v_1$ to $v_2$ via the area ratio $\beta^2$, the substitution yields $\beta^4$ in the denominator under the square root.

## 2.4 Expansion Factor (Y) for Compressible Fluids

For **gases and vapours**, the fluid density changes as it accelerates through the orifice. An **expansion factor Y** (also called *expansion coefficient* or *Y-factor*) accounts for this. The simplified ISO 5167-1 form is:

$$Y = 1 - (0.41 + 0.35 \beta^4) \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\kappa \cdot P_1}$$

where **κ** is the **isentropic exponent** ($C_p / C_v$).

- For liquids → $Y \approx 1$ (density is essentially constant).
- For gases → $Y < 1$ (typically 0.95 to 0.99 in practice).

## 2.5 Master Formula (General Form)

| Fluid Type | Volumetric Flow | Mass Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Incompressible** | $Q = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $W = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2 \rho \, \Delta P}$ |
| **Compressible** | $Q = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot Y \cdot A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_1}}$ | $W = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot Y \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \, \Delta P}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the mass-flow equation for compressible flow, the density used is the **upstream density $\rho_1$** (before the constriction), NOT the downstream density.

## 2.6 The "Practical" Engineering Form (KTU Examination Standard)

In the field, differential pressure is measured as a *head* of the manometer fluid or directly in engineering units. The general *flow-coefficient* form used by KTU textbooks and in the ESE is:

$$Q = N \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{h_w}$$

or in differential-pressure form:

$$Q = K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$$

where **N** is a numerical constant whose value depends on the unit system (a common source of mark-loss — see valuation warning in Section 5).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables | Domain / Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta = d/D$ | d = bore dia, D = pipe ID | $0.20 \le \beta \le 0.75$ |
| 2 | $E_v = 1/\sqrt{1-\beta^4}$ | Velocity-of-approach factor | All orifice flows |
| 3 | $Q_{ideal} = A_2\sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | Ideal (no loss) flow | Theoretical upper bound |
| 4 | $Q = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P/\rho}$ | Incompressible real flow | Liquids |
| 5 | $W = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot Y \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P}$ | Compressible mass flow | Gases / Vapours |
| 6 | $Y = 1 - (0.41 + 0.35\beta^4)\Delta P/(\kappa P_1)$ | Expansion factor | $\Delta P / P_1 \le 0.25$ |
| 7 | $C_d \approx 0.5959 + 0.0312 \beta^{2.1} - 0.184 \beta^8 + \dots$ | Stolz equation (ISO 5167-2) | $Re_D \ge 10^4$, $\beta \le 0.75$ |
| 8 | $h_{perm} \approx (1 - \beta^2) \cdot \Delta P$ | Permanent pressure loss | Approximate |
| 9 | $Re_D = 4 \dot{m} / (\pi D \mu)$ | Pipe Reynolds number | For $C_d$ validity |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flow measurement of steam** in boiler-turbine-generator (BTG) packages — uses β ≈ 0.5 to 0.6.
- **Natural gas custody transfer** — orifice with flange taps is the legal standard.
- **Feedwater and condensate** measurement in thermal power stations.
- **Chemical dosing lines** in process plants (e.g., acid, alkali, amine).
- **HVAC chilled water** circuits in large buildings.

> [!NOTE]
> The **discharge coefficient $C_d$** is itself a *function* of both $\beta$ and $Re_D$. The empirical correlation most commonly used in KTU references is the **Reader-Harris / Stolz equation** (ISO 5167-2:2003). For exam problems, a constant value of $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.62$ is usually supplied or assumed.

## 2.7 Tap Locations — A Quick Comparative Note (Highly Testable)

| Tap Type | Upstream Location | Downstream Location | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corner Taps** | 1 mm from plate face | 1 mm from plate face | Clean liquids, small pipes |
| **Flange Taps** | 25.4 mm (1 in) from plate | 25.4 mm from plate | **Most common**; ISO 5167 standard |
| **D and D/2 Taps** | 1 D from plate | 0.5 D from plate | Large pipes, gas service |
| **Vena Contracta Taps** | 1 D from plate | At vena contracta | Research, high accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Orifice Flow Equation (Incompressible Case)

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in the ESE. We derive it line by line, with every algebraic transition made explicit.

### Step 1 — Setup and Coordinate Definition

Consider a horizontal pipe of internal diameter **D** carrying an incompressible fluid. An orifice plate with bore diameter **d** is installed. Two pressure tappings are taken:
- Point 1: upstream (full pipe area, before the orifice).
- Point 2: at the **vena contracta** (minimum jet area).

Let $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (upstream area) and $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ (vena contracta area).

### Step 2 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For steady, incompressible, 1-D flow:

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

Solving for $v_1$ in terms of $v_2$:

$$v_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot v_2 = \beta^2 \cdot v_2 \quad \text{where } \beta = \frac{d}{D}$$

### Step 3 — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)

Assuming the pipe is horizontal (so elevation $z_1 = z_2$), with no friction and no pump between points 1 and 2:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$

Rearranging to isolate the differential head:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$

Define the differential pressure head as $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$:

$$h = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$

### Step 4 — Substitution Using Continuity

Substitute $v_1 = \beta^2 v_2$:

$$h = \frac{v_2^2 - (\beta^2 v_2)^2}{2g} = \frac{v_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)}{2g}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Theoretical Throat Velocity $v_2$

$$v_2^2 = \frac{2 g h}{1 - \beta^4}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} = E_v \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### Step 6 — Volumetric Flow Rate (Ideal Case)

$$Q_{ideal} = A_2 \cdot v_2 = A_2 \cdot E_v \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### Step 7 — Introduce the Discharge Coefficient $C_d$

The **real** jet is *smaller* than the geometric bore (the jet contracts further past the bore) and there are small viscous losses. We therefore introduce an empirical discharge coefficient $C_d$:

$$Q_{real} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot E_v \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### Step 8 — Convert Head to Differential Pressure

Since $h = \Delta P / (\rho g)$:

$$Q = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final derived form (KTU gold-standard):**
> $$\boxed{\,Q = C_d \, E_v \, A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}\,}$$
> For **mass flow**, simply multiply by $\rho$ at the upstream condition.

## 3.2 Mass Flow Derivation for Compressible Fluids

For a gas of upstream density $\rho_1$, using $P_1 v_1 = P_2 v_2 / \text{(isentropic relation)}$ and the same Bernoulli framework:

$$W = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P} \cdot \underbrace{\left[1 - (0.41 + 0.35 \beta^4)\frac{\Delta P}{\kappa P_1}\right]}_{Y \text{ (expansion factor)}}$$

So the full mass-flow equation for a gas:

$$W = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot Y \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 ESE, when the problem says "sizing of orifice for steam" or "natural gas", you **must** include the Y-factor. Skipping Y is one of the most common single-mark losses.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem (14-mark standard):**
A horizontal pipe of **ID 100 mm** carries water at **30 °C**. An orifice plate of bore **d = 50 mm** is installed with flange taps. The measured differential pressure is **ΔP = 10 kPa**. Given $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho = 995.7 \text{ kg/m}^3$, calculate: (a) the volumetric flow rate, (b) the mass flow rate, (c) the permanent pressure loss.

### Solution

**(a) Step 1 — Beta ratio**
$$\beta = d/D = 50/100 = 0.5$$

**(a) Step 2 — Velocity-of-approach factor**
$$E_v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.5^4}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.9375}}$$

$$E_v = \frac{1}{0.9683} = 1.0328$$

**(a) Step 3 — Orifice (throat) area**
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0025)}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**(a) Step 4 — Plug into master equation**
$$Q = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

Compute the term under the square root first:

$$\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho} = \frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{995.7} = 20.086 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\sqrt{20.086} = 4.482 \text{ m/s}$$

Now multiply:

$$Q = 0.62 \times 1.0328 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.482$$

$$Q = 5.62 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.62 \text{ L/s}$$

**(b) Mass flow rate**
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 995.7 \times 5.62 \times 10^{-3} = 5.60 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(c) Permanent pressure loss**
$$h_{perm} \approx (1 - \beta^2) \cdot \Delta P = (1 - 0.25) \times 10 \text{ kPa} = 7.5 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *approximate* permanent loss; ISO 5167-2 provides a more accurate empirical form using $C_d$ and a downstream pressure-recovery coefficient, but the $(1-\beta^2)\Delta P$ form is universally accepted for KTU 2024 scheme.

## 3.4 Reverse-Sizing (Determining Bore Diameter for a Required Flow)

Given: $Q = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$, $D = 200 \text{ mm}$, $\Delta P_{max} = 25 \text{ kPa}$, $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Find: bore diameter d.

**Rearrange the master equation:**

$$A_2 = \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot E_v} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{2 \Delta P}}$$

Because $E_v$ depends on $\beta$ which depends on $d$, this is solved **iteratively**. A typical KTU procedure:

1. **Assume** $E_v \approx 1.0$ (first guess, valid for $\beta < 0.5$).
2. Compute $A_2$ from the rearranged equation.
3. Convert $A_2$ to $d$ and compute a new $\beta$.
4. Recompute $E_v$ and repeat until $d$ converges (usually 2–3 iterations).

This **iterative reverse sizing** is a frequent 14-mark question in KTU Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Orifice Assembly — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Pipe Internal Diameter D] --> B[Orifice Plate Concentric Sharp Edged]
    B --> C[Bore Diameter d]
    C --> D[Beta Ratio Calculation beta equals d over D]
    D --> E[Velocity of Approach Factor Ev equals 1 over sqrt 1 minus beta to the 4]
    E --> F[Upstream Pressure Tapping P1]
    E --> G[Downstream Pressure Tapping P2 at Vena Contracta]
    F --> H[Differential Pressure Transmitter DP Cell]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Square Root Extraction]
    I --> J[Volumetric Flow Q]
    J --> K[Flow Totalizer]
    J --> L[Flow Controller Output]
    L --> M[Control Valve Final Element]
    H --> N[Indicator and Recorder]
```

## 4.2 Tap-Location Decision Flow (Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Choose Tap Configuration] --> B{Pipe Diameter}
    B -- D less than 50 mm --> C[Corner Taps]
    B -- 50 to 200 mm --> D[Flange Taps 25.4 mm offset]
    B -- D greater than 200 mm --> E[D and D by 2 Taps]
    C --> F[Validation per ISO 5167 2]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Fluid Type}
    G -- Liquid --> H[Use Incompressible Equation]
    G -- Gas or Steam --> I[Include Expansion Factor Y]
    H --> J[Final Orifice Bore Sizing]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Analytical-Solution Workflow (Detailed Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Statement: Find Q or d] --> B[Identify Fluid Type]
    B -- Liquid --> C[Set Y equals 1]
    B -- Gas --> D[Compute Y from Kappa and P1]
    C --> E[Compute Beta equals d over D]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Ev equals 1 over sqrt 1 minus beta to the 4]
    F --> G[Identify Given Cd value or use 0.62]
    G --> H{Is Q Required or d Required}
    H -- Q Required --> I[Apply Master Equation Q equals Cd Ev A2 sqrt 2 dP over rho]
    H -- d Required --> J[Iterative Reverse Sizing]
    I --> K[Compute Mass Flow m equals rho Q]
    J --> L[Verify Beta in 0.20 to 0.75]
    L --> M[Output Final Bore Diameter d]
    K --> N[Compute Permanent Pressure Loss]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Solution and Tabulation]
```

## 4.4 Annotation of the Vena Contracta

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upstream Section P1 High] --> B[Orifice Plate Constriction]
    B --> C[Vena Contracta P2 Minimum Cross Section]
    C --> D[Recovery Zone Partial Pressure Recovery]
    D --> E[Downstream Section P3 Slightly less than P1]
    B -.-> F[Delta P equals P1 minus P2 Measurable Signal]
    C -.-> F
    E -.-> G[Permanent Pressure Loss equals P1 minus P3]
    F --> H[DP Transmitter Senses]
    H --> I[Flow Computer]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the terms "beta ratio" and "velocity of approach factor" for an orifice meter. State their typical range of values as per ISO 5167-2.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Beta ratio (β):** The ratio of the orifice bore diameter *d* to the internal pipe diameter *D*:
$$\beta = \frac{d}{D}$$
Recommended range: **0.20 ≤ β ≤ 0.75** (ISO 5167-2).

- **Velocity of approach factor ($E_v$):** A correction factor that accounts for the fact that the upstream fluid velocity is not zero when the constriction is approached. It is defined as:
$$E_v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}$$
Typical value: **1.0 to 1.2** for the permissible β range. Each factor carries 1.5 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Why is the discharge coefficient $C_d$ introduced in the orifice flow equation, even though Bernoulli's equation is theoretically sufficient? State its typical value for a sharp-edged orifice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ideal Bernoulli-based equation assumes:
1. **Inviscid** (frictionless) flow.
2. The **vena contracta** has the *exact* geometric area $A_2 = \pi d^2/4$ as the bore.
3. The fluid **contracts** to a cross-section smaller than the bore due to stream-line curvature past the plate.

In reality, the jet contracts further past the bore, and viscous effects cause additional losses. A dimensionless **discharge coefficient $C_d$** corrects for both phenomena. For a **sharp-edged concentric orifice per ISO 5167-2**, $C_d$ typically lies in the range **0.60 to 0.62** depending on β and Reynolds number. (1 mark for each correct reason + 1 mark for typical value).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**A horizontal pipe of internal diameter 150 mm carries water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) at a flow rate of 30 m³/hr. A concentric sharp-edged orifice plate of bore diameter 75 mm is installed with flange taps. Assume $C_d = 0.62$.**

**(a)** Derive the orifice flow equation from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the differential pressure developed across the orifice, the mass flow rate, and the permanent pressure loss. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady, 1-D, incompressible, inviscid flow (no friction in the control volume).
2. Horizontal pipe (so $z_1 = z_2$, no elevation change).
3. Pressure tappings are at the upstream pipe and the **vena contracta**.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Continuity: $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ ⟹ $v_1 = \beta^2 v_2$ where $\beta = d/D$. — **(1 mark)**

Bernoulli (between section 1 and the vena contracta 2): $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$. — **(1 mark)**

Isolate differential head: $h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$. — **(1 mark)**

Substitute $v_1$: $h = \frac{v_2^2(1 - \beta^4)}{2g}$, so $v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}\sqrt{2gh}$. — **(1 mark)**

Multiply by real area with $C_d$: $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot E_v \cdot \sqrt{2gh} = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$. — **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 0.150$ m, $d = 0.075$ m, $Q = 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 30/3600 = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.62$.

**Step 1 — Beta ratio:** $\beta = 75/150 = 0.5$. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2 — Velocity-of-approach factor:**
$E_v = 1/\sqrt{1 - 0.5^4} = 1/\sqrt{0.9375} = 1.0328$. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 3 — Throat area:** $A_2 = \pi (0.075)^2 / 4 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}$ m². **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 4 — Solve for ΔP.** Rearranging:
$$\Delta P = \left(\frac{Q}{C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\rho}{2}$$

Compute numerator-coefficient:

$$\frac{Q}{C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{0.62 \times 1.0328 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{2.829 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.945 \text{ m/s}$$

Square it: $(2.945)^2 = 8.673 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$.

Multiply by $\rho/2 = 500$:

$$\Delta P = 8.673 \times 500 = 4336.5 \text{ Pa} = 4.336 \text{ kPa}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate:**
$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-3} = 8.333$ kg/s. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Permanent pressure loss:**
$h_{perm} \approx (1 - \beta^2) \Delta P = (1 - 0.25) \times 4.336 = 3.252$ kPa. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question B (Option 2 / Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Saturated steam (κ = 1.3) flows through a horizontal pipe of internal diameter 200 mm at an upstream pressure of 5 bar (gauge). The steam density is 2.5 kg/m³. An orifice plate is to be sized to handle a maximum mass flow of 5 kg/s with a maximum differential pressure not exceeding 0.5 bar. Assume $C_d = 0.61$.**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **expansion factor Y** and derive its simplified form for compressible flow. When is it taken as unity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the required bore diameter **d** of the orifice plate. Use an iterative procedure and verify that the final β lies in the ISO 5167 range. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

#### Part (a) — Expansion Factor Theory (7 marks)

For **incompressible fluids** (liquids), density remains constant as the fluid accelerates, so the Bernoulli-derived equation holds directly. For **compressible fluids** (gases, vapours), however, density drops as pressure drops at the throat, and the *actual* mass flow is *less* than what an incompressible formula would predict. **(2 marks)**

The **expansion factor Y** (also called *Y-factor* or *expansion coefficient*) is a dimensionless multiplier less than 1 that accounts for this density change. It is defined such that the real mass flow is:

$$\dot{m}_{real} = Y \cdot \dot{m}_{incompressible\ estimate}$$

For a **horizontally-mounted, sharp-edged concentric orifice**, the simplified ISO 5167-1 / Stolz form is:

$$Y = 1 - (0.41 + 0.35 \beta^4) \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\kappa \cdot P_1}$$

where **κ = $C_p/C_v$** is the isentropic exponent, $P_1$ is the upstream absolute pressure, and $\Delta P$ is the measured differential pressure. **(3 marks)**

Y is taken as **unity (Y = 1)** when the fluid is a **liquid**, OR when the differential pressure ratio $\Delta P / P_1$ is very small (< 0.02) for gases. **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Iterative Sizing (7 marks)

**Given:** $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/s, $\Delta P_{max} = 0.5$ bar = $5 \times 10^4$ Pa, $P_1 = 5$ bar (gauge) = 6 bar abs = $6 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\rho_1 = 2.5$ kg/m³, $\kappa = 1.3$, $C_d = 0.61$, $D = 0.200$ m.

**Iteration 1 — Assume $E_v = 1$ and estimate Y from an initial guess $\beta_0 = 0.6$ (typical).** **(1 mark)**

Initial Y estimate:
$$Y \approx 1 - (0.41 + 0.35 \times 0.6^4) \times \frac{0.5 \times 10^5}{1.3 \times 6 \times 10^5}$$
$$= 1 - (0.41 + 0.45 \times 0.1296) \times (0.0641)$$
$$= 1 - (0.41 + 0.0454) \times 0.0641$$
$$= 1 - 0.4554 \times 0.0641 = 1 - 0.0292 = 0.9708$$

**Step 2 — Compute required $A_2$ from:**

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot Y \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P} = \sqrt{2 \times 2.5 \times 5 \times 10^4} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^5} = 500 \text{ m/s}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\dot{m}}{C_d \cdot E_v \cdot Y \cdot 500} = \frac{5}{0.61 \times 1 \times 0.9708 \times 500}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{5}{296.1} = 1.689 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Convert to d:**
$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_2}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.689 \times 10^{-2}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{2.150 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1466 \text{ m} = 146.6 \text{ mm}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — New β:**
$$\beta = d/D = 146.6 / 200 = 0.733$$

**Verification (ISO 5167 range check):** $\beta = 0.733$ is **just inside** the upper bound of 0.75 ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Iteration 2 (refinement):** With $\beta = 0.733$:
$$E_v = 1/\sqrt{1 - 0.733^4} = 1/\sqrt{1 - 0.2886} = 1/\sqrt{0.7114} = 1.187$$

Recompute $A_2$:

$$A_2 = \frac{5}{0.61 \times 1.187 \times 0.9708 \times 500} = \frac{5}{351.6} = 1.422 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.422 \times 10^{-2}}{\pi}} = 0.1346 \text{ m} = 134.6 \text{ mm}$$

New β: $134.6 / 200 = 0.673$. The value is **stable** at β ≈ 0.67–0.73, so the design bore is **d ≈ 135 mm** (chosen standard). **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Orifice Sizing (ESE Module 3):**
> 1. **Forgetting $E_v$ (velocity-of-approach factor).** Always include $1/\sqrt{1-\beta^4}$. Marks lost: **1–2 per question**.
> 2. **Forgetting $Y$ for compressible fluids.** Steam and natural gas problems *demand* the expansion factor.
> 3. **Using downstream density** $\rho_2$ **instead of upstream $\rho_1$** in the compressible mass-flow equation.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion in N (flow coefficient).** The numerical constant depends on whether ΔP is in Pa, bar, or mm H₂O, and whether h is in m or mm.
> 5. **Not validating the β-ratio range** ($0.20 \le \beta \le 0.75$). An answer outside this range is **non-standard** and will attract 0 marks on the final iteration.
> 6. **Permanent pressure loss formula confusion.** Use $(1 - \beta^2) \cdot \Delta P$ — *not* $(1 - \beta^4) \cdot \Delta P$.
> 7. **Confusing $C_d$ with $C$ (flow coefficient).** $C$ in the practical formula already *includes* $C_d$, $E_v$, and $A_2$ in a single number — don't multiply by $C_d$ again.
> 8. **Skipping assumptions in the derivation.** Examiners award 1–2 marks *solely* for clearly listing Bernoulli/continuity assumptions (steady, incompressible/inviscid, horizontal, vena contracta location).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **orifice meter** is the most common **differential-pressure (DP) type primary flow element**.
- ✅ Master equation (incompressible): $\;Q = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}\;$.
- ✅ Master equation (compressible): $\;\dot{m} = C_d \cdot E_v \cdot Y \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P}\;$.
- ✅ **Beta ratio** $\beta = d/D$; standard range $0.20$ to $0.75$.
- ✅ **Velocity-of-approach factor** $E_v = 1 / \sqrt{1 - \beta^4}$ — always > 1.
- ✅ **Discharge coefficient** $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.62$ for sharp-edged ISO 5167-2 orifice.
- ✅ **Expansion factor Y** is < 1 for gases; = 1 for liquids.
- ✅ **Flange taps** (25.4 mm offset) are the *default* choice; corner taps for small pipes; D–D/2 taps for large pipes.
- ✅ **Vena contracta**: point of minimum cross-section and minimum pressure, located ~0.35D to 0.8D downstream of the plate (β-dependent).
- ✅ **Permanent pressure loss** ≈ $(1 - \beta^2) \cdot \Delta P$ — this is the *unrecovered* head lost to turbulence.
- ✅ **Turndown** is limited to **4:1** because the output is proportional to $\sqrt{\Delta P}$.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** must be ≥ $10^4$ for ISO 5167-2 to apply.
- ✅ **Tap placement matters**: 1 mark is reserved for selecting the *correct* tap configuration in design problems.
- ✅ **Reverse sizing** (finding d for a given Q) requires an **iterative** approach — typically 2 iterations converge.
- ✅ The **Stolz equation** gives $C_d$ as a function of β and $Re_D$; KTU problems typically *supply* a constant $C_d$ value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Venturi and Flow Nozzle Sizing - Liquid, Gas, and Steam Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Venturi and Flow Nozzle Sizing - Liquid, Gas, and Steam Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Differential Pressure (DP) Flow Element** is a primary flow measurement device in which a known obstruction (Venturi, flow nozzle, or orifice) is introduced into the flow stream, creating a measurable pressure drop $\Delta P$ that is mathematically related to the flow rate through Bernoulli's principle and the continuity equation.

- A **Venturi Meter** is a converging–diverging device with a smoothly contoured throat that minimizes turbulence and head loss. It is characterized by high discharge coefficient ($C_d \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$) and low permanent pressure loss (typically 10–15% of the differential pressure).
- A **Flow Nozzle** is a high-velocity convergent device with an elliptical or radius-contoured inlet. It is more compact than a Venturi and is specifically used for high-temperature, high-pressure services (e.g., superheated steam).

**Sizing** is the iterative engineering procedure by which the beta ratio ($\beta = d/D$), throat diameter $d$, and differential pressure $\Delta P$ are selected such that the flowmeter accurately measures the full process flow range (turndown ratio) while remaining within the Reynolds number validity envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Sizing of Venturi and Flow Nozzle is a core outcome of PEAET795. The KTU valuation key awards full marks only when the student demonstrates correct selection of fluid properties, beta ratio bounds, and Reynolds number iteration.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine a wide river flowing peacefully into a **narrow gorge** and then back into a wide channel.

- The water in the gorge **shoots through faster** because the same amount of water must pass every second (continuity).
- This increased velocity creates a **drop in pressure** at the gorge (Bernoulli).
- The wider the river upstream ($D$) and the narrower the gorge ($d$), the more dramatic the pressure drop for a given flow rate.
- Now imagine measuring how fast the water shoots through the gorge — that is essentially what a Venturi or Flow Nozzle does to a fluid in a pipe.

**In one line:** *The fluid is forced through a constriction; the velocity goes up, the pressure goes down, and we use that pressure drop to calculate how much is flowing.*

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Reference:** **ISO 5167-3** (Venturi) and **ISO 5167-3 / ISO 5167-4** (Flow Nozzle) prescribe geometry, tap locations, and $C_d$ correlations.
> - **Beta ratio ($\beta$):** The single most important design variable in DP flowmeter sizing.
> - **Discharge coefficient ($C_d$):** An empirical correction that accounts for non-ideal flow (viscous losses, turbulence, boundary layer effects).
> - **Expansion factor ($Y$):** Required for gases and steam to correct for compressibility effects during expansion across the device.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Process Fluid Enters** the inlet section of diameter $D$ with upstream pressure $P_1$ and velocity $v_1$.
2. **Converging Section** smoothly accelerates the flow toward the throat of diameter $d$ (Venturi) or nozzle exit.
3. **Throat / Vena Contracta** is reached: velocity $v_2$ is maximum and pressure $P_2$ is minimum.
4. **Pressure Taps** (located at the inlet and the throat) register the differential pressure $h = P_1 - P_2$ (or $\Delta P$).
5. **Diverging Section (Venturi only)** gradually recovers a large portion of the kinetic energy, lowering permanent pressure loss.
6. **DP Transmitter** measures $\Delta P$ and converts to flow via a square-root extractor (or in modern digital systems, via software).

**The Why behind the steps:**

- Why a *throat*? To create a fixed, known contraction with a precise area $A_2$.
- Why *pressure taps*? Because $v_2 = f(\Delta P)$ is calculable if we know the geometry.
- Why a *diverging cone*? To recover pressure (Venturi advantage over orifice and nozzle).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Beta ratio | $\beta = d/D$ | dimensionless | Typical: $0.30 \le \beta \le 0.75$ |
| 2 | Area ratio | $A_2/A_1 = \beta^2$ | dimensionless | — |
| 3 | General mass flow | $\dot{m} = C_d \, A_2 \, Y \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \rho_1 \, \Delta P}{1-\beta^4}}$ | **kg/s** | Master equation |
| 4 | General volumetric flow (liquid) | $Q = C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho \, (1-\beta^4)}}$ | **m³/s** | $Y = 1$ for liquids |
| 5 | Discharge coeff. (As-cast Venturi) | $C_d \approx 0.984$ | dimensionless | Constant for $\text{Re} > 2 \times 10^5$ |
| 6 | Discharge coeff. (Machined Venturi) | $C_d \approx 0.995$ | dimensionless | Constant for $\text{Re} > 10^6$ |
| 7 | $C_d$ for ASME Flow Nozzle (high Re) | $C_d = 0.9900 - 0.2262 \, \beta^{4.1}$ | dimensionless | Valid $\text{Re}_D > 7 \times 10^4$ |
| 8 | $C_d$ for ISA 1932 Nozzle (low Re) | $C_d = 0.9900 - 0.2262 \, \beta^{4.1} - \dfrac{0.00175 \beta^{4.1} - 0.0033}{10^6/\text{Re}_D}$ | dimensionless | Valid for lower Re |
| 9 | Expansion factor $Y$ (Nozzle) | $Y = 1 - (0.333 + 1.145\beta^4 - 0.5185\beta^8)(1 - P_2/P_1)$ | dimensionless | Subsonic; $P_2/P_1 \ge 0.75$ |
| 10 | Expansion factor $Y$ (Venturi) | $Y \approx 1$ (often taken) | dimensionless | More accurate: $Y = 1 - (0.25 + 0.5\beta^4)(1 - P_2/P_1)$ |
| 11 | Reynolds number (pipe) | $\text{Re}_D = \dfrac{4 \, \dot{m}}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$ | dimensionless | General form |
| 12 | Reynolds number (volumetric) | $\text{Re}_D = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Liquid: $D$ is pipe ID |
| 13 | Gas density (ideal) | $\rho_1 = \dfrac{P_1 \, M}{Z \, R_u \, T_1}$ | **kg/m³** | $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(kmol·K)** |
| 14 | Permanent pressure loss | $\Delta \omega_{\text{Venturi}} \approx 0.10\,\Delta P$ | **Pa** | Venturi |
| 15 | Permanent pressure loss | $\Delta \omega_{\text{Nozzle}} \approx 0.30\,\Delta P$ | **Pa** | Nozzle (no recovery cone) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In real practice, $\Delta P$ should be limited to **≤ 25% of the available line pressure** for gases and steam, and **≤ 100 kPa** for liquids — this prevents excessive pumping cost and process disturbance.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Application | Element Used |
|----------|-------------|--------------|
| Power Plants | Boiler feedwater, condensate, superheated steam | Venturi (feedwater) / Nozzle (steam) |
| Oil & Gas | Custody transfer of natural gas | Flow Nozzle (high P, high T) |
| Chemical | Corrosive liquid services in large lines | Venturi (low wear) |
| HVAC & Water | District cooling/heating | Venturi (low ΔP recovery) |
| Refineries | Crude oil, hot residue streams | Flow Nozzle (compact) |
