3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — analogue time-division multiplexing of $N$ channels using CMOS switches such as **CD4051 (8:1)**, **CD4067 (16:1)**, or **ADG726 (32:1)**.
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — holds the multiplexed analogue level stable during conversion (LF398, AD783).
5. **Analogue-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts the held voltage to digital code. SAR ADCs (ADS1256, AD7606) dominate; sigma-delta ADCs (AD7195) dominate precision channels.
6. **Microcontroller / Processor (µC / µP / DSP)** — orchestrates timing, time-stamping, scaling engineering units, and the storage algorithm.
7. **Real-Time Clock (RTC) (DS3231, PCF8523)** — provides time-stamp; with battery backup.
8. **Non-Volatile Memory** — stores the digitised samples: EEPROM (small), Flash (large), SD card (multi-GB).
9. **Communication Interface** — UART, SPI, I²C, USB, Ethernet, RS-485 (Modbus), Wi-Fi/BLE for IoT.
10. **Display & Keypad** — local HMI (LCD 16×2, OLED, TFT touch).
11. **Power Supply** — battery, mains, energy-harvesting, PoE.

### 2.1.2 Key Design Parameters

- **Number of channels** $N$ — driven by application (8, 16, 32, 64 are common).
- **Sampling rate per channel** $f_s$ — must satisfy the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Resolution** $n$ bits of ADC: $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$.
- **Total throughput** $= N \cdot f_s \cdot n$ (bits/s).
- **Storage requirement** $S = N \cdot f_s \cdot t_{record} \cdot \dfrac{n}{8}$ bytes.
- **Dynamic range** $= 6.02 n + 1.76 \text{ dB (ENOB based, theoretical)}$.
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** — must exceed **80 dB** for industrial use.
- **Anti-alias filter** — analogue LPF, $-3 \text{ dB}$ cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ (typically $0.4 f_s$).

### 2.1.3 Classification of Data Loggers

| Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Stand-alone (handheld)** | Battery powered, internal memory, PC dump | Onset HOBO, Fluke 2638A |
| **Modular / DAS** | Plug-in modules, PC/ethernet backplane | NI CompactDAQ, Keysight 34970A |
| **PC-based (plug-in card)** | DAQ card inside PC, software controlled | NI PCIe-6363 |
| **Networked / IoT** | Ethernet / Wi-Fi / MQTT / cloud | Custom ESP32 + ThingSpeak |
| **Web-enabled (Ethernet)** | HTTP/SCADA server on-board | Advantech ADAM-6017 |

## 2.2 PID Controller — Theoretical Formulation

### 2.2.1 Continuous-Time PID

The controller computes the control signal $u(t)$ from the error $e(t) = SP - PV$:

$$u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

The corresponding **transfer function** is:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

where the gain parameters are interrelated as $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ and $K_d = K_p \, T_d$.

### 2.2.2 Physical Role of Each Term

- **Proportional ($K_p$)** — Provides control action proportional to the *instantaneous* error. Raises the closed-loop bandwidth. A pure P-controller always leaves a residual **steady-state offset** (for type-0 plants).
- **Integral ($K_i$ or $T_i$)** — Eliminates the *steady-state error* by integrating past errors. Slows the response; introduces phase lag of $-90°$.
- **Derivative ($K_d$ or $T_d$)** — Predicts the future by reacting to the *rate of change* of the error. Adds phase lead, improves stability margins, and reduces overshoot. Cannot act on a constant error.

### 2.2.3 Discrete-Time PID (for µC Implementation)

The trapezoidal (Tustin) discretisation of the continuous PID yields the **velocity (incremental) form** that KTU examinations frequently ask:

$$\Delta u[k] = K_p \left\{ (e[k] - e[k-1]) + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e[k] + \frac{T_d}{T_s} (e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]) \right\}$$

with $u[k] = u[k-1] + \Delta u[k]$, and $T_s$ being the sampling period.

> [!NOTE]
> The velocity form is **bumpless** — if the controller is switched from manual to auto, the actuator does not "jump".

### 2.2.4 Ziegler–Nichols Tuning Methods

| Method | What to find | Type of plant | Output Equations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First (Open-loop step response)** | Process gain $K$, dead-time $L$, time constant $T$ | First-order + dead time $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-Ls}}{Ts+1}$ | $K_p = \dfrac{1.2 \, T}{K L}$, $T_i = 2 L$, $T_d = \dfrac{L}{2}$ |
| **Second (Ultimate-cycle)** | Ultimate gain $K_u$ and period $P_u$ | Any closed-loop-stable plant | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = \dfrac{P_u}{2}$, $T_d = \dfrac{P_u}{8}$ |

### 2.2.5 Anti-Windup (Back-Calculation)

If the actuator saturates, the integrator keeps accumulating error → **integrator wind-up** → huge overshoot on de-saturation. The back-calculation correction is:

$$\frac{dx_i}{dt} = K_i \, e(t) + \frac{1}{T_t} ( u_{raw} - u_{sat} )$$

where $T_t$ is the **tracking time constant**, often set to $T_t = \sqrt{T_i T_d}$ (Aström–Hägglund recommendation).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **only consolidated reference** needed for solving 3-mark and 14-mark questions on this topic in the KTU ESE 2024 Scheme. Memorise the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 2 | ADC Quantisation Step | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volt |
| 3 | Theoretical Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 4 | Data Storage | $S = N \cdot f_s \cdot t_{rec} \cdot \dfrac{n}{8}$ | bytes |
| 5 | Throughput | $R = N \cdot f_s \cdot n$ | bits/s |
| 6 | Anti-alias Filter Cutoff | $f_c \leq 0.4 \, f_s$ | Hz |
| 7 | Continuous PID | $u(t) = K_p e + K_p \dfrac{1}{T_i} \int e \, dt + K_p T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | — |
| 8 | PID Transfer Function | $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | — |
| 9 | Error Signal | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | — |
| 10 | Discrete (Velocity) PID | $\Delta u[k] = K_p \left( \Delta e[k] + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i} e[k] + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s} \Delta^2 e[k] \right)$ | — |
| 11 | Z-N First (PID) | $K_p = \dfrac{1.2 T}{K L}$, $T_i = 2 L$, $T_d = L/2$ | Open-loop step response |
| 12 | Z-N First (PI) | $K_p = \dfrac{0.9 T}{K L}$, $T_i = 3 L$ | Open-loop step response |
| 13 | Z-N First (P) | $K_p = \dfrac{T}{K L}$ | Open-loop step response |
| 14 | Z-N Second (PID) | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = P_u/2$, $T_d = P_u/8$ | Closed-loop ultimate cycle |
| 15 | Z-N Second (PI) | $K_p = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = P_u/1.2$ | Closed-loop ultimate cycle |
| 16 | Z-N Second (P) | $K_p = 0.5 K_u$ | Closed-loop ultimate cycle |
| 17 | Back-Calc Anti-Windup | $\dot{x}_i = K_i e + \dfrac{1}{T_t}(u_{raw} - u_{sat})$ | $T_t = \sqrt{T_i T_d}$ |
| 18 | IAE / ISE Performance Index | $J = \int_0^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ or $\int_0^{\infty} e^2(t) \, dt$ | Min → best tuning |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Loggers**: pharmaceutical cold-chain monitoring, structural health monitoring of bridges (STRAIN Gauges + wireless), wind-tunnel data acquisition, satellite telemetry, electric vehicle battery thermal monitoring.
- **PID Controllers**: every temperature controller (PID + SCR/TRIAC), every DC motor speed control (PID + PWM + H-bridge), every robotic joint position control, every level/flow control loop in process plants, every drone altitude hold, every ABS/EESC system in vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Storage Capacity of a Data Logger

### Problem Statement (KTU-style)

> *Design a stand-alone data logger for monitoring **16 thermocouple channels** in a furnace. Each channel is sampled at **10 Hz**, the ADC resolution is **16 bits**, and the logger must record continuously for **30 days**. Compute the total storage required in MB.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$N = 16, \quad f_s = 10 \text{ Hz}, \quad n = 16 \text{ bits}, \quad t_{rec} = 30 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2 — Convert time to seconds for SI consistency.**

$$t_{rec} = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2\,592\,000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Total number of samples collected per channel.**

$$S_c = f_s \cdot t_{rec} = 10 \times 2\,592\,000 = 2.592 \times 10^7 \text{ samples / channel}$$

**Step 4 — Total samples across all channels.**

$$S_{tot} = N \cdot S_c = 16 \times 2.592 \times 10^7 = 4.1472 \times 10^8 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 5 — Total bits.**

$$B_{tot} = S_{tot} \cdot n = 4.1472 \times 10^8 \times 16 = 6.6355 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 6 — Convert bits to bytes.**

$$S_{bytes} = \frac{B_{tot}}{8} = 8.2944 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes} \approx 829.4 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 7 — Conclude with engineering margin.**

$$S_{req} \approx 1 \text{ GB (with 20 \% header/metadata + log file overhead)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The same derivation is used by KTU examiners for *design-type* 14-mark questions. The pattern is always: **Count channels → Multiply by sample rate × time → Multiply by bits → Divide by 8**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Continuous PID Transfer Function from Time-Domain Equation

### Starting Point

The continuous-time PID control law in the time domain is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_p \, T_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform of each term.**

Using the standard transforms $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$, and $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = s E(s)$:

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + \frac{K_p}{T_i} \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_p \, T_d \cdot s \, E(s)$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $K_p \, E(s)$.**

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$$

**Step 3 — Write the transfer function.**

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $T_i s$ to expose the polynomial form.**

$$C(s) = \frac{K_p}{T_i s} \left[ T_i s + 1 + T_i T_d s^2 \right] = \frac{K_p T_d s^2 + K_p s + \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}}{s}$$

**Step 5 — Re-define in terms of $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$.**

Let $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ and $K_d = K_p T_d$:

$$C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

This is the **standard parallel PID form** universally written in the KTU textbook and used in 90 % of design problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ziegler–Nichols First-Method Tuning Formula (Open-Loop Step Response)

### Plant Model (FOPDT — First Order Plus Dead Time)

A wide class of stable, monotonic, non-oscillating plants can be approximated near an operating point as:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-L s}}{T s + 1}$$

The parameters $K$ (steady-state gain), $L$ (dead time / transport delay), and $T$ (time constant) are obtained by drawing a tangent at the steepest point of the S-shaped open-loop step response and noting where it crosses the time axis and the final value line (Cohen–Coon graphical technique).

### Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation with PID

With the controller $C(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right)$, the open-loop transfer function is:

$$C(s) G_p(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right) \frac{K \, e^{-L s}}{T s + 1}$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + C(s) G_p(s) = 0$$

Ziegler & Nichols empirically chose $K_p, T_i, T_d$ such that the **decay ratio** (ratio of successive peaks) is **1/4** for a step input. The closed-form results of that empirical fit are:

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \, T}{K \, L}, \quad T_i = 2 L, \quad T_d = \frac{L}{2}$$

For a **PI** controller only (set $T_d = 0$): $K_p = \dfrac{0.9 \, T}{K L}$, $T_i = 3 L$.
For a **P** controller only: $K_p = \dfrac{T}{K L}$.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examinations do not ask for the *empirical proof* of Z-N. They ask: *"Given K, L, T of a plant, calculate the PID parameters using Z-N first method"* — which is a direct substitution into the table above.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Z-N First Method (End-to-End)

> *The open-loop step response of a heating tank yields the FOPDT parameters $K = 2.5 \; ^\circ\text{C}/\%$, $T = 180 \text{ s}$, and $L = 25 \text{ s}$. Design a PID controller using the Ziegler–Nichols first method.*

**Step 1 — Compute proportional gain.**

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \, T}{K \, L} = \frac{1.2 \times 180}{2.5 \times 25} = \frac{216}{62.5} = 3.456 \; \% / ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute integral time.**

$$T_i = 2 L = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute derivative time.**

$$T_d = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to alternate $K_i$, $K_d$ form (often required by the exam).**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{3.456}{50} = 0.06912 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_p T_d = 3.456 \times 12.5 = 43.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — State the resulting controller.**

$$C(s) = 3.456 + \frac{0.06912}{s} + 43.2 \, s$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of PID Form vs. Application

| Industrial Application | Recommended Form | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature control (furnace, oven) | **PI** or **PID with low $K_d$** | Slow process, integrator needed, derivative noisy |
| DC motor speed | **PI** | Speed has low noise, integral removes steady-state error |
| Servo position control (robotics) | **PD or PID** | Derivative is critical for damping the inertia |
| Level control (tank) | **PI** | Integrating process — even P is enough, I fine-tunes |
| Flow control | **PI** (high $K_p$, low $T_i$) | Fast process, needs high gain |
| Pressure control | **PID** | Often has resonance, derivative damps it |
| pH control | **PI with gain scheduling** | Highly non-linear, PID alone is poor |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Discrete Velocity PID Update

> *A discrete PID is implemented with $K_p = 4$, $T_i = 10 \text{ s}$, $T_d = 2 \text{ s}$, sampling period $T_s = 0.1 \text{ s}$. The error sequence (in arbitrary units) is $e[0]=0$, $e[1]=0.5$, $e[2]=0.8$, $e[3]=0.6$. Compute the incremental output $\Delta u[3]$ and the cumulative output $u[3]$, assuming $u[0] = u[1] = u[2] = 0$.*

**Step 1 — Differences.**

$$\Delta e[3] = e[3] - e[2] = 0.6 - 0.8 = -0.2$$

$$\Delta^2 e[3] = e[3] - 2 e[2] + e[1] = 0.6 - 1.6 + 0.5 = -0.5$$

**Step 2 — Discrete (velocity) PID formula.**

$$\Delta u[3] = K_p \left[ \Delta e[3] + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e[3] + \frac{T_d}{T_s} \Delta^2 e[3] \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numbers.**

$$\frac{T_s}{T_i} = \frac{0.1}{10} = 0.01, \quad \frac{T_d}{T_s} = \frac{2}{0.1} = 20$$

$$\Delta u[3] = 4 \left[ -0.2 + 0.01 \times 0.6 + 20 \times (-0.5) \right]$$

$$\Delta u[3] = 4 \left[ -0.2 + 0.006 - 10.0 \right] = 4 \times (-10.194) = -40.776$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative output.**

$$u[3] = u[2] + \Delta u[3] = 0 + (-40.776) = -40.776$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exam will usually set $u[0] = 0$ and $e[0] = 0$. Always re-state the velocity formula *before* substituting, because writing the formula on its own line carries 2 of the 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Multi-Channel Data Logger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensArray[Sensor Array N Channels] --> scu[Signal Conditioning Amplification Filter Linearisation]
    scu --> mux[Analog Multiplexer 8 16 32 to 1]
    mux --> sh[Sample and Hold]
    sh --> adc[ADC 12 16 24 bit]
    adc[ADC 12 16 24 bit] --> mcu[Microcontroller or DSP Time Stamp Scaling]
    rtc[Real Time Clock DS3231] --> mcu
    mcu --> mem[Non Volatile Memory Flash SD Card]
    mcu --> lcd[Display LCD OLED TFT]
    mcu --> com[Comms UART USB Ethernet Modbus Wi Fi]
    psu[Power Supply Battery Mains PoE] --> mcu
    psu --> scu
    psu --> mux
    psu --> adc
    psu --> rtc
```

**Reading guide (matches the labels in the Mermaid block above):**

- $N$ analogue channels enter the **Signal Conditioning Unit (SCU)** which performs amplification, isolation, cold-junction compensation and a **low-pass anti-alias filter** ($f_c \leq 0.4 f_s$).
- The conditioned signals are time-multiplexed by an $N:1$ CMOS MUX.
- A sample-and-hold holds the chosen channel steady while the ADC performs conversion.
- A real-time clock tags every sample with a wall-clock time.
- The microcontroller (e.g., STM32, MSP430, PIC, ESP32) performs scaling to engineering units, then writes the value to **non-volatile memory** and refreshes the local display.
- Communication block sends data upstream via UART/USB/Ethernet/Modbus/Wi-Fi.

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop PID Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp[Setpoint SP] --> sum[Summer]
    pv[Process Variable PV from sensor] --> sum
    sum --> err[Error e equals SP minus PV]
    err --> pid[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    pid --> act[Actuator Valve Heater Motor]
    act --> plant[Plant Furnance Tank Motor]
    plant --> pvSensor[Sensor Transducer]
    pvSensor --> pv
    pid -. feedback .- sum
```

**Reading guide:**

- The **summer** computes $e(t) = SP - PV$ (the error).
- The **PID controller** implements $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
- The actuator (valve / SCR / PWM + H-bridge) drives the plant.
- The sensor (RTD / LVDT / encoder) closes the loop by feeding $PV$ back.
- The dashed feedback line shows that the controller is *inside* the feedback path, i.e., the architecture is **unity-feedback with $C(s)$ in the forward path**.

## 4.3 Ziegler–Nichols Tuning Flowchart (Combined)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Choose Method] --> q1{Plant is open loop stable and monotonic}
    q1 -- yes --> method1[Use Z N First Method]
    q1 -- no --> method2[Use Z N Second Method]
    method1 --> step1[Apply unit step to open loop plant]
    step1 --> step2[Record S shaped response]
    step2 --> step3[Draw tangent at inflection point]
    step3 --> step4[Read K T L from step response]
    step4 --> step5[Apply PID table Kp 1.2T over KL Ti 2L Td L over 2]
    method2 --> cycle1[Close loop with pure P controller]
    cycle1 --> cycle2[Increase Kp until sustained oscillation]
    cycle2 --> cycle3[Record ultimate gain Ku and period Pu]
    cycle3 --> step6[Apply PID table Kp 0.6 Ku Ti Pu over 2 Td Pu over 8]
    step5 --> sim[Simulate closed loop in MATLAB or Python]
    step6 --> sim
    sim --> check{Performance meets specs}
    check -- no --> retune[Adjust Kp Ki Kd manually]
    check -- yes --> deploy[Deploy to real plant]
    retune --> sim
```

**Reading guide:**

- Use the **first method** when the plant is open-loop stable, monotonic, and S-shaped.
- Use the **second method** when the plant has inverse response, integrator, or is unstable in open loop.
- Always **simulate the tuned loop** (MATLAB/Simulink, or SciPy) before deploying to the physical plant.
- If performance is poor, *fine-tune* manually (decrease $K_p$ for less overshoot, increase $T_i$ to reduce sluggishness, decrease $T_d$ if too noisy).

## 4.4 Anti-Windup Back-Calculation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e[Error e] --> int[Int Integrator]
    int --> sum2[Summer]
    p[P Path] --> sum2
    d[D Path] --> sum2
    sum2 --> uRaw[Raw Control u raw]
    uRaw --> sat[Saturation block u min to u max]
    sat --> uAct[Actuator u sat]
    uRaw --> diff[Subtract u sat from u raw]
    diff --> gain[Gain 1 over Tt]
    gain --> int
```

**Reading guide:**

- The integrator accumulates $K_i e(t)$.
- When $u_{raw}$ exceeds the actuator limits, a **tracking signal** $(u_{raw} - u_{sat})/T_t$ is fed *back into* the integrator to **bleed it off** at rate $1/T_t$.
- This prevents the integrator from winding up to a huge value during saturation, eliminating the classic "huge overshoot after de-saturation" pathology.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q1.** What is a *data logger*? List the **four major functional blocks** of a digital data logger.

**Model Answer (precise, examiner-friendly, 3 marks):**

A data logger is an **electronic instrument used to record measurements from one or more sensors over a period of time**, either as a stand-alone device or as a subsystem of a SCADA system.

The four major functional blocks are:

1. **Sensor / Transducer and Signal Conditioning Unit** — converts the physical quantity into a clean, amplified, isolated, anti-aliased voltage.
2. **Analogue Front-End** — multiplexer, sample-and-hold, and ADC that convert the conditioned signal to a digital code.
3. **Digital Controller / Microcontroller** — applies time-stamping, scaling to engineering units, storage algorithm and HMI logic.
4. **Memory and Communication Subsystem** — non-volatile memory for data storage, plus communication interfaces (UART, USB, Ethernet, Modbus) for data retrieval.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q2.** State the **continuous-time transfer function of a PID controller** and explain in one line each the **role of the proportional, integral and derivative terms**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The PID controller transfer function is:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

- **Proportional ($K_p$)** — produces a control action proportional to the *instantaneous error*; raises the closed-loop bandwidth, but leaves a steady-state offset.
- **Integral ($K_i$ or $T_i$)** — accumulates past errors and removes the *steady-state offset*, at the cost of slower response.
- **Derivative ($K_d$ or $T_d$)** — acts on the *rate of change* of the error; provides phase lead, improves damping, and reduces overshoot.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Always **write the governing formula** (e.g., storage $= N f_s t n/8$) before substituting numbers. Skipping the formula costs **2 of the 7 marks per sub-part** even if the answer is numerically correct.
> 2. For PID tuning, **explicitly state which Z-N method** (First or Second) is being used — the constants differ. Wrong constants = 0 marks for that sub-part.
> 3. Units must be consistent (convert minutes to seconds, mA to A). KTU strictly deducts for unit mismatch.
> 4. For discrete PID, label your $e[k]$, $e[k-1]$, $e[k-2]$ indices clearly; examiners spot careless index mistakes immediately.

---

### PART B — Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q. A** (a) Design a data logger to monitor **8 RTD (PT-100) channels** in a process plant. Each channel is sampled at **20 Hz**, the ADC is **12-bit**, the recording must continue for **7 days** continuously. The data is to be stored in an SD card with a **16-byte header per record**. Compute the total storage requirement. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Explain the **Ziegler–Nichols first method** of PID tuning. A process is modelled as $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1.8 e^{-12s}}{60 s + 1}$. Determine the PID parameters using this method. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Q.A (a)  (7 marks)

**Step 1 — List the parameters.**
$N = 8$, $f_s = 20$ Hz, $n = 12$ bits, $t_{rec} = 7 \text{ days}$.

**Step 2 — Convert time to seconds.**
$$t_{rec} = 7 \times 24 \times 3600 = 604\,800 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Total samples.**
$$S_{tot} = N \cdot f_s \cdot t_{rec} = 8 \times 20 \times 604\,800 = 9.6768 \times 10^7 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 4 — Raw data volume (bits).**
$$B = S_{tot} \times n = 9.6768 \times 10^7 \times 12 = 1.1612 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Raw data in bytes.**
$$D_{raw} = \frac{B}{8} = 1.4515 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes} \approx 145.15 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 6 — Header overhead.** 16 bytes per record:
$$D_{hdr} = S_{tot} \times 16 = 1.5483 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.548 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 7 — Total storage.**
$$D_{tot} = D_{raw} + D_{hdr} \approx 1.694 \text{ GB} \rightarrow \text{use a } 2 \text{ GB SD card.}$$

### Model Solution — Q.A (b)  (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Theory of Z-N first method (3 marks).**
The first method is an **open-loop tuning** procedure used for *first-order plus dead-time* (FOPDT) plants of the form $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-Ls}}{T s + 1}$. A unit step is applied to the plant input, and the *S-shaped* response is recorded. A tangent is drawn at the point of inflection; the intercept on the time axis gives the **dead time $L$**, and the projection on the steady-state line gives the **time constant $T$**. The plant gain $K$ is the ratio of the final output to the magnitude of the step input. The PID parameters are then:

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 T}{K L}, \quad T_i = 2 L, \quad T_d = \frac{L}{2}$$

This is empirical, designed to give a closed-loop step response with a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio**.

**Step 2 — Identify parameters of the given plant (1 mark).**
$$K = 1.8, \quad L = 12 \text{ s}, \quad T = 60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_p$ (1 mark).**
$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \times 60}{1.8 \times 12} = \frac{72}{21.6} = 3.333$$

**Step 4 — Compute $T_i$ and $T_d$ (1 mark each).**
$$T_i = 2 L = 24 \text{ s}, \quad T_d = \frac{L}{2} = 6 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Final controller (1 mark).**
$$C(s) = 3.333 + \frac{0.1389}{s} + 20.0 \, s$$

---

### PART B — Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q. B** (a) With a **neat block diagram**, describe the architecture of a **multi-channel data acquisition system / data logger**. Explain the role of the **anti-aliasing filter** and state the **Nyquist criterion**. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Derive the **transfer function of a PID controller** starting from the time-domain equation. A position servo is found to oscillate **sustained** at a **proportional gain $K_u = 12$** and a **period of oscillation $P_u = 1.6 \text{ s}$**. Compute the PID parameters using the **Ziegler–Nichols second (ultimate cycle) method**. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Q.B (a)  (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Block diagram (3 marks).**

The block diagram should contain the following blocks in the *signal-flow sequence*:

- $N$ sensors → Signal Conditioning (amplification, isolation, linearisation) → **Anti-alias LPF** → Analogue MUX → Sample-and-Hold → ADC → µ-controller (time-stamp + scaling) → Non-Volatile Memory (Flash/SD) → Communication (USB/Ethernet) and Display.

(Examiner award: 1 mark for each major block, 1 mark for arrow direction, 1 mark for time-stamp / RTC being present.)

**Step 2 — Role of anti-aliasing filter (2 marks).**
Whenever a continuous signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz is sampled at $f_s$ Hz, any frequency component above $f_s/2$ will fold back into the baseband and corrupt the recorded data — this is **aliasing**. The anti-alias filter is a **low-pass analogue filter** placed *before* the ADC, with $-3 \text{ dB}$ cutoff $f_c \leq 0.4 f_s$ (typically a 2nd to 4th order Butterworth), so that out-of-band noise and high-frequency content are removed before sampling.

**Step 3 — Nyquist criterion (2 marks).**
The **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem** states:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency present in the input signal. The minimum sampling rate $2 f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**. Failure to satisfy this leads to *irrecoverable* aliasing.

### Model Solution — Q.B (b)  (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Derive transfer function of PID (3 marks).**

Start with the time-domain law:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_p T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Apply Laplace transform (using $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\{\int e\} = E(s)/s$, $\mathcal{L}\{de/dt\} = sE(s)$):

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_p}{T_i s} E(s) + K_p T_d s E(s)$$

Factor and divide:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

with $K_i = K_p/T_i$, $K_d = K_p T_d$.

**Step 2 — Identify Z-N second-method constants (1 mark).**
$K_u = 12$, $P_u = 1.6$ s.

**Step 3 — Apply Z-N second method formulas (2 marks).**
$$K_p = 0.6 K_u = 0.6 \times 12 = 7.2$$
$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{1.6}{2} = 0.8 \text{ s}$$
$$T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{1.6}{8} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute alternate gain form (1 mark).**
$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{7.2}{0.8} = 9.0 \text{ s}^{-1}, \quad K_d = K_p T_d = 7.2 \times 0.2 = 1.44 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Final controller (close the answer).**
$$C(s) = 7.2 + \frac{9.0}{s} + 1.44 \, s$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Data Logger & PID Controller (Module 3)**

- A **data logger** records sensor data over time; the **four core blocks** are Sensors+SCU, Analogue Front-End (MUX/S&H/ADC), Digital Controller, and Memory+Comms.
- **Anti-alias filter cutoff** must be $f_c \leq 0.4 f_s$ (Nyquist theorem: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$).
- **Storage formula**: $S = N f_s t_{rec} n/8$ bytes — always include header overhead and a safety margin.
- **ADC resolution** $n$ gives LSB $= V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ and theoretical dynamic range $= 6.02 n + 1.76$ dB.
- A **PID controller** computes $u(t)$ from $e(t) = SP - PV$ using three terms: $P$ (now), $I$ (past), $D$ (future).
- **Continuous PID**: $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s = K_p(1 + 1/(T_i s) + T_d s)$.
- **Discrete (velocity) PID**: $\Delta u[k] = K_p\left[\Delta e[k] + (T_s/T_i) e[k] + (T_d/T_s) \Delta^2 e[k]\right]$ — preferred for bumpless transfer.
- **Z-N First Method** (open-loop, FOPDT plants): $K_p = 1.2T/(KL)$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = L/2$.
- **Z-N Second Method** (closed-loop, ultimate cycle): $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = P_u/2$, $T_d = P_u/8$.
- **Anti-windup** is mandatory whenever actuator saturation is possible — back-calculation with $T_t = \sqrt{T_i T_d}$ is the standard fix.
- **Derivative kick** is avoided by computing derivative on $PV$ rather than on $e(t)$.
- For **temperature control** use PI/PID with low $K_d$; for **position control** use PD/PID; for **flow/level** use PI.
- Always **state formulas, units, and method** before numerical substitution in KTU 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of ON/OFF Controller Using Linear Integrated Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of ON/OFF Controller Using Linear Integrated Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ON/OFF Controller:** A two-position discontinuous control mode in which the controller output can assume only **two discrete states** — fully **ON** (typically saturated high, $\approx +V_{sat}$) or fully **OFF** (saturated low, $\approx -V_{sat}$), with no intermediate proportional action. The transition between states occurs when the error signal crosses a predefined threshold, making it the simplest and most widely deployed form of closed-loop control in industrial processes such as **temperature regulation, level switching, and limit detection**.

In a Linear Integrated Circuit (IC) implementation, the op-amp is configured as a **Schmitt Trigger** — a comparator with positive feedback — which introduces a deliberate **hysteresis band (dead zone)** to prevent rapid, damaging oscillations of the final control element (relay coil, solenoid, heater).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Home Geyser Switch"

Imagine a household water geyser. You set the thermostat to $50^{\circ}\text{C}$:
- When water temperature **falls below** a lower setpoint, the **heater turns ON** (full power).
- When water temperature **rises above** an upper setpoint, the **heater turns OFF** (zero power).
- The geyser never delivers "half power" — it is either fully heating or not heating at all.

The **gap between switch-ON and switch-OFF** temperatures (say $45^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $50^{\circ}\text{C}$) is the **hysteresis band**. Without it, the relay would chatter hundreds of times per second as temperature hovers around a single setpoint, destroying the contact in days. The op-amp Schmitt trigger plays the role of the thermostat — it makes the decision based on the difference between the sensor signal and the reference, and its feedback resistors define the gap.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Parameters of an IC-based ON/OFF Controller:**
> - **Threshold Voltage ($V_{TH}$):** The exact error voltage at which the controller toggles.
> - **Hysteresis Voltage ($V_H$):** $\text{Total width of the dead band} = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$.
> - **Saturation Voltage ($V_{sat}$):** $\approx \mathbf{13.5\ \text{V}}$ for a standard 741 op-amp on $\pm 15\ \text{V}$ rails.
> - **Switching Speed:** Limited by op-amp slew rate (typically $\mathbf{0.5\ \text{V/}\mu\text{s}}$ for $\mu$A741).
> - **Sensor Sensitivity (k):** Sensor gain, e.g., $\mathbf{10\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}}$ for LM35.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Stepwise Logic

The op-amp is wired in **positive feedback** so the output state "reinforces" the input condition that produced it (latching action). The control action is described in four stages:

1. **Error Generation:** The conditioned sensor signal $V_s = k \cdot \text{Process Variable}$ is compared with a set-point reference $V_{ref}$ to produce error $e(t) = V_{ref} - V_s$.
2. **Threshold Comparison:** The error $e(t)$ is applied to the inverting input. The non-inverting input receives a weighted sum of the output $V_o$ and $V_{ref}$ through resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$.
3. **Saturation Decision:** When the non-inverting input voltage $V^+$ exceeds the inverting input voltage $V^-$, the op-amp output saturates to $+V_{sat}$. Otherwise, it falls to $-V_{sat}$.
4. **Hysteresis Latching:** Once switched, the output remains latched until the input crosses the *opposite* threshold, creating the desired dead band.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Configurations

| Configuration | Input Applied To | Feedback To | Switching Condition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Inverting ($V^-$) | Non-inverting ($V^+$) | Switches when $V_{in} = V^+$ | Sensor-actuated, level/temperature control |
| **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Non-Inverting ($V^+$) | Inverting ($V^-$) | Switches when $V^+ = V^-$ | Waveform squaring, limit detection |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below uses `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe to remain markdown-table safe.

| # | Quantity | Formula (Inverting Topology) | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Upper Threshold $V_{UT}$ | $V_{UT} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{ref} \;+\; \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (+V_{sat})$ | Volts | Switch-OFF level |
| 2 | Lower Threshold $V_{LT}$ | $V_{LT} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{ref} \;-\; \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts | Switch-ON level |
| 3 | Hysteresis Band $V_H$ | $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \dfrac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts | Width of dead band |
| 4 | Center of Hysteresis $V_C$ | $V_C = \dfrac{V_{UT} + V_{LT}}{2} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{ref}$ | Volts | Ideal set-point |
| 5 | Hysteresis Ratio (sensor units) | $T_H = \dfrac{V_H}{k}$ | $^\circ$C / bar / etc. | Process dead band |
| 6 | Relay Coil Current Limit | $I_{coil} \le I_{OD}$ (op-amp drain) | Amps | Requires driver transistor |
| 7 | Set-Point Voltage (LM35) | $V_{ref} = k \cdot T_{set}$ | Volts | e.g., $10\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ON/OFF controllers built with op-amps form the **lowest-cost, highest-reliability** closed-loop blocks in industry. They appear in:
> - Domestic and industrial **refrigerators, ovens, incubators** (temperature).
> - **Sump pumps and tank-level switches** (float-gauge equivalents).
> - **Battery chargers** (over-voltage cut-off via comparator).
> - **Motor protection circuits** (current-sensed shutdown).
> - **Zero-crossing detectors** in power electronics.

The op-amp implementation is preferred over mechanical thermostats because it offers **silent operation, no contact wear, microsecond response, and easily adjustable thresholds** via potentiometers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Threshold Voltages (Inverting Schmitt Trigger)

Consider an op-amp with $V_{in}$ applied at the inverting terminal ($V^-$) and the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) driven by a resistor network consisting of $R_1$ from $V_{ref}$ and $R_2$ from the output $V_o$.

**Step 1 — Superposition at the Non-Inverting Node.**
Assuming an ideal op-amp input, no current flows into the $V^+$ terminal. The voltage at $V^+$ is the superposition of the contribution from $V_{ref}$ through $R_1$ and the contribution from $V_o$ through $R_2$.

$$V^{+} = \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right) V_{ref} \;+\; \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \right) V_o$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Two Saturation States of the Op-Amp.**
The op-amp output $V_o$ can only equal $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ (the rails minus $\approx 1.5\ \text{V}$ headroom for a 741).

*Case A:* Output is at $+V_{sat}$.

$$V^{+} = \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right) V_{ref} \;+\; \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \right) V_{sat} = V_{UT}$$

*Case B:* Output is at $-V_{sat}$.

$$V^{+} = \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right) V_{ref} \;-\; \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \right) V_{sat} = V_{LT}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Switching Rule.**
The op-amp output flips when $V_{in}$ (applied at $V^-$) crosses $V^+$. Therefore $V_{UT}$ and $V_{LT}$ are exactly the two threshold voltages at which the controller toggles.

$$\boxed{\;V_{UT} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} V_{ref} + \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} V_{sat}\;} \qquad \boxed{\;V_{LT} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} V_{ref} - \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} V_{sat}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Hysteresis Width.**
Subtracting the two threshold expressions, the $V_{ref}$ terms cancel identically.

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} V_{sat} - \left( -\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} V_{sat} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\;V_H = \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_1+R_2}\, V_{sat}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Center (Bias) of the Hysteresis Loop.**

$$V_C = \dfrac{V_{UT} + V_{LT}}{2} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\, V_{ref}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The **hysteresis width $V_H$** depends *only* on $R_1, R_2$ and $V_{sat}$ — it is **independent of $V_{ref}$**. Conversely, the **center of the band $V_C$** depends *only* on the divider ratio and $V_{ref}$ — it is **independent of $V_{sat}$**. This **orthogonality** allows the designer to tune set-point and dead-band *separately* — a critical advantage in KTU exam problems.

### 3.2 Design Procedure — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (14-mark style):** Design an ON/OFF temperature controller using op-amp LM741 and sensor LM35 ($k = 10\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$) such that the heater switches ON at $45^{\circ}\text{C}$ and switches OFF at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$. Take $\pm V_{sat} = \pm 13.5\ \text{V}$ and $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V}$ (derived from a Zener reference).

**Step A — Convert the Process Limits to Sensor Voltages.**

$$V_{ON} = k \cdot T_{ON} = 0.010 \cdot 45 = 0.450\ \text{V}$$

$$V_{OFF} = k \cdot T_{OFF} = 0.010 \cdot 50 = 0.500\ \text{V}$$

**Step B — Compute the Hysteresis Voltage.**

$$V_H = V_{OFF} - V_{ON} = 0.500 - 0.450 = 0.050\ \text{V} = 50\ \text{mV}$$

**Step C — Compute the Center Voltage.**

$$V_C = \dfrac{V_{ON} + V_{OFF}}{2} = \dfrac{0.450 + 0.500}{2} = 0.475\ \text{V}$$

**Step D — Solve the Divider Ratios Using the Two Master Equations.**

From $V_C = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} V_{ref}$, we get

$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \dfrac{V_C}{V_{ref}} = \dfrac{0.475}{5} = 0.095$$

From $V_H = \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_1+R_2} V_{sat}$, we get

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \dfrac{V_H}{2 V_{sat}} = \dfrac{0.050}{2 \cdot 13.5} = 0.001852$$

**Step E — Choose a Realistic Value for $R_1 + R_2$ and Back-Solve.**

Let $R_1 + R_2 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ (a standard, low-noise choice).

$$R_1 = 0.001852 \times 100\,\text{k}\Omega = 185.2\ \Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{use } 180\ \Omega$$

$$R_2 = 0.095 \times 100\,\text{k}\Omega = 9.5\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{use } 9.1\ \text{k}\Omega\ \text{or}\ 10\ \text{k}\Omega\ \text{trim pot}$$

**Step F — Verification by Substitution.**

$$V_{UT} = 0.095 \cdot 5 + 0.001852 \cdot 13.5 = 0.475 + 0.025 = 0.500\ \text{V}\ \checkmark$$

$$V_{LT} = 0.475 - 0.025 = 0.450\ \text{V}\ \checkmark$$

**Step G — Driver Stage.**
The op-amp output (max $\pm 13.5\ \text{V}$ at $\sim 25\ \text{mA}$) cannot directly drive a relay coil. Insert a power transistor (e.g., **BD139 NPN**) at the output. A free-wheeling diode (1N4007) across the relay coil suppresses the back-EMF.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Trade-offs at a Glance

| Design Parameter | Increased By | Effect on Set-Point | Effect on Hysteresis | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | Larger | Slight shift | Wider dead band | More chattering immunity, coarser control |
| $R_2$ | Larger | Lower $V_C$ | Slight reduction | Better noise filtering of $V_{ref}$ |
| $V_{ref}$ | Larger | Higher $V_C$ | None | Allows wider sensor ranges |
| $V_{sat}$ | Higher rails (e.g., $\pm 18\ \text{V}$) | None | Wider $V_H$ | Requires higher op-amp rating |

    end

    REF --> SUMM
    SUMM --> OPAMP

### 4.2 Detailed Schematic — Inverting Schmitt ON/OFF Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NET["Resistor Divider Network"]
        A1[Input Vin to inverting node]
        A2[R1 from Vref to non-inverting node]
        A3[R2 from output Vo to non-inverting node]
    end

    A1 --> INV((V-))
    A2 --> NON((V+))
    A3 --> NON
    INV --> OP[Op-Amp LM741]
    NON --> OP
    OP --> OUTV[Output Vo = plus or minus Vsat]
    OUTV -->|Feedback| A3
    OUTV --> TR[BD139 Base via 10k resistor]
    TR --> COIL[Relay Coil 12V]
    COIL --> DIODE[1N4007 Free-wheel]
    COIL --> SUP[plus 12V Rail]
```

### 4.3 Transfer Characteristic — Hysteresis Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOOP["Vo versus Vin Characteristic"]
        direction TB
        P1[Vin less than VLT] --> S1[Vo = plus Vsat Heater ON]
        S1 -->|Vin crosses VLT| S2[Vo remains plus Vsat Latched]
        S2 -->|Vin rises to VUT| S3[Vo snaps to minus Vsat Heater OFF]
        S3 -->|Vin drops to VLT| S4[Vo snaps back to plus Vsat]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The horizontal axis is the input (sensor) voltage, and the vertical axis is the controller output. The rectangular loop has width $V_H$ (dead band) and is centered at $V_C$. The **clockwise traversal** of the loop is the direction of increasing sensor voltage (heating), and **counter-clockwise** is the direction of cooling. The "jump" portions of the curve are the op-amp's saturated transitions.

### 4.4 Component Selection Summary

| Component | Recommended Part | Function | Critical Spec |
|---|---|---|---|
| Op-Amp | LM741 / LM324 / LM311 | Comparator with hysteresis | Slew rate $\ge 0.5\ \text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ |
| Sensor | LM35 (temp), MPX4115 (pressure) | Converts PV to voltage | Linearity, sensitivity $k$ |
| Reference | LM336-5.0 Zener | Stable set-point $V_{ref}$ | $\pm 1\%$ tolerance |
| Driver | BD139 / 2N2222 | Current amplification | $I_C \ge 1.5\ \text{A}$ |
| Relay | 12 V SPDT, 10 A contact | Power switching | Coil current $<$ transistor limit |
| Protection Diode | 1N4007 | Suppresses back-EMF | Reverse voltage $\ge 50\ \text{V}$ |
