## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define an ON/OFF controller. Why is a hysteresis band necessary in its practical implementation? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An ON/OFF controller is a two-position controller whose output switches abruptly between fully ON ($+V_{sat}$) and fully OFF ($-V_{sat}$) when the error signal crosses a single set-point. It is mathematically a **signum function** of the error.

A hysteresis band (dead zone) is essential because, in its absence, even microscopic noise around the set-point would cause the relay or heater to cycle hundreds of times per second — a phenomenon called **chattering** or **dithering**. This rapidly destroys electromechanical contacts, creates electrical noise, and overheats the actuator. The Schmitt-trigger topology introduces two distinct thresholds $V_{UT}$ and $V_{LT}$ so that small input variations within the band do not toggle the output.

---

> **`Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** With a neat sketch, explain how an op-amp can be used as an ON/OFF controller. Mention the role of positive feedback. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An op-amp is configured as a **Schmitt trigger** by routing a fraction of its output back to the non-inverting input through a resistor $R_2$ (positive feedback). The inverting input receives the conditioned sensor signal $V_s$, and the non-inverting input receives a weighted sum of the reference $V_{ref}$ and the output. When $V_s$ exceeds the threshold derived from this weighted sum, the output snaps to one rail; when it falls below the opposite threshold, it snaps to the other rail. Positive feedback is what makes the switching *regenerative* (fast) and *latching* (stable), and it is also what creates the hysteresis gap.

**Sketch (descriptive):** Op-amp symbol with $V^-$ tied to $V_s$ via $R_{in}$, $V^+$ tied to $V_{ref}$ via $R_1$ and to $V_o$ via $R_2$, output saturating to $\pm 13.5\ \text{V}$ driving a relay-transistor stage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

> **`Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module 3]`** Design an ON/OFF temperature controller using an op-amp LM741 and an LM35 sensor ($k = 10\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$) to control an electric heater in an incubator. The desired switching thresholds are: Heater **ON** at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ and Heater **OFF** at $55^{\circ}\text{C}$. Take $\pm V_{sat} = \pm 13\ \text{V}$ and use $V_{ref} = 2.5\ \text{V}$. Show the complete circuit, calculate the resistor values, and explain the operation. **(14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Circuit Description and Sensor-Voltage Conversion (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert the process limits to equivalent sensor voltages.**
LM35 produces $10\ \text{mV}$ per $^\circ\text{C}$. Therefore,

$$V_{ON} = 0.010 \times 40 = 0.400\ \text{V}$$

$$V_{OFF} = 0.010 \times 55 = 0.550\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the hysteresis band and the center of the band.**

$$V_H = V_{OFF} - V_{ON} = 0.550 - 0.400 = 0.150\ \text{V}$$

$$V_C = \dfrac{V_{ON} + V_{OFF}}{2} = \dfrac{0.400 + 0.550}{2} = 0.475\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the resistor ratios from the governing equations.**

For the inverting Schmitt configuration,

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \dfrac{V_H}{2 V_{sat}} = \dfrac{0.150}{2 \times 13} = 0.005769$$

$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \dfrac{V_C}{V_{ref}} = \dfrac{0.475}{2.5} = 0.190$$

**Step 4 — Choose a standard sum and back-solve.**

Let $R_1 + R_2 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ (preferred for low-bias-current noise in a 741 circuit).

$$R_1 = 0.005769 \times 100\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 577\ \Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{use } 560\ \Omega\ \text{standard}$$

$$R_2 = 0.190 \times 100\,\text{k}\Omega = 19.0\ \text{k}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{use } 18\ \text{k}\Omega\ \text{(or 20 k pot for fine trim)}$$

**Step 5 — Verify.**

$$V_{UT} = 0.190 \times 2.5 + 0.005769 \times 13 = 0.475 + 0.075 = 0.550\ \text{V} \ \checkmark$$

$$V_{LT} = 0.475 - 0.075 = 0.400\ \text{V} \ \checkmark$$

#### **Part (b) — Operation, Circuit Sketch, and Driver Stage (7 Marks)**

**Operation:**
- When the incubator temperature falls below $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ (sensor $< 0.400\ \text{V}$), the inverting input is *below* the non-inverting input ($V^+ = V_{LT} = 0.400\ \text{V}$), so the op-amp output saturates to $+13\ \text{V}$. This drives the BD139 NPN transistor into saturation, energising the relay coil, closing the NO contact, and switching the heater **ON**.
- As the heater warms the chamber, the sensor voltage rises. The output remains latched at $+13\ \text{V}$ until the temperature reaches $55^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($0.550\ \text{V}$). At this point, the inverting input exceeds the non-inverting input, and the op-amp output snaps to $-13\ \text{V}$, turning the transistor OFF, de-energising the relay, and switching the heater **OFF**.
- The heater remains OFF until the temperature drops back to $40^{\circ}\text{C}$, completing the cycle.

**Circuit Description (text-form schematic):**
The conditioned LM35 output is fed to the inverting input of the 741 through a $10\ \text{k}\Omega$ current-limiting resistor. The non-inverting input is connected to the junction of $R_1$ ($560\ \Omega$ to $V_{ref} = 2.5\ \text{V}$) and $R_2$ ($18\ \text{k}\Omega$ to output). The op-amp output drives the base of a BD139 through a $4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$ resistor. The relay coil (12 V, $120\ \Omega$) is connected between the $+12\ \text{V}$ rail and the collector, with a 1N4007 free-wheeling diode reverse-biased across the coil. The NO contact of the relay switches the $230\ \text{V}$ AC mains to the heater.

**Driver Stage Justification:**
The 741 can source/sink only $\sim 25\ \text{mA}$ typically, which is insufficient to drive a relay coil drawing $\sim 100\ \text{mA}$. The BD139 ($I_C = 1.5\ \text{A}$, $V_{CEO} = 80\ \text{V}$) provides the necessary current gain, and the diode protects the transistor from the inductive kick of the coil.

---

> **`Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]`** With the help of a circuit diagram, derive the expressions for the upper and lower threshold voltages of an op-amp ON/OFF controller using positive feedback. A non-inverting Schmitt trigger has $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 14\ \text{V}$, and $V_{ref} = 0\ \text{V}$. Determine **(i)** the hysteresis voltage, **(ii)** the two threshold voltages, and **(iii)** the process dead band if the sensor sensitivity is $k = 2\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$. **(14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Threshold Voltages (7 Marks)**

For a **non-inverting Schmitt trigger**, the input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting** terminal, and the output is fed back to the **inverting** terminal through a divider $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_2$ (to output). Using superposition at the inverting node,

$$V^{-} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\right) \cdot V_o$$

The switching condition is $V_{in} = V^-$.

When $V_o = +V_{sat}$:

$$V_{UT} = V_{in} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\right) V_{sat}$$

When $V_o = -V_{sat}$:

$$V_{LT} = V_{in} = -\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\right) V_{sat}$$

**Hysteresis width:**

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \dfrac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

**Note:** Since $V_{ref} = 0$ in the non-inverting configuration (no reference injected at the divider), the hysteresis band is symmetric about zero.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 14\ \text{V}$, $V_{ref} = 0\ \text{V}$, $k = 2\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the divider ratio.**

$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{100}{10 + 100} = \dfrac{100}{110} = 0.9091$$

**Step 2 — Compute the upper threshold $V_{UT}$.**

$$V_{UT} = 0.9091 \times (+14) = +12.727\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the lower threshold $V_{LT}$.**

$$V_{LT} = 0.9091 \times (-14) = -12.727\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the hysteresis voltage $V_H$.**

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 12.727 - (-12.727) = 25.454\ \text{V}$$

(equivalently, $V_H = 2 \times 0.9091 \times 14 = 25.454\ \text{V}$)

**Step 5 — Convert the hysteresis voltage to a process dead band.**

$$T_H = \dfrac{V_H}{k} = \dfrac{25.454\ \text{V}}{0.002\ \text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}} = 12{,}727^{\circ}\text{C}$$

This is absurdly large — in practice, the sensor output must be attenuated by an instrumentation amplifier *before* it is fed to the comparator, so that the *sensor voltage* (not the op-amp input) is constrained to lie within the comparator's supply rails. A more realistic design would insert a gain-of-$\frac{1}{100}$ pre-stage, giving an effective dead band of $\sim 127^{\circ}\text{C}$ — still large, but reasonable for a *furnace* application, and easily retunable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors in superposition:** The non-inverting input voltage has *two* terms — a contribution from $V_{ref}$ and a contribution from $V_o$. Students frequently drop the $V_{ref}$ term. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusion between inverting and non-inverting formulas:** In the inverting Schmitt, the divider ratio is $R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ at the *reference* leg; in the non-inverting case, it is $R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ at the *output* leg. Mixing them up gives a wrong hysteresis band. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting to convert sensor units:** A 50 mV hysteresis is meaningless unless you also state the corresponding $5^{\circ}\text{C}$ process dead band (for LM35). Always include both. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping the driver stage:** Drawing only the op-amp without the relay-driver transistor loses 2–3 marks even if the math is perfect. KTU expects a *complete design*, not just an equation.
> 5. **Ignoring free-wheeling diode:** A relay coil without a 1N4007 reverse-biased across it will destroy the BD139 in milliseconds. Examiners explicitly look for this. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ON/OFF Controller Using Linear ICs**
> - ON/OFF = **two-position discontinuous** controller; output is always at $\pm V_{sat}$.
> - Implemented with an **op-amp Schmitt trigger** (LM741, LM324, LM311).
> - **Inverting Schmitt** → input at $V^-$, feedback at $V^+$, thresholds depend on $V_{ref}$ and $V_{sat}$.
> - **Non-inverting Schmitt** → input at $V^+$, feedback at $V^-$, thresholds symmetric about $V_{ref}$.
> - **Hysteresis band** $V_H$ protects relays from chattering.
> - **Design orthogonality:** $V_H$ depends only on $R_1, R_2, V_{sat}$; $V_C$ depends only on $R_1, R_2, V_{ref}$ — tune them independently.
> - **Process dead band** $T_H = V_H / k$ where $k$ is the sensor sensitivity.
> - **Always include** a driver transistor (BD139 / 2N2222) and a free-wheeling diode (1N4007) for relay coils.
> - Standard values: $V_{sat} \approx 13.5\ \text{V}$ for $\pm 15\ \text{V}$ rails; LM35 $k = 10\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.
> - The hysteresis is the **width** of the rectangular loop on the $V_o$–$V_{in}$ plot; the center of the loop is the **set-point**.
> - For a **non-inverting** Schmitt, the divider ratio is $R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ (opposite of the inverting case).
> - KTU typical marks split: **3 marks** for definition + diagram, **3 marks** for derivation, **5 marks** for numerical design, **3 marks** for circuit & driver explanation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic PID Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic PID Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electronic PID Controller** is an analog, continuous-time feedback controller implemented using operational amplifiers (op-amps), resistors, and capacitors that generates a control signal $u(t)$ as a weighted linear combination of three error-domain operations: **Proportional (P)**, **Integral (I)**, and **Derivative (D)**. Mathematically, the time-domain control law is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $e(t)$ is the error signal (set-point minus measured process variable), and $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are the tunable proportional, integral, and derivative gains respectively.

In the **Laplace (s-) domain**, the controller transfer function becomes:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Electronic PID Controller belongs to the family of "continuous (analog) controllers" as opposed to digital/PLC-based implementations. It forms the **theoretical backbone** of all process control loops, including those later implemented in DCS/PLC platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Form Note:** Some textbooks (e.g., Ogata, Nise) write the PID in *standard form* using $T_i$ (integral time) and $T_d$ (derivative time). Always verify which form your numerical problem is using before solving.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Smart Car Driver"

Imagine you are driving a car and your task is to maintain a **constant cruise speed of 80 km/h** on a highway.

| Controller Action | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional (P)** | You press the accelerator **in proportion to** how far your current speed is from 80 km/h. Bigger gap = harder pedal. | Reacts to the **present error magnitude** |
| **Integral (I)** | You notice that even after pressing the pedal, the car stays at 78 km/h. You keep **accumulating** the small error and keep increasing pressure until the speed finally reaches 80. | Eliminates **persistent steady-state error** |
| **Derivative (D)** | You see a hill coming. Even if the error is small, you anticipate the future drop and press harder **early** to prevent it. | Reacts to the **rate of change** (anticipation) |

Together, **P handles "now," I handles "the past buildup," and D handles "the future trend."** The Electronic PID Controller does exactly this — but using op-amp circuits instead of a human foot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why electronic?** Before microcontrollers and PLCs became cheap, the PID algorithm was physically realized using **op-amp summers, integrators, and differentiators**. These circuits run in **real-time, continuous analog fashion** with no quantization, making them extremely fast and reliable in harsh industrial environments.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operational amplifier supply voltage:** typically **$\pm 12\,\text{V}$** to **$\pm 15\,\text{V}$ DC** (e.g., using LM741, LM324, TL081).
- **Input impedance of ideal op-amp:** $Z_{in} \to \infty$ (effectively **$> 10^6\,\Omega$** in practice).
- **Open-loop gain:** $A_{OL} \to \infty$ (typically **$> 10^5$** for real op-amps).
- **Controller output saturation limit:** determined by op-amp rail voltage (e.g., **$\pm 13\,\text{V}$** for $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply).
- **Controller output is commonly scaled to standard process signals:** **$4$–$20\,\text{mA}$** (current loop) or **$0$–$10\,\text{V}$** (voltage signal).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of an Electronic PID

### (A) Proportional (P) Controller

**Operating principle:** The controller output is directly proportional to the instantaneous error.

$$u_P(t) = K_p \, e(t) \quad \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} \quad \frac{U_P(s)}{E(s)} = K_p$$

- **Effect:** Increases system **speed of response** and reduces (but does not eliminate) steady-state error.
- **Drawback:** A pure P-controller always leaves a residual **steady-state error** called *offset*.
- **Op-amp circuit:** Inverting amplifier with gain set by feedback/input resistors.

---

### (B) Integral (I) Controller

**Operating principle:** The controller output is proportional to the **time integral of the error** (accumulated past error).

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau \quad \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} \quad \frac{U_I(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{K_i}{s}$$

- **Effect:** Drives the **steady-state error to zero** for a step input (a property called *zero steady-state error to step*).
- **Drawback:** Slow response; can cause **oscillations** and large **overshoot**.
- **Op-amp circuit:** Inverting integrator (feedback capacitor + input resistor).

---

### (C) Derivative (D) Controller

**Operating principle:** The controller output is proportional to the **rate of change of error** (predicted future trend).

$$u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \quad \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} \quad \frac{U_D(s)}{E(s)} = K_d \, s$$

- **Effect:** Adds **damping**, reduces **overshoot**, and improves **transient stability**.
- **Drawback:** Amplifies high-frequency noise (never used alone).
- **Op-amp circuit:** Inverting differentiator (feedback resistor + input capacitor). In practice, a **first-order lag** is added to make it a real differentiator.

---

## 2.2 Combined PID Transfer Function

Adding all three branches (in parallel) at the summing junction:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s$:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU problems:** The numerator is a **second-order polynomial in $s$**, and the only pole is at the **origin ($s=0$)**. This single integrator pole is *the* reason the PID eliminates steady-state error for step inputs.

---

## 2.3 Alternative Form (Time-Constant Form)

Often written using **integral time $T_i$** and **derivative time $T_d$**:

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} + T_d \, s \right]$$

Conversion relations:

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} \qquad , \qquad K_d = K_p \, T_d$$

This form is **preferred in industry** (e.g., Ziegler–Nichols tuning) because $T_i$ and $T_d$ have intuitive time units (seconds).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable. Memorize both forms and the op-amp circuit relations.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PID time-domain output | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i\!\int e\, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Volts (or mA after scaling) |
| 2 | PID s-domain transfer function | $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Dimensionless if normalized |
| 3 | Standard (time-constant) form | $G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | $T_i, T_d$ in **seconds** |
| 4 | Relation $K_i$ to $K_p, T_i$ | $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ | — |
| 5 | Relation $K_d$ to $K_p, T_d$ | $K_d = K_p \, T_d$ | — |
| 6 | P-controller gain (inverting amp) | $K_p = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | I-controller gain (inverting integrator) | $K_i = \dfrac{1}{R_1 C_f}$ | $s^{-1}$ |
| 8 | D-controller gain (inverting differentiator) | $K_d = R_f C_1$ | $s$ |
| 9 | Steady-state error to step (PI/PID) | $e_{ss} = 0$ | Type-1 system property |
| 10 | Steady-state error to step (P-only) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p G(s)\big\vert_{s=0}}$ | Non-zero offset |

**Critical units reminder:** $K_p$ is **dimensionless**, $K_i$ has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$, and $K_d$ has units of **s**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electronic PID controllers are the workhorse of:

- **Industrial process control:** Temperature ovens, level tanks, pressure vessels, flow loops.
- **Motion control:** Servo motor position/velocity loops.
- **Power electronics:** SMPS (switched-mode power supplies) voltage regulation.
- **Automotive:** Cruise control, engine air-fuel ratio, anti-lock braking.
- **Aerospace:** Autopilot altitude/heading hold.
- **Robotics:** Joint position control.

The same mathematical PID law is also **digitally implemented** in modern PLCs and DCS — but understanding the electronic (analog) origin helps students interpret the digital realization, especially regarding sampling time and discretization effects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Op-Amp Inverting Amplifier → Proportional Action

### Circuit Description
- Input signal $e(t)$ fed through resistor $R_1$ into the **inverting input** of an op-amp.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting input.
- Non-inverting input grounded.

### Derivation Using Ideal Op-Amp Constraints

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground).**
Since the op-amp is ideal, the inverting input is at the same potential as the non-inverting input (which is grounded). So the inverting node is a **virtual ground**, $v^- = 0$.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the inverting node.**
No current flows into the ideal op-amp input, so the current through $R_1$ equals the current through $R_f$:

$$\frac{e(t) - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - u_P(t)}{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $u_P(t)$.**

$$u_P(t) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}\, e(t)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the proportional gain.**

$$K_p = \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The negative sign simply indicates inversion; in practice a sign-inverting summer or an extra inverting stage is added to produce a positive control action when required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Op-Amp Integrator → Integral Action

### Circuit Description
- Input $e(t)$ through $R_1$ into the inverting input.
- Feedback element is a **capacitor $C_f$** (instead of $R_f$).
- Non-inverting input grounded.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground).**
Current into the node from the input resistor:

$$i_{in}(t) = \frac{e(t) - 0}{R_1} = \frac{e(t)}{R_1}$$

**Step 2 — This same current flows into the capacitor (KCL).**
The voltage across the capacitor is $0 - u_I(t) = -u_I(t)$. Using the capacitor constitutive relation $i = C_f \dfrac{dv}{dt}$:

$$i_{in}(t) = C_f \frac{d(0 - u_I(t))}{dt} = -C_f \frac{du_I(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and integrate.**

$$\frac{e(t)}{R_1} = -C_f \frac{du_I(t)}{dt}$$

$$\frac{du_I(t)}{dt} = -\frac{e(t)}{R_1 C_f}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$u_I(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Identify the integral gain.**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{R_1 C_f}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Op-Amp Differentiator → Derivative Action

### Circuit Description
- Input $e(t)$ through a **capacitor $C_1$** into the inverting input.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ from output to inverting input.
- Non-inverting input grounded.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Current through the input capacitor.**
With $v^- = 0$ (virtual ground), the current through $C_1$ is:

$$i_{in}(t) = C_1 \frac{d(e(t) - 0)}{dt} = C_1 \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — This current flows through $R_f$ to the output.**
Using Ohm's law on the feedback resistor:

$$i_{in}(t) = \frac{0 - u_D(t)}{R_f} = -\frac{u_D(t)}{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve.**

$$C_1 \frac{de(t)}{dt} = -\frac{u_D(t)}{R_f}$$

$$u_D(t) = -R_f C_1 \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the derivative gain.**

$$K_d = R_f C_1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical pitfall:** A pure differentiator amplifies high-frequency noise. A real circuit adds a small series resistor $R_1$ with $C_1$ to form a **first-order high-frequency roll-off**, giving the practical transfer function $G_D(s) \approx \dfrac{K_d s}{1 + \tau s}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Combining P, I, D at the Summing Junction

### Circuit Description
- Three op-amp branches (P, I, D) each producing $u_P$, $u_I$, $u_D$.
- Their outputs feed into a **summing amplifier** with input resistors $R_1$ and feedback resistor $R_{sf}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Write KCL at the summing node (virtual ground).**

$$\frac{u_P(t)}{R_1} + \frac{u_I(t)}{R_1} + \frac{u_D(t)}{R_1} = -\frac{u(t)}{R_{sf}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $u(t)$.**

$$u(t) = -\frac{R_{sf}}{R_1} \left[ u_P(t) + u_I(t) + u_D(t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute expressions for $u_P$, $u_I$, $u_D$ from Sections 3.1–3.3.**

$$u(t) = -\frac{R_{sf}}{R_1} \left[ -K_p e(t) - K_i \!\int e\, dt - K_d \frac{de}{dt} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange (and absorb the inverting stage sign).**

$$u(t) = \frac{R_{sf}}{R_1} \left[ K_p e(t) + K_i \!\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

Defining the overall gain as $\beta = \dfrac{R_{sf}}{R_1}$, the final composite PID law is:

$$\boxed{\, u(t) = \beta K_p e(t) + \beta K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + \beta K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \,}$$

with effective gains $\beta K_p$, $\beta K_i$, and $\beta K_d$ — easily tuned by varying $R_{sf}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Laplace-domain equivalent:** The overall transfer function is the **sum** of the three branches because they are in parallel:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

This linearity (superposition) holds only because each op-amp branch is **independent** and they meet at a summing node with no loading interactions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A PID controller has $K_p = 2$, $T_i = 10\,\text{s}$, $T_d = 1\,\text{s}$. The error signal is a unit step $e(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$. Compute the controller output $u(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

**Solution — Step 1: Convert to $K_i$ and $K_d$.**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_p \, T_d = 2 \times 1 = 2\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Compute each component for a unit step error.**

- Proportional: $u_P(t) = K_p e(t) = 2 \times 1 = 2$
- Integral: $u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t 1\, d\tau = 0.2 \, t$
- Derivative: $u_D(t) = K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$. For a step, $\dfrac{de}{dt} = 0$ for $t > 0$ and is an impulse at $t = 0$. So for $t > 0$, $u_D(t) = 0$.

**Step 3: Total output for $t > 0$.**

$$u(t) = 2 + 0.2\, t + 0 = 2 + 0.2\, t$$

The output rises linearly with slope $0.2\,\text{V/s}$ after the initial impulse kick. This illustrates the **PI-dominant action** with derivative active only at transients.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Electronic PID in a Closed-Loop System

The figure below shows how the electronic PID controller sits inside a complete feedback control loop. The **error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$** is generated by the comparator and fed into the controller. The controller's output $u(t)$ drives the **actuator** (e.g., a valve), which influences the **process/plant** to produce the measured output $y(t)$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    setpoint["Reference r(t)"]
    comparator["Comparator Node"]
    pid["Electronic PID Controller Gc s"]
    actuator["Actuator and Final Control Element"]
    plant["Process Plant Gp s"]
    sensor["Measurement Sensor H s"]
    output["Process Variable y(t)"]
    disturbance["Disturbance d t"]

    setpoint -->|positive input| comparator
    output -->|feedback to sensor| sensor
    sensor -->|measured signal| comparator
    comparator -->|error e t| pid
    pid -->|control u t| actuator
    actuator -->|manipulated variable| plant
    plant -->|output| output
    disturbance -.->|additive load| plant
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the closed loop from `setpoint` → `comparator` → `pid` → `actuator` → `plant` → `output` → `sensor` → back to `comparator`. The disturbance is an additive external load on the plant.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of the Electronic PID

This figure decomposes the PID controller into its three **parallel op-amp branches** summed at a common node.

```mermaid
graph LR
    errorIn["Error Input e t"]
    pBranch["Proportional Branch Gain Kp"]
    iBranch["Integral Branch Gain Ki over s"]
    dBranch["Derivative Branch Gain Kd times s"]
    summer["Summing Junction"]
    pidOut["Controller Output u t"]

    errorIn --> pBranch
    errorIn --> iBranch
    errorIn --> dBranch
    pBranch --> summer
    iBranch --> summer
    dBranch --> summer
    summer --> pidOut
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-specific note:** The three branches are **independent parallel paths**. This is why the overall transfer function is the **algebraic sum** of individual transfer functions — a classic exam question.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Op-Amp Realization of Each Branch

This functional architecture maps the **physical op-amp circuits** for each controller mode.

```mermaid
graph LR
    errSig["Error e t from comparator"]
    invP["Inverting Amplifier with Rf and R1 → yields Kp e t"]
    invI["Inverting Integrator with R1 and Cf → yields Ki integral e dt"]
    invD["Inverting Differentiator with C1 and Rf → yields Kd de dt"]
    sigSummer["Summing Amplifier with Rs and Rsf → combines all three"]
    finalOut["Final Output u t sent to actuator"]

    errSig --> invP
    errSig --> invI
    errSig --> invD
    invP --> sigSummer
    invI --> sigSummer
    invD --> sigSummer
    sigSummer --> finalOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardware insight:** Many industrial analog PID controllers (e.g., Honeywell, Foxboro) used exactly this topology in the 1960s–1980s with discrete op-amp ICs. The summing amplifier's feedback resistor $R_{sf}$ is often a **potentiometer** used for fine-tuning the overall loop gain.

---

## 4.4 State-Flow Diagram: Effect of Each Action on a Step Response

```mermaid
graph LR
    sp["Set-point Step Applied"]
    pOnly["P-only Action: Fast rise but offset remains"]
    piAction["PI Action: Zero offset but possible overshoot"]
    pidAction["PID Action: Fast rise, zero offset, minimal overshoot"]
    steady["Reached steady state with required output"]

    sp --> pOnly
    pOnly -->|add I branch| piAction
    piAction -->|add D branch| pidAction
    pidAction --> steady
```

This figure is highly useful for KTU essay-type questions asking *"What is the role of each term?"* — the cumulative improvement from $P \to PI \to PID$ is the most common visual to include.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar]**

**Q: Define an Electronic PID Controller. Write its transfer function in the s-domain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Electronic PID Controller** is an analog, continuous-time feedback controller implemented using operational amplifiers that produces a control signal $u(t)$ as a sum of three error-derived terms: Proportional (present error), Integral (accumulated past error), and Derivative (predicted future error).

**Transfer function:**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s$$

In standard form:

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right)$$

> **Mark split:** Definition 1m, transfer function 1m, standard form 1m.

---

### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]**

**Q: List the three actions of a PID controller and state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Action | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional (P)** | Provides immediate corrective action; increases response speed | Cannot eliminate steady-state error (offset) |
| **Integral (I)** | Eliminates steady-state error to step inputs | Slow response, can cause oscillations and large overshoot |
| **Derivative (D)** | Anticipates future error; reduces overshoot, adds damping | Amplifies high-frequency noise; ineffective for constant errors |

> **Mark split:** Three actions identified (1m) + advantages and disadvantages (2m, distributed).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts — typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice: attempt **either** option A **or** option B in full.

---

### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the transfer function of an electronic PID controller using op-amp circuits. Show the P, I, and D realizations explicitly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Proportional Branch (2 marks):**

Inverting amplifier with input $R_1$ and feedback $R_f$:

$$u_P(t) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} e(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_P(s) = \frac{R_f}{R_1} = K_p$$

**(ii) Integral Branch (2 marks):**

Inverting integrator with input $R_1$ and feedback capacitor $C_f$:

$$u_I(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f} \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_I(s) = \frac{1}{R_1 C_f s} = \frac{K_i}{s}$$

where $K_i = \dfrac{1}{R_1 C_f}$.

**(iii) Derivative Branch (2 marks):**

Inverting differentiator with input capacitor $C_1$ and feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$u_D(t) = -R_f C_1 \frac{de(t)}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_D(s) = R_f C_1 s = K_d s$$

where $K_d = R_f C_1$.

**(iv) Summation (1 mark):**

Since the three branches are in parallel and combined through a summing amplifier:

$$G_c(s) = G_P(s) + G_I(s) + G_D(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

---

**(b) A temperature control loop uses a PID controller with $K_p = 4$, $T_i = 8$ s, $T_d = 2$ s. The set-point changes as a unit step. Compute the controller output for $t > 0$ and comment on the steady-state behavior. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the actual gains (1 mark).**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 4 \times 2 = 8\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Write the controller output expression (1 mark).**

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $e(t) = 1$ (unit step) for $t \geq 0$ (2 marks).**

- Proportional: $u_P(t) = 4 \times 1 = 4$
- Integral: $u_I(t) = 0.5 \int_0^t 1 \, d\tau = 0.5 t$
- Derivative: $\dfrac{de}{dt} = 0$ for $t > 0$ (impulse only at $t = 0$); so $u_D(t) = 0$ for $t > 0$.

**Step 4: Total output (1 mark).**

$$u(t) = 4 + 0.5 t \quad \text{for } t > 0$$

**Step 5: Comment on steady-state behavior (2 marks).**

For a step input, the proportional term provides the **immediate** corrective kick of $4$ units. The integral term then **grows linearly** with time, ensuring the controller keeps pushing the process variable toward the set-point. Because the PID contains an integrator ($1/s$ pole), the **steady-state error to a step input is zero** — the controller output will continue to grow (or be saturated by actuator limits) until the error vanishes. The derivative term is inactive in steady state for a step and contributes only to **transient damping** during set-point changes.

---

### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model Paper Pattern – Alternative]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an electronic PID controller in a closed-loop process. How does each term (P, I, D) influence the system response? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

The closed-loop configuration contains:

- **Set-point $r(t)$** compared with feedback $y(t)$ at a summing junction to produce error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- **Electronic PID Controller** producing $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- **Actuator** (e.g., control valve, heater drive) that converts $u(t)$ to a physical manipulation.
- **Process plant** with transfer function $G_p(s)$ producing output $y(t)$.
- **Sensor** with transfer function $H(s)$ feeding the measured variable back to the summing junction.

(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 — students should redraw it for full marks.)

**Influence of each term on response (4 marks, distributed 1m + 2m + 1m):**

- **Proportional term $K_p$:** Increases **rise time** and **bandwidth**; reduces (but does not eliminate) steady-state error. Increasing $K_p$ alone increases speed but causes more overshoot and oscillation.
- **Integral term $K_i$ (or $1/T_i$):** **Eliminates steady-state error** for step and ramp inputs. Reduces the time to settle. Increasing integral action speeds up error removal but may cause **integrator wind-up** and oscillations.
- **Derivative term $K_d$ (or $T_d$):** Adds **damping**, reduces **overshoot** and **settling time**, and improves **phase margin**. It predicts future error and counteracts sudden changes. Cannot be used alone as it gives zero output for constant error.

---

**(b) An op-amp-based PID controller is to be designed with $K_p = 5$, $K_i = 1\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $K_d = 0.5\,\text{s}$. If the integrator branch uses $C_f = 10\,\mu\text{F}$, find the values of all resistors and capacitors needed. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Choose design variables.**

Let us pick a common capacitor value. The P and D branches can share the same feedback resistor for simplicity, but typically they are designed independently.

**Step 2: Proportional branch (2 marks).**

$$K_p = \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 5 R_1$$

Choose $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \Rightarrow R_f = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 3: Integral branch (2 marks).**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{R_1 C_f} = 1 \quad \text{with} \quad C_f = 10\,\mu\text{F}$$

$$R_1 = \frac{1}{K_i \cdot C_f} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^5\,\Omega = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Derivative branch (2 marks).**

$$K_d = R_f C_1 = 0.5$$

Choose $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ (from the integrator branch) for uniformity:

$$C_1 = \frac{K_d}{R_f} = \frac{0.5}{100 \times 10^3} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F} = 5\,\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 5: Summary (1 mark).**

| Branch | $R_{in}$ | $R_f$ | $C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $50\,\text{k}\Omega$ | — |
| I | $100\,\text{k}\Omega$ | — | $C_f = 10\,\mu\text{F}$ |
| D | — (capacitor at input) | $100\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $C_1 = 5\,\mu\text{F}$ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in $K_i$ and $K_d$:** $K_p$ is **dimensionless**, $K_i$ is in $\text{s}^{-1}$, and $K_d$ is in **s**. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing standard form with ideal form:** When $T_i$ and $T_d$ are given, you must convert using $K_i = K_p/T_i$ and $K_d = K_p T_d$ **before** using the formula $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
> 3. **Wrong sign in op-amp derivations:** Remember, the inverting input gives a **negative** output. The summing amplifier inverts the sign again, but be consistent in your derivation.
> 4. **Derivative on a step:** A common error is to write $u_D(t) = K_d$ for a step input. Correctly, $u_D(t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ and an **impulse** of strength $K_d$ at $t = 0$.
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram clearly:** KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the block diagram is unlabeled or missing arrows.
> 6. **Missing the "comment on steady-state" part:** Many students only compute $u(t)$ numerically and forget the qualitative analysis — that costs 2 marks easily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PID = Proportional + Integral + Derivative** — three parallel error-processing actions summed at a node.
- **Time-domain law:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$
- **s-domain TF:** $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$
- **Standard (industrial) form:** $G_c(s) = K_p \!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$
- **Conversions:** $K_i = K_p / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$
- **Op-amp P-gain:** $K_p = R_f / R_1$
- **Op-amp I-gain:** $K_i = 1 / (R_1 C_f)$
- **Op-amp D-gain:** $K_d = R_f C_1$
- **P-controller:** fast, simple, but has **steady-state offset**.
- **I-controller:** removes offset, but **slow** and **oscillatory**.
- **D-controller:** **anticipatory**, adds damping, but **amplifies noise** — never used alone.
- **PID has a single pole at origin ($s=0$):** ensures **zero steady-state error to step input**.
- **Derivative action for a step:** contributes an **impulse at $t=0$** and zero thereafter.
- **Electronic realization:** three independent op-amp branches → summing amplifier.
- **Real-world form factor:** controller output is typically scaled to **$4$–$20$ mA** or **$0$–$10$ V** for industrial actuators.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always write the **transfer function, draw the block diagram, state the unit of every gain, and comment on steady-state behavior.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Microcontroller-Based Digital Two-Degree of Freedom PID Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Microcontroller-Based Digital Two-Degree of Freedom PID Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Two-Degree-of-Freedom (2-DOF) PID Controller:** A discrete-time control algorithm implemented on a microcontroller (or microprocessor/DSP/FPGA) that contains two independent tuning parameter sets — one governing **setpoint tracking** (servo/regulatory response) and another governing **disturbance rejection** (regulatory response). Unlike the conventional single-DOF PID where one set of gains must compromise between fast setpoint response and good load rejection, the 2-DOF structure decouples these two conflicting objectives.

A **microcontroller-based** implementation means the entire control law — sampling, difference equation evaluation, anti-windup, bumpless transfer, output saturation, and actuator driving — is executed in software on a resource-constrained embedded platform (e.g., 8051, PIC, MSP430, ARM Cortex-M, Arduino/ATmega) using a fixed sampling period $T_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Two-Degree of Freedom"?** The term "degree of freedom" in classical control refers to the number of independent closed-loop transfer functions that can be shaped independently. A 1-DOF PID has only one tunable transfer function from reference $R$ to output $Y$ (or from disturbance $D$ to $Y$). A 2-DOF controller has two — one from $R \rightarrow Y$ and one from $D \rightarrow Y$ — that can be tuned separately.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a manual car**:

- Your **right foot on the accelerator** is the **proportional + integral (PI) action** that fights road slope and wind (disturbances).
- Your **eyes on the road, judging how far you are from the desired lane position** is the **setpoint tracking** — how aggressively you approach a target speed or lane.
- In a **1-DOF PID**, one driver setting has to do BOTH jobs: chase the speed limit AND fight the headwind. If you tune for fast speed recovery on a highway, the car jerks every time the wind gusts. If you tune for smooth cruise, the car lazily catches up to your desired speed.

- In a **2-DOF PID**, it's like having a **cruise-control system with two knobs**: one labeled *"How fast do you want to reach the setpoint?"* and another labeled *"How hard do you reject disturbances?"*. They are tuned **independently**. The result is a car that reaches the setpoint briskly (without overshoot) AND shrugs off gusts smoothly.

> [!TIP]
> **The microcontroller analogy:** Think of the microcontroller as the car's onboard computer. Every $T_s$ (say, every **10 ms**), it samples the speed sensor, computes the new accelerator pedal position using the 2-DOF PID difference equation, and writes the result to a PWM output that drives the throttle actuator. All of this happens deterministically in real time.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Sampling Period $T_s$ | **1 ms – 100 ms** (process-dependent) |
| Proportional Gain $K_p$ | **0.01 – 100** (dimensionless or with units) |
| Integral Time $T_i$ | **0.1 s – 1000 s** |
| Derivative Time $T_d$ | **0 s – 10 s** (often 0 in process control) |
| Setpoint Weight $\alpha$ (2-DOF) | **0 ≤ α ≤ 1** (commonly 0.4–0.6) |
| Derivative Filter Coefficient $N$ | **3 – 20** |
| Output Saturation | **0% – 100%** of actuator span |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution: 1-DOF PID → 2-DOF PID

#### 2.1.1 Conventional (1-DOF) Continuous-Time PID

The **ideal (parallel) form** of a 1-DOF PID controller is:

$$u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

where:
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ is the error between setpoint $r$ and measured output $y$.
- $K_p$ is the proportional gain.
- $T_i$ is the integral time constant (seconds).
- $T_d$ is the derivative time constant (seconds).

**The fundamental limitation:** The same $K_p, T_i, T_d$ are used for BOTH:
1. Following a changing setpoint $r(t)$ → *servo response*.
2. Rejecting load disturbance $d(t)$ → *regulatory response*.

These two goals are **physically antagonistic**:
- Fast setpoint response → high $K_p$ → oscillatory, overshoots.
- Smooth disturbance rejection → lower $K_p$ → sluggish setpoint tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 2-DOF Insight:** A controller has $n$ degrees of freedom if it can independently shape $n$ closed-loop transfer functions. For SISO (single-input, single-output) systems, the maximum useful DOF is **2** (because the closed loop is described by the sensitivity $S$ and complementary sensitivity $T = 1-S$, with $S + T = 1$).

### 2.2 Architecture of the 2-DOF PID

The general 2-DOF PID controller structure is:

$$U(s) = K_p \left[ (\beta R(s) - Y(s)) + \frac{1}{T_i s}(R(s) - Y(s)) + \frac{T_d s}{1 + \frac{T_d}{N}s}(\gamma R(s) - Y(s)) \right]$$

The three **setpoint weights** are:
- $\beta$ — proportional setpoint weight (typically 0 to 1).
- $\alpha$ (or implicit 1) — integral setpoint weight (often 1; some structures use 0 to prevent integral windup on setpoint changes).
- $\gamma$ — derivative setpoint weight (typically 0 to 1).

**Common 2-DOF PID variants (KTU-relevant):**

| Variant | β | α | γ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PI-D** (most popular) | 1 | 1 | 0 | Derivative acts on measurement only — prevents "derivative kick" on setpoint change. |
| **I-PD** | 0 | 1 | 0 | All terms act on measurement only — robust against setpoint step. |
| **PID** (full 2-DOF) | 0 < β < 1 | 1 | 0 < γ < 1 | General form — most flexibility. |
| **PD-PI series** | β, γ tunable | — | — | Cascade form. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivative Kick:** A classical 1-DOF PID with derivative on the error experiences a huge instantaneous spike in $u(t)$ whenever the setpoint $r$ changes abruptly (like a step). This is called "derivative kick." By putting the derivative on the measurement only ($\gamma = 0$), the 2-DOF structure **completely eliminates** derivative kick.

### 2.3 Why a Microcontroller? Why Discretize?

A **microcontroller** is used because:
1. **Cost-effective** for high-volume embedded products.
2. **Flexible** — control law can be changed by reprogramming.
3. **Energy-efficient** — battery-operated field devices.
4. **Real-time deterministic** — RTOS or bare-metal scheduling guarantees sample-period adherence.
5. **On-chip peripherals** — ADC (for sensor reading), PWM/timer (for actuator output), UART/SPI/I²C (for HMI/communication).

But microcontrollers are **discrete-time machines**: they sample, compute, and output at instants $t = kT_s$, $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ Therefore, the continuous PID must be discretized.

**Discretization Methods (KTU syllabus essentials):**

| Method | Integral $I(k) = \int e\,dt$ | Derivative $D(k) = \frac{de}{dt}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward (Euler) Rectangular** | $I(k) = I(k-1) + T_s \cdot e(k)$ | $D(k) = \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$ |
| **Backward (Backward Euler)** | $I(k) = I(k-1) + T_s \cdot e(k)$ | $D(k) = \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$ (same for derivative) |
| **Trapezoidal (Tustin / Bilinear)** | $I(k) = I(k-1) + \frac{T_s}{2}[e(k) + e(k-1)]$ | $D(k) = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}} e(k)$ |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$ | Tracking error at sample $k$ | Same as controlled variable |
| 2 | $u(k) = K_p\left[ e(k) + \frac{T_s}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T_s}[e(k)-e(k-1)] \right]$ | **Positional form** PID (1-DOF) | Controller output units |
| 3 | $\Delta u(k) = K_p\left[ \Delta e(k) + \frac{T_s}{T_i}e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T_s}[\Delta e(k) - \Delta e(k-1)] \right]$ | **Velocity form** (preferred for microcontrollers) | Increment per sample |
| 4 | $u_{2\text{-DOF}}(k) = K_p\left[ (\beta r(k) - y(k)) + \frac{T_s}{T_i}\sum (r-y) + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(\gamma r(k) - \gamma r(k-1) - [y(k)-y(k-1)])\right]$ | **2-DOF positional form** | Controller output |
| 5 | $U(z) = K_p\left[(\beta - 1)R(z) + E(z) + \frac{T_s}{T_i}\frac{1}{1-z^{-1}}E(z) + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\frac{1-z^{-1}}{1 + \frac{T_d}{N}T_s(1-z^{-1})^{-1}}(R(z)\gamma - Y(z))\right]$ | 2-DOF PID in $z$-domain | Transfer function |
| 6 | $D_{\text{filtered}}(s) = \frac{T_d s}{1 + \frac{T_d}{N}s}$ | Derivative with **first-order filter** (avoids pure-D amplification of noise) | — |
| 7 | $G_{c,\text{2DOF}}(s) = K_p\left[\beta + \frac{1}{T_i s} + \frac{T_d s}{1 + \frac{T_d}{N}s}\right] \quad \text{on } R$ | 2-DOF controller transfer from $R$ to $U$ | — |
| 8 | $G_{c,\text{2DOF}}(s) = -K_p\left[1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + \frac{T_d s}{1 + \frac{T_d}{N}s}\right] \quad \text{on } Y$ | 2-DOF controller transfer from $Y$ to $U$ | — |
| 9 | $\text{If } \vert u(k) \vert > u_{\max} \Rightarrow u(k) = u_{\max} \cdot \text{sign}(u(k))$ | **Output saturation / clamping** | — |
| 10 | $I_{\text{anti-windup}}(k) = I(k) + K_{\text{aw}} \cdot T_s \cdot (u_{\text{sat}}(k) - u(k))$ | **Anti-windup (back-calculation)** | — |
| 11 | $f_s = \frac{1}{T_s} \geq 10 f_c$ | **Shannon-Nyquist sampling rule** for control (practical: $f_s \geq 10\times$ closed-loop bandwidth) | Hz |
| 12 | $\omega_d = \frac{2}{T}\tan\left(\frac{\omega_a T}{2}\right)$ | **Bilinear (Tustin) prewarping** for $s \rightarrow z$ | rad/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Velocity Form? (Exam favorite!)** The velocity (incremental) form $\Delta u(k)$ is preferred in microcontroller implementations because:
> 1. **Bumpless transfer** from manual to auto mode is automatic (the output increments, not jumps).
> 2. **No integral windup accumulation** in the controller — integrator state can be reconstructed.
> 3. **Numerical robustness** — the integral term is replaced by a summation, which is less sensitive to ADC quantization.
> 4. **Easier actuator integration** with delta-sigma output stages.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Temperature control** in plastics extruders and HVAC — 2-DOF PID decouples "reach setpoint fast" from "reject door-opening disturbance smoothly."
- **Motor speed control** in CNC machines and EV drivetrains.
- **Robotic manipulator joint control** — setpoint changes are frequent; disturbance rejection is critical.
- **Aerospace actuators** — flight control surfaces need precise setpoint tracking AND wind disturbance rejection.
- **Process industries** — flow, level, pressure loops in chemical plants, where PI-D is the de-facto industry standard (ISA 1996 form: $\gamma = 0$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous PID to Discrete (Velocity Form)

**Step 1 — Start with the continuous-time 1-DOF PID (ideal form):**

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_p T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Sample the signal at $t = kT_s$.** Let $u(k) = u(kT_s)$, $e(k) = e(kT_s)$.

**Step 3 — Approximate the integral using the rectangular (backward-Euler) rule:**

$$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the derivative using the backward-difference rule:**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the continuous PID to obtain the discrete (positional) form:**

$$u(k) = K_p e(k) + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}\left[e(k) - e(k-1)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Compute $u(k-1)$ similarly:**

$$u(k-1) = K_p e(k-1) + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}\left[e(k-1) - e(k-2)\right]$$

**Step 7 — Subtract $u(k-1)$ from $u(k)$ (define $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$):**

\begin{aligned}
\Delta u(k) &= u(k) - u(k-1) \\[4pt]
&= K_p \bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i}\bigl[e(k)\bigr] + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]
\end{aligned}

**Step 8 — Regroup the coefficients. Define:**

\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= K_p\left(1 + \frac{T_s}{T_i} + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\right) \\[4pt]
a_1 &= K_p\left(-1 + \frac{2T_d}{T_s}\right) \\[4pt]
a_2 &= -K_p \frac{T_d}{T_s}
\end{aligned}

**Step 9 — The compact velocity-form difference equation becomes:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta u(k) = a_0\, e(k) + a_1\, e(k-1) + a_2\, e(k-2)\;}$$

This is the **1-DOF digital PID velocity form** — it computes the *increment* in actuator command per sampling instant, which is what a microcontroller does in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $a_0, a_1, a_2$:** They are the controller's "taps" — analogous to an FIR filter. The PID has effectively become a 3-tap finite-impulse-response filter acting on the error history, plus an internal state for the integral (which is now implicit in the summation if you go back to positional form).

### 3.2 Derivation: Discrete 2-DOF PID (PI-D Variant, the Industry Standard)

The most widely used 2-DOF variant in industrial practice is the **PI-D controller** ($\beta = 1$, $\gamma = 0$):

**Step 1 — Write the controller as two parallel branches:**

$$U(s) = \underbrace{K_p(\beta R - Y)}_{\text{P branch}} + \underbrace{\frac{K_p}{T_i s}(R - Y)}_{\text{I branch}} + \underbrace{\frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + \frac{T_d}{N}s}(0\cdot R - Y)}_{\text{D branch on measurement}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\beta = 1$, $\gamma = 0$ for the PI-D form:**

$$U(s) = K_p(R - Y) + \frac{K_p}{T_i s}(R - Y) - \frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + \frac{T_d}{N}s} Y$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply the derivative filter denominator. Let $D_f(s) = \frac{T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s}$ acting on $-Y(s)$. Then:**

$$\left(1 + \frac{T_d}{N}s\right) U_D(s) = -T_d s\, Y(s)$$

**Step 4 — Discretize using the backward-difference derivative approximation. Define $a = \frac{T_d/N}{T_d/N + T_s}$ (this is the standard discretization constant for a first-order lag). Then the filtered derivative becomes:**

$$U_D(k) = a \cdot U_D(k-1) - K_p(1-a)\left[y(k) - y(k-1)\right]$$

This is a **first-order IIR filter** with one-pole low-pass characteristic — perfect for embedded implementation.

**Step 5 — Combine all three branches. The final discrete 2-DOF PI-D algorithm executed per sample is:**

\begin{aligned}
P(k) &= K_p \bigl[r(k) - y(k)\bigr] \\[4pt]
I(k) &= I(k-1) + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i}\bigl[r(k) - y(k)\bigr] + K_{\text{aw}} \bigl(u_{\text{sat}}(k-1) - u(k-1)\bigr) \\[4pt]
D(k) &= a \cdot D(k-1) - K_p(1-a)\bigl[y(k) - y(k-1)\bigr] \\[4pt]
u(k) &= P(k) + I(k) + D(k) \\[4pt]
u_{\text{sat}}(k) &= \text{clamp}\bigl(u(k),\, u_{\min},\, u_{\max}\bigr)
\end{aligned}

**Step 6 — The final closed-loop behavior is now decoupled:**
- Setpoint response is governed by $K_p$ via $P(k)$ (proportional on $e$) plus $I(k)$ (integral on $e$).
- Disturbance rejection is governed by the **full $K_p + I + D$ action on the measurement**, decoupled from setpoint transient behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-windup explanation (Step 5, I(k) line):** When the actuator saturates, the integrator must STOP accumulating error, otherwise the integral term grows without bound. The back-calculation term $K_{\text{aw}} (u_{\text{sat}} - u)$ feeds back the saturation error, *unwinding* the integrator at rate $K_{\text{aw}} \cdot T_s$. Typical $K_{\text{aw}} = 1/T_i$ or $K_{\text{aw}} = 1/T_t$ where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant.

### 3.3 Derivation: Bilinear (Tustin) Transformation of the Integral Term

**Step 1 — Start with the integral operator in $s$-domain:** $I(s) = \frac{1}{s} E(s)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the bilinear substitution:** $s \approx \frac{2}{T_s}\cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integral transfer function:**

$$I(z) = \frac{T_s}{2}\cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} E(z)$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply to get the difference equation:**

$$\left(1 - z^{-1}\right) I(z) = \frac{T_s}{2}\left(1 + z^{-1}\right) E(z)$$

**Step 5 — Convert from $z$-operator to time-domain samples using the shift property $z^{-1} I(z) \leftrightarrow I(k-1)$:**

$$I(k) - I(k-1) = \frac{T_s}{2}\bigl[e(k) + e(k-1)\bigr]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $I(k)$:**

$$\boxed{\;I(k) = I(k-1) + \frac{T_s}{2}\bigl[e(k) + e(k-1)\bigr]\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Tustin vs Forward Euler:** The Tustin (trapezoidal) integrator gives EXACT zero steady-state error for a ramp input (the rectangular integrator has a phase-lag error). This is why high-performance digital controllers (motor drives, aerospace) prefer Tustin discretization.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: 1-DOF vs 2-DOF

| Property | 1-DOF PID | 2-DOF PID (PI-D) |
|---|---|---|
| Independent tuning parameters | 3 ($K_p, T_i, T_d$) | 4 or 5 ($K_p, T_i, T_d, \beta, \gamma, N$) |
| Setpoint overshoot control | Coupled with disturbance rejection | Independent via $\beta, \gamma$ |
| Derivative kick | Present (if $D$ on $e$) | Eliminated ($\gamma = 0$) |
| Implementation complexity (LOC) | ~10 lines | ~25 lines |
| Computational load per sample | ~6 mults, ~5 adds | ~10 mults, ~9 adds |
| Memory (state) | 2 words | 4–5 words |
| Industry adoption | Legacy, simple loops | **Modern default** (ABB, Honeywell, Yokogawa, Siemens) |

### 3.5 Sample Calculation — Velocity-Form Coefficients

> **Given:** $K_p = 2.5$, $T_i = 10\,\text{s}$, $T_d = 2\,\text{s}$, $T_s = 0.1\,\text{s}$.

**Compute the three velocity-form coefficients:**

\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= K_p\left(1 + \frac{T_s}{T_i} + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\right) = 2.5 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.1}{10} + \frac{2}{0.1}\right) \\[4pt]
    &= 2.5 \times (1 + 0.01 + 20) = 2.5 \times 21.01 = 52.525 \\[8pt]
a_1 &= K_p\left(-1 + \frac{2T_d}{T_s}\right) = 2.5 \times \left(-1 + \frac{2 \times 2}{0.1}\right) \\[4pt]
    &= 2.5 \times (-1 + 40) = 2.5 \times 39 = 97.5 \\[8pt]
a_2 &= -K_p \frac{T_d}{T_s} = -2.5 \times \frac{2}{0.1} = -2.5 \times 20 = -50
\end{aligned}

**Verification — at steady state, $e(k) = e(k-1) = e(k-2) = e_\infty$:**

$$\Delta u(\infty) = 52.525 e_\infty + 97.5 e_\infty - 50 e_\infty = 100.025\, e_\infty$$

For a P-controller with $K_p = 2.5$, $\Delta u = 2.5 e_\infty$ — but because of the velocity form, $\Delta u$ being nonzero at constant $e$ indicates the integral is **correctly accumulating** (the P-action is constant, the I-action grows).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 2-DOF PID Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rIn[R] --> sumA
    yFB[Y] --> sumA
    sumA{Σ} --> eNode["e = r - y"]
    eNode --> pBranch["P: Kp × (β·r - y)"]
    eNode --> iBranch["I: Kp/Ti · ∫(r - y) dt"]
    yFB --> dBranch["D: Kp·Td·s/(1+Td·s/N) × (-y)"]
    pBranch --> adder{U}
    iBranch --> adder
    dBranch --> adder
    adder --> sat["Saturation clamp"]
    sat --> uOut["u"]
    uOut --> plant["Plant Gp(s)"]
    plant --> yOut["y → yFB (feedback)"]
    sat --> awfb["Anti-windup feedback"]
    awfb --> iBranch
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Microcontroller-Based Implementation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr[Power On] --> init["Init ADC, PWM, Timer, UART"]
    init --> waitInt["Wait for Timer ISR every Ts"]
    waitInt --> sampleY["ADC: read sensor → y(k)"]
    sampleY --> readR["Read setpoint r from HMI/EEPROM"]
    readR --> computeErr["e(k) = r(k) - y(k)"]
    computeErr --> pCalc["Compute P = Kp × β × r - Kp × y"]
    pCalc --> iCalc["I(k) = I(k-1) + Kp·Ts/Ti × e + Kaw × (usat - u)"]
    iCalc --> dCalc["D(k) = a·D(k-1) - Kp(1-a)·(y(k) - y(k-1))"]
    dCalc --> uCalc["u(k) = P + I(k) + D(k)"]
    uCalc --> clamp["usat(k) = clamp u(k) to 0-100%"]
    clamp --> pwmOut["Update PWM duty cycle = usat/100 × PR"]
    pwmOut --> shift["Shift states: y(k-1)←y(k), D(k-1)←D(k), I(k-1)←I(k), usat(k-1)←usat(k)"]
    shift --> waitInt
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Anti-Windup Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    iOut["I(k) raw"] --> sum
    uOut["u(k) raw"] --> sat
    sat --> usat["usat(k)"]
    usat --> sum
    sum --> diff["esat = usat - u"]
    diff --> kaw["× Kaw"]
    kaw --> addI["Add to next I(k+1) update"]
    addI --> iOut
```

### 4.4 Hardware Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensor["Physical Sensor\n(Thermocouple / RTD / LVDT / Encoder)"] --> sigCond["Signal Conditioning\n(Amplify + Filter)"]
    sigCond --> adc["12-bit ADC\n(Sample every Ts)"]
    adc --> mcu["Microcontroller\n(ARM Cortex-M / PIC / 8051)"]
    hmi["HMI: Keypad / Display"] --> mcu
    mcu --> pwmdrv["PWM / DAC Output Driver"]
    pwmdrv --> actuator["Actuator\n(Heater / Valve / Motor Driver)"]
    actuator --> plant["Plant / Process"]
    plant --> sensor
    mcu --> comm["Comm: UART / SPI / I²C / Modbus\n(SCADA / HMI / Remote Tuning)"]
    mcu --> eeprom["EEPROM / Flash\n(Persistent Kp, Ti, Td, β, γ)"]
    mcu --> wdt["Watchdog Timer\n(Fault recovery)"]
```

### 4.5 Signal-Flow Description

1. **Sensor stage:** Physical quantity (temperature, pressure, position, speed) is transduced into a low-level voltage/current.
2. **Signal conditioning:** Amplification, anti-aliasing low-pass filter (cutoff ≤ $f_s/2$ per Nyquist), linearization.
3. **ADC:** Quantized to 12–16 bits. Sampling is **timer-triggered** to guarantee fixed $T_s$.
4. **MCU core:** Runs the 2-DOF PID difference equation. Math is typically in **fixed-point Q-format** (e.g., Q15) for speed and determinism on cheap MCUs; FPU used on higher-end Cortex-M4/M7.
5. **Output stage:** PWM duty cycle or DAC voltage → actuator driver → physical valve/motor/heater.
6. **Feedback:** Sensor re-reads the effect; loop closes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between 1-DOF and 2-DOF PID controller with the help of a block diagram. Why is the 2-DOF structure preferred in modern industrial controllers?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 1-DOF PID | 2-DOF PID |
|---|---|---|
| Independent closed-loop transfer functions | 1 | 2 (setpoint response + disturbance response) |
| Setpoint weight | Implicit (1) | Explicit ($\beta, \gamma$) |
| Derivative kick | Present | Eliminated (with $\gamma=0$) |
| Tuning of setpoint vs disturbance | Coupled | Decoupled |

A **1-DOF PID** has a single tunable block: $U(s) = K_p\left[E(s) + \frac{1}{T_i s}E(s) + T_d s E(s)\right]$ with $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$. The closed-loop transfer $Y/R$ and the disturbance transfer $Y/D$ are both shaped by the same three gains.

A **2-DOF PID** has:

$$U(s) = K_p\left[\beta R(s) - Y(s)\right] + \frac{K_p}{T_i s}\left[R(s) - Y(s)\right] + \frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s}\left[\gamma R(s) - Y(s)\right]$$

The setpoint-tracking transfer $Y/R$ is shaped by $K_p, \beta, \gamma$ (and $T_d$ via filter), while the disturbance-rejection transfer $Y/D$ is shaped by $K_p, T_i, T_d$ acting on $-Y(s)$. The 2-DOF is preferred because (i) setpoint overshoot can be tuned independently of disturbance rejection, (ii) derivative kick is avoided, (iii) ISA 1996 PI-D form matches industrial practice.

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of "derivative kick" in a conventional PID controller. How does the 2-DOF structure eliminate it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivative Kick** is the sudden, large spike in the controller output $u(t)$ that occurs when the setpoint $r(t)$ changes abruptly (a step change), because the derivative term $T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} = T_d \frac{d[r(t) - y(t)]}{dt}$ contains a Dirac-like impulse whenever $r$ steps.

In a 1-DOF PID: $e(t)$ changes from $0$ to $r_{\text{step}}$ instantaneously, so $de/dt \to \infty$ at the step instant, causing the actuator to saturate and possibly damaging it.

In a 2-DOF PID with $\gamma = 0$ (PI-D form), the derivative acts **only on the measured output** $y(t)$, not on the setpoint:

$$D_{\text{action}} = \frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s}\bigl[\gamma R(s) - Y(s)\bigr] = -\frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s} Y(s) \quad \text{when } \gamma = 0$$

Since the plant output $y(t)$ cannot change instantaneously (it has finite bandwidth), $\frac{dy}{dt}$ is bounded and the derivative kick is **completely eliminated**.

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Part B - Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the velocity (incremental) form of a digital PID controller from the continuous-time ideal PID equation. Clearly state the discretization approximations used.

**(b) [7 marks]** A microcontroller-based digital 2-DOF PI-D controller is implemented with the following parameters: $K_p = 1.8$, $T_i = 8\,\text{s}$, $T_d = 1.5\,\text{s}$, $T_s = 0.05\,\text{s}$, $N = 10$. Compute:
   (i) The three velocity-form coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2$ for the 1-DOF equivalent.
   (ii) The filtered-derivative filter coefficient $a$ in the 2-DOF form.
   (iii) The first five PWM increments $\Delta u(k)$ if the error sequence is $e(0) = 0, e(1) = 5, e(2) = 8, e(3) = 7, e(4) = 5, e(5) = 3$ (in sensor units).

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Start with the continuous-time ideal PID:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_p T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Sample at $t = kT_s$: $u(k) = u(kT_s)$, $e(k) = e(kT_s)$.

**Approximation 1 — Backward-Euler integration:**
$$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \approx T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

**Approximation 2 — Backward-difference derivative:**
$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \approx \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

**Substitute** to get the discrete (positional) form:

$$u(k) = K_p e(k) + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr]$$

Compute $u(k-1)$ and subtract:

\begin{aligned}
\Delta u(k) &= u(k) - u(k-1) \\
&= K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]
\end{aligned}

Regroup the coefficients:

$$a_0 = K_p\!\left(1 + \frac{T_s}{T_i} + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\right),\quad a_1 = K_p\!\left(-1 + \frac{2T_d}{T_s}\right),\quad a_2 = -\frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}$$

Final velocity form: $\Delta u(k) = a_0 e(k) + a_1 e(k-1) + a_2 e(k-2)$.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Velocity-form coefficients:**

\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= 1.8 \left(1 + \frac{0.05}{8} + \frac{1.5}{0.05}\right) = 1.8 (1 + 0.00625 + 30) = 1.8 \times 31.00625 = 55.811 \\
a_1 &= 1.8 \left(-1 + \frac{2 \times 1.5}{0.05}\right) = 1.8 \times (-1 + 60) = 1.8 \times 59 = 106.2 \\
a_2 &= -1.8 \times \frac{1.5}{0.05} = -1.8 \times 30 = -54.0
\end{aligned}

**(ii) Derivative filter coefficient:**

$$a = \frac{T_d / N}{T_d / N + T_s} = \frac{1.5/10}{1.5/10 + 0.05} = \frac{0.15}{0.15 + 0.05} = \frac{0.15}{0.20} = 0.75$$

**(iii) Compute $\Delta u(k)$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$:**

$\Delta u(1) = 55.811 \times 5 + 106.2 \times 0 + (-54.0) \times 0 = 279.06$

$\Delta u(2) = 55.811 \times 8 + 106.2 \times 5 + (-54.0) \times 0 = 446.49 + 531.0 = 977.49$

$\Delta u(3) = 55.811 \times 7 + 106.2 \times 8 + (-54.0) \times 5 = 390.68 + 849.6 - 270.0 = 970.28$

$\Delta u(4) = 55.811 \times 5 + 106.2 \times 7 + (-54.0) \times 8 = 279.06 + 743.4 - 432.0 = 590.46$

$\Delta u(5) = 55.811 \times 3 + 106.2 \times 5 + (-54.0) \times 7 = 167.43 + 531.0 - 378.0 = 320.43$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget the **$e(k-2)$ term** in the velocity form, which represents the contribution of the **second-order difference** of the error (i.e., the acceleration of the error). Omitting it produces a non-PID controller that behaves like a PI with a first-order lead. **Full 3-tap is mandatory** for proper PID action.

---

#### **Part B - Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the block diagram of a 2-DOF PID controller. Explain the role of setpoint weights $\beta$ and $\gamma$. How does the 2-DOF structure decouple setpoint tracking from disturbance rejection?

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain how a 2-DOF PI-D controller is implemented on a microcontroller. Discuss the role of (i) anti-windup, (ii) bumpless transfer, and (iii) output saturation in the digital implementation.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Block diagram and theory (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (textual description):**

Three parallel branches take inputs from $R$ and $Y$ separately:
- **P branch:** $K_p[\beta R - Y]$ → setpoint enters with weight $\beta$.
- **I branch:** $\frac{K_p}{T_i s}[R - Y]$ → setpoint enters with full weight 1.
- **D branch:** $\frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s}[\gamma R - Y]$ → setpoint enters with weight $\gamma$.

**Role of $\beta$ (proportional setpoint weight, $0 \le \beta \le 1$):**
- Controls **how aggressively** the controller responds to a setpoint change.
- $\beta = 1$: full proportional action on $r$ — fast setpoint response, but risk of overshoot.
- $\beta = 0$: P-action only on measurement — soft, overdamped setpoint response, no proportional overshoot.
- Typical $\beta = 0.4$ to $0.6$ — a balanced choice.

**Role of $\gamma$ (derivative setpoint weight, $0 \le \gamma \le 1$):**
- Controls whether the derivative term sees the setpoint.
- $\gamma = 0$: derivative on measurement only (PI-D form, the industry default).
- $\gamma = 1$: derivative on error (1-DOF behavior, with derivative kick).
- $\gamma = 0$ is preferred to **eliminate derivative kick**.

**Decoupling of setpoint tracking and disturbance rejection:**
- The transfer function from $R$ to $U$ depends on $\beta$ and $\gamma$, but the transfer from disturbance $D$ (acting at plant input) to $U$ depends only on $-Y$ paths — completely independent.
- This means the **setpoint response** is shaped by $K_p, \beta, \gamma$ while the **load-disturbance rejection** is shaped by $K_p, T_i, T_d$. You can tune them independently — no compromise.

**(b) Microcontroller implementation (7 marks):**

**Flowchart (textual):**

1. **Power-up initialization:** Configure ADC (channel, resolution, sample time), PWM (frequency, resolution), Timer (period = $T_s$), UART, watchdog.
2. **Enter main loop or wait for Timer ISR.** The Timer ISR fires every $T_s$ — this guarantees a **fixed sampling period** regardless of computation time variability.
3. **Sample sensor:** Trigger ADC, wait for conversion, read $y(k)$.
4. **Read setpoint:** From HMI, EEPROM, or communication bus — $r(k)$.
5. **Compute error:** $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$.
6. **Compute P-term:** $P(k) = K_p[\beta r(k) - y(k)]$.
7. **Update I-term with anti-windup:** $I(k) = I(k-1) + (K_p T_s / T_i) e(k) + K_{\text{aw}} (u_{\text{sat}}(k-1) - u(k-1))$.
8. **Compute filtered D-term:** $D(k) = a D(k-1) - K_p(1-a)[y(k) - y(k-1)]$ where $a = T_d/N / (T_d/N + T_s)$.
9. **Sum:** $u(k) = P(k) + I(k) + D(k)$.
10. **Saturate:** $u_{\text{sat}}(k) = \text{clamp}(u(k), u_{\min}, u_{\max})$.
11. **Output:** PWM duty cycle $\propto u_{\text{sat}}(k)$, or DAC voltage.
12. **State update for next sample:** $y(k-1) \leftarrow y(k)$, $D(k-1) \leftarrow D(k)$, $I(k-1) \leftarrow I(k)$, $u_{\text{sat}}(k-1) \leftarrow u_{\text{sat}}(k)$.
13. Return to step 2.

**Role of the three key features:**

**(i) Anti-windup:**
When the actuator saturates ($u > u_{\max}$ or $u < u_{\min}$), the integrator must NOT keep accumulating error — otherwise on coming out of saturation, the system will exhibit a large overshoot called **integrator windup**. Back-calculation feeds back the saturation error $(u_{\text{sat}} - u)$ to the integrator at rate $K_{\text{aw}}$ (typically $K_{\text{aw}} = 1/T_t$ where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant, often $T_t = \sqrt{T_i T_d}$).

**(ii) Bumpless transfer:**
When the operator switches the controller from MANUAL to AUTO mode, the output must NOT jump suddenly. In velocity form, $\Delta u$ is computed from current error — there is no jump by construction. In positional form, $I(k)$ must be pre-loaded with the current manual output to ensure continuity.

**(iii) Output saturation:**
Real actuators have physical limits (valve 0–100%, motor ±10 V, PWM 0–255). The controller output must be **clamped** before being sent to the actuator. The saturated value $u_{\text{sat}}$ is the one sent to the plant, but the unsaturated $u$ is used for the integrator anti-windup feedback. This is the **"clamping + tracking"** architecture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Exam Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing the structure** — Drawing 1-DOF and labeling it 2-DOF. Always show the $\beta$ and $\gamma$ paths explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the filter** on the derivative — pure differentiator is unrealizable and amplifies high-frequency noise. The first-order filter $\frac{T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s}$ with $N \in [3, 20]$ is essential.
> 3. **Missing anti-windup** — Many textbook solutions show the PID without anti-windup; the **KTU examiner will deduct 2 marks** for this in a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Wrong discretization** — using forward Euler for the integral gives a *lagging* integrator; use backward Euler or Tustin for the integral term.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: 2-DOF PID**

- [ ] A **Degree of Freedom** in control = the number of **independently tunable** closed-loop transfer functions.
- [ ] 1-DOF PID: only ONE tuning set $(K_p, T_i, T_d)$ for both setpoint and disturbance.
- [ ] 2-DOF PID: TWO independent tuning paths — one for setpoint, one for measurement.
- [ ] The 2-DOF controller equation:
$$U(s) = K_p\!\left[\beta R - Y\right] + \frac{K_p}{T_i s}\!\left[R - Y\right] + \frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s}\!\left[\gamma R - Y\right]$$
- [ ] **Setpoint weights:** $\beta$ (P-weight, 0–1), $\gamma$ (D-weight, 0–1), I-weight is implicitly 1.
- [ ] **PI-D form** ($\beta=1, \gamma=0$) is the **industrial standard** (ISA 1996).
- [ ] **Derivative kick** is eliminated by setting $\gamma = 0$ (derivative on measurement only).
- [ ] **Derivative filter** $\frac{T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s}$ prevents noise amplification; $N \in [3, 20]$.
- [ ] **Velocity form** is preferred for microcontrollers (bumpless transfer, anti-windup, numerical robustness):
$$\Delta u(k) = a_0 e(k) + a_1 e(k-1) + a_2 e(k-2)$$
- [ ] **Anti-windup** uses back-calculation: $I(k) = I(k-1) + (\text{normal update}) + K_{\text{aw}}(u_{\text{sat}} - u)$.
- [ ] **Sampling theorem for control:** $T_s \le 0.1 / \omega_c$ (i.e., $f_s \ge 10 \times$ closed-loop bandwidth).
- [ ] **Discretization methods:** Backward Euler (simple, stable for integrators) and Tustin/Bilinear (better phase accuracy, exact for ramp).
- [ ] Tustin prewarping: $\omega_d = \frac{2}{T_s}\tan(\omega_a T_s / 2)$ to preserve frequency response at a critical frequency.
- [ ] **Microcontroller implementation**: fixed-point arithmetic (Q15/Q31) on cheap MCUs, FPU on Cortex-M4/M7.
- [ ] **Real-time guarantee**: Use hardware timer ISR for sampling; never use software delay loops.
- [ ] **Bumpless transfer**: ensured automatically by velocity form; in positional form, pre-load $I(0)$ with manual output.
- [ ] **Key equation to memorize for exam**:
$$U(s) = K_p\!\left[\beta R(s) - Y(s)\right] + \frac{K_p}{T_i s}\!\left[R(s) - Y(s)\right] - \frac{K_p T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N)s} Y(s)$$
- [ ] The **2-DOF PI-D** has the I-term and P-term on error, D-term on measurement.
- [ ] Watchdog timer, EEPROM storage of $K_p, T_i, T_d, \beta, \gamma$, and communication (UART/Modbus) for SCADA integration.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic: "Beta-Gamma, I-on-Error, D-on-Measurement"** — This is the PI-D 2-DOF form in one breath. Memorize this for the KTU exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontroller-Based Data Logger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microcontroller-Based Data Logger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Microcontroller-Based Data Logger** is an embedded electronic instrumentation system that uses a microcontroller as its central processing unit to automatically acquire, time-stamp, process, store, and/or transmit measurements from one or more sensors over a defined period of time. According to the KTU 2024 PEAET795 syllabus (Module 3), the data logger is a stand-alone, battery-operable instrument that combines **signal conditioning**, **analog-to-digital conversion (ADC)**, **non-volatile memory**, and **a real-time clock (RTC)** under firmware control to produce a permanent digital record of physical phenomena.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The student must be able to design the **hardware architecture**, **firmware flow**, **sampling strategy**, **memory budgeting**, and **communication interface** (UART / I2C / SPI / USB) of a microcontroller-based data logger. Marks are heavily awarded for the *block diagram*, *ADC selection*, *sampling theorem application*, and *memory/storage calculation*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **dedicated hospital nurse** stationed beside a patient's bed:

- The nurse (the **microcontroller**) wakes up at fixed intervals (the **sampling clock**).
- She reads the patient's temperature and pulse through medical instruments (the **sensors**).
- She writes the readings in a register with the exact date and time (the **memory + RTC**).
- At the end of the day, the doctor reviews the entire register on a clipboard (the **communication interface / display**).

The nurse never sleeps, never forgets, never writes the wrong time, and never runs out of paper until her shift ends (the **battery budget**). A *microcontroller-based data logger* is the electronic version of this tireless nurse — applied to weather stations, cold-chain pharma transport, structural health monitoring of bridges, automotive ECU diagnostics, and industrial process logging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A data logger is an *electronic black-box journal* that converts the analog world into digital numbers, tags each number with a timestamp, and stores them safely for later analysis — all orchestrated by a single microcontroller chip.

---

## 1.3 Primary Functions of the System

1. **Acquisition** – Converting physical quantities (temperature, pressure, strain, flow) into electrical signals.
2. **Conditioning** – Amplifying, offsetting, and filtering sensor signals.
3. **Digitization** – Converting analog voltages to binary numbers using an ADC.
4. **Time-Stamping** – Attaching calendar-time information from an RTC.
5. **Storage** – Writing samples to non-volatile memory (Flash / EEPROM / SD card).
6. **Retrieval / Display** – Showing live or stored data on LCD / sending to PC.
7. **Power Management** – Sleeping between samples to conserve battery.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Data Logger Design

The KTU 2024 evaluator expects the following quantitative metrics to appear in any design answer:

- **Sampling Rate** $f_s$ in **samples per second (S/s or Hz)**.
- **ADC Resolution** in **bits** (typical: 8, 10, 12, 16, 24).
- **Dynamic Range** in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Storage Capacity** in **bytes, kilobytes, megabytes**.
- **Battery Life** in **hours / days / months**.
- **Total System Current** in **milliampere (mA)**.
- **Operating Voltage** typically **3.3 V** or **5 V DC**.
