**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three classical ESD models** (HBM, MM, CDM) and explain their typical applications. Compare the peak currents and discharge times for a 5 kV pre-charge voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **stored electrostatic energy** in a human body model for a 5 kV pre-charge. If a TVS diode of breakdown voltage 6 V and dynamic resistance 0.5 Ω is used to clamp the discharge, calculate the clamping current and the energy dissipated by the TVS. Comment on why a hybrid grounding scheme is preferred in mixed-signal instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Three ESD Models

The three classical ESD models are:

1. **Human Body Model (HBM):** Represents a charged person touching a device. Equivalent circuit: 100 pF capacitor in series with 1.5 kΩ resistor. Used for component-level qualification.

2. **Machine Model (MM):** Represents a charged metallic tool or fixture. Equivalent circuit: 200 pF capacitor in series with 0 Ω (no resistance). Produces a damped-oscillatory discharge, more severe than HBM.

3. **Charged Device Model (CDM):** Represents a charged IC package itself. Equivalent circuit: 4 pF capacitor with very low inductance. Used for manufacturing-handling qualification.

**Peak current for 5 kV pre-charge:**

$$I_{\text{HBM}} = \frac{5000}{1500} \approx 3.33\,\text{A} \;\; (\text{decay time } \tau \approx 150\,\text{ns})$$

$$I_{\text{MM}} = \frac{5000}{0.001} \approx 5000\,\text{A (peak of oscillation)} \;\; (\text{very short duration})$$

$$I_{\text{CDM}} \approx 5–10\,\text{A peak with sub-nanosecond rise time}$$

The HBM is the most common in real-world field service, while MM and CDM stress ICs more aggressively during automated assembly. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Energy and TVS Calculation

**Step 1 — Stored energy at 5 kV**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2}(100 \times 10^{-12})(5000)^2 = 1.25\,\text{mJ}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage across TVS during clamping**
Assuming the source impedance of 1.5 kΩ and the TVS clamps at 6 V, the clamping current is:

$$I_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{V_{\text{source}} - V_{\text{clamp}}}{R_{\text{HBM}} + R_{\text{TVS}}} = \frac{5000 - 6}{1500 + 0.5} \approx 3.33\,\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated in TVS**

$$P_{\text{TVS}} = V_{\text{clamp}} \times I_{\text{clamp}} = 6 \times 3.33 = 20\,\text{W (peak)}$$

**Step 4 — Energy integrated over discharge window**
Since $\tau \approx 150$ ns, an approximate energy delivered to the TVS:

$$E_{\text{TVS}} \approx P_{\text{peak}} \times \tau = 20 \times 150 \times 10^{-9} = 3\,\mu\text{J}$$

This is a small fraction (0.24%) of the total 1.25 mJ because the bulk of the energy is dissipated in the HBM resistance and not in the TVS.

**Hybrid Grounding in Mixed-Signal Systems:**
Mixed-signal PCBs combine low-level analog (µV-level) and fast-edge digital (ns-edge) signals. A single ground topology cannot optimize both:

- Analog returns must be **single-point** to avoid injecting digital switching noise into the ADC input.
- Digital returns must be **multi-point** to minimize ground bounce and lead inductance.

The compromise is a **hybrid ground**: separate analog and digital ground planes joined at a single point (the ADC's AGND pin), after which they become a unified chassis/earth ground. This topology is universally adopted in modern PLC analog input modules and data-acquisition boards. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **Faraday cage principle** and its application in shielding instrumentation enclosures. With reference to the skin-depth formula, justify why a thin copper-plated sheet is more effective at RF than at low frequencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-meter instrumentation cable carries a 4–20 mA signal from a field sensor. Discuss the **complete shield termination strategy** you would adopt to protect the loop against ESD, including the role of the drain wire, shield bonding, and chassis ground. State two relevant IEC standards. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Faraday Cage and Skin Depth

A **Faraday cage** is an enclosed conductive shell. When an external electrostatic field is applied, the free electrons in the conductor redistribute such that the electric field inside the cavity is zero. This principle is exploited in instrumentation enclosures to keep external ESD-induced fields from reaching sensitive internal circuits.

For a plane wave, the **skin depth** of a shield material is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

As frequency $\omega$ increases, $\delta$ decreases. At 100 MHz, the skin depth of copper is roughly **6.6 µm**; even a 35 µm copper plating provides ~5 skin-depths of absorption loss, yielding >100 dB of attenuation.

At low frequencies (e.g., 50 Hz), $\delta$ for copper is ~9 mm, so a 1 mm sheet is too thin. This is why low-frequency magnetic shielding requires **high-permeability materials** (μ-metal, permalloy) rather than just copper.

**Justification for ESD:** An ESD event contains spectral content from DC up to several GHz. The high-frequency components are attenuated by the skin effect in copper, but the low-frequency quasi-static component is not. This is why **shielding must be paired with proper grounding** — the shield itself must be tied to a low-impedance earth path so that the low-frequency current component is also diverted. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Cable Shield Termination Strategy

For a 4–20 mA current loop:

1. **Use a shielded twisted-pair (STP) cable.** Twisting cancels magnetically-coupled noise; the shield handles capacitively-coupled and radiated noise.

2. **Terminate the shield at one end only — typically the receiver (control-room) side.** This prevents ground loops if the field sensor and the receiver are at different earth potentials. A common alternative for high-noise environments is **double-ended grounding with a heavy-gauge bonding conductor** between the two chassis.

3. **Connect the drain wire to the receiver's chassis ground** through a low-inductance bond (preferably a 360° backshell clamp, not a "pigtail" pigtail which adds ~10 nH of inductance per cm and defeats HF shielding).

4. **Insert a TVS diode and a 100 Ω series resistor** at the receiver's input to clamp any residual ESD surge.

5. **Maintain shield continuity through any junction boxes** — never break the shield at intermediate terminal blocks.

6. **Bond the instrument chassis to the protective earth (PE) bus** with a short, fat conductor (≤ 100 mm length, ≥ 2.5 mm² cross-section).

**Relevant Standards:**
- **IEC 61000-4-2:** ESD immunity test methodology (air discharge up to ±15 kV, contact discharge ±8 kV).
- **IEC 61000-4-4:** Electrical Fast Transient (EFT/burst) immunity — closely related to ESD.
- **IEC 61326-1:** EMC requirements for electrical equipment for measurement, control, and laboratory use — directly applicable to industrial instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "ground" and "earth":** Earth is a physical connection to soil; ground is a *reference node* in a circuit. They are not the same. Using them interchangeably will cost you 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in formulas:** $C$ in pF vs F changes peak-current answers by a factor of 1 × 10¹². Always write units.
> 3. **Pigtail terminations:** Examiners specifically look for whether the student mentions that a "pigtail" drain wire is poor practice because of its high-frequency inductance. This is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Single ground topology applied everywhere:** Don't state that one topology suits all designs. Always tie your grounding choice to the *frequency* of operation.
> 5. **Skipping the standard reference:** Naming IEC 61000-4-2 is a 1-mark quick win that most students omit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ESD is a **sub-nanosecond** transient that can deliver **3–30 A peak currents** into instrumentation inputs.
- The **Human Body Model** is the canonical representation: **100 pF** + **1.5 kΩ**.
- Three models — HBM, MM, CDM — represent **people, machines, and the IC itself**, respectively.
- Grounding topologies: **single-point** for low-frequency analog, **multi-point** for high-frequency digital, **hybrid** for mixed-signal PCBs.
- **Shielding effectiveness** is measured in dB; copper at 100 MHz gives > 1000 dB absorption.
- The **skin depth** $\delta$ is the depth at which the field decays to **1/e** (~37%); thinner-than-δ coatings are ineffective.
- Always **terminate shields with 360° backshell clamps**, never with pigtails.
- Connect shields at **one end** to break ground loops, unless double-ended bonding is justified by the noise environment.
- **TVS diodes** are the workhorse active protection; **ferrite beads** handle high-frequency ringing.
- Bonding the instrument chassis to **protective earth (PE)** with a short, fat conductor is mandatory for safety and EMC.
- Relevant standards to memorize: **IEC 61000-4-2, IEC 61000-4-4, IEC 61326-1, MIL-STD-461**.
- For a 5 kV HBM pre-charge: stored energy = **1.25 mJ**, peak current ≈ **3.33 A**, time constant = **150 ns**.
- ESD effects on ICs: **latch-up, gate-oxide rupture, data corruption, parameter drift**.
- Mixed-signal PCBs use **separate analog and digital ground planes joined at the ADC**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Alarm and Annunciation Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Alarm and Annunciation Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Alarm and Annunciation Circuit** is a dedicated, fail-safe hardware and/or software sub-system within an industrial process control architecture whose function is to **detect abnormal process conditions** (exceeding pre-defined trip setpoints), **classify** the abnormality by priority, **display** it visually and acoustically to the operator, and **latch/reset** the event according to a pre-defined annunciator sequence specified under **ISA 18.1 / IEC 62682** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> An annunciator is an *enunciator* of abnormal process conditions. It consists of three functional blocks:
> 1. **Input Module** – accepts contact-closure (potential-free) or analog signals.
> 2. **Logic Module** – executes the chosen annunciator sequence (A, M, F, AF, A-4, etc.).
> 3. **Output Module** – drives the visual indicators (lamps, LEDs, beacons) and the audible device (horn, buzzer, siren).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are driving a car. The **dashboard warning lights** (oil pressure, engine temperature, fuel level, seat-belt) and the **buzzer** that beeps when you forget to fasten your seat-belt together form an **annunciation system**.

- The **fuel sensor** (analog level) and **oil pressure switch** (discrete contact) are the *inputs*.
- The car's **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** processes these inputs against pre-set thresholds – this is the *logic*.
- The **red dashboard lamp** and **beeping sound** are the *outputs* that grab your attention.
- When you press the **acknowledge button** on the steering wheel, the beep stops but the light stays on – this is the *Manual Acknowledge (M-sequence)*.

The industrial annunciator is essentially the **"scaled-up, engineered, certified, and explosion-proof"** version of this car dashboard, designed for continuous operation in harsh chemical, power, and refinery plants where missed alarms can cost lives and millions of rupees.

## 1.3 Standard Reference

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with the **International Society of Automation (ISA) Standard 18.1 – "Annunciator Sequences and Specifications"**. The standard lists **12 basic sequences** denoted by letter codes (A, M, F, AF, AM, A-1…A-4, M-1…M-4). Students must memorize at least **A, M, F, and AF/MF (first-out)** for the board examination.

## 1.4 Why This Circuit is Critical in Engineering

| Industry Sector | Typical Use |
|---|---|
| Power Plants (Boiler, Turbine) | Trip annunciation, furnace flame-fail |
| Oil & Gas (Refinery) | Over-pressure, gas detection, ESD |
| Chemical Process | Reactor temperature runaway |
| Nuclear | Criticality safety, radiation alarms |
| Water Treatment | Pump failure, level alarms |

A non-functional or poorly designed annunciator directly caused the **Bhopal (1984)** and **Three Mile Island (1979)** disasters – a key reason the KTU curriculum mandates study of annunciator logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an Annunciator

A complete annunciator system contains **three fundamental sub-systems** working in cascade:

### 2.1.1 Input Section
- **Potential-free (dry) contact** inputs (NO or NC).
- **Active voltage inputs** (24 V DC, 110 V AC) – these need opto-isolation.
- **Analog inputs** (4-20 mA) routed through a comparator/Schmitt trigger.
- Typical input must be **noise-filtered** using an RC debounce filter with time-constant $\tau = RC$.

### 2.1.2 Logic Section
The heart of the system. Implemented using:
- **Electromechanical relays** (legacy, still found in KTU 2024 syllabus questions).
- **Hard-wired CMOS/TTL gates** (discrete logic).
- **PLC ladder logic** (modern installations).
- **Microcontroller/Dedicated Annunciator ICs** (e.g., National Semiconductor LM1810 – historical reference).

### 2.1.3 Output Section
- **Lamp Drivers**: Each alarm "window" has two lamps (one for ON, one for OFF/ACK state) – driven by separate drivers.
- **Audible**: Piezo-horn or electro-mechanical buzzer, with a 2nd-tone acknowledge silencer.
- **Relay Outputs**: To SCADA / DCS / Trip logic.

## 2.2 ISA 18.1 Standard Annunciator Sequences (KTU High-Yield)

The single most exam-important classification. Memorize the table below.

| Sequence | Flashes on Trip? | Audible Sounds? | Returns to Normal Automatically? | Acknowledge Required? | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** (Automatic) | Yes | Yes | Yes, when process returns to normal | No (self-ack) | Non-critical alarms (level-normal) |
| **M** (Manual) | Yes | Yes | No – stays latched | **Yes** | Critical process trips |
| **F** (Follow-up) | No (steady) | No | Yes, immediate | No | High/low indicators |
| **AF** (Auto-First-out) | Yes (first only) | Yes | No | Yes | Boiler light-up sequence |
| **MF** (Manual-First-out) | Yes (first only) | Yes | No | Yes | Turbine startup |
| **A-4 / M-4** | Yes | Yes | Yes, with **time delay** | Depends | Filtered / debounced alarms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"First-Out"** means: When multiple alarms arrive in rapid succession, the **first one to trip flashes** while all subsequent ones appear **steady**. This tells the operator the **root cause** of the cascade.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trip Setpoint | $S_{trip} = S_{nom} + k \cdot \sigma$ | Setpoint = nominal + safety margin | engineering unit (bar, °C) |
| Hysteresis Band | $H = \text{SP}_{high} - \text{SP}_{low}$ | Prevents relay "chatter" at the threshold | engineering unit |
| RC Debounce Time | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Filter time-constant for contact de-bounce | seconds |
| Time-Delay Sequence | $t_{A\text{-}4} = R_t \cdot C_t$ | Set using a 555-timer or software counter | seconds |
| Relay Coil Voltage | $V_{coil} = I_{coil} \cdot R_{coil}$ | Must match supply, derated 10% | Volts (V) |
| Boolean Alarm Logic (OR) | $Y = A + B$ | Either input trips the alarm | Boolean (0/1) |
| Boolean Alarm Logic (AND) | $Y = A \cdot B$ | Both conditions must trip | Boolean (0/1) |
| Latch Equation (S-R Flip-Flop) | $Q_{n+1} = S + \overline{R} \cdot Q_n$ | Memory of past alarm | Boolean |
| Horn Current | $I_{horn} = \dfrac{V_{supply}}{R_{horn}}$ | For driver transistor sizing | Amperes |
| Lamp Power | $P_{lamp} = V \cdot I$ | Each window lamp dissipation | Watts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Escape Notice:** Absolute value bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are intentionally rendered using `\vert` in the table above to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax, as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.4 Design Philosophy: Fail-Safe Principle

> [!WARNING]
> The cardinal rule of alarm circuit design: **The circuit MUST be in a SAFE state when energized, and produce an ALARM when power is lost or the contact opens on a fail-safe basis.** A normally-energized (NE) relay that drops out on power failure ensures a loss of power is treated as an alarm condition – never a "normal" indication.

## 2.5 Components of the Visual Display

1. **Lamp Test Push-Button** – allows verifying all lamps before plant startup.
2. **Acknowledge Push-Button** – silences horn, returns flashing to steady ON.
3. **Reset Push-Button** – clears the alarm after the process returns to normal (Manual sequence).
4. **First-Out Group Selector** – for multi-group first-out systems.
5. **Repeater Outputs** – 4-20 mA or Modbus to the central DCS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Designing the RC Debounce Filter for the Trip Input

A mechanical contact "bounces" for typically 1 ms to 10 ms during closure. If fed directly to a logic gate, it produces multiple false edges. We must filter it.

**Step 1: State the requirement**
We want the output to be "clean" $T_{clean} = 5$ ms after the first contact closure.

**Step 2: Choose a first-order RC low-pass filter**
The output voltage across the capacitor during a step input of amplitude $V$ follows:

$$V_{out}(t) = V \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 3: Define the switching threshold**
For a CMOS Schmitt-trigger gate (e.g., 74HC14), the input must reach 50% of $V$ to switch:

$$V_{out}(T_{clean}) = 0.5 \cdot V$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\tau$**
Substituting the threshold condition:

$$0.5 \cdot V = V \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-T_{clean}/\tau} \right)$$

Dividing both sides by $V$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-T_{clean}/\tau}$$

Rearranging:

$$e^{-T_{clean}/\tau} = 0.5$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\dfrac{T_{clean}}{\tau} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

Therefore:

$$\tau = \dfrac{T_{clean}}{0.693}$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values**
With $T_{clean} = 5$ ms:

$$\tau = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693} \approx 7.21 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6: Choose R and C components**
A standard resistor value $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ is chosen. Then:

$$C = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{7.21 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{3}} = 0.721 \text{ μF}$$

We select the next standard capacitor value, $C = 0.82 \text{ μF}$ (or $1 \text{ μF}$ for safety).

**Final Design**: $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1 \text{ μF}$, providing $\tau = 10$ ms – well above the 5 ms requirement.

## 3.2 Derivation: Latching Logic Using an S-R Flip-Flop (M-Sequence Memory)

A Manual Acknowledge sequence requires the alarm to **latch** until reset. This is implemented with an S-R (Set-Reset) flip-flop.

**Step 1: Identify inputs**
- $S$ (Set) = process trip signal (after debounce).
- $R$ (Reset) = push-button "Acknowledge + Reset".
- $Q$ (Output) = drives the lamp/horn logic.

**Step 2: Write the characteristic equation**
For a NAND-based S-R latch with active-low inputs:

$$Q_{n+1} = \overline{S}_n \cdot \overline{R}_n \cdot Q_n \; + \; \overline{S}_n \cdot R_n \cdot \overline{Q}_n$$

Simplifying, when the latch is enabled ($\overline{S}_n = 1$):

$$Q_{n+1} = R_n \; + \; \overline{R}_n \cdot Q_n$$

**Step 3: Map to alarm sequence**
- On trip: $S = 0$ (active), $R = 1$ (inactive) $\Rightarrow Q$ becomes $1$ → lamp ON, horn ON.
- On Acknowledge: $R = 0$ momentarily, $Q$ latches; horn silenced, lamp steady (need additional gating for flashing).
- On Process Normal + Reset: $S = 1$ (released), $R = 0$ momentarily $\Rightarrow Q = 0$ → lamp OFF.

**Step 4: Derive the audible silencer equation**
Let $H$ = horn output, $L$ = lamp output, $A$ = acknowledge input, $F$ = flash oscillator (square wave at 1 Hz).

$$H = Q \cdot \overline{A} \quad \text{(horn sounds while alarm is set and unacknowledged)}$$

$$L = Q \cdot F \; + \; Q \cdot A \cdot \overline{F} \quad \text{(flashes until acknowledged, then steady ON)}$$

This is the **M-sequence lamp/horn equation** expected in the KTU 14-mark derivation question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Annunciator Sequence for Process Applications

| Process Scenario | Required Operator Action | Recommended Sequence | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace flame failure | Investigate immediately, hold state | **M** (Manual) | Operator must verify before reset |
| Tank level high-high | Immediate auto-shutdown | **AF** (First-out) | First cause identification |
| Bearing temperature high | Auto-trend, no immediate trip | **A-4** (with delay) | Filters noise spikes |
| Flow low | Continuous display | **F** (Follow-up) | Operator awareness only |
| Boiler drum level – 3-element | Identify first deviation | **MF** (Manual First-out) | Critical during startup |
| Pump starter contactor | Just monitor | **A** (Automatic) | No latch needed |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: First-Out Logic with Two Inputs

A boiler has two trip inputs: $A$ (atomizing steam pressure low) and $B$ (fuel oil pressure low). Design the logic such that whichever trips **first** flashes, and the other is **steady**.

**Step 1: Boolean variables**
- $A$ = atomizing-steam-low (1 = tripped).
- $B$ = fuel-oil-low (1 = tripped).
- $F$ = flash oscillator (1 Hz square wave).
- $L_A$, $L_B$ = lamp outputs.

**Step 2: First-out condition**
Input $A$ is the "first-out" if $A$ is high AND $B$ was low at the moment $A$ triggered. Define a memory bit $M_A$:

$$M_A = A \cdot \overline{B} \cdot \overline{M_A} \; + \; M_A \cdot \overline{\text{RESET}}$$

**Step 3: Lamp equations**

$$L_A = A \cdot F \; + \; A \cdot \overline{F} \cdot \text{ACK} \quad \text{(flashes if first, then steady)}$$

$$L_A = A \cdot F \cdot M_A \; + \; A \cdot M_A \cdot \text{ACK} \; + \; A \cdot \overline{M_A}$$

Similarly for $L_B$ with $M_B$. The horn:

$$H = (A + B) \cdot \overline{\text{ACK}}$$

This logic can be implemented with **two flip-flops and four NAND gates**, a typical 14-mark problem in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Annunciator Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS1[Field Sensor Contact NO]
    FS2[Field Sensor Contact NC]
    IF1[Input Filter RC and Opto Isolator]
    IF2[Input Filter RC and Opto Isolator]
    LC[Logic Core Sequence Engine M or A]
    MEM[Memory Latch S-R Flip Flop]
    FLSH[Flash Oscillator 1 Hz]
    DRV1[Lamp Driver Lamp Window]
    DRV2[Horn Driver Audible]
    ACK[ACK Push Button]
    RST[Reset Push Button]

    FS1 --> IF1
    FS2 --> IF2
    IF1 --> LC
    IF2 --> LC
    LC --> MEM
    FLSH --> LC
    ACK --> LC
    RST --> MEM
    MEM --> DRV1
    LC --> DRV2
```

## 4.2 Logic Flow: M-Sequence (Manual Acknowledge) State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> Flashing: Trip contact closes S=1
    Flashing --> Flashing: Process still abnormal
    Flashing --> SteadyON: Operator presses ACK
    SteadyON --> SteadyON: Process still abnormal
    SteadyON --> Normal: Process normal AND Reset pressed
    Flashing --> Flashing: Acknowledge ignored until process normal
    Normal --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note the strict two-step reset: the alarm latches in **Steady-ON** state after acknowledgement and only transitions back to **Normal** when (a) the process has physically returned to normal AND (b) the operator presses the **Reset** push-button. This is the textbook Manual-acknowledge ISA-M sequence.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: First-Out (AF) Annunciator Group

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Group 1 Sensor 1]
    G2[Group 1 Sensor 2]
    G3[Group 1 Sensor 3]
    FFLG[First Out Latch Bank]
    FOSC[Flash Oscillator 1 Hz shared]
    AND1[AND gate]
    AND2[AND gate]
    AND3[AND gate]
    LP1[Lamp 1 Window Red]
    LP2[Lamp 2 Window Red]
    LP3[Lamp 3 Window Red]
    HORN[Horn Driver]
    ACKBTN[Acknowledge Button]

    G1 --> AND1
    G2 --> AND2
    G3 --> AND3
    AND1 --> FFLG
    AND2 --> FFLG
    AND3 --> FFLG
    FFLG --> AND1
    FFLG --> AND2
    FFLG --> AND3
    FOSC --> AND1
    FOSC --> AND2
    FOSC --> AND3
    FFLG --> HORN
    ACKBTN --> HORN
    AND1 --> LP1
    AND2 --> LP2
    AND3 --> LP3
```

## 4.4 Schematic Representation of a Single-Channel M-Sequence Relay Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[24V DC Supply]
    K1[Coil of Trip Relay K1]
    K1C[NC contact of K1 fail-safe]
    K2[Coil of Latch Relay K2]
    K2NO[NO contact of K2 self-hold]
    K3[Coil of Horn Relay K3]
    K3NO[NO contact of K3]
    LMP[Alarm Lamp 24V]
    HRN[Horn 24V]
    ACK[ACK Push Button NO]
    RST[Reset Push Button NO]

    V1 --- K1
    K1 --- K1C
    K1C --- K2
    K2 --- K2NO
    K2NO --- K2
    K2 --- RST
    RST --- V1
    K2 --- K3
    K3 --- K3NO
    K3NO --- HRN
    K2 --- LMP
    ACK --- K3
```

**Operating sequence of the relay schematic:**

1. Under normal conditions, the trip contact (fail-safe NC) is closed. Trip relay K1 is energized; the NC contact K1C is held open, preventing K2 from energizing.
2. On a trip (process high), the contact opens, K1 de-energizes, K1C closes, and K2 latches ON via its own NO contact (self-hold).
3. The lamp LMP lights (steady, since K3 has not yet picked up).
4. The horn HRN is energized via K3 (closed while not yet acknowledged).
5. Pressing ACK opens the horn driver K3, silencing the horn; the lamp stays ON because K2 remains latched.
6. Once the process returns to normal AND the operator presses RESET, K2 releases, and the lamp extinguishes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fail-safe NC** trip contact is what guarantees the *Bhopal-proof* behaviour: any wire break in the field is interpreted as a trip.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an annunciator. List the four most common ISA 18.1 sequences. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

An **annunciator** is a hardware/logic system that monitors process variables, compares them with preset trip levels, and provides visual (lamps) and audible (horn) indication of abnormal conditions along with acknowledge and reset functions.

The four most common ISA 18.1 sequences are:

1. **A-sequence (Automatic)** – Flashing audible-visual; returns to normal automatically when the process variable returns to its normal range. No operator action needed.
2. **M-sequence (Manual Acknowledge)** – Flashing until the operator acknowledges; latches in steady-ON state and requires manual reset after process normal.
3. **F-sequence (Follow-up)** – Steady ON while abnormal, automatic return to OFF when normal; no audible, no flashing.
4. **AF-sequence (Automatic First-Out)** – When several alarms occur simultaneously, the **first** to trip is distinguished by flashing while subsequent ones appear steady.

**KTU 2024 Mark Distribution (Examiner's Key):** Definition 1 mark + four sequences with brief description 2 marks.

---

### Question 2: What is a "First-Out" annunciator, and why is it important in boiler light-up? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **First-Out (FO) annunciator** is a specialized multi-channel annunciator that identifies and highlights the **first alarm in a group of alarms** that occur in rapid succession (typically within 1-3 seconds of each other).

**Importance in boiler light-up:** During the critical boiler ignition phase, a single root cause (e.g., loss of atomizing steam) can trigger a cascade of secondary trips (low flame, high furnace pressure, low steam flow, etc.). A first-out annunciator enables the operator to immediately identify the **primary cause** rather than wasting critical time chasing the visible secondary symptoms. This drastically reduces diagnostic time during an emergency and prevents re-light attempts based on incorrect root-cause analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the fail-safe principle** – Many students design trip contacts as NO. In a real plant, the trip must be **NC (normally closed)** so that wire-breakage itself raises the alarm. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing ISA sequences** – Writing "M" when meaning "A-4" or vice-versa. Memorize the table in Section 2.2 verbatim.
> 3. **Missing the flash equation** – A 14-mark M-sequence question must derive the lamp equation $L = Q \cdot F + Q \cdot A \cdot \overline{F}$. Half the marks (7) are for this.
> 4. **No units in numerical answers** – Always write "$\tau = 7.2$ **ms**" not just "$\tau = 7.2$".
> 5. **Skipping the derivation of the latch equation** – Even when the question says "design", the working S-R characteristic equation must be shown.

---

### Question A: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Explain the functional block diagram of an annunciator system. With neat sketches, describe the input section, logic section, and output section. State the purpose of a lamp-test push-button and acknowledge push-button.**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The annunciator is divided into three cascaded sections:

- **Input Section** – Accepts process contact closures (NO/NC potential-free), analog signals (4-20 mA), or voltage inputs (24/110/240 V). Includes **RFI/EMI filters**, **optical isolation**, and **RC debounce networks** to reject contact bounce and electrical noise.

- **Logic Section** – Implements the chosen annunciator sequence. In modern systems this is a **microcontroller**; in older systems it uses **CMOS gates, S-R flip-flops, and 555-timer-based flash oscillators**. The logic section holds the trip latch, the acknowledge latch, and generates the flash waveform (typically 1 Hz with 50% duty cycle).

- **Output Section** – Drives the **window lamps** (each window has two independent lamps – ON-state and OFF-state – to detect lamp failure), the **audible horn** (or electronic sounder), and **repeater relays** for SCADA/DCS.

**(2) Push-Buttons (2 marks each):**

- **Lamp-Test Push-Button (LTPB):** A momentary switch that energizes all window lamps simultaneously, allowing the operator to verify that **every filament is intact** before plant startup. It must be **normally-closed** wired to a relay, so that on a power-failure the lamps cannot be tested, again ensuring fail-safe behavior.

- **Acknowledge Push-Button (ACK):** A momentary push-button that (a) silences the horn, (b) changes the lamp from flashing to steady-ON, and (c) prepares the latch for reset once the process returns to normal. In M-sequence, it does **not** clear the alarm; it only acknowledges the operator's awareness.

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Design a Manual Acknowledge (M) annunciator channel using an S-R flip-flop. Derive the lamp and horn equations, and explain the sequence of operations for a typical trip event.**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Design with S-R Flip-Flop (3 marks):**

Choose an S-R flip-flop with active-low inputs (e.g., 74LS279 or CD4043).

Inputs:
- $\overline{S}$ = inverted trip signal (low on trip).
- $\overline{R}$ = inverted (ACK + Process-Normal) signal (low to reset).

The characteristic equation for a NAND S-R latch is:

$$Q_{n+1} = S + \overline{R} \cdot Q_n$$

**Working:** On a trip, $\overline{S} \to 0$ (active), forcing $Q = 1$. The alarm is now latched in the **ON** state. The flip-flop holds $Q = 1$ even after the trip signal is removed.

**(2) Lamp and Horn Equations (3 marks):**

Let $F$ = flash-oscillator output (1 Hz square wave, 50% duty).
Let $A$ = acknowledge input (1 when ACK pressed).
Let $R_{eq}$ = reset request (1 when process is normal AND reset pressed).

Lamp equation:

$$L = (Q \cdot F) + (Q \cdot A) \cdot \overline{F} = Q \cdot [F + A \cdot \overline{F}]$$

Horn equation:

$$H = Q \cdot \overline{A}$$

**(3) Sequence of Operations (1 mark):**

1. **Normal:** $Q = 0$, $L = 0$, $H = 0$ (all quiet).
2. **Trip occurs:** $\overline{S} \to 0$, $Q \to 1$. $L$ begins flashing (1 Hz), $H$ sounds.
3. **Operator presses ACK:** $A = 1$. $L$ goes steady-ON, $H$ is silenced.
4. **Process returns to normal AND operator presses Reset:** $R_{eq} = 1$, $\overline{R} \to 0$, $Q \to 0$. $L$ extinguishes. System returns to Normal.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a First-Out (AF) annunciator system. How is the first alarm identified from a group of subsequent alarms arriving within 1 second?**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The First-Out system consists of:
- A **group of input channels** (e.g., 8 channels sharing one FO logic card).
- A **first-out detector** made of an S-R latch bank – one latch per channel.
- A **shared flash oscillator** at 1 Hz.
- **Priority logic** that allows only the **earliest** triggered channel to flash; all others are clamped to steady.

**(2) First-Alarm Identification Logic (3 marks):**

Let $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ be the trip signals of the $n$ channels. The first-out latch for channel 1 is enabled **only** if $C_1$ arrives **before** any other $C_i$:

$$LATCH_1 = C_1 \cdot \overline{C_2} \cdot \overline{C_3} \cdots \overline{C_n}$$

Once $LATCH_1$ is set, it disables the latches of all other channels (priority lockout). The first channel therefore **flashes** ($L_1 = C_1 \cdot F$), while subsequent channels are **steady**:

$$L_i = C_i \cdot \overline{LATCH_1} \quad \text{for } i > 1$$

**(3) Time Window (1 mark):**

The "first-out window" is typically 1-3 seconds. Alarms arriving within this window are considered simultaneous; the very first edge sets the FO latch. This is implemented using a **monostable multivibrator (one-shot)** of 1-second duration on each input.

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Design an RC debounce filter for the input of an annunciator channel. The input contact is a mechanical push-button with a maximum bounce time of 8 ms. The Schmitt-trigger gate switches at 50% of $V_{cc}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Identify requirement (1 mark):**
Bounce time $T_b = 8$ ms. Schmitt-trigger threshold $V_{th} = 0.5 V_{cc}$.

**(2) Step input to RC low-pass (2 marks):**
The capacitor voltage response to a step $V$:

$$V_c(t) = V \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t/RC} \right)$$

**(3) Solve for $\tau$ (2 marks):**
For the Schmitt-trigger to switch at $V_c = 0.5 V$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-T_b/\tau}$$

$$e^{-T_b/\tau} = 0.5$$

$$\tau = \dfrac{T_b}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{8 \text{ ms}}{0.693} \approx 11.54 \text{ ms}$$

**(4) Component selection (2 marks):**
Choose $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$C = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{11.54 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^3} = 1.154 \text{ μF}$$

Select next standard value: $C = 1.2 \text{ μF}$ (or $1.5 \text{ μF}$ for safety).

**Final design:** $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1.5 \text{ μF}$, $\tau = 15$ ms – safely filters the 8 ms bounce.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition**: Annunciator = enunciator of abnormal process conditions, ISA 18.1 standard.
- [x] **Three blocks**: Input → Logic → Output (memorize the sub-components of each).
- [x] **Four key ISA sequences**: **A** (auto), **M** (manual), **F** (follow-up), **AF/MF** (first-out).
- [x] **Fail-safe principle**: Trip contact must be **NC**, relay **normally-energized**. Wire break = alarm.
- [x] **M-sequence equations** (frequently asked): $L = Q \cdot F + Q \cdot A \cdot \overline{F}$ and $H = Q \cdot \overline{A}$.
- [x] **First-out equation**: $LATCH_1 = C_1 \cdot \overline{C_2} \cdot \overline{C_3} \cdots \overline{C_n}$.
- [x] **Debounce RC design**: $\tau = T_{bounce} / \ln(2) \approx 1.44 \cdot T_{bounce}$.
- [x] **Lamp-test** = verifies all lamps; **ACK** = silences horn + steady ON; **Reset** = clears latch after process normal.
- [x] **Time-delay sequences (A-4, M-4)** use 555-timer or software counter with $t = R_t C_t$.
- [x] **Hysteresis band** must be greater than noise to prevent relay chatter.
- [x] **Audible device** must have a different frequency from the visual flash so the operator can identify it by sound alone (e.g., 1 Hz flash, 2.5 kHz horn).
- [x] **First-out window** is typically 1-3 seconds (one-shot monostable).
- [x] **Repeater output** to SCADA/DCS is mandatory in modern KTU-relevant plants.
- [x] **Historical case study**: Bhopal, Three Mile Island – every KTU paper may ask a 2-mark "Why are annunciators critical?" question.
- [x] **Unit notation**: Always write units for every numerical answer ($\tau$ in **ms**, $C$ in **μF**, $R$ in **kΩ**).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
