> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Alarm and Annunciation Circuits Using Analog and Digital Circuits # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Alarm Circuit:** A self-contained analog or digital electronic network that continuously monitors a process variable (PV) — such as temperature, pressure, flow, level, or voltage — and produces a binary output transition whenever the measured signal crosses a pre-set **threshold (setpoint)** value. The binary output is used to drive visual (lamp, LED) and/or audible (horn, buzzer) indicators, and to initiate protective or corrective action (e.g., tripping a relay, shutting a valve). **Annunciation Circuit:** A more sophisticated extension of the alarm circuit that not only detects the out-of-limit condition but also *identifies, classifies, sequences, prioritises, and displays* the abnormal condition in a structured panel — often following a standardised sequence defined by the **ANSI/ISA-18.1** standard. Annunciators typically support features such as **Acknowledge (ACK)**, **Reset (RST)**, **Test (TST)**, **First-Out (FO) detection**, and **lamp-flash logic**. > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an *Alarm* detects the abnormal condition, whereas an *Annunciator* is the operator–machine interface that communicates the alarm state, sequence, and acknowledgement status to the human operator. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital ICU Think of an **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** in a hospital: - Each patient is connected to a **bedside monitor** (the *alarm circuit*). When the heart rate (process variable) drops below 50 bpm (low setpoint) or the SpO₂ (oxygen saturation) falls under 90 % (high setpoint for alarm), the monitor emits a loud **beep** and the screen beside that bed starts **flashing red**. - The **central nursing station** (the *annunciator panel*) collects these individual bed alarms, **ranks them** (which patient is most critical?), **latches** them (so the nurse doesn't miss them while away), and allows the nurse to **acknowledge** (silence the horn) or **reset** the alarm after attending to the patient. - If two patients crashed at the *exact same second*, the first-up logic would freeze the *first* patient's alarm on its display — this is **First-Out Indication**, a hallmark of advanced annunciators. In a process plant (refinery, power station, paper mill), the same logic applies — but the "patients" are boilers, turbines, reactors, distillation columns, and the "ICU" is the **Control Room Annunciator Panel**. ## 1.3 Standardised Terminology (ISA-18.1 / IEC 62682) | Symbol | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **PV** | Process Variable | The physical quantity being measured (T, P, L, F) | | **SP** | Setpoint / Trip Point | The threshold at which the alarm is triggered | | **DV** | Deviation | $\text{DV} = \text{PV} - \text{SP}$ | | **H** | High Alarm | Activates when PV > HH setpoint | | **L** | Low Alarm | Activates when PV < LL setpoint | | **HH / LL** | High-High / Low-Low | Critical, second-tier trip alarms | | **ACK** | Acknowledge | Operator silences horn; lamp may stay lit or go to steady | | **RST** | Reset | Alarm returns to normal only after PV is back inside limits | | **TST** | Test | Pressing the test pushbutton simulates an alarm to verify panel | | **FO** | First-Out | Identifies the *first* alarm that occurred in a multi-alarm burst | | **OOS** | Out-Of-Service | Channel is bypassed for maintenance | > [!NOTE] > The deadband (hysteresis) is **mandatory** in any practical alarm circuit to prevent *chattering* — repeated alarm triggering when the PV hovers right at the setpoint due to noise. Typical deadband values: **2 % to 5 % of span** for industrial applications. ## 1.4 Classification of Alarm Circuits Based on signal type and implementation: 1. **Analog Alarm Circuits** — built around op-amp **comparators** (LM311, LM339, µA741), window detectors, and precision rectifiers. Used when the PV is an analog signal (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, RTD, TC). 2. **Digital Alarm Circuits** — built around **logic gates** (74LS00, 74LS08, 74LS32), **flip-flops** (74LS74 D-FF, 74LS279 SR-FF), and **priority encoders** (74LS148). Used when the PV is already in digital form or when complex sequencing is required. 3. **Hybrid / µP-Based Annunciators** — modern systems using microcontrollers, PLCs, or DCS function blocks (ISA-18.1 compliant). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Generic Annunciator Channel Every annunciator channel — whether implemented with electromechanical relays, analog ICs, or digital logic — contains **four functional blocks**: 1. **Input Conditioning (Sensor + Transducer + Signal Conditioner):** Converts physical PV to a normalised voltage/current (e.g., 0–5 V) compatible with the comparator/logic circuitry. 2. **Trip Detection (Comparator or Logic Threshold):** Compares the conditioned PV against the setpoint reference. Output is a binary `HIGH` (normal) or `LOW` (alarm), or vice versa. 3. **Memory / Latch (Flip-Flop or Latching Relay):** Captures and *retains* the alarm state until acknowledged, so a momentary excursion is not missed. This is the **Sequence Memory**. 4. **Output Driver (Lamp, Horn, Relay Coil, Triac):** Drives the operator-interface devices — typically a flashing lamp and a horn, with optional relay contacts for tripping external loads. ## 2.2 ISA-18.1 Annunciator Sequences (High-Yield for KTU) The **ISA-18.1** standard defines multiple standard sequences. The two most important for the KTU syllabus are: ### 2.2.1 ISA Sequence A — Automatic Reset (Non-Latching) - Alarm lamp flashes and horn sounds **only while the alarm condition exists**. - When PV returns to normal → horn silences, lamp extinguishes **automatically**, with **no operator action** required. - Used for **non-critical** alarms where momentary upsets are acceptable. ### 2.2.2 ISA Sequence M — Manual Reset (Latching) - Alarm lamp flashes and horn sounds the moment an abnormal condition is detected, **even if the PV has already returned to normal**. - Operator must press **ACK** → horn silences, lamp goes to *steady ON* (still indicating an unacknowledged-but-cleared alarm). - Operator must press **RST** *after* the PV has returned to normal → lamp extinguishes. - Used for **critical** safety alarms (e.g., boiler high-pressure trip). > [!IMPORTANT] > For a 14-mark KTU question, you must draw the **state-transition diagram** for the requested sequence, write the **boolean logic equations** for each state, and implement them using **74LS series ICs** or a **relay ladder diagram**. ### 2.2.3 Other Sequences (Briefly for Context) | Sequence | Description | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **F-1** | Manual reset, *no* flash after ACK (steady ON) | Critical alarms, no re-flash | | **F-2** | Manual reset, *re-flash* if a new alarm occurs after ACK | Process alarms | | **F-3** | Automatic reset, momentary ACK silences horn | Annoyance alarms | | **F-4** | Manual reset, first-out re-flash on subsequent alarms | Turbine trips | | **R-1** | Ring-back: returns to normal → brief horn | Reminder alarms | ## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All equations are written in LaTeX. Use `\vert` for absolute value in tables to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax. | # | Concept | Equation | Description | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Deviation | $\text{DV} = V_{\text{PV}} - V_{\text{SP}}$ | Comparator input difference | | 2 | Comparator Output (Open-Collector) | $V_{o} = V_{\text{sat}} \quad \text{if} \quad V_{+} < V_{-}$ | Inverting comparator logic | | 3 | Hysteresis Width | $\Delta V_{\text{HYS}} = \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}} \cdot V_{\text{sat}}$ | Schmitt trigger anti-chatter | | 4 | Trip Threshold (Schmitt) | $V_{\text{TH}}^{+} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}\right) - V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}$ | Upper threshold | | 5 | Trip Threshold (Schmitt) | $V_{\text{TH}}^{-} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}\right) + V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}$ | Lower threshold | | 6 | Window Comparator | $\text{ALARM} = \overline{(V_{\text{PV}} > V_{\text{HH}}) \cdot (V_{\text{PV}} < V_{\text{LL}})}$ | True only when *inside* window | | 7 | Rate-of-Change | $\dfrac{d\text{PV}}{dt} > \dot{\text{PV}}_{\text{SP}}$ | d/dt alarm | | 8 | Lamp Current Limit | $I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{\text{CC}} - V_{F}}{R_{S}}$ | Series resistor for LED | | 9 | Horn SPL Loss with Distance | $\Delta L = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right) \;\text{dB}$ | Inverse-square law | | 10 | Latch Enable | $Q_{n+1} = D \cdot \overline{\text{RST}} \cdot \text{CLK}$ | D-flip-flop based latch | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level HH/LL trips, turbine vibration trip, generator overcurrent trip. - **Oil & Gas:** ESD-1 (Emergency Shutdown) level alarms, flare stack pilot-flame-failure alarm. - **Hospitals:** Patient monitor alarms (pulse, SpO₂, EtCO₂). - **Aerospace:** Cabin pressure, engine N1/N2 over-speed, fuel low-level. - **Manufacturing:** CNC tool wear, coolant flow, spindle temperature. The **safety integrity level** (SIL-1, SIL-2, SIL-3 per IEC 61508) of an alarm system is a function of the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** — modern digital annunciators achieve PFD ≈ **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴**, qualifying for SIL-2 installations. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis Width (Comparator-Based Analog Alarm) **Scenario:** A temperature transmitter outputs **4–20 mA** corresponding to **0–200 °C**. We want an alarm when T > 150 °C (HH trip), with a 4 °C deadband (anti-chatter). **Step 1 — Convert span to voltage:** $$V_{\text{PV(min)}} = 4 \;\text{mA} \times 250\;\Omega = 1.0 \;\text{V} \quad (T = 0\;°\text{C})$$ $$V_{\text{PV(max)}} = 20 \;\text{mA} \times 250\;\Omega = 5.0 \;\text{V} \quad (T = 200\;°\text{C})$$ **Step 2 — Compute voltage per °C (sensitivity):** $$k = \dfrac{V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}}}{T_{\text{max}} - T_{\text{min}}} = \dfrac{5.0 - 1.0}{200 - 0} = 0.020 \;\text{V/°C}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the trip voltage at T = 150 °C:** $$V_{\text{SP}} = V_{\text{min}} + k \cdot T_{\text{SP}} = 1.0 + 0.020 \times 150 = 4.0 \;\text{V}$$ **Step 4 — Compute deadband voltage equivalent to 4 °C:** $$\Delta V_{\text{DB}} = k \cdot \Delta T = 0.020 \times 4 = 0.080 \;\text{V}$$ **Step 5 — Apply Schmitt trigger equations (using LM311, $V_{\text{sat}} = \pm 13.5\;\text{V}$):** We need the *upper* threshold $V_{\text{TH}}^{+} = V_{\text{SP}} = 4.0\;\text{V}$ and the *lower* threshold $V_{\text{TH}}^{-} = V_{\text{SP}} - \Delta V_{\text{DB}} = 3.92\;\text{V}$. Setting $V_{\text{ref}} = 2.5\;\text{V}$ and choosing $R_{1} = 10\;\text{k}\Omega$, solve for $R_{2}$: $$V_{\text{TH}}^{+} = V_{\text{ref}}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}\right) - V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}$$ Substituting: $$4.0 = 2.5 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{10\,\text{k}}{R_{2}}\right) - 13.5 \cdot \dfrac{10\,\text{k}}{R_{2}}$$ $$4.0 = 2.5 + \dfrac{25\,000}{R_{2}} - \dfrac{135\,000}{R_{2}}$$ $$4.0 - 2.5 = -\dfrac{110\,000}{R_{2}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 1.5 = -\dfrac{110\,000}{R_{2}}$$ $$R_{2} = -\dfrac{110\,000}{1.5} = -73.33\;\text{k}\Omega$$ The negative result indicates our $V_{\text{ref}}$ is on the wrong side; flip to $V_{\text{ref}} = -2.5\;\text{V}$: $$4.0 = -2.5\!\left(1 + \dfrac{10\,\text{k}}{R_{2}}\right) - 13.5 \cdot \dfrac{10\,\text{k}}{R_{2}}$$ $$4.0 + 2.5 = -\dfrac{25\,000}{R_{2}} - \dfrac{135\,000}{R_{2}} = -\dfrac{160\,000}{R_{2}}$$ $$R_{2} = -\dfrac{160\,000}{6.5} = -24.6\;\text{k}\Omega$$ Magnitude: $R_{2} \approx 24\;\text{k}\Omega$ (in practice, use a 25 kΩ trimpot and re-calibrate). **Step 6 — Verify:** $$\Delta V_{\text{HYS}} = \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}} \cdot 2V_{\text{sat}} = \dfrac{10}{24.6} \times 27 = 10.98\;\text{V}$$ This hysteresis is too wide. For a *small* deadband, we use a **non-inverting** topology with a *positive feedback fraction* of $\beta = \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{1}+R_{2}} \approx 0.01$: $$\beta = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{DB}}}{2V_{\text{sat}}} = \dfrac{0.080}{27} = 0.00296$$ $$R_{1} = 2.96\;\text{k}\Omega, \quad R_{2} = 1\;\text{M}\Omega$$ > [!TIP] > The point of this derivation is to show that **the deadband must be implemented in *voltage* units, not temperature units**, by scaling through the transducer sensitivity $k$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ISA Sequence M Logic Using 74LS74 D-Flip-Flop A **manual-reset (Sequence M)** annunciator requires three stable states: 1. **NORMAL** — lamp OFF, horn OFF. 2. **ALARM-UNACK** — lamp FLASHING, horn ON. 3. **ALARM-ACK** — lamp STEADY-ON, horn OFF (until reset). 4. **ALARM-RESET** — requires PV-normal AND operator presses RST → returns to NORMAL. ### 3.2.1 State Variables Let: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \text{alarm input (1 = abnormal)} \\ \text{ACK} &= \text{acknowledge pushbutton} \\ \text{RST} &= \text{reset pushbutton} \\ L_{\text{FL}} &= \text{lamp flash command} \\ L_{\text{ST}} &= \text{lamp steady-on command} \\ H &= \text{horn command} \\ F &= \text{flash oscillator output (e.g., 1 Hz square wave)} \\ Q_{A} &= \text{alarm latch output (D-FF)} \\ Q_{F} &= \text{first-out latch} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.2 Boolean Equations (Sequence M) **Alarm Latch (D-flip-flop with asynchronous reset):** $$D = A, \quad \overline{\text{PR}} = 1, \quad \overline{\text{CLR}} = \overline{\text{RST}}$$ So: $$Q_{A}^{n+1} = A \cdot \overline{\text{RST}}_{\text{rising edge}}$$ **Lamp Flash Command** (active only when alarm is unacknowledged): $$L_{\text{FL}} = Q_{A} \cdot \overline{\text{ACK}} \cdot F$$ **Lamp Steady Command** (active after ACK but before RST, and alarm is still latched): $$L_{\text{ST}} = Q_{A} \cdot \text{ACK}$$ **Horn Command** (active in ALARM-UNACK only): $$H = Q_{A} \cdot \overline{\text{ACK}}$$ **Final Lamp Output (ORed with flasher):** $$L = L_{\text{FL}} + L_{\text{ST}} = Q_{A} \cdot (\overline{\text{ACK}} \cdot F + \text{ACK})$$ **Reset Logic** (Sequence M requires PV-back-to-normal AND RST press): $$\text{RST}_{\text{effective}} = \overline{A} \cdot \text{RST}_{\text{pb}}$$ ### 3.2.3 Truth Table (Sequence M) | $A$ | $\text{ACK}$ | $\text{RST}$ | $Q_{A}$ (prev) | $L_{\text{FL}}$ | $L_{\text{ST}}$ | $H$ | $L$ (lamp) | State | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | OFF | NORMAL | | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $F$ | 0 | 1 | FLASH + HORN | ALARM-UNACK | | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | STEADY | ALARM-ACK | | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $F$ | 0 | 1 | FLASH + HORN | Re-flash on new alarm | | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | OFF | RETURN-TO-NORMAL | ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Window Comparator (Hi/Lo Combined Analog Alarm) A **window comparator** detects when the PV is *outside* an acceptable band — i.e., either too high or too low. Two LM393 comparators are used: **Upper Comparator Output (HIGH alarm):** $$C_{\text{HI}} = \begin{cases} \text{LOW} & \text{if } V_{\text{PV}} > V_{\text{HH}} \\ \text{HIGH} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Lower Comparator Output (LOW alarm):** $$C_{\text{LO}} = \begin{cases} \text{LOW} & \text{if } V_{\text{PV}} < V_{\text{LL}} \\ \text{HIGH} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Combined ALARM output (OR of both, with LED indicators):** $$\text{ALARM} = \overline{C_{\text{HI}}} + \overline{C_{\text{LO}}}$$ $$\text{ALARM} = 1 \quad \text{iff} \quad V_{\text{PV}} < V_{\text{LL}} \;\text{OR}\; V_{\text{PV}} > V_{\text{HH}}$$ Inside the window ($V_{\text{LL}} \leq V_{\text{PV}} \leq V_{\text{HH}}$), the ALARM line is LOW (no alarm) — the green "SAFE" LED glows. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection for KTU Design Problems | Subsystem | Recommended IC / Device | KTU Exam Favourite? | Key Spec | | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Comparator | LM311, LM339, µA710 | ✅ | Open-collector, $\pm 18\;\text{V}$ | | Op-Amp | µA741, LM358, TL082 | ✅ | General purpose | | Dual D-FF | 74LS74, 74HC74 | ✅✅ | Sequence latch | | Quad SR-FF | 74LS279 | ✅ | Set/Reset latch for ACK | | 8:3 Priority Encoder | 74LS148 | ✅✅ | First-Out detection | | 3-to-8 Decoder | 74LS138 | ✅ | Lamp-matrix driver | | 555 Timer | NE555 | ✅✅ | 1 Hz flasher | | Optocoupler | 4N35, MOC3021 | ✅ | Galvanic isolation | | Triac | BT136, BTA16 | ✅ | Horn/siren driver | | LED | Red/Yellow/Green 5 mm | ✅ | 20 mA, 2.0 V forward | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Generic Annunciator Channel The diagram below shows the **signal flow** from the physical sensor through to the operator interface, with logic-level interconnections for ISA Sequence M. ```mermaid flowchart LR SENSOR[Sensor / Transducer] --> COND[Signal Conditioner
4-20 mA to 0-5 V] COND --> CMP[Comparator Stage
LM311 with Hysteresis] CMP --> LAT[D Flip Flop Latch
74LS74 - Q A] LAT --> AND1[AND Gate
74LS08] FLASH[NE555 Flasher
1 Hz F] AND1 --> DRV1[Lamp Driver
NPN Transistor] AND2[AND Gate for ACK] LAT --> AND2 AND2 --> DRV2[Steady Lamp Driver] AND3[AND Gate for Horn
74LS08] LAT --> AND3 AND3 --> TRIAC[Triac MOC3021] TRIAC --> HORN[Horn or Siren] ACKPB[Acknowledge PB] ACKPB --> ACKFF[D Flip Flop
ACK Latch 74LS74] ACKFF --> AND1 ACKFF --> AND2 ACKFF --> AND3 RSTPB[Reset PB - gated by NOT A] RSTPB --> CLR[Asynchronous Clear
of LAT and ACKFF] TSTPB[Test PB] TSTPB --> LAT ``` ## 4.2 First-Out Detection Logic Block (Multi-Channel) In a multi-channel panel, a **priority encoder (74LS148)** is used to identify which channel tripped first during a burst alarm (e.g., a turbine trip that cascades 30+ alarms in 50 ms). ```mermaid flowchart TD CH1[Channel 1 Alarm] --> PE[Priority Encoder
74LS148] CH2[Channel 2 Alarm] --> PE CH3[Channel 3 Alarm] --> PE CH4[Channel 4 Alarm] --> PE CH5[Channel 5 Alarm] --> PE CH6[Channel 6 Alarm] --> PE CH7[Channel 7 Alarm] --> PE CH8[Channel 8 Alarm] --> PE PE --> DEC[3 to 8 Decoder
74LS138] DEC --> L1[Lamp 1 - First Out FLASH] DEC --> L2[Lamp 2 - Steady] DEC --> L3[Lamp 3 - Steady] DEC --> L4[Lamp 4 - Steady] DEC --> L5[Lamp 5 - Steady] DEC --> L6[Lamp 6 - Steady] DEC --> L7[Lamp 7 - Steady] DEC --> L8[Lamp 8 - Steady] PE --> GS[Group Signal Output] GS --> HORN[Common Horn Driver] FOB[First Out Trigger] --> LAT2[D Flip Flop
Freeze Encoder] LAT2 --> PE ``` ## 4.3 Analog Alarm Circuit — Window Comparator with LED Indication The schematic below uses two LM393 comparators and three LEDs (Green = safe, Yellow = High, Red = Low) — a classic KTU lab experiment. ```mermaid flowchart LR PV[V PV 0 to 5 V] --> CMP1[LM393 Comparator 1
V ref = V HH] PV --> CMP2[LM393 Comparator 2
V ref = V LL] VHH[V HH Reference] --> CMP1 VLL[V LL Reference] --> CMP2 CMP1 --> NOT1[NOT gate - LED driver] NOT1 --> YLED[Yellow LED
HIGH alarm] CMP2 --> NOT2[NOT gate - LED driver] NOT2 --> RLED[Red LED
LOW alarm] CMP1 --> AND3[AND of both CMP outputs] CMP2 --> AND3 AND3 --> GLED[Green LED
SAFE inside window] YLED --> HORN[Horn via 555 one-shot] RLED --> HORN ``` ## 4.4 ISA Sequence M — State Transition Diagram The state machine representation is essential for a 14-mark KTU question. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NORMAL NORMAL --> ALARM_UNACK : A becomes 1 ALARM_UNACK --> ALARM_ACK : ACK pressed ALARM_ACK --> NORMAL : A = 0 AND RST pressed ALARM_UNACK --> ALARM_UNACK : A remains 1 ALARM_ACK --> ALARM_ACK : A remains 1 NORMAL --> ALARM_UNACK : TST pressed ALARM_UNACK --> NORMAL : TST released and A = 0 ``` **Lamp/Horn behaviour per state (Sequence M):** - **NORMAL:** Lamp OFF, Horn OFF. - **ALARM_UNACK:** Lamp FLASHING (1 Hz), Horn ON. - **ALARM_ACK:** Lamp STEADY-ON, Horn OFF. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define an annunciator and distinguish between ISA Sequence A and ISA Sequence M. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **annunciator** is an operator–machine interface system that monitors multiple alarm inputs, displays the abnormal condition via panel lamps and horns, and supports standardised acknowledge/reset sequences defined by ANSI/ISA-18.1. **Distinction table (any 3 valid points, 1 mark each):** | Feature | Sequence A (Automatic Reset) | Sequence M (Manual Reset) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Latching | Non-latching — clears when PV returns | Latching — holds even after PV normal | | ACK behaviour | Not mandatory | Mandatory to silence horn | | Reset | Automatic | Manual (ACK + RST) | | Application | Non-critical alarms | Critical safety trips | ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is *first-out indication* in a multi-channel annunciator? Mention any one IC used to implement it. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **First-out indication** is the ability of the annunciator to identify and *freeze the display* of the *first* alarm channel that tripped during a multi-alarm event (e.g., a turbine trip that causes cascade alarms within milliseconds). The first channel is typically displayed as **flashing** while all subsequently-tripped channels are displayed as **steady-on**, allowing the operator to diagnose the root cause. **IC used:** 74LS148 (8-line to 3-line priority encoder) or 74LS138 (3-to-8 decoder), often gated by a 74LS74 D-FF to latch the first-out state. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Analog Window Comparator Design **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* Design an analog window comparator circuit using **LM393** to monitor a process variable of 0–5 V representing a tank level. The HIGH-HIGH (HH) trip is at **4.2 V** and the LOW-LOW (LL) trip is at **1.0 V**. Show the complete circuit with LED indicators and derive the boolean logic. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Explain how **hysteresis (Schmitt action)** is added to each comparator to prevent alarm chattering. Derive the expression for the hysteresis width and calculate the resistor values for a **deadband of 0.15 V** using $V_{\text{sat}} = 13.5\;\text{V}$ and $R_{1} = 10\;\text{k}\Omega$. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Window Comparator Design (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Two LM393 comparators:** - **CMP1 (HIGH detector):** Non-inverting input = $V_{\text{PV}}$; inverting input = $V_{\text{HH}} = 4.2\;\text{V}$. - Output $C_{\text{HI}}$ = **LOW** when $V_{\text{PV}} > 4.2\;\text{V}$ (alarm), **HIGH** otherwise. - **CMP2 (LOW detector):** Non-inverting input = $V_{\text{LL}} = 1.0\;\text{V}$; inverting input = $V_{\text{PV}}$. - Output $C_{\text{LO}}$ = **LOW** when $V_{\text{PV}} < 1.0\;\text{V}$ (alarm), **HIGH** otherwise. **Step 2 — LED driver logic:** - **Yellow LED (HIGH alarm):** Lit when $C_{\text{HI}}$ = LOW → use inverter (74LS04) + NPN transistor (BC547) + 330 Ω series resistor. - **Red LED (LOW alarm):** Lit when $C_{\text{LO}}$ = LOW → same driver stage. - **Green LED (SAFE):** Lit when $C_{\text{HI}}$ = HIGH AND $C_{\text{LO}}$ = HIGH → AND gate (74LS08) + driver. **Step 3 — Boolean equation for combined ALARM output:** $$\text{ALARM} = \overline{C_{\text{HI}}} + \overline{C_{\text{LO}}}$$ Or, using De Morgan: $$\text{ALARM} = \overline{C_{\text{HI}} \cdot C_{\text{LO}}}$$ This is implemented with a single 74LS00 NAND gate whose inputs are $C_{\text{HI}}$ and $C_{\text{LO}}$. **Step 4 — Horn driver:** ALARM line → MOC3021 optocoupler → BT136 triac → 230 V AC horn. **Step 5 — Circuit diagram (description, 2 marks):** Draw the two LM393 comparators with their reference voltages derived from a **precision Zener (LM336-5.0)** and a **resistive divider** with 1 % tolerance resistors. Pull-up resistors (4.7 kΩ) at each comparator output. LEDs as described. Power supply $\pm 15\;\text{V}$ for LM393. **Part (b) — Hysteresis Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Why hysteresis is needed:** When the PV hovers near the setpoint due to noise, the comparator output oscillates → lamp flickers, horn chatters, relay contact arcs. Hysteresis creates **two distinct thresholds**: $V_{\text{TH}}^{+}$ (upper, alarm-on) and $V_{\text{TH}}^{-}$ (lower, alarm-off), separated by the deadband $\Delta V_{\text{HYS}}$. **Step 2 — Positive feedback:** Add resistor $R_{1}$ from comparator output to non-inverting input, and $R_{2}$ from non-inverting input to ground (or to $V_{\text{ref}}$). The non-inverting input becomes: $$V_{+} = \beta \cdot V_{o} + (1 - \beta) \cdot V_{\text{ref}}$$ where $\beta = \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$ (assuming $V_{\text{ref}}$ at the divider tap). **Step 3 — Upper threshold** (output is at $V_{\text{sat}}^{+} = +13.5\;\text{V}$): $$V_{\text{TH}}^{+} = \beta \cdot (+13.5) + (1 - \beta) \cdot V_{\text{ref}}$$ **Step 4 — Lower threshold** (output is at $V_{\text{sat}}^{-} = -13.5\;\text{V}$): $$V_{\text{TH}}^{-} = \beta \cdot (-13.5) + (1 - \beta) \cdot V_{\text{ref}}$$ **Step 5 — Hysteresis width:** $$\Delta V_{\text{HYS}} = V_{\text{TH}}^{+} - V_{\text{TH}}^{-} = 2 \beta \cdot V_{\text{sat}} = 2 V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical calculation:** Given $\Delta V_{\text{HYS}} = 0.15\;\text{V}$, $V_{\text{sat}} = 13.5\;\text{V}$, $R_{1} = 10\;\text{k}\Omega$: $$\beta = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{HYS}}}{2 V_{\text{sat}}} = \dfrac{0.15}{27} = 5.556 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{1} + R_{2}} = \beta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad R_{1} + R_{2} = \dfrac{R_{1}}{\beta} = \dfrac{10\,\text{k}}{5.556 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.80\;\text{M}\Omega$$ $$R_{2} = 1.80\;\text{M}\Omega - 10\;\text{k}\Omega \approx 1.79\;\text{M}\Omega$$ Use a **1.8 MΩ, 1 % metal-film** resistor in practice. The deadband is now 0.15 V — equivalent to **3 %** of the 5 V span — a typical industrial value. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Digital ISA Sequence M Annunciator **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* With the aid of a neat state-transition diagram and boolean logic equations, explain the operation of an **ISA Sequence M (Manual Reset)** annunciator channel. Implement the lamp-flash logic using a **74LS74 D-flip-flop**, a **1 Hz NE555 flasher**, and standard 74LS00/08 logic gates. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Design a **first-out detection** subsystem for an 8-channel annunciator panel using a **74LS148 priority encoder** and a **74LS138 decoder**. Show how the first channel to trip is identified and frozen on the display while subsequent alarms appear as steady-on. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — ISA Sequence M Implementation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — State diagram (already drawn in Section 4.4) — describe the three states (1 mark):** - **NORMAL:** Lamp OFF, Horn OFF, $Q_{A} = 0$. - **ALARM_UNACK:** Lamp FLASHING, Horn ON, $Q_{A} = 1$, ACK latch = 0. - **ALARM_ACK:** Lamp STEADY-ON, Horn OFF, $Q_{A} = 1$, ACK latch = 1. **Step 2 — Hardware realisation:** - One half of **74LS74** is used as the **alarm latch** ($D = A$, $\overline{\text{CLR}} = \overline{\text{RST}_{\text{eff}}}$). - A second 74LS74 is used as the **ACK latch** (set by ACK pushbutton, cleared by RST). - A **NE555** in astable mode produces a 1 Hz square wave $F$: $$F = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_{A} + 2R_{B}) \cdot C}$$ With $R_{A} = 1\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_{B} = 68\;\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 10\;\mu\text{F}$ → $F \approx 1.0\;\text{Hz}$. **Step 3 — Boolean equations (recap, 2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{\text{FL}} &= Q_{A} \cdot \overline{\text{ACK}_{\text{latch}}} \cdot F \\ L_{\text{ST}} &= Q_{A} \cdot \text{ACK}_{\text{latch}} \\ L &= L_{\text{FL}} + L_{\text{ST}} \\ H &= Q_{A} \cdot \overline{\text{ACK}_{\text{latch}}} \\ \text{RST}_{\text{eff}} &= \overline{A} \cdot \text{RST}_{\text{pb}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Logic IC count (1 mark):** - 74LS74 × 1 (two FFs: alarm + ACK) - 74LS08 × 1 (quad AND — for the four AND terms) - 74LS32 × 1 (OR for lamp driver) - 74LS04 × 1 (inverters for $\overline{\text{ACK}}$) - NE555 × 1 (flasher) **Step 5 — Lamp and horn driver (2 marks):** The combined lamp line $L$ drives a **BD139 NPN transistor** (or ULN2803 Darlington array) with a 24 V/2 W incandescent lamp or a high-brightness LED cluster. The horn line $H$ drives a **MOC3021 optocoupler** → **BT136 triac** → **230 V AC, 110 dB industrial siren**. A 5 A fuse protects the triac. **Part (b) — First-Out Detection (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Purpose (1 mark):** In a multi-channel annunciator, when a process upset (e.g., turbine trip) causes *simultaneous* alarms, the operator must know **which channel tripped first** to diagnose the root cause. The first-out subsystem freezes the *first* input's display as FLASHING and holds it until ACK. **Step 2 — 74LS148 priority encoder (2 marks):** - Inputs: $I_{0}$ to $I_{7}$ (active LOW, one per alarm channel). - Outputs: $A_{2}, A_{1}, A_{0}$ (3-bit binary of the highest-priority active input, active LOW). - **EI** (Enable Input) and **GS** (Group Signal) flags: GS goes LOW when *any* input is active. The encoder continuously monitors the 8 alarm lines. Whichever input goes LOW *first* will be latched in the output code (priority is fixed: $I_{7}$ is highest, $I_{0}$ is lowest). **Step 3 — Freeze via 74LS74 latch (2 marks):** - The encoder outputs $A_{2}A_{1}A_{0}$ are connected to the $D$ inputs of three 74LS74 FFs. - The clock of these FFs is triggered by the *first* GS pulse (using a monostable 74LS221 one-shot, 1 µs pulse). - After the clock edge, the 3-bit code is frozen and held on $Q_{2}Q_{1}Q_{0}$. **Step 4 — 74LS138 decoder (1 mark):** The frozen 3-bit code feeds the **74LS138** (3-to-8 decoder). The corresponding output $Y_{k}$ goes LOW, which: - Drives the **FLASH** command to the lamp of the first-out channel (via 1 Hz flasher AND-gated with $Y_{k}$). - Disables further encoder updates by feeding $Y_{k}$ back to the encoder's **EI** pin (latch-enable). **Step 5 — Steady-on for subsequent channels (1 mark):** All *other* channels (whose alarms are also active) drive their lamps in *steady-on* mode by AND-gating their alarm latch output with the *inverse* of their $Y_{k}$ line (i.e., the channel is steady-on if it is alarming AND it is *not* the first-out channel). $$\text{Lamp}_{\text{channel } k} = Q_{A,k} \cdot \left[\overline{Y_{k}} \cdot \text{STEADY} \;+\; Y_{k} \cdot F\right]$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers** > 1. **Confusing Sequence A with Sequence M** — 50 % of students mix up the latching behaviour. Memorise: *A = Automatic* (no latch), *M = Manual* (latched). > 2. **Forgetting the deadband (hysteresis)** — A comparator *without* hysteresis loses 1–2 marks in analog design questions. Always mention $\Delta V_{\text{HYS}}$ and show $R_{1}$, $R_{2}$ values. > 3. **Not drawing pull-up resistors** at LM311/LM393 outputs (open-collector). Examiner deducts ½ mark if missing. > 4. **Confusing 74LS148 (encoder) with 74LS138 (decoder)** — they look similar in pin count (16-pin DIP) but are opposite-direction devices. Misnaming loses 1 mark. > 5. **No mention of galvanic isolation** (MOC3021, 4N35) when driving 230 V AC horn from logic-level circuitry. Examiner *always* tests isolation awareness. > 6. **Forgetting the truth table** for the sequence — at least one complete truth table is expected for 7-mark sub-parts. > 7. **Wrong polarity** in the Schmitt derivation (using $V_{\text{TH}}^{-} = \beta V_{\text{sat}} + (1-\beta)V_{\text{ref}}$ instead of $-\beta V_{\text{sat}} + ...$) — sign error loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Important Things to Remember for KTU Module 4 — Alarm & Annunciation Circuits** - ✅ **Alarm** = detection; **Annunciator** = display + sequence + acknowledge/reset. - ✅ **ISA Sequence A** → Automatic reset, non-latching. Lamp ON and Horn ON only while alarm persists. - ✅ **ISA Sequence M** → Manual reset, latching. Requires ACK to silence horn and RST to clear lamp. - ✅ **Standard sequence set:** A, M, F-1, F-2, F-3, F-4, R-1 (ANSI/ISA-18.1). - ✅ **Hysteresis is mandatory** to prevent chattering. Use Schmitt trigger or positive feedback on comparator. - ✅ **Window comparator** = two comparators + OR/AND logic to detect "outside-band" condition. - ✅ **First-out detection** uses 74LS148 priority encoder + 74LS138 decoder + 74LS74 freeze latch. - ✅ **Flasher** = NE555 in astable mode, frequency ≈ 1 Hz (60–120 flashes/min per ISA). - ✅ **Horn driver** = Logic → Optocoupler (MOC3021) → Triac (BT136) → 230 V AC horn. **Galvanic isolation is mandatory**. - ✅ **Lamp driver** = Logic → NPN transistor (BC547 / BD139) or Darlington array (ULN2803) → 24 V/2 W lamp. - ✅ **ACK and RST pushbuttons** must be **debounced** (using 74LS14 Schmitt inverter + RC, or 74LS279 SR-FF). - ✅ **Test pushbutton** simulates an alarm by forcing the alarm line HIGH for the entire panel. - ✅ **Deadband / Hysteresis width formula:** $\Delta V_{\text{HYS}} = 2 \beta V_{\text{sat}} = 2 V_{\text{sat}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}$. - ✅ **Window comparator boolean:** $\text{ALARM} = \overline{C_{\text{HI}} \cdot C_{\text{LO}}} = \overline{C_{\text{HI}}} + \overline{C_{\text{LO}}}$. - ✅ **Priority encoder (74LS148)** has active-LOW inputs and outputs; an **EI** pin enables/disables the device. - ✅ **3-to-8 decoder (74LS138)** has active-LOW outputs; one of eight goes LOW based on the 3-bit input. - ✅ **Industrial annunciators** must comply with **SIL-1 / SIL-2** (IEC 61508) for safety instrumented functions. - ✅ **Typical deadband = 2 %–5 %** of span; **flasher rate = 60–120 flashes/min** per ISA-18.1. - ✅ **Sequence M state machine:** NORMAL → ALARM_UNACK (on $A=1$) → ALARM_ACK (on ACK) → NORMAL (on $\overline{A} \cdot \text{RST}$).

topics.title: Design of Programmable Logic Controller for Any Two Simple Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET795 - INSTRUMENTATION SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Programmable Logic Controller for Any Two Simple Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of PLC-Based System Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC):** A PLC is a solid-state, industrially hardened digital computer specifically designed to perform logical decision-making and sequential control functions in harsh industrial environments. It uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions that implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control, in real time, various machines or processes.

**PLC System Design** is the structured engineering methodology used to translate a physical industrial process requirement (the *control problem*) into an executable set of Boolean instructions (typically **Ladder Logic** or *Ladder Diagram – LD*) that runs on the PLC processor. The design process encompasses the definition of inputs/outputs, assignment of memory addresses, creation of the logic program, and verification against the operational specification.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The PLC as a Bilingual Factory Translator"

Imagine a factory floor where two very different languages are spoken:
- The **sensors and switches** (push buttons, limit switches, level sensors, proximity switches) speak the language of *voltage* (0 V or 24 V).
- The **actuators and motors** (solenoid valves, contactors, conveyors, lamps) also speak the language of *voltage*, but they require specific, complex *timing* and *sequencing* to work properly.

A PLC acts as a **bilingual factory translator** — it listens to the sensors, thinks in **Boolean logic and timers**, and then commands the actuators. Unlike a hard-wired relay panel, the PLC's "thinking rules" are not soldered in copper; they are **soft-coded lines of logic** that the engineer can rewrite any number of times. This makes the PLC a *flexible translator* whose vocabulary can be upgraded whenever the process changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ladder" Logic?** The Ladder Diagram (LD) was invented to allow electricians familiar with relay schematics to program PLCs using familiar rungs of contacts and coils. Visually, the program looks like a *ladder*: two vertical "rails" (power lines) with horizontal "rungs" (logic lines).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU-Specific)

- **Standard PLC I/O Voltage:** **24 V DC** (sinking/sourcing) and **120/230 V AC** for industrial discrete I/O.
- **Standard Scan Cycle Time:** Typically **1 ms to 20 ms** for a small-to-medium PLC program.
- **IEC 61131-3 Standard:** The international norm defining the **5 PLC programming languages**: Ladder Diagram (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Structured Text (ST), Instruction List (IL), and Sequential Function Chart (SFC).
- **Memory Bit Addressing Convention:** Typical — `I0.0` (Input byte 0, bit 0), `Q0.0` (Output byte 0, bit 0), `M0.0` (Internal Memory / Marker).

### 1.4 Sequential Steps in PLC System Design (Overview)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a **6-step methodology** for any PLC design:

1. **Define the Control Task** — write a textual specification of what the process must do.
2. **Identify all Input and Output Devices** — list every sensor and actuator.
3. **Assign I/O Addresses** — map each device to a specific PLC memory bit.
4. **Develop the Logic Program** — convert the control sequence into Ladder Logic.
5. **Simulate and Test** — verify the program against the specification.
6. **Deploy, Commission, and Document** — install on site and provide operator documentation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever a question asks to "design a PLC for an application," examiners specifically look for the **Problem Statement → I/O List → Ladder Diagram → Explanation** flow. Skipping the I/O list almost always costs 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Phase Design Methodology — Detailed Breakdown

#### Phase 1: Define the Control Objective
The engineer writes a precise narrative: *"When push button START is pressed, conveyor M1 runs. When proximity sensor detects a bottle, conveyor stops for 5 seconds while filling valve opens. Once filled, conveyor restarts..."* This narrative eliminates ambiguity.

#### Phase 2: I/O Identification
Every physical device is classified:
- **Discrete (Digital) Inputs:** Push buttons, limit switches, photo-sensors, proximity sensors, level switches.
- **Discrete (Digital) Outputs:** Solenoid valves, contactors, indicator lamps, motor starters, relays.
- **Analog I/O (Optional):** Temperature, pressure, flow transmitters.

#### Phase 3: Address Assignment
Each device is assigned a unique memory address following the manufacturer's convention (e.g., Siemens S7: `I0.0`–`I0.7`, `Q0.0`–`Q0.7`; Allen-Bradley: `I:1/0`, `O:2/0`). Internal auxiliary bits use `M` (memory).

#### Phase 4: Ladder Logic Development
Ladder Logic executes **rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom, left-to-right** on every scan. Each rung has:
- **Contacts** (input conditions) on the LEFT side.
- **Coils** (output actions) on the RIGHT side.
- A contact is **NO (Normally Open)** or **NC (Normally Closed)**.
- A coil is energized only if the left-side Boolean expression evaluates to **TRUE (logic 1)**.

#### Phase 5: Simulation
Most modern PLCs (Siemens TIA Portal, Allen-Bradley Studio 5000) provide offline simulation environments where the engineer can "force" inputs and verify outputs.

#### Phase 6: Commissioning
The verified program is downloaded to the physical PLC, all field wiring is connected, and the system is tested under real load conditions.

### 2.2 The PLC Scan Cycle — Theoretical Foundation

The PLC processor repeatedly executes a deterministic **Scan Cycle**:

$$T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$$

Where:
- $T_{input}$ = time to read the status of all input modules into the **Process Image Input (PII)** table.
- $T_{program}$ = time to execute the user program from top rung to bottom.
- $T_{output}$ = time to write the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table to the physical output modules.
- $T_{housekeeping}$ = time for diagnostics, communication, memory housekeeping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** The scan cycle is deterministic — for a given program, $T_{scan}$ is nearly constant. This guarantees **predictable, repeatable control**, which is the cornerstone of industrial automation. A typical small PLC executes in **5–10 ms** per cycle.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scan Cycle Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Sets the maximum input-to-output response delay of the controller. |
| Timer Preset | $T_{preset}$ | seconds (s) | Engineering value at which the timer output (Done bit) becomes 1. |
| Counter Preset | $C_{preset}$ | integer pulses | Number of input transitions before the counter output (Done bit) becomes 1. |
| Output Coils (NO) | — (XIC in AB, NO contact in Siemens) | Boolean | Energized only when the rung condition is TRUE. |
| Output Coils (NC) | — (XIO in AB, NC contact in Siemens) | Boolean | Energized when rung condition is FALSE (inverted logic). |
| Latch / Unlatch | (S) / (R) coils | Boolean | Holds output ON even after input goes OFF — used for START/STOP memory. |
| Ton (On-Delay Timer) | Output = 1 when $t \geq T_{preset}$ | s | Delays turn-on of an output by a preset duration. |
| Toff (Off-Delay Timer) | Output = 1 until $t \geq T_{preset}$ after input OFF | s | Delays turn-off of an output by a preset duration. |
| CTU (Up Counter) | $C_{current} = C_{current} + 1$ per pulse | pulses | Counts rising edges; output = 1 at $C_{preset}$. |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` for vertical bars in tables; for example, write $T_{scan} \mid_{max}$ not $T_{scan} \vert_{max}$ inside table cells to avoid markdown-breaking vertical pipes.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PLC design is the **backbone of modern industry**. It is applied in:
- **Discrete manufacturing:** Automotive assembly lines, bottling plants, packaging machines.
- **Process industries:** Chemical reactors, water treatment, oil & gas.
- **Infrastructure:** Traffic control, elevator control, building automation, escalators.
- **Power systems:** Substation automation, generator sequencing, SCADA integration.

A well-designed PLC system replaces hundreds of mechanical relays, reduces wiring by up to **80%**, drastically lowers fault-finding time, and enables **rapid process reconfiguration** simply by changing software — not copper wiring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Two Complete PLC Designs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Directive:** "Design a PLC program for *any two simple applications*." We have selected the two most examiner-friendly, frequently-asked, and industrially relevant applications:
> 1. **Application A: Four-Way Traffic Light Control System**
> 2. **Application B: Automatic Bottle Filling Plant**

Both designs follow the rigorous KTU-mandated flow: **Problem Statement → I/O List → Memory Allocation → Ladder Diagram → Step-by-Step Working Explanation → Timing/Sequence Analysis**.

---

### 3.1 APPLICATION A: Four-Way Traffic Light Control System

#### Step 1 — Problem Statement

Design a PLC-based controller for a **two-road (North–South and East–West) intersection traffic signal**. The sequence of operation is:

- **Phase 1:** North–South gets **GREEN** for **25 s**; East–West gets **RED**.
- **Phase 2:** North–South turns **YELLOW** for **5 s**; East–West remains **RED**.
- **Phase 3:** East–West gets **GREEN** for **25 s**; North–South gets **RED**.
- **Phase 4:** East–West turns **YELLOW** for **5 s**; North–South remains **RED**.
- The cycle then **repeats** indefinitely.
- A **Master ON/OFF switch** must enable or disable the entire system. A **START push button** initiates the cycle. A **STOP push button** halts it (with all lights going to RED).
- A **FLASH BLINK** must be applied to the YELLOW lamps during transition for pedestrian warning.

#### Step 2 — Input/Output Device Identification

| Device Category | Physical Device | PLC I/O Type |
|---|---|---|
| Master Switch | 2-Position Selector | Digital Input |
| Start Button (PB1) | NO Push Button | Digital Input |
| Stop Button (PB2) | NC Push Button | Digital Input |
| NS-Red Lamp | 24 V Incandescent / LED | Digital Output |
| NS-Yellow Lamp | 24 V Incandescent / LED | Digital Output |
| NS-Green Lamp | 24 V Incandescent / LED | Digital Output |
| EW-Red Lamp | 24 V Incandescent / LED | Digital Output |
| EW-Yellow Lamp | 24 V Incandescent / LED | Digital Output |
| EW-Green Lamp | 24 V Incandescent / LED | Digital Output |

#### Step 3 — PLC Memory Address Allocation (Siemens S7-1200 Convention)

| Memory Address | Device |
|---|---|
| I0.0 | Master ON/OFF Switch |
| I0.1 | START Push Button (PB1) |
| I0.2 | STOP Push Button (PB2) |
| Q0.0 | North–South RED Lamp |
| Q0.1 | North–South YELLOW Lamp |
| Q0.2 | North–South GREEN Lamp |
| Q0.3 | East–West RED Lamp |
| Q0.4 | East–West YELLOW Lamp |
| Q0.5 | East–West GREEN Lamp |
| M0.0 | System RUN Latch Coil |
| T1 | NS-Green Timer (25 s preset) |
| T2 | NS-Yellow Timer (5 s preset) |
| T3 | EW-Green Timer (25 s preset) |
| T4 | EW-Yellow Timer (5 s preset) |
| T5 | 0.5 s Flash Pulse Generator |

#### Step 4 — Ladder Logic Program (Rung-by-Rung)

**Rung 1 — Master Control Latch:**
Master switch (I0.0) AND START button (I0.1) sets the System RUN memory M0.0; STOP (I0.2, NC) resets M0.0.

**Rung 2 — NS-Red Lamp (default ON when system is in Phase 3 or 4):**
Energized when M0.0 is ON AND NOT (NS-Green active) AND NOT (NS-Yellow active).

**Rung 3 — NS-Green Lamp (Q0.2):**
Energized when M0.0 is ON AND NS-Yellow Timer T2 is NOT done AND NS-Green Timer T1 is NOT yet timed out. (Logic: T1_Done = 0 AND T2_Done = 0 AND system running, with a flashing constraint — simplified to steady green here).

**Rung 4 — NS-Yellow Lamp (Q0.1) with Flash:**
Energized when M0.0 is ON AND T1 is DONE AND T2 is NOT yet done. Multiplied by flash pulse T5.

**Rung 5 — EW-Red Lamp (Q0.3):**
Energized when M0.0 is ON AND NOT (EW-Green active) AND NOT (EW-Yellow active).

**Rung 6 — EW-Green Lamp (Q0.5):**
Energized when M0.0 is ON AND T2 is DONE AND T3 is NOT yet done.

**Rung 7 — EW-Yellow Lamp (Q0.4) with Flash:**
Energized when M0.0 is ON AND T3 is DONE AND T4 is NOT yet done, multiplied by flash pulse T5.

**Rung 8 — Cycle Reset:**
When T4 (5 s) is DONE, it pulses M0.1 (cycle-reset flag) for one scan, which resets T1, T2, T3, T4 simultaneously, restarting the cycle.

#### Step 5 — Working Sequence Analysis

| Phase | Duration | NS Red | NS Yellow | NS Green | EW Red | EW Yellow | EW Green |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (NS-Go) | 0 – 25 s | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | OFF |
| 2 (NS-Yel) | 25 – 30 s | OFF | FLASH | OFF | ON | OFF | OFF |
| 3 (EW-Go) | 30 – 55 s | ON | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | ON |
| 4 (EW-Yel) | 55 – 60 s | ON | OFF | OFF | OFF | FLASH | OFF |
| 5 (Cycle reset) | 60 s | — | — | — | — | — | — |

**Total cycle time:**

$$T_{cycle} = T_{NS-green} + T_{NS-yellow} + T_{EW-green} + T_{EW-yellow}$$

Substituting the standard design values:

$$T_{cycle} = 25\,\text{s} + 5\,\text{s} + 25\,\text{s} + 5\,\text{s} = 60\,\text{s}$$

**Number of cycles per hour:**

$$N_{cycles/hr} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{60} = 60\,\text{cycles/hour}$$

This means **60 complete green-yellow cycles per hour** are executed — a standard urban intersection design.

#### Step 6 — Key Design Features

- **Safety Interlock:** NS-Green (Q0.2) and EW-Green (Q0.5) are **mutually exclusive** via the timer interlock chain (T1_Done → T2 → T3 → T4). A cross-traffic green-on-green accident is structurally impossible.
- **Fail-Safe Default:** On power-up or STOP, all lamps default to OFF; the NS-Red (Q0.0) and EW-Red (Q0.3) defaults are wired in the program to come ON whenever the corresponding green/yellow are OFF.
- **Pedestrian Warning:** The flashing yellow is achieved using a free-running timer T5 (preset 0.5 s) toggling an internal bit.

---

### 3.2 APPLICATION B: Automatic Bottle Filling Plant

#### Step 1 — Problem Statement

Design a PLC-based control system for an **automatic bottle filling and capping station** with the following sequence:

1. An **empty bottle** is placed on the conveyor and moves toward the filling nozzle.
2. A **proximity sensor (S1)** detects the bottle's arrival at the filling station.
3. The **conveyor motor (M1) stops**, and a **filling solenoid valve (V1)** opens.
4. Liquid flows into the bottle for a **preset time of 4 s** (T1).
5. After filling, the valve closes; the conveyor restarts and moves the bottle to the **capping station**.
6. A **second proximity sensor (S2)** detects the bottle at the capper.
7. A **capping solenoid (V2)** is energized for **2 s** (T2) to lower the cap.
8. The conveyor restarts; the cycle repeats for the next bottle.
9. A **START push button (PB1)** and **STOP push button (PB2)** control the system.
10. A **counter** records the number of bottles filled; at a preset of **10 bottles**, a "BATCH COMPLETE" lamp glows and the system halts for operator reset.

#### Step 2 — Input/Output Device Identification

| Device Category | Physical Device | PLC I/O Type |
|---|---|---|
| Start Button (PB1) | NO Push Button | Digital Input |
| Stop Button (PB2) | NC Push Button | Digital Input |
| Bottle-At-Filler Sensor (S1) | Inductive Proximity Switch | Digital Input |
| Bottle-At-Capper Sensor (S2) | Inductive Proximity Switch | Digital Input |
| Reset Batch Button (PB3) | NO Push Button | Digital Input |
| Conveyor Motor Starter (M1) | 3-Phase Contactor | Digital Output |
| Filling Solenoid Valve (V1) | 24 V Solenoid | Digital Output |
| Capping Solenoid Valve (V2) | 24 V Solenoid | Digital Output |
| RUN Indicator Lamp (L1) | 24 V LED | Digital Output |
| BATCH COMPLETE Lamp (L2) | 24 V LED | Digital Output |

#### Step 3 — PLC Memory Address Allocation

| Memory Address | Device |
|---|---|
| I0.0 | START Push Button (PB1) |
| I0.1 | STOP Push Button (PB2) |
| I0.2 | Bottle at Filler (S1) |
| I0.3 | Bottle at Capper (S2) |
| I0.4 | Reset Batch Button (PB3) |
| Q0.0 | Conveyor Motor (M1) |
| Q0.1 | Filling Valve (V1) |
| Q0.2 | Capping Valve (V2) |
| Q0.3 | RUN Lamp (L1) |
| Q0.4 | BATCH COMPLETE Lamp (L2) |
| M0.0 | System RUN Latch |
| M0.1 | Bottle-Present-at-Filler Memory |
| M0.2 | Filling-in-Progress Memory |
| M0.3 | Bottle-Present-at-Capper Memory |
| M0.4 | Capping-in-Progress Memory |
| T1 | Fill Timer (preset 4 s) |
| T2 | Cap Timer (preset 2 s) |
| C1 | Bottle Counter (preset 10) |

#### Step 4 — Ladder Logic Program (Rung-by-Rung)

**Rung 1 — System Run Latch:**
`M0.0` is set (latched) when PB1 (I0.0) is pressed, provided PB2 (I0.1) is not pressed. PB2 (NC) resets M0.0.

**Rung 2 — RUN Lamp:**
Q0.3 (RUN Lamp) is energized when M0.0 is ON.

**Rung 3 — Conveyor Motor:**
Q0.0 (M1) runs when M0.0 is ON AND bottle is NOT at filler (NOT I0.2) AND bottle is NOT at capper (NOT I0.3) AND no filling/capping in progress.

**Rung 4 — Bottle Detected at Filler → Start Filling:**
When S1 (I0.2) detects a bottle AND M0.0 is ON AND filling is NOT in progress, set memory M0.2 (filling in progress).

**Rung 5 — Filling Solenoid:**
V1 (Q0.1) is energized when M0.2 is ON; simultaneously TON timer T1 starts counting with preset 4 s.

**Rung 6 — End of Filling:**
When T1 is DONE (4 s elapsed), reset M0.2 and restart conveyor by satisfying Rung 3.

**Rung 7 — Bottle Detected at Capper → Start Capping:**
When S2 (I0.3) detects the filled bottle AND M0.0 is ON AND capping is NOT in progress, set memory M0.4.

**Rung 8 — Capping Solenoid:**
V2 (Q0.2) is energized when M0.4 is ON; simultaneously TON timer T2 starts with preset 2 s.

**Rung 9 — End of Capping → Increment Counter:**
When T2 is DONE, reset M0.4, increment counter C1, and restart conveyor.

**Rung 10 — Counter & Batch Complete:**
Counter C1 has preset 10. When C1 reaches the preset, output C1_Done energizes lamp L2 (Q0.4) and resets M0.0 (stops system).

**Rung 11 — Counter Reset:**
PB3 (I0.4) resets counter C1 to zero for the next batch.

#### Step 5 — Step-by-Step Working Explanation

| Time (s) | Event | Active Outputs | Active Timers | Counter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | PB1 pressed; system latches M0.0 | Q0.3 (Lamp), Q0.0 (Conveyor) | — | 0 |
| 3 | Bottle reaches S1; M0.2 sets | Q0.1 (Fill Valve) | T1 running (0–4 s) | 0 |
| 7 | T1 done; fill ends, conveyor resumes | Q0.0 (Conveyor) | T1 reset | 0 |
| 10 | Bottle reaches S2; M0.4 sets | Q0.2 (Cap Valve) | T2 running (0–2 s) | 0 |
| 12 | T2 done; cap ends, conveyor resumes; C1 = 1 | Q0.0 (Conveyor) | T2 reset | 1 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| t_final | C1 reaches 10; L2 ON; M0.0 resets | Q0.4 (L2) | — | 10 |
| Reset | PB3 pressed; C1 = 0; ready for next batch | — | — | 0 |

**Per-bottle cycle time:**

$$T_{bottle} = T_{approach} + T_{fill} + T_{transfer} + T_{cap}$$

Assuming transfer times of 3 s each, the per-bottle cycle is:

$$T_{bottle} = 3\,\text{s} + 4\,\text{s} + 3\,\text{s} + 2\,\text{s} = 12\,\text{s/bottle}$$

**Hourly throughput:**

$$N_{bottles/hr} = \frac{3600}{T_{bottle}} = \frac{3600}{12} = 300\,\text{bottles/hour}$$

**Time per batch (10 bottles):**

$$T_{batch} = 10 \times 12 = 120\,\text{s} = 2\,\text{minutes per batch}$$

#### Step 6 — Key Design Features

- **Sequential Latching Memory Bits (M0.1, M0.2, M0.3, M0.4):** These internal "state memory" coils act as *flags* that allow multi-step sequences to remember their current stage across multiple scans.
- **Interlock via Memory:** The conveyor (Q0.0) is automatically suspended whenever any filling or capping flag is active — preventing a bottle from moving prematurely.
- **Counter-Based Batch Control:** Uses a single CTU counter to enforce a fixed batch size, with the DONE bit shutting down the line for operator intervention.
- **Reset Safety:** Reset is a deliberate operator action (PB3) — prevents accidental batch counter reset during production.
- **STOP Dominance:** The NC stop button (I0.1) breaks the latch directly, achieving fail-safe shutdown in any state.

### 4.5 Generic Ladder Logic Rung Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[L1 Power Rail - 24V DC] --> C1[Contact 1 - NO]
    C1 --> C2[Contact 2 - NC]
    C2 --> C3[Contact 3 - NO]
    C3 --> C4[Output Coil - Lamp or Motor]
    C4 --> L2[L2 Return Rail - 0V]
    style L1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style C1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style C2 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style C3 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style C4 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style L2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C
```

> [!NOTE]
> A ladder rung executes left-to-right. Power flows from L1 to L2 only if **all contacts in series** are TRUE; the output coil is then energized. **Parallel branches** represent OR logic.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. List the major steps involved in designing a PLC-based control system. Explain the significance of the I/O list.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The major steps in PLC system design are: (i) Define the control objective, (ii) Identify all input and output devices, (iii) Assign I/O addresses, (iv) Develop the Ladder Logic program, (v) Simulate and test, and (vi) Commission and document.
The **I/O list** is significant because it acts as the *contract* between the electrical hardware design (wiring diagrams) and the software design (ladder program). It prevents address conflicts, simplifies troubleshooting, and serves as a maintenance reference. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. Explain the function of the PLC scan cycle. State one consequence if the scan time is too long.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **scan cycle** is the repetitive, deterministic execution sequence of the PLC processor comprising input reading (PII update), program execution, output writing (PIO update), and housekeeping. It guarantees that inputs are read and outputs updated at predictable intervals.
If the scan time is too long (e.g., > 50 ms for a high-speed process), the controller **cannot respond fast enough** to rapidly changing inputs, leading to **missed pulses, delayed responses, and process instability** — unacceptable in high-speed packaging or motion control applications. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024, Module 4 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Traffic Light Control**

**(a)** Design a PLC-based control system for a two-road (North–South and East–West) traffic intersection. The NS road must show GREEN for 30 s, YELLOW for 5 s, RED otherwise; the EW road must show GREEN for 30 s, YELLOW for 5 s, RED otherwise. Include a START and a master ON/OFF switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the I/O address allocation table and explain the Ladder Logic rung-by-rung. State the total cycle time and the number of cycles per hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete **Problem Statement (Step 1)**, **I/O Identification (Step 2)**, and **Memory Address Allocation (Step 3)**. Briefly state in exam:
- 6 lamps (NS-Red/Yellow/Green and EW-Red/Yellow/Green) are the 6 digital outputs.
- START button, STOP button, and Master switch are the 3 digital inputs.
- Three internal memory bits for latching and four TON timers (T1 = 30 s, T2 = 5 s, T3 = 30 s, T4 = 5 s) define the four phases.
- The sequence is: Phase 1 (T1 timing, NS-Green) → Phase 2 (T2 timing, NS-Yellow) → Phase 3 (T3 timing, EW-Green) → Phase 4 (T4 timing, EW-Yellow) → reset. **(7 marks — Problem statement 2 + I/O list 2 + Sequence logic 2 + Safety interlock 1)**

**Model Solution (b):**
Provide the address table (as in Section 3.1, Step 3). Draw the ladder diagram with at least 8 rungs (latch, NS-Red default, NS-Green, NS-Yellow, EW-Red default, EW-Green, EW-Yellow, cycle reset). The total cycle time is:

$$T_{cycle} = 30 + 5 + 30 + 5 = 70\,\text{s}$$

Number of cycles per hour:

$$N_{cycles/hr} = \frac{3600}{70} \approx 51.4\,\text{cycles/hr}$$

**(7 marks — I/O table 2 + Ladder diagram 4 + Cycle calculation 1)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Bottle Filling Plant**

**(a)** Design a PLC program for an automatic bottle filling plant with the sequence: conveyor ON → bottle detected at sensor S1 → conveyor stops → filling valve opens for 4 s → valve closes → conveyor restarts → bottle detected at sensor S2 → conveyor stops → capper energizes for 2 s → counter increments. Draw the Ladder Logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the I/O assignment table. Explain how the counter is used to stop the system after every 10 bottles. Calculate the throughput per hour assuming 4 s transfer time between stations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Refer to Section 3.2, Steps 1 to 4 of these notes. The ladder program uses 5 rungs minimum:
- Rung 1: System run latch (M0.0) controlled by PB1 and PB2.
- Rung 2: Conveyor (Q0.0) runs when no filling/capping is active.
- Rung 3: S1 (I0.2) + M0.0 → set M0.2 (fill memory).
- Rung 4: M0.2 → energize V1 (Q0.1) and start T1 (4 s).
- Rung 5: T1 done → reset M0.2, restart conveyor; S2 (I0.3) → set M0.4; M0.4 → V2 (Q0.2) + T2 (2 s); T2 done → reset M0.4, increment C1. **(7 marks — Sequence explanation 2 + Ladder diagram 4 + Memory interlock 1)**

**Model Solution (b):**
Provide the I/O table (as in Section 3.2, Step 3). The counter C1 is a CTU block that counts each bottle. When C1 reaches preset 10, its DONE bit (C1_Q) energizes a BATCH COMPLETE lamp (Q0.4) and resets the system latch M0.0. The operator presses PB3 to reset C1 to zero.
Per-bottle cycle time:

$$T_{bottle} = 4 + 4 + 4 + 2 = 14\,\text{s/bottle}$$

Throughput:

$$N_{bottles/hr} = \frac{3600}{14} \approx 257\,\text{bottles/hr}$$

Time per batch of 10:

$$T_{batch} = 10 \times 14 = 140\,\text{s}$$

**(7 marks — I/O table 2 + Counter logic 3 + Throughput calculation 2)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PLC Design Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping the I/O list** — Examiners award 2–3 marks purely for the I/O address allocation table. Many students go directly to the ladder diagram and lose these easy marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Stop / Master interlock** — A ladder diagram without a master enable or stop interlock is *unsafe* and loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not latching the Start command** — Using a momentary push button directly to drive a coil without a self-holding (latch) contact causes the output to drop the moment the button is released. Examiners specifically check for `(S)` / `(R)` or equivalent seal-in contact.
> 4. **Cross-traffic green-on-green** — In traffic light designs, the NS-Green and EW-Green outputs **must be mutually exclusive** via timer interlocks. Failure to demonstrate this loses up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Missing numerical calculations** — Cycle time, throughput, and counter-preset calculations are easy marks. Always include them.
> 6. **Forgetting the default RED** — In traffic light logic, RED is the *safe default*; wire RED to come ON whenever the corresponding GREEN/YELLOW is OFF.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (PLC Design):**

- **PLC** = solid-state, industrially hardened digital computer for real-time sequential control.
- **IEC 61131-3** standard defines **5 programming languages**; Ladder Diagram (LD) is the most widely used.
- **6 design steps:** Define task → Identify I/O → Assign addresses → Develop logic → Simulate → Commission.
- **Scan cycle formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ (deterministic, typically 5–20 ms).
- **I/O convention:** `I` = input, `Q` = output, `M` = internal memory, `T` = timer, `C` = counter.
- **NO contact** = true when input is ON; **NC contact** = true when input is OFF.
- **Output coil ( )** = energized when rung is TRUE; **Set (S) / Reset (R)** = latched bistable coils.
- **TON (On-Delay Timer)** = output becomes TRUE only after the input has been TRUE continuously for the preset time.
- **TOF (Off-Delay Timer)** = output remains TRUE for the preset time after the input goes FALSE.
- **CTU (Up Counter)** = counts rising edges of input; output TRUE when current value equals preset.
- **Traffic Light cycle:** $T_{cycle} = 4 \times T_{phase} = 60$ s typical (25 + 5 + 25 + 5).
- **Bottle Filling throughput:** $N_{bottles/hr} = 3600 / T_{bottle}$.
- **Safety rule #1:** All outputs must have a defined **OFF-state default** (fail-safe).
- **Safety rule #2:** Opposing outputs (e.g., NS-Green vs EW-Green) must be **mechanically/logically interlocked** — never energize simultaneously.
- **Safety rule #3:** The STOP button is always wired **NC** (Normally Closed) so that a wire break halts the system.
- **Latching memory bits (M0.x)** are the *flags* that allow multi-step sequences to "remember" their current stage.
- **Counters** are used for batch control, length measurement (pulse counting), and event sequencing.
- **Always draw the I/O list first**, then the **memory allocation table**, then the **ladder diagram**, then the **timing/sequence explanation** — this is the KTU 2024 examiner-preferred answer order.
- **Throughput calculation** is mandatory: $N = 3600 / T_{cycle}$ in operations per hour.
- **Counter-based batch control** = CTU counter + DONE bit driving an indicator lamp and a system reset.
- **Two applications to master:** Traffic Light Control and Bottle Filling Plant — these appear in nearly every KTU exam cycle.
- **Memorize the address convention** for at least one PLC family (Siemens S7 preferred for KTU).
- **Remember the scan cycle is deterministic** — that is the PLC's core advantage over microcontrollers for industrial control.
